title: Notification seeks to make amendments (Fourth Amendment) to the CGST Rules, 2017.
text:
section 12 of the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017 (15 of 
2017) shall be credited to the Fund:

Provided that an amount equivalent to fifty per cent. of the amount of

integrated tax determined under sub-section (5) of section 54 of the Central Goods 
and Services Tax Act, 2017, read with section 20 of the Integrated Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017, shall be deposited in the Fund.

(2) Where any amount, having been credited to the Fund, is ordered or directed to be 
paid to any claimant by the proper officer, appellate authority or court, the same shall 
be paid from the Fund.

(3) Accounts of the Fund maintained by the Central Government shall be subject to 
audit by the Comptroller and Auditor General of India.

(4) The Government shall, by an order, constitute a Standing Committee (hereinafter 
referred to as the „Committee‟) with a Chairman, a Vice-Chairman, a Member 
Secretary and such other members as it may deem fit and the Committee shall make 
recommendations for proper utilisation of the money credited to the Fund for welfare 
of the consumers.

(5) (a) The Committee shall meet as and when necessary, generally four times in a 
year;

(b) the Committee shall meet at such time and place as the Chairman, or in his 
absence, the Vice-Chairman of the Committee may deem fit;

(c) the meeting of the Committee shall be presided over by the Chairman, or in his 
absence, by the Vice-Chairman;

(d) the meeting of the Committee shall be called, after giving at least ten days‟ notice 
in writing to every member;

(e) the notice of the meeting of the Committee shall specify the place, date and hour 
of the meeting and shall contain statement of business to be transacted thereat;

(f) no proceeding of the Committee shall be valid, unless it is presided over by the 
Chairman or Vice-Chairman and attended by a minimum of three other members.

(6)  The Committee shall have powers -

(a) to require any applicant to get registered with any authority as the Central

Government may specify;

(b) to require any applicant to produce before it, or before a duly authorised

officer of the Central Government or the State Government, as the case may 
be, such books, accounts, documents, instruments, or commodities in custody 
and control of the applicant, as may be necessary for proper evaluation of the 
application;

(c) to require any applicant to allow entry and inspection of any premises, from

which activities claimed to be for the welfare of consumers are stated to be
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the Consumer Welfare Fund (CWF) rules — crediting 50% of integrated tax from Section 54 CGST refunds, payouts on orders, CAG audit, and the Standing Committee's constitution/powers. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving refund amounts flowing into the CWF and the Committee's gatekeeping role for utilization applications.

questions.q: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 06AABFM1234Q1Z5, Haryana) filed a refund claim for Rs 48 lakh of accumulated ITC on zero-rated supplies. The proper officer sanctioned the full amount under the integrated tax head after verification. Meridian's CFO now wants to understand: of the total sanctioned refund, what portion is the government required to deposit into the Consumer Welfare Fund, and under what authority? Additionally, if Meridian later wins an appeal that directs a further Rs 6 lakh payout from the Fund, what is the prescribed mechanism for that disbursement — does the appellate authority release the money directly, or must it come from the Fund through the proper officer?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's proviso specifying that 50% of integrated tax determined under Section 54 CGST read with Section 20 IGST must be deposited in the Fund is indispensable, as is sub-rule (2) prescribing that amounts ordered by proper officer/appellate authority/court shall be paid FROM the Fund.

questions.q: A consumer rights NGO based in Tamil Nadu has applied to the Standing Committee of the Consumer Welfare Fund for a Rs 15 lakh grant to run awareness camps about GST on essential commodities. The Committee's next meeting has only the Vice-Chairman and two other members present, as the Chairman is abroad and one member resigned last month. The NGO is unregistered and has refused to open its premises for inspection, citing that its accounts are with an external auditor. Can the Committee validly convene this meeting and deliberate on the application? Separately, can it compel the NGO to allow entry and inspection of the premises from which the consumer-welfare activities are claimed to operate, and to produce its books and accounts — and what happens if the NGO refuses?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-rule (5)(f) requiring the Chairman or Vice-Chairman plus minimum three other members for a valid meeting is directly tested, as is sub-rule (6)(a)–(c) granting the Committee power to require registration, document production, and premises inspection — all specific provisions in this chunk.

title: Regarding merging of commercial invoice and packing list
text:
Circular No. 01/15-Customs

F. No. 450/25/2013-Cus IV (Pt.)

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise and Customs

***

New Delhi, dated 12.01.2015

To

All Chief Commissioners of Customs / Customs (Preventive)

All Chief Commissioners of Customs and Central Excise

All Commissioners of Customs / Customs (Preventive)

All Commissioners of Customs and Central Excise

Sir/ Madam,

Subject: Merging of Commercial invoice and packing list - reg

Simplification of Customs procedures for enhanced ease of doing business and trade facilitation is the top priority of the
Government. One of the identified areas for such simplification is reduction in the number of mandatory documents required by
Customs for import and export of goods.

2.         As per the extant Customs procedures for both  import and export, an importer / exporter is required to submit a
commercial invoice and packing list along with  the Customs declaration form viz. Bill of Entry/Shipping Bill. Both commercial
invoice and packing list are critical for Customs purposes as the former evidences the value of the import/ export goods while
the latter facilitates examination of goods for ascertaining correctness of duty and quantity.  However, there are many identical
data fields in a commercial invoice and packing list. Therefore, an exercise was undertaken to explore the feasibility whether
these documents can be merged into one document, which would have the advantage of reducing the total number of
documents to be submitted to Customs with resultant benefit to trade. In this regard, it is seen that the following data fields /
information are invariably contained in a packing list (other than the common data fields / details of commercial invoice):

Description of Goods;

Marks and Numbers;

Quantity;

Gross Weight;

Net Weight;

Number of Packages;

Types of Packages (such as pallet, box, crates, drums etc.).

3.         The Board has decided that as a measure of simplification, in case an importer/exporter submits a commercial
invoice cum packing list that contain above mentioned data fields / information in addition to the details in a commercial
invoice, a separate packing list should not be insisted upon by Customs. However, the option should be given to the
importer/exporter to do so. In other words, for Customs purposes a c ommercial invoice cum packing list (with details of
marks and numbers as mentioned in para 2 above) would suffice but if importer/exporter desires to give a separate packing
list for some reason, the same would also be accepted, as at present.

4.         Board desires that all Chief Commissioner of Customs should ensure that above guidelines are complied with
scrupulously by the field formations. Wide publicity may also be given to trade and industry stakeholders to sensitize them
about the guidelines. Chief Commissioners of Customs should also monitor compliance thereof at their level.

5.         Difficulty, faced, if any may be brought to the notice of the Board.

Yours sincerely,
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Circular 01/15-Customs on merging commercial invoice and packing list into a single document for Customs clearance. It specifies the exact data fields required in a combined document and confirms that Customs cannot insist on a separate packing list if the combined document contains all required fields. This supports HIGH-complexity questions involving documentary compliance disputes during import/export clearance.

questions.q: Meridian Imports LLP, based in Mumbai, is importing industrial compressors from a supplier in Germany. Their customs broker filed the Bill of Entry at JNCH Nhava Sheva along with a single commercial invoice that includes unit prices, total value, payment terms, and shipping terms — but does not list the gross weight, net weight, or marks and numbers for each package. The appraising officer has refused to process the consignment, demanding a separate packing list. Meridian's in-house counsel argues that since CBIC has allowed merged documents, the officer cannot insist on a separate packing list. The customs broker counters that the merged document is incomplete and therefore the officer is within rights to demand a separate packing list. Who is correct, and what specific data fields must the combined commercial invoice cum packing list contain for Customs to accept it as a standalone document without requiring a separate packing list?
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 2 and 3 of the circular enumerate the exact data fields (gross weight, net weight, marks and numbers, number/types of packages, quantity, description) that must be present in a combined document. If any are missing, the document is incomplete and the officer's demand is justified — this chunk is the sole authoritative source for that field list.

questions.q: Sarvottam Agri Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1ZP), a Maharashtra-based exporter of basmati rice, ships a consolidated consignment to three different buyers in UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar under a single Shipping Bill filed at Mundra port. Their freight forwarder prepares one combined commercial invoice cum packing list covering all three buyers, but the document only shows aggregate quantity and total gross weight for the entire shipment without breaking down marks and numbers, number of packages, and types of packages buyer-wise. The Customs officer at the port refuses clearance, stating the merged document must contain package-level details. Sarvottam's CA argues that the circular on merging documents was meant to reduce documentation burden and that aggregate figures should suffice for a single Shipping Bill. Meanwhile, the buyer in Saudi Arabia requires a separate packing list for their import clearance, which Sarvottam has prepared but not submitted to Indian Customs. Does the circular mandate package-level granularity in the combined document, and is Sarvottam obligated to submit the separate packing list prepared for the Saudi buyer to Indian Customs as well?
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 3 specifies that the combined document must contain ALL the data fields listed in para 2 (including marks and numbers, number of packages, types of packages) to replace the separate packing list — aggregate figures would not satisfy these field requirements. Para 3 also clarifies that submitting a separate packing list remains optional for the exporter, so the Saudi buyer's copy need not be filed with Indian Customs. Both answers hinge on this circular's specific provisions.

title: Clarification on place of supply of Online Services supplied by the suppliers of services to unregistered recipients
text:
(a) made to a registered person shall be the location of such person;

(b) made to any person other than a registered person shall be, -

(i) the location of the recipient where the address on record exists; and

(ii) the location of the supplier of services in other cases.”

3.5 
As per sub-section (2) of Section 31 of the CGST Act,

“(2) A registered person supplying taxable services shall, before or after the provision of  service

but within a prescribed period, issue a tax invoice, showing the description, value, tax charged

thereon and such other particulars as may be prescribed:”

3.6  
Rule 46 of CGST Rules provides as below:

" 46. Subject to rule 54, a tax invoice referred to in section 31 shall be issued by the registered

person containing the following particulars, namely,-

…

(f) name and address of the recipient and the address of delivery, along with the name of the State

and its code, if such recipient is unregistered and where the value of the taxable supply is less

than fifty thousand rupees and the recipient requests that such details be recorded in the tax

invoice;

Provided that in cases involving supply of online money gaming or in cases that where any

taxable service is supplied by or through an electronic commerce operator or by a supplier of

online information and database access or retrieval services to a recipient who is unregistered,

irrespective of the value of such supply, a tax invoice issued by the registered person shall

contain the name of the State of the recipient and the same shall be deemed to be the address on

record of the recipient;

…..”

4. Clarification:

4.1 
Section 12 of the IGST Act provides that except in cases specified in sub-sections (3) to

(14) of the said section, when the services are supplied to a registered person, the place of supply

of services shall be the location of the recipient and when the services are supplied to an

unregistered person, the place of supply of the said services shall be the location of the

recipient, if his address is available on record,  and shall be the location of the supplier, if the

address is not available on record.

Page 3 of 5
claude_reasoning: This chunk clarifies place-of-supply rules for online/OIDAR services to unregistered recipients under IGST Section 12, combined with Rule 46 invoice requirements (state name on invoice = deemed address on record). It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-jurisdiction place-of-supply determination, invoice compliance for e-commerce/OIDAR suppliers, and the interplay between IGST Act and CGST Rules.

questions.q: PixelStream Media LLP, registered in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABFP1234R1ZX), operates an OTT video streaming platform. They supply subscriptions to unregistered individual consumers across India. For a subscriber in Jaipur, Rajasthan, the platform's app collected the subscriber's name and city but not their full address — however the invoice auto-populated 'Rajasthan' as the state. For another subscriber who used a VPN and provided no location details whatsoever, the invoice was issued without any state name. The CFO wants to know: in each case, what is the place of supply for GST purposes, which tax applies (CGST+SGST or IGST), and whether the invoices as issued are compliant — specifically, does merely recording the state name on the invoice for the Jaipur subscriber legally constitute having an 'address on record' so that Rajasthan becomes the place of supply?
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso to Rule 46(f) deems the state name recorded on a tax invoice by an OIDAR/online service supplier to be the 'address on record' of an unregistered recipient, which directly determines whether IGST Section 12(2)(b)(i) or (ii) applies — this deemed-address mechanism is the crux of both scenarios.

questions.q: GameVerse Technologies Pvt Ltd, a real-money online gaming platform registered in Karnataka (GSTIN 29AABCG5678M1Z5), supplies services to unregistered players nationwide. During an internal audit for Q3 FY 2025-26, they discovered that for transactions below ₹50,000 where players did not request address details, invoices were issued without the recipient's state name — relying on the general Rule 46(f) exemption for low-value B2C supplies. The tax team argues this was correct since each transaction was under ₹50,000. However, the external auditor flags that online money gaming has a specific carve-out. Who is right, and what is the GST and place-of-supply consequence if the state was not captured — does the supply default to an intra-state supply in Karnataka, and can GameVerse retrospectively correct the invoices and GST returns to claim the correct treatment?
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso to Rule 46(f) explicitly carves out 'supply of online money gaming' from the ₹50,000 threshold, requiring the recipient's state name on every invoice irrespective of value — this carve-out overrides the general rule the tax team relied on, and the deemed-address-on-record mechanism determines whether place of supply falls under Section 12(2)(b)(i) or defaults to supplier location under (ii).

title: Seeks to amend Notification No.130/2010- Customs dated 23.12.2010 to extend the exemption benefits to Air Canada.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION

3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 32/2025-Customs

New Delhi dated the 30th June, 2025

G.S.R...(E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 25 of the Customs Act,

1962 (52 of 1962), the Central Government, on being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to 
do, hereby makes the following further amendments in the notification of the Government of India in the 
Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 130/2010-Customs, dated the 23rd December, 2010, 
published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 
1008(E), dated the 23rd December, 2010, namely :-

In the said notification, in the TABLE, after S. No. 47 and entries relating thereto, the following serial

number and entries shall be inserted, namely:-

(1) 
(2) 
(3)

“48. 
Canada 
Air Canada”.

2. 
This notification shall come into force on the 1st day of July, 2025.

[F. No. 190354/87/2022-TRU]

(Dheeraj Sharma)

Under Secretary to Government of India

Note: The principal notification No. 130/2010-Customs, dated the 23rd December, 2010 was published in the 
Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-Section (i), vide number G.S.R. 1008(E), dated the 23rd 
December, 2010 and was last amended vide notification No. 45/2022-Customs, dated the 31st August, 2022, 
published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 670(E), 
dated the 31st August, 2022.
claude_reasoning: This chunk is Notification 32/2025-Customs amending Notification 130/2010-Customs to extend customs duty exemption benefits to Air Canada (Canada, S.No.48) effective 1-Jul-2025. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving cross-act interaction (Customs exemption vs GST/IGST on imports), multi-entity reasoning (linking the parent notification's exemption scheme with section 25 powers and airline-specific entries), and scenario-based practitioner queries about duty liability on stores/equipment imported by or for Air Canada.

questions.q: Skybridge Aviation Services LLP, based in Mumbai, has a ground-handling and catering contract with Air Canada for its newly launched Delhi–Toronto route. In the first week of July 2025, Skybridge imports aircraft catering equipment and in-flight meal containers from a Canadian supplier, shipping them to IGI Airport, New Delhi. Skybridge claims that since the goods are exclusively for use by Air Canada — an airline now covered under the amended exemption notification for foreign airlines — the consignment should be exempt from basic customs duty under the same benefit. Customs at IGI Airport has issued a query memo asking Skybridge to justify the exemption claim. Separately, Skybridge is also unclear whether IGST on these imports can be claimed as input tax credit under GST, given that their output supply (catering services to Air Canada) may qualify as an export of service or a zero-rated supply. How should Skybridge respond to the customs query, and what is the correct duty and tax treatment of this import?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that Notification 32/2025 extends exemption benefits specifically to 'Air Canada' as an airline entry (S.No.48) in the table of Notification 130/2010. Determining whether a third-party ground handler like Skybridge can claim this exemption — which is airline-specific, not contractor-specific — requires interpreting the scope of the table entry and the parent notification's conditions, making this chunk indispensable.

questions.q: TransGlobal Duty Free Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 07AABCT1234R1ZP), a licensed warehouse operator at Delhi's IGI Airport, supplies bonded stores — liquor, tobacco, and confectionery — to multiple foreign airlines for consumption on outbound international flights. Prior to 1 July 2025, TransGlobal was paying basic customs duty on stores supplied to Air Canada because Air Canada was not listed in the exemption notification applicable to foreign airlines. Now, Air Canada's procurement manager has asked TransGlobal to retrospectively seek a refund of customs duty paid on bonded stores supplied between January and June 2025, arguing that the amendment should be read as clarificatory and therefore retrospective. Additionally, for stores supplied from 1 July 2025 onward, Air Canada wants TransGlobal to confirm that no customs duty, no IGST, and no compensation cess will apply. What is the correct legal position on both the retrospective refund claim and the prospective duty/tax treatment of bonded stores supplied to Air Canada from July 2025?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states 'This notification shall come into force on the 1st day of July, 2025,' establishing prospective applicability. It also shows Air Canada was only added as S.No.48 by this amendment — meaning it was NOT covered before this date. Both facts are essential to rejecting the retrospective refund claim and confirming the prospective exemption, making this chunk the indispensable authority.

title: Application for Withdrawl
text:
3. The form may be filled up if amount from one major / minor head is intended to be transferred to another major/minor head. Minor head for

transfer of amount may be same or different.

4. The amount from one minor head can also be transferred to another minor head under the same major head.  
5. Amount can be transferred from the head only if balance under that head is available at the time of transfer.]

252
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the rules for transferring amounts between major/minor heads in the electronic cash ledger (Form for withdrawal/transfer under Section 18(12)(b)), including constraints on same/different minor heads and balance availability. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving cash ledger management across tax heads with real practitioner scenarios.

questions.q: Pinnacle Polymers LLP, registered in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AABFP1234M1ZQ), made an excess deposit of ₹8,50,000 under the IGST head while paying for an inter-state purchase from a supplier in Maharashtra. However, their actual IGST liability for the month turned out to be only ₹2,10,000, and they now have a pending CGST liability of ₹4,00,000 and SGST liability of ₹2,40,000 from intra-state sales within Gujarat. Their accountant wants to transfer the surplus amount lying under the IGST minor head in the electronic cash ledger to the CGST and SGST heads rather than claiming a refund. Can the firm transfer amounts from the IGST head to both CGST and SGST heads directly through the cash ledger, and what conditions must be satisfied at the time of initiating such transfer — particularly, does the balance need to be available in the source head at the exact time of filing, or can it be done in anticipation of a future deposit?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's rule that 'amount can be transferred from the head only if balance under that head is available at the time of transfer' and the provision allowing transfer from one major/minor head to another (including different minor heads) are indispensable to determining whether this cross-head transfer is permissible and what the timing constraint is.

questions.q: Sarvottam Engineering Pvt Ltd, based in Jaipur, Rajasthan (GSTIN 08AADCS5678N1Z5), inadvertently deposited ₹12,00,000 under the 'Interest' minor head of CGST instead of the 'Tax' minor head while making their monthly GST payment through the electronic cash ledger. They have zero outstanding interest liability but have a confirmed CGST tax demand of ₹11,50,000 for the same period, plus a separate penalty of ₹50,000 under CGST. Their CA is asking whether the entire ₹12,00,000 can be reallocated — first ₹11,50,000 from the 'Interest' minor head to the 'Tax' minor head under CGST, and then ₹50,000 from 'Interest' to 'Penalty' minor head under the same CGST major head — all in a single application, or whether separate applications are needed for each transfer, and whether the second transfer can proceed if the first one has already reduced the available balance.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's explicit provision that 'amount from one minor head can also be transferred to another minor head under the same major head' and the balance-availability-at-time-of-transfer rule are directly required to resolve whether sequential intra-major-head transfers are valid and how the diminishing balance affects the second transfer.

Note: - The principal notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017, was

claude_reasoning: This chunk is Notification 02/2024-Integrated Tax (Rate) effective 15-Jul-2024, amending IGST rate schedule 1/2017. It covers: reclassification of paper/paperboard packaging (HSN 4819), carve-outs for milk cans (iron/steel and aluminium), addition of solar cookers, and critically a proviso that agricultural farm produce in packages >25 kg or >25 litre is NOT 'pre-packaged and labelled'. These rate amendments and the agricultural packaging proviso support multi-entity, cross-act, and legal-interpretation scenarios.

questions.q: Our client, Kisaan Agri Commodities LLP based in Indore (Madhya Pradesh), supplies unbranded whole wheat grain to Rajesh Trading Co. in Nagpur (Maharashtra). They pack the wheat in 30 kg jute bags with only the net weight printed on the bag — no brand name, no retail sale declaration. Until June 2024, they were treating this as exempt (nil-rated) on the basis that it is not 'pre-packaged and labelled' agricultural produce. Now, for invoices raised from August 2024 onwards, the buyer's CA is insisting that since the bag shows weight and exceeds 25 kg, it must be treated as pre-packaged and labelled, attracting 5% IGST. Kisaan's management wants to know: (a) post the July 2024 amendment, does a 30 kg package of wheat with only net quantity marked — no brand, no declaration under the Legal Metrology Act — qualify as 'pre-packaged and labelled' for GST purposes, and (b) since this is an inter-state supply from MP to Maharashtra, does the IGST rate notification or the CGST rate notification govern the classification, and is there any divergence between the two on this specific proviso?
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso inserted after Schedule VII Explanation clause (ii) explicitly states that agricultural farm produce in packages >25 kg shall NOT be considered 'pre-packaged and labelled' notwithstanding the Legal Metrology Act — this is the dispositive provision for determining whether the 30 kg wheat bag attracts GST.

questions.q: Bharat Steel Fabricators Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCB1234R1ZP, Pune) manufactures milk storage and transport cans in both stainless steel and aluminium variants. They have a purchase order dated 20-Jul-2024 from Amul Dairy Cooperative in Anand, Gujarat — 500 steel milk cans (HSN 7310) and 300 aluminium milk cans (HSN 7612) to be shipped directly to Amul's collection centres across Gujarat. Until now Bharat Steel was charging 18% IGST on both variants. Their tax consultant says that a mid-2024 rate notification has moved milk cans to a lower slab, but Bharat Steel is unsure whether both metals get the same treatment, what the new applicable IGST rate is for each variant, and from exactly which date the reduced rate kicks in — especially since the PO was placed on 20-Jul-2024 but some cans were dispatched on 12-Jul-2024 (before the notified effective date) and the rest on 22-Jul-2024. How should Bharat Steel correctly invoice these two shipments?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clauses (ii) and (iv)/(v) of this notification carve out milk cans of iron/steel from serial 224 and milk cans of aluminium from serial 273, moving aluminium milk cans to serial 275A — the specific serial-number amendments and the effective date of 15-Jul-2024 are indispensable to determining the correct rate for each metal type and each dispatch date.

title: Amendments  in the notification  number 33/2002-Customs(N.T.), dated the 29th May, 2002

claude_reasoning: This chunk amends Duty Drawback Schedule (Notification 33/2002-Customs N.T.) via Notification 80/2002, covering drawback rates/descriptions for textiles (Chapters 52, 57, 62, 63, 64) and critically amends General Note 2(e) to deny drawback when Rule 18 rebate on excise duty is availed. The CENVAT-availed vs not-availed distinctions and specific rate caps create rich cross-provision complexity.

questions.q: Rajaram Textiles LLP, based in Panipat, Haryana, exports a consignment of cotton chenille rag rug durries (cotton content 72% by weight) to a buyer in Dubai. The FOB value of the shipment is Rs. 18,50,000. Rajaram has availed CENVAT credit on the imported synthetic yarn blended into the durries. Separately, Rajaram also exported dyed handloom cotton lungies worth Rs. 4,20,000 FOB in the same shipping bill, without availing any CENVAT credit. Their customs broker is claiming drawback on both items under the textile drawback schedule. Can Rajaram claim duty drawback on both product lines, and if so, under which serial numbers and at what rates? Does the CENVAT availment on the durries affect the lungi claim or are they assessed independently?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifically substitutes the description for Serial 57.02 to include 'Cotton Durries / Cotton Chenille Rag Rug Durries' where cotton predominates by weight, and Serial 52.01 explicitly excludes lungies from dyed/coloured handloom fabrics — both product classifications and the CENVAT condition are indispensable from this notification.

questions.q: Snowpeak Apparels Pvt Ltd, registered in Ludhiana, Punjab, manufactures men's blazers from wool-worsted blended fabric (wool content 38%) and exports them to a UK buyer at an FOB value of Rs. 32,00,000. Snowpeak has availed CENVAT facility on inputs. However, Snowpeak also filed a rebate claim under Rule 18 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002 on the excise duty paid on some of the same input fabrics used in manufacturing these blazers, before their CA flagged a potential conflict. What is the applicable drawback rate for the blazers, and does the simultaneous claim of Rule 18 rebate on input materials disqualify Snowpeak from receiving any drawback under General Note 2(e)? Would the position differ if the blazers were made from shoddy yarn with 40% wool content?
questions.why_this_chunk: Serial 62.151 inserted by this notification prescribes the 2.8% FOB drawback rate specifically for woollen garments with wool content not less than 35% when CENVAT is availed, excluding shoddy fabric/yarn. The amended General Note 2(e) substituted by clause (a) of this very notification bars drawback when Rule 18 rebate is claimed — both provisions are indispensable.

title: Fourteenth amendment to the CGST Rules, 2017.
text:
5D 
Aggregate value of deemed supplies under Schedule I of the CGST Act, 2017 
shall be declared here. Any deemed supply which is already part of the 
turnover in the audited Annual Financial Statement is not required to be 
included here.

5E 
Aggregate value of credit notes which were issued after 31st of March for any 
supply accounted in the current financial year but such credit notes were 
reflected in the annual return (GSTR-9) shall be declared here.

5F 
Trade discounts which are accounted for in the audited Annual Financial 
Statement but on which GST was leviable (being not permissible) shall be 
declared here.

5G 
Turnover included in the audited Annual Financial Statement for April 2017 to 
June 2017 shall be declared here.

5H 
Unbilled revenue which was recorded in the books of accounts on the basis of 
accrual system of accounting during the current financial year but GST was 
not payable on such revenue in the same financial year shall be declared here.

5I 
Value of all advances for which GST has not been paid but the same has been 
recognized as revenue in the audited Annual Financial Statement shall be 
declared here.

5J 
Aggregate value of credit notes which have been accounted for in the audited 
Annual Financial Statement but were not admissible under Section 34 of the 
CGST Act shall be declared here.

5K 
Aggregate value of all goods supplied by SEZs to DTA units for which the 
DTA units have filed bill of entry shall be declared here.

5L 
There may be cases where registered persons might have opted out of the 
composition scheme during the current financial year. Their turnover as per 
the audited Annual Financial Statement would include turnover both as 
composition taxpayer as well as normal taxpayer. Therefore, the turnover for 
which GST was paid under the composition scheme shall be declared here.

5M 
There may be cases where the taxable value and the invoice value differ due to 
valuation principles under section 15 of the CGST Act, 2017 and rules 
thereunder. Therefore, any difference between the turnover reported in the 
Annual Return (GSTR 9) and turnover reported in the audited Annual 
Financial Statement due to difference in valuation of supplies shall be declared 
here.

5N 
Any difference between the turnover reported in the Annual Return (GSTR9) 
and turnover reported in the audited Annual Financial Statement due to 
foreign exchange fluctuations shall be declared here.

5O 
Any difference between the turnover reported in the Annual Return (GSTR9) 
and turnover reported in the audited Annual Financial Statement due to 
reasons not listed above shall be declared here.

5Q 
Annual turnover as declared in the Annual Return (GSTR 9) shall be declared 
here. This turnover may be derived from Sr. No. 5N, 10 and 11 of Annual 
Return (GSTR 9).
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GSTR-9C reconciliation statement instructions (Tables 5D–5Q), detailing how to reconcile audited Annual Financial Statement turnover with GSTR-9 annual return turnover — including deemed supplies, credit notes, trade discounts, unbilled revenue, advances, SEZ-to-DTA supplies, composition scheme transitions, valuation differences under Section 15, and forex fluctuations. These reconciliation fields are fertile ground for HIGH-complexity practitioner scenarios involving multiple adjustments simultaneously.

questions.q: Our client, Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234E1ZP), headquartered in Pune, Maharashtra, is preparing its GSTR-9C reconciliation for FY 2024-25. Their audited financials show total revenue of ₹18.4 crore, but the GSTR-9 annual return reports turnover of only ₹16.9 crore. The differences arise from three simultaneous issues: (a) they transitioned out of composition scheme in September 2024, with ₹62 lakh turnover under composition in H1, (b) they issued credit notes totalling ₹38 lakh in May 2025 for defective polymer batches supplied in February 2025 — these are reflected in GSTR-9 but not in the audited financials for FY 2024-25, and (c) they recorded ₹45 lakh of unbilled revenue on an accrual basis for a long-term supply contract with Reliance Industries where GST was not yet payable since invoicing had not occurred. Additionally, their audited financials include ₹12 lakh in volume-based trade discounts given to distributors that were not passed through credit notes and on which GST was originally charged. How should each of these items be reported across Tables 5E, 5F, 5H, and 5L of the reconciliation statement, and what is the correct reconciled turnover that should appear at Table 5Q?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Table 5L (composition scheme transition turnover), 5E (post-March credit notes reflected in GSTR-9), 5H (unbilled accrual revenue where GST not yet payable), and 5F (trade discounts on which GST was leviable) are each indispensable for classifying these four adjustments correctly and arriving at the 5Q reconciled figure.

questions.q: Prashant & Associates LLP, a CA firm in Bengaluru, is auditing Vayuveg Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AADCV5678F1Z2) for FY 2024-25. Vayuveg operates as an EOU and also procures inputs from Kandla SEZ. Their audited Annual Financial Statement shows ₹24.6 crore turnover, while GSTR-9 shows ₹23.1 crore. The auditors have identified the following reconciling items: (i) ₹48 lakh of goods received from the SEZ unit for which Vayuveg filed bills of entry as a DTA recipient, (ii) deemed supplies of ₹32 lakh under Schedule I comprising stock transfers to their Hyderabad branch (GSTIN 36AADCV5678F1Z8) that are already included in the audited revenue, (iii) a forex fluctuation loss of ₹27 lakh on export receivables that reduced audited revenue but has no GST impact, and (iv) credit notes of ₹22 lakh issued to a Dubai buyer for quality rejections — these were booked in FY 2024-25 accounts but are not admissible under Section 34 since the recipient is outside India and no tax adjustment is possible. The auditors need to know: which specific tables in the reconciliation statement should capture each of these four items, should the SEZ-to-DTA supply value be excluded or included when computing the gap, and what is the treatment of the inadmissible export credit notes — do they increase or decrease the reconciled turnover at 5Q?
questions.why_this_chunk: This question directly requires Table 5K (SEZ-to-DTA bill of entry supplies), 5D (deemed supplies under Schedule I with the proviso about excluding those already in audited financials), 5N (forex fluctuation differences), and 5J (credit notes not admissible under Section 34) — all specific instructions from this chunk — plus understanding how they feed into 5Q.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GSTR-9 annual return instructions for declaring ITC availed in a subsequent financial year (per second proviso to section 16(2)), refund aggregation (Tables 15A-15D), and demand/order details (Tables 15E-15G). It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving cross-year ITC reclaim timing, annual return disclosure obligations, and interplay between GSTR-3B monthly filings and GSTR-9 annual reconciliation.

questions.q: Meridian Auto Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZP, Maharashtra) received capital goods from a vendor in Gujarat in March 2019 worth ₹48 lakh plus ₹8.64 lakh IGST. The company's accounts team missed claiming ITC on these invoices in the March 2019 GSTR-3B. They eventually claimed the ITC in the GSTR-3B filed for July 2019. Separately, during FY 2018-19, the company had reversed ₹2.1 lakh of ITC under the second proviso to section 16(2) on certain invoices where payment was not made within 180 days; these payments were finally settled in November 2019, and the ITC was reclaimed in the November 2019 GSTR-3B. Now, while preparing the annual return for FY 2018-19, the CA is confused: should the ₹8.64 lakh ITC availed in July 2019 for goods received in FY 2018-19 be disclosed in the FY 2018-19 annual return, and where exactly should the ₹2.1 lakh reclaimed ITC be reported — in the FY 2018-19 annual return or the FY 2019-20 annual return? The company also wants to know if they can simply skip these tables given the optional relaxation available for early financial years.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly prescribes that ITC for goods received in a previous FY but availed in April-September of the next FY must be declared in the annual return using Table 4(A) of GSTR-3B details, and that ITC reversed per second proviso to section 16(2) but reclaimed in the next FY must be reported in that next FY's annual return. It also specifies the option to not fill these tables for FY 2017-18, 2018-19, and 2019-20.

questions.q: Prakash Electricals LLP (GSTIN 29AAFFP5678K1Z5, Karnataka) had the following events during FY 2019-20 and FY 2020-21: (a) refund of ₹12.4 lakh claimed in January 2020 for inverted duty structure on wiring harnesses — ₹9.8 lakh was sanctioned in March 2020 and ₹2.6 lakh was rejected; (b) a separate IGST export refund of ₹6.2 lakh filed in March 2020 for which only an acknowledgement was received but no order was passed by 31 March 2020; (c) a demand order was issued in February 2020 confirming ₹3.5 lakh of CGST and ₹3.5 lakh of SGST on alleged wrongful ITC, of which the firm paid ₹2 lakh under protest; and (d) ITC of ₹1.8 lakh on services received in FY 2019-20 was claimed only in the August 2020 GSTR-3B after the invoices were traced during an internal audit. The firm's tax consultant needs to know exactly how each of these four items should be reported across Tables 15A through 15G and the ITC reconciliation section in the FY 2019-20 GSTR-9, and whether the firm can exercise the option to leave any of these tables blank given the relaxation notifications.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk provides the authoritative instructions for Tables 15A-15D (refund claimed/sanctioned/rejected/pending aggregation excluding provisional refunds and non-GST claims), Tables 15E-15G (confirmed demands and taxes paid thereon), the ITC-availed-in-next-FY disclosure rule referencing Table 4(A) of GSTR-3B for April-September window, and the optional relaxation inserted by Notification 56/2019 and extended by Notification 79/2020 for FY 2017-18, 2018-19, and 2019-20.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GSTR-9C reconciliation Tables 5E–5K, which require reconciling audited financial statements with GST returns — covering credit notes post-March, non-permissible trade discounts, pre-GST turnover (Apr–Jun 2017), unbilled revenue, advances recognized as revenue, inadmissible credit notes under Section 34, and SEZ-to-DTA supplies. These tables are rich ground for HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-entity reconciliation, cross-act interpretation, and real-world audit scenarios.

questions.q: Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234R1ZP), a Mumbai-based manufacturer, follows accrual accounting and reported Rs 4.82 crore as unbilled revenue in its audited financials for FY 2023-24 — representing goods dispatched in March 2024 but invoiced only in April 2024. Separately, during the same FY, Meridian issued credit notes totalling Rs 1.15 crore to three buyers across Gujarat and Karnataka for quality rejections, but the department is contesting that these credit notes do not satisfy the conditions under Section 34 of the CGST Act because the recipients have not reversed their ITC. While filing the annual reconciliation statement, Meridian's CA is unsure: (a) should the Rs 4.82 crore unbilled revenue be added to turnover in the reconciliation even though GST was discharged on it in April 2024 returns, and (b) for the Rs 1.15 crore in disputed credit notes, must the full amount be declared as an addition to turnover in the reconciliation if Section 34 admissibility is under challenge, and what is the interplay between the GSTR-9 annual return figures and the audited P&L for these two adjustments?
questions.why_this_chunk: Table 5H specifically governs unbilled revenue recognized on accrual basis where GST was not payable in the same FY, and Table 5J requires declaration of credit notes accounted in audited financials but inadmissible under Section 34 CGST Act — both are indispensable to determining the correct reconciliation treatment.

questions.q: Pinnacle Electronics LLP (GSTIN 29AAGFP5678Q1Z5) in Bengaluru operates as a DTA unit and procured semiconductor components worth Rs 2.3 crore during FY 2023-24 from Foxconn India SEZ unit in Tamil Nadu, filing bills of entry for each consignment. In the same year, Pinnacle offered trade discounts of Rs 38 lakh to its channel partners — these discounts were post-supply volume incentives not linked to specific invoices, recorded as expenses in the audited annual accounts but no credit notes were issued and no GST adjustment was made at the time of supply. Pinnacle's auditor is now preparing the GSTR-9C reconciliation and wants to know: (a) how should the Rs 2.3 crore SEZ-to-DTA procurement value be treated in the turnover reconciliation given that bills of entry were filed but these are inward supplies not outward turnover, and (b) must the Rs 38 lakh in post-supply trade discounts — which were not passed through tax credit notes and hence GST was collected on the full invoice value — be declared as an upward adjustment to reconciled turnover, and under which specific table of the reconciliation statement should each item appear?
questions.why_this_chunk: Table 5K mandates declaration of SEZ-to-DTA supplies where bills of entry were filed, and Table 5F requires declaration of trade discounts in audited financials on which GST was leviable (being not permissible as deduction) — both tables are directly needed to resolve the reconciliation treatment for each item.

title: Seeks to extend the due date for furnishing FORM GSTR-3B for the month of October, 2024 for registered persons whose principal place of business is in the district of Murshidabad in the state of West Bengal.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3,

SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

CENTRAL BOARD OF INDIRECT TAXES AND CUSTOMS

NOTIFICATION

No. 30/2024 – CENTRAL TAX

New Delhi, the 10th December, 2024

G.S.R.....(E).— In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and

Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017), the Commissioner, on the recommendations of the Council, hereby

extends the due date for furnishing the return in FORM GSTR-3B for the month of October, 2024 till the

eleventh day of December, 2024, for the registered persons whose principal place of business is in the district of

Murshidabad in the state of West Bengal and are required to furnish return under sub-section (1) of section 39

read with clause (i) of sub-rule (1) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

2. This notification shall be deemed to have come into force with effect from the 20th day of November, 2024.

[F. No. CBIC-20001/10/2024-GST]

(Raushan Kumar)

Under Secretary
claude_reasoning: This chunk is Notification 30/2024-CT extending the GSTR-3B due date for October 2024 to 11-Dec-2024 specifically for registered persons in Murshidabad district, West Bengal, issued under s.39(6) CGST Act. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving district-specific relief interplay with late-fee/interest provisions and multi-location business scenarios.

questions.q: Biswas Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 19AABCB1234R1Z5) is a monthly filer with its principal place of business registered in Murshidabad, West Bengal, and an additional place of business in Malda, West Bengal. The company filed its GSTR-3B for October 2024 on 8th December 2024 — well after the normal 20th November deadline. The GST portal auto-computed a late fee of ₹2,950 and interest under section 50 on a net tax liability of ₹4,18,000. The company's accountant believes no late fee or interest is payable because the government extended the deadline for Murshidabad-based taxpayers. However, the jurisdictional officer is arguing that because the company also has an additional place of business in Malda (which was not covered by any extension notification), the extension does not apply. Who is correct, and is interest and late fee payable for the period between 21st November and 8th December 2024?
questions.why_this_chunk: The notification specifically extends the due date for persons whose 'principal place of business' is in Murshidabad district — the answer turns on whether the relief is keyed to principal place of business (as this notification states) regardless of additional places of business, and the deemed-effective date of 20th November 2024 in paragraph 2.

questions.q: Ganguly Iron & Steel LLP (GSTIN 19AADCG5678P1ZQ), a monthly GSTR-3B filer with principal place of business in Berhampore, Murshidabad, West Bengal, made an inter-state supply of TMT bars worth ₹32,00,000 to a dealer in Jharkhand on 18th October 2024 and collected IGST of ₹5,76,000. The LLP filed its GSTR-3B for October 2024 on 10th December 2024, reporting both CGST/SGST on intra-state sales and IGST on the Jharkhand supply. The Jharkhand buyer wants to claim ITC of the IGST in his own October 2024 GSTR-3B (filed on 20th November). The buyer's CA is concerned that because the supplier's return was filed late (after the normal due date but before 11th December), the ITC may be reflected late in GSTR-2B and could attract a mismatch or reversal under rule 36(4). Does the extended due date available to Murshidabad-based suppliers affect the timeline of ITC reflection in the recipient's GSTR-2B, and could the recipient face any adverse consequences for claiming ITC before the supplier's return is actually filed?
questions.why_this_chunk: The notification's extension of the GSTR-3B deadline to 11-Dec-2024 for Murshidabad registrants is central — it determines whether Ganguly's 10-Dec filing is 'on time,' which in turn affects whether GSTR-2B auto-population for the Jharkhand buyer treats the supplier's return as timely and whether the ITC claim triggers rule 36(4) mismatch concerns.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the CGST Rules 2017 Part B Forms header specifically titled 'Application for filing objection against provisional attachment of property' (Form GST DRC-22), along with its extensive amendment history. It anchors questions about the procedural mechanism for objecting to provisional attachment orders under Section 83/Rule 159, and the evolving notification framework governing these forms.

questions.q: Our client, Zenith Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCZ1234R1ZP), a manufacturer in Raigad, Maharashtra, received a provisional attachment order on 12-Mar-2026 from the Commissioner, CGST Pune, freezing their primary current account at HDFC Bank (₹1.87 crore balance) and a plot of industrial land in Taloja MIDC. The attachment was passed during an ongoing investigation into alleged fake ITC of ₹4.2 crore involving purchases from three suspected shell entities in Gujarat. Zenith claims the land was already mortgaged to SBI under a term loan sanctioned in 2021, and the bank account holds funds earmarked for statutory dues including ESI, PF, and an upcoming advance tax instalment. The promoter wants to know: what is the prescribed mechanism under the CGST Rules to formally challenge this attachment, who should the objection be addressed to, and can a third party like SBI — which has a prior registered mortgage — independently file such an objection to protect its security interest in the Taloja plot?
questions.why_this_chunk: This chunk is the authoritative source for the existence and notification history of the prescribed CGST Rules Part B form for 'filing objection against provisional attachment of property' (DRC-22). Any answer about the formal procedural mechanism for objecting to provisional attachment must reference this notified form and its rule framework.

questions.q: Meridian Agro Exports LLP, registered in both Karnataka (GSTIN 29AAFFM5678K1ZC) and Telangana (GSTIN 36AAFFM5678K1ZQ), is under simultaneous investigation by the DGGI Hyderabad Zonal Unit for alleged circular trading and by the Karnataka State GST authorities for short payment of IGST on exports. On 28-Feb-2026, the CGST Commissioner Hyderabad provisionally attached Meridian's warehouse in Shamshabad and two bank accounts. Separately, on 5-Mar-2026, the Karnataka Joint Commissioner attached their Bengaluru godown under KGST provisions. Meridian's counsel argues that the CGST attachment should lapse because the investigation has been pending for over a year without issuance of a show-cause notice, and further contends that the proper objection procedure under the Central rules was never communicated to them. Meanwhile, one of the attached bank accounts is a joint account with their logistics partner, Swiftline Transport, who had no involvement in the alleged offence. What procedural remedies are available under the CGST Rules framework for both Meridian and Swiftline to formally object to these attachments, and does the one-year lapse argument affect the prescribed objection route?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes the notified form mechanism under CGST Rules Part B for objecting to provisional attachment of property — the exact procedural remedy both Meridian (as taxpayer) and Swiftline (as affected third party with interest in a joint account) must invoke. The amendment notification trail is also relevant to determining which version of the objection form applies at the relevant dates.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Notification 105/2009-Customs amending exemption notifications under TPS/DFCE schemes, specifically the customs duty exemption for goods imported against Duty Credit Certificates under para 3.7 of Foreign Trade Policy by status holders (marine sector vs other sectors), invoking section 25(1) of the Customs Act 1962. The distinction between marine-sector status holders (who get exemption on para 3.7 goods AND serial 509 of Notfn 21/2002) versus other-sector status holders (only para 3.7 goods) supports high-complexity questions involving eligibility disputes and cross-notification interplay.

questions.q: Oceanic Harvest Exports Pvt Ltd, a Three-Star Export House based in Kochi, Kerala, holds status recognition under the marine sector and has been issued a Duty Credit Certificate under paragraph 3.7 of the Foreign Trade Policy. They now wish to import two consignments: (a) specialized refrigeration compressors listed in serial number 509 of notification 21/2002-Customs dated 1.3.2002, and (b) a set of industrial packaging machines that fall under para 3.7 of the FTP but are NOT covered under serial 509 of the 21/2002 notification. Separately, their sister concern — Oceanic Agri Processing LLP — holds identical Three-Star status but under the agricultural sector and wants to import the same refrigeration compressors against its own Duty Credit Certificate. Which of these three import consignments qualify for customs duty exemption under the amended DFCE/TPS framework, and what is the legal basis for denying or granting exemption to the agri-sector entity for goods listed in serial 509?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly creates a two-tier exemption: marine-sector status holders get exemption on both para 3.7 FTP goods AND serial 509 of Notfn 21/2002 goods, while status holders of 'other sectors' get exemption only on para 3.7 FTP goods. This distinction is indispensable to determining that the agri-sector LLP cannot claim exemption on serial 509 goods.

questions.q: Triton Marine Systems LLP, a status holder in the marine sector based in Visakhapatnam, Andhra Pradesh, received a Duty Credit Certificate under para 3.7 of the Foreign Trade Policy in March 2009. In April 2009, after Notification 105/2009-Customs was issued amending the parent Notification 73/2006-Customs, Triton imported deep-sea fishing nets worth ₹1.8 crore against this certificate. Customs authorities at Vizag port are now questioning whether the substituted wording in the amended notification — which replaced the operative clause of Notification 73/2006 — applies retrospectively to certificates issued before the amendment date, and whether the goods must satisfy both the para 3.7 specification and the serial 509 requirement simultaneously or in the alternative. Triton's counsel argues the amendment is merely clarificatory and the exemption vests on the date the certificate was issued under the unamended notification. How should this dispute be resolved, and does section 25(1) of the Customs Act permit such mid-stream amendment to affect already-issued entitlements?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk shows the exact substitution language replacing the operative clause of Notification 73/2006-Customs via Notification 105/2009, and the amendment history (last amended by 19/2009). Resolving the temporal application question requires interpreting whether this substitution created new conditions or merely restated existing ones — the specific wording 'the following shall be substituted, namely' in this chunk is the crux of that legal interpretation.

title: Special Warehouse (Custody and Handling of Goods) Regulations, 2016
text:
Special Warehouse (Custody and Handling of Goods) Regulations, 2016

[MF (D.R.) Notification No.69 /2016- Customs (N.T.) dated 14-05-2016]

In exercise of the powers conferred by section 157 read with section 58A and sub-section (2) of Section 73A of the Customs
Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Central Board of Excise and Customs hereby makes the following regulations, namely:-

Regulation 1. Short Title and commencement. -

(1) These Regulations shall be called as the Special Warehouse (Custody and Handling of Goods) Regulations,2016.

(2) They shall come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette.

Regulation 2. Definitions.-

(1) In these regulations, unless the context otherwise requires, -

(a) "Act" means the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962);

(b) " bond officer " means an officer of customs in charge of a warehouse;

(c)"Controller of Certifying Authorities" means the authority appointed under subsection (1) of section 17 of the Information
Technology Act, 2000 (21 of2000);

(d) "section" means section of the Act.

(2) The words and expressions used herein and not defined in these regulations but defined in the Act shall have the same
meanings as respectively assigned to them in the Act.

Regulation 3. Appointment of warehouse keeper. -

(1) A licensee shall appoint a warehouse keeper who has experience in warehousing operations and customs procedures, to
discharge functions under these regulations on his behalf.

(2) The warehouse keeper shall obtain a digital signature from authorities licensed by the Controller of Certifying Authorities for
filing electronic documents required under the Act, rules or regulations made thereunder.

Regulation 4. Facilities, equipment and personnel. -

A licensee shall provide at the warehouse in respect of which a licence has been issued, -

(a) such facilities, equipment and personnel as are sufficient to control access to the warehouse and provide secure storage of
the goods in it, including -

( i ) doors, windows and other building components of sturdy construction;

(ii) facility for locking the warehouse by the bond officer;

(iii) signage that prominently indicates that the site or building is a customs bonded warehouse.

(b) adequate personnel, equipment and space for the examination of goods by officers of customs; and

(c) a computerised system for accounting of receipt, storage, operations and removal of goods.

Regulation 5. Control over special warehouses. -

The bond officer shall cause the warehouse to be locked and no person shall enter the warehouse or deposit or remove any
goods therefrom, except in his presence.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the Special Warehouse (Custody and Handling of Goods) Regulations, 2016 — specifically the appointment of warehouse keepers (Reg 3), mandatory facilities/equipment/personnel requirements (Reg 4), and the bond officer's exclusive control over access and movement of goods (Reg 5). These regulations interact with Sections 58A and 73A of the Customs Act 1962, creating fertile ground for HIGH-complexity questions involving cross-act interaction, multi-entity reasoning, and scenario-based compliance disputes.

questions.q: Meridian Logistics Pvt Ltd, a licensed special warehouse operator in JNPT (Nhava Sheva), Mumbai, stores imported sensitive electronic components under bond. During a surprise inspection on 12-Mar-2026, the Deputy Commissioner of Customs finds that (a) the warehouse keeper listed on record resigned two months ago and Meridian has been operating with a general manager who has no prior warehousing or customs experience, (b) the computerised accounting system has been offline for three weeks due to a server migration — during which period goods receipts and removals were recorded only in a manual register, and (c) the bond officer's lock on the secondary access door was replaced by Meridian's own padlock without authorisation. Meridian argues that the manual register is a valid interim measure and that the general manager effectively performs the warehouse keeper role. As their customs counsel, what specific regulatory violations has Meridian committed, what is the bond officer's authority regarding continued operations, and what exposure does Meridian face regarding the goods currently stored under bond?
questions.why_this_chunk: Regulation 3(1) mandates appointment of a warehouse keeper with experience in warehousing operations and customs procedures — the unqualified general manager violates this. Regulation 4(c) requires a computerised system for accounting of receipt, storage, operations and removal — the manual register does not satisfy this. Regulation 4(a)(ii) requires facility for locking by the bond officer, and Regulation 5 gives the bond officer exclusive control over locking and access — replacing the bond officer's lock violates both. All three violations are anchored directly in this chunk's text.

questions.q: Varuna Warehousing LLP holds a special warehouse licence in Mundra, Gujarat for storing imported crude palm oil under bond. They have appointed a warehouse keeper, Mr. Ashok Patel, who holds a valid digital signature certificate. In April 2026, Varuna enters into a sub-arrangement with Oceanic Commodities Pvt Ltd, allowing Oceanic's staff to independently access the warehouse during night shifts to expedite sampling and removal of goods for their bill-of-entry clearances — Oceanic's team uses a duplicate key provided by Varuna. The bond officer stationed at the warehouse works only day shifts (9 AM–6 PM) and is unaware of the night-shift access. When a consignment of 200 MT is found short during physical verification, Varuna contends that the night-access arrangement is a private commercial matter between two consenting parties and that the bond officer's presence requirement applies only during 'official customs hours.' As customs counsel to the jurisdictional Commissioner, evaluate whether Varuna's position is legally tenable, what regulatory breaches have occurred, and whether the warehouse licence itself is at risk under the governing regulations read with the parent provisions of the Customs Act.
questions.why_this_chunk: Regulation 5 is unequivocal that no person shall enter the warehouse or remove any goods except in the bond officer's presence — there is no carve-out for 'official hours' or private arrangements, making Varuna's defence untenable. Regulation 4(a)(ii) requires the facility for locking by the bond officer, and the duplicate key arrangement circumvents this. Regulation 4(a) requires facilities sufficient to control access — the unsupervised night access fails this standard. The question requires interpreting these provisions in conjunction with Section 58A and Section 73A of the Customs Act referenced in the chunk's enabling provision.

title: Seeks to amend the CGST Rules, 2017
text:
[To be published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section 
(i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise and Customs

Notification No. 22/2017 – Central Tax

New Delhi, the 17th August, 2017

G.S.R. ( )E.:- In exercise of the powers conferred by section 164 of the Central Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017), the Central Government hereby makes the following 
rules further to amend the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, namely:-

(1) These rules may be called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fifth Amendment)

Rules, 2017.

(2) Save as otherwise provided, they shall come into force on the date of their

publication in the Official Gazette.

2.  
In the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017,

(i) in rule 3, in sub-rule (4), for the words “sixty days”, the words “ninety days” shall 
be substituted;

(ii) in rule 17, with effect from the 22nd June, 2017, in sub-rule (2), after the words,

“said form”, the words “or after receiving a recommendation from the Ministry of 
External Affairs, Government of India” shall be inserted;

(iii) in rule 40, with effect from the 1st day of July, 2017, in sub-rule (1), for clause 
(b), the following shall be substituted, namely:-

“(b) the registered person shall within a period of thirty days from the date of 
becoming eligible to avail the input tax credit under sub-section (1) of section 
18, or within such further period as may be extended by the Commissioner by a 
notification in this behalf, shall make a declaration, electronically, on the 
common portal in FORM GST ITC-01 to the effect that he is eligible to avail 
the input tax credit as aforesaid:

Provided that any extension of the time limit notified by the

Commissioner of State tax or the Commissioner of Union territory tax shall be 
deemed to be notified by the Commissioner.”;

(iv) after rule 44, the following rule shall be inserted, namely:-

“44A. Manner of reversal of credit of Additional duty of Customs in respect of 
Gold dore bar.- The credit of Central tax in the electronic credit ledger taken in 
terms of the provisions of section 140 relating to the CENVAT Credit carried 
forward which had accrued on account of payment of the additional duty of
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers CGST (Fifth Amendment) Rules 2017, notably: extending registration timeline from 60 to 90 days (Rule 3(4)), ITC-01 declaration timeline for newly eligible persons under s.18(1) with commissioner extension powers (Rule 40(1)(b)), and reversal of CENVAT credit for additional customs duty on gold dore bar (Rule 44A). These amendments support HIGH-complexity questions involving transitional ITC claims and gold dore bar credit reversal scenarios.

questions.q: Aureus Bullion Pvt Ltd, a gold refinery in Ahmedabad (GSTIN 24AABCA****Q1Z5), imported 50 kg of gold dore bars in June 2017 under the pre-GST regime, paying additional duty of customs (CVD) of approximately ₹1.12 crore. They carried forward this CENVAT credit into GST via TRAN-1. Now in August 2017, their CA is advising them that the credit sitting in their electronic credit ledger as Central Tax — originally accrued from the additional customs duty on gold dore — may need to be reversed under a specific mechanism introduced by the Fifth Amendment Rules. Meanwhile, Aureus also has legitimate ITC on other inputs (chemicals, machinery) carried forward. The CFO wants to know: (a) whether there is a prescribed manner for reversing only the gold-dore-related additional customs duty credit without disturbing the rest of their transitioned CENVAT credit, and (b) if the reversal mechanism has retrospective effect from 1 July 2017, what is their exposure for the period July–August 2017 where they may have already utilized that credit against outward CGST liability on refined gold sales?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 44A inserted by this notification prescribes the specific manner of reversal of credit of additional duty of customs on gold dore bar carried forward under s.140 — this is the sole authoritative source for the reversal mechanism and its effective date.

questions.q: NovaTech Solutions LLP, a software consultancy in Bengaluru, was operating as an unregistered entity providing exempt services until 14 September 2017, when a government notification brought their services into the taxable net. They applied for GST registration on 20 September 2017 and received it on 12 October 2017. They hold invoices for various inputs (laptops, cloud subscriptions, office rent) procured during the exempt period. Their tax advisor has told them they must file a declaration on the common portal in Form GST ITC-01 within 30 days of becoming eligible for ITC under the voluntary/mandatory registration provisions, but NovaTech missed that window — it is now 25 November 2017, i.e., 42 days past their eligibility date. NovaTech's advisor in Karnataka argues that the Commissioner of State Tax in Karnataka issued a general extension order, and that such an extension should be deemed applicable for CGST purposes as well without a separate central notification. Is this interpretation correct — can NovaTech rely on a State Commissioner's extension of the ITC-01 filing deadline as being automatically valid for their Central Tax credit claim, and what is the legal basis for such deemed equivalence?
questions.why_this_chunk: The substituted Rule 40(1)(b) in this notification sets the 30-day ITC-01 deadline and, critically, its proviso deems any time-limit extension notified by the Commissioner of State Tax or Union Territory Tax as notified by the (Central) Commissioner — this deemed-equivalence proviso is the specific legal basis NovaTech's advisor is relying on.

title: New Export Procedure of goods under claim for rebate of Excise Duty

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers AR4 form procedural requirements for claiming rebate of excise duty on exports — specifically the color-coded copy scheme, the mandatory certificate regarding Modvat credit availment under Rules 57A/13(1)(b), and the declaration linking exports to Advance Licence obligations or Duty Drawback claims. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving interplay between excise rebate procedures, Modvat credit, and Customs drawback/Advance Licence schemes.

questions.q: Rajan Polymers Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer-exporter based in Thane, Maharashtra, exported consignments of polyester staple fibre worth INR 1.85 crore to a buyer in Bangladesh during March 2001. The company had availed Modvat credit under Rule 57A on inputs used in manufacturing the exported goods. Separately, they also held a quantity-based Advance Licence for a portion of this consignment covering INR 62 lakhs worth of goods, obligating them to export against that licence. When preparing the AR4 forms, their excise consultant filled a single set of AR4 forms covering the entire consignment and struck out the Advance Licence declaration for the full quantity, certifying only Modvat availment. The rebate sanctioning authority has now objected, saying the AR4 declarations are defective for the Advance Licence portion. Can the company claim excise duty rebate on the Advance Licence portion given that the AR4 declaration did not retain the appropriate Advance Licence discharge certification, and what is the correct way the declarations should have been structured across the two portions of the consignment?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies the exact AR4 declaration text requiring exporters to retain the applicable portion certifying whether export is in discharge of Advance Licence obligation or under Duty Drawback — striking out the wrong option is the procedural defect at issue, and only this chunk provides the prescribed declaration wording.

questions.q: Sunrise Chemicals LLP, a unit in Vadodara, Gujarat, manufactures specialty dyes and exports them on a rebate claim basis. For a shipment to Kenya in February 2001 valued at INR 48 lakhs, the company prepared AR4 forms but inadvertently used white-colored paper for all six copies instead of the prescribed color scheme, merely handwriting 'Duplicate', 'Triplicate', etc. on each copy without any color band. The Customs officer at Mundra port accepted and endorsed the forms at the time of export. Now the jurisdictional Central Excise rebate sanctioning authority — whose postal address was also omitted from the AR4 — is rejecting the rebate claim on two grounds: (a) non-compliance with the color-coding requirements rendering the forms invalid, and (b) absence of the rebate authority's address making it impossible to verify jurisdictional competence. Sunrise argues the forms are substantially compliant since Customs already verified the export. Is the rebate sanctioning authority justified in rejecting the claim on these procedural grounds, and does the Customs endorsement at the port cure the AR4 deficiencies?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk prescribes the exact six-copy color scheme (White/Buff/Pink/Green/Blue/Yellow) and the alternative of a color band, and separately mandates that the rebate sanctioning authority's complete postal address be clearly mentioned — both specific procedural requirements central to the dispute are found exclusively in this chunk.

title: Intimation to Liquidator for recovery of amount
text:
i. UIN:

through return or by filing application.

263
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GST refund application procedures including electronic cash/credit ledger mechanics, export refund rules (goods vs services), SEZ supply refund formula under rule 89(4), adjusted total turnover definition, BRC/FIRC requirements, and amended invoice treatment. These procedural and definitional provisions support HIGH-complexity questions involving export/SEZ refund calculations with multi-entity, multi-jurisdiction scenarios.

questions.q: Meridian Pharma LLP (GSTIN 24AABFM1234K1ZP, registered in Gujarat) exports active pharmaceutical ingredients to a buyer in Nigeria. During Q3 2025-26, Meridian made exports worth Rs 4.2 crore without payment of IGST and also supplied Rs 85 lakh worth of APIs to Kandla SEZ developer without payment of tax. Meridian's total turnover for the quarter was Rs 9.5 crore, which includes Rs 60 lakh of exempt domestic supplies (supply of unbranded generic medicines). Meridian's net ITC availed on inputs and input services during the quarter is Rs 1.15 crore. When Meridian's CA files the refund application for accumulated ITC on both exports and SEZ supplies, how should the 'adjusted total turnover' be computed — specifically, should the Rs 60 lakh exempt domestic supply be excluded while retaining the zero-rated export and SEZ values? And must the SEZ refund and export refund be computed separately under the rule 89(4) formula, or can they be clubbed into one application?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's definition of 'adjusted total turnover' (point 9) — excluding exempt supplies other than zero-rated supplies — is indispensable, as is point 14 requiring SEZ-without-tax refunds to follow the rule 89(4) formula, and point 13 requiring export-without-tax details in Statement-3.

questions.q: Softwave Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AADCS5678L1Z5, Karnataka) provides cloud-based SaaS to a client in the UK and also to a unit in SEEPZ SEZ, Mumbai. In January 2026, Softwave raised an invoice of USD 95,000 for the UK client but the FIRC was received only in March 2026 — and the bank account on the FIRC is a new EEFC account that Softwave opened in February 2026 but has not yet updated in its GST registration. Meanwhile, Softwave discovers that the original invoice to the UK client understated the value by USD 8,000 and issues a debit note in February 2026 correcting it. Softwave's CA wants to file the refund application for accumulated ITC covering both the UK export of services and the SEZ supply without payment of tax. What are the procedural roadblocks Softwave will face with respect to the bank account mismatch, and how should the refund amount be recalculated to account for the amended invoice value? Will the BRC/FIRC timing affect admissibility of the refund claim for the relevant tax period?
questions.why_this_chunk: Point 6 (bank account must match registration data and must be amended first), point 11 (BRC/FIRC mandatory for export of services refund), and point 12 (refund allowed based on amended value where invoice details are amended) are each indispensable to resolving this scenario.
