claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GSTR-9 annual return instructions for Tables 7E-8C, specifically ITC reversal reporting options (Tables 7A-7H), GSTR-2A auto-population cut-off dates by financial year, and the treatment of ITC availed in the next financial year under Section 16(4). These are fertile ground for HIGH-complexity questions involving ITC reconciliation disputes, year-crossing credit claims, and the interplay between GSTR-2A snapshots and annual return filing.

questions.q: Priya Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234M1ZT, Maharashtra) received inward supplies worth ₹4.2 crore from various vendors during FY 2019-20, on which it availed ITC of ₹75.6 lakh. However, ₹18.4 lakh of that ITC relates to invoices that appeared in Priya Polymers' GSTR-2A only after 1st November 2020 — the suppliers filed their GSTR-1 belatedly in December 2020 and January 2021. Additionally, ₹6.2 lakh of ITC pertains to supplies received in March 2020 but the credit was actually claimed in GSTR-3B of May 2020 (i.e., in FY 2020-21). While filing the GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20, the accountant is confused about how to reconcile Table 8A (auto-populated from GSTR-2A) with the ITC actually availed, where to report the ₹6.2 lakh next-year credit in Table 8C, and whether the ₹18.4 lakh mismatch due to belated supplier filings will trigger a notice. How should Priya Polymers correctly fill Tables 8A through 8C of its annual return, and what is the exposure if the GSTR-2A snapshot does not reflect the full ITC claimed?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that for FY 2019-20, GSTR-2A as on 1st November 2020 is auto-populated in Table 8A, and Table 8C specifically covers ITC on supplies received during the FY but availed in the next FY within the Section 16(4) window — both rules are indispensable to resolving this reconciliation problem.

questions.q: Keshav Engineering LLP (GSTIN 29AAFK5678N1ZP, Karnataka) is finalizing its GSTR-9 for FY 2018-19. During that year, the firm reversed ITC of ₹12.3 lakh under Rule 42 (common credit on exempt supplies), ₹3.1 lakh under Rule 43 (capital goods), and also carried forward ₹8.7 lakh of transitional credit claimed via TRAN-1 that was later found ineligible and reversed. The tax manager wants to use the simplified reporting option of clubbing all reversals into a single table rather than splitting them across Tables 7A to 7E individually. He also wants to include the TRAN-1 reversal in the same lump-sum entry. The firm's CA has additionally noted that for FY 2018-19, the GSTR-2A data and Tables 8A-8D details can be uploaded as a signed PDF without CA certification in GSTR-9C. Is the tax manager's approach of reporting all reversals — including TRAN-1 — in a single consolidated table permissible, and can the firm rely on the PDF upload option for the ITC reconciliation statement without obtaining CA certification?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that for FY 2018-19, reversals may be reported entirely under Table 7H, BUT TRAN-1 (Table 7F) and TRAN-2 (Table 7G) reversals must be mandatorily reported separately. It also confirms the PDF upload option without CA certification for Tables 8A-8D for FY 2018-19 — both provisos are essential to answering correctly.

title: The Central Excise (Appeals) Rules, 2001.
text:
the Registrar, or, as the case may be, in the office of such officer.

1. Qualifications for authorized representatives.- For the purposes of clause (c) of sub-section (2) of section 35Q of the Act,

an authorized representative shall include a person who has acquired any of the following qualifications namely:-

(a) a Chartered Accountant within the meaning of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 (38 of 1949); or

(b) a Cost Accountant within the meaning of the Cost and Works Accountants Act, 1959 (23 of 1959); or

(c) a Company Secretary within the meaning of the Company Secretaries Act, 1980 (56 of 1980) who has obtained a
certificate of practice under section 6 of that Act; or

(d) a post-graduate or an Honours degree holder in Commerce or a post-graduate degree or diploma holder in Business
Administration from any recognised university; or

(e) a person formerly employed in the Department of Customs and Central Excise or Narcotics and has retired or resigned
from such employment after having rendered service in any capacity in one or more of the said departments for not less than
ten years in the aggregate.

Explanation.- In this rule "recognised University" means any of the Universities specified below, namely:-

I. Indian Universities

Any Indian University incorporated under any law for the time being in force in India;

II. Rangoon University;

III. English and Welsh Universities

The Universities of Birmingham, Bristol, Cambridge, Durham, Leeds, Liverpool, London, Manchester, Oxford, Reading,
Sheffield and Wales;

IV. Scottish Universities

The Universities of Aberdeen, Edinburgh, Glasgow and St. Andrews;

V. Irish Universities

The Universities of Dublin (Trinity College), the Queen"s University, Belfast and the National University of Dublin;

VI. Pakistan Universities

Any Pakistan University incorporated by any law for the time being in force;

VII. Bangladesh Universities

Any Bangladesh University incorporated by any law for the time being in force.

1. Authority under section 35Q (5) (b) of the Act.- The Commissioner of Central Excise having jurisdiction in the

proceedings in which a person who is not a legal practitioner is found guilty of misconduct in connection with that
proceeding under the Act shall be the authority for the purposes of clause (b) of sub-section (5) of section 35Q of the Act.

Appendix

Form No. EA-1

( see Rule 3)

1.No. .....................of..................2001
2.Name and address of the appellant.

3.Designation and address of the officer passing the decision or order appealed against and the date of the

decision or order.

4.Date of communication of the decision or order appealed agaisnt to the appelant.
5.Address to which notices may be sent to the appellant.
6.(i) Description and classification of goods

(ii) Period of dispute
(iii) Amount of duty, if any, demanded for the period mentioned in item (ii)
(iv) Amount of refund if any claimed for the period mentioned in item (i)
(v) Amount of fine imposed.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the qualifications required for authorized representatives under section 35Q(2)(c) of the Central Excise Act 1944, including the specific list of recognised universities and the misconduct authority. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving disputes over representational eligibility, cross-referencing professional qualification acts, and jurisdictional misconduct proceedings.

questions.q: Our client, Meridian Polymers LLP based in Vadodara, Gujarat, is contesting a Central Excise duty demand of ₹1.47 crore for misclassification of PVC compounds during 2018-2020. They want their in-house tax consultant, Mr. Ashok Desai, to represent them before CESTAT. Mr. Desai holds an Honours degree in Commerce from the University of Dhaka (awarded in 1988, before Bangladesh was formed as a separate jurisdiction from Pakistan) and later obtained a post-graduate diploma in Business Administration from IGNOU. He also worked as a Superintendent in the Central Excise department from 1992 to 2001 before resigning. The department is challenging his eligibility to appear as authorized representative, arguing his Commerce degree is from neither an Indian nor a currently-recognised foreign university since Dhaka wasn't listed when these rules were framed. Can Mr. Desai represent Meridian Polymers, and on which qualifying ground(s)?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's exhaustive list of recognised universities — specifically the Pakistan and Bangladesh university categories and the qualifying criteria under clauses (d) and (e) — is indispensable. Dhaka University pre-1971 falls under Pakistan Universities; post-1971 under Bangladesh Universities. His 9-year excise service falls short of the 10-year threshold in clause (e), making the university recognition question decisive.

questions.q: Prakash & Associates, a Cost Accounting firm in Chennai, has been representing Suvarna Steels Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 33AABCS1234F1Z5) in a legacy Central Excise appeal before the Commissioner (Appeals) concerning a ₹83 lakh CENVAT credit reversal demand for the period April 2016 to June 2017. During proceedings, the department alleges that Mr. Venkatesh of Prakash & Associates — who holds a CMA (formerly ICWA) qualification under the Cost and Works Accountants Act, 1959 — fabricated a purchase register extract submitted as evidence. The Commissioner (Appeals) wants to initiate misconduct proceedings against Mr. Venkatesh under section 35Q(5)(b). However, the original order was passed by the Commissioner of Central Excise, Coimbatore, while the appeal is being heard at the Chennai commissionerate. Which Commissioner has jurisdiction to proceed with the misconduct case, and does Mr. Venkatesh's CMA qualification validly entitle him to appear as authorized representative in the first place?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause (b) on Cost Accountant qualification under the 1959 Act directly governs Mr. Venkatesh's eligibility, and the specific rule designating 'the Commissioner of Central Excise having jurisdiction in the proceedings' as the misconduct authority is the precise provision needed to resolve the Coimbatore-vs-Chennai jurisdictional question.

title: Annexure-B
text:
26

otherwise. Rule 4(5)(a)(iii) of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004  provides for  reversal of Cenvat 
credit when the inputs and capital goods are not received within the stipulated time.  It is 
however silent on the question whether interest should also be charged at the time of 
reversals of Cenvat Credit taken on the inputs or capital goods sent for job work and not 
received within the stipulated period of 180 days and if yes, what should be the period for 
which interest should be charged, i.e. period starting from the date of clearance from the 
factory or for the period starting after 180 days of clearance of such inputs and capital 
goods.

Discussion & Decision

The conference noted that when the rule provides for a time-frame within which the goods 
cleared for job-work are required to be returned and the infringement of the same would 
invite action as provided for under the recovery provisions of the Cenvat Credit Rules 2004. 
The conference noted that Hon’ble Tribunal in case of General Motors India Ltd [2010 (260) 
ELT 81] has ruled that Section 11AB of the Central Excise Act, is the provision under which 
interest is to be demanded which provides that interest is liable from the first date of month 
succeeding the month in which the duty ought to have been paid under the Act. Applying 
this ratio, the tribunal concluded that interest is liable to be paid after the expiry of the 
period of 180 days from the date of issue of capital goods to the job worker. The same 
principle would apply in case of inputs sent to the job worker.

B.28 - Meerut Zone - Cenvat Credit – Availment of Credit of Duty Paid on Welding 
Electrodes Used for Repairing Work.

Issue:

Credit of duty paid on welding electrodes used for repairing work in factory has been in 
dispute since 1996. Several hundreds of Show Cause Notices are pending on account of 
contradictory judgments. Hon’ble Allahabad High Court, in the case of Upper Ganga Sugar & 
Industries Ltd.in CEA No. 135/2005, has held that Welding Electrodes used in repairing and 
maintenance are not eligible for Cenvat Credit .Hon’ble Rajasthan High Court in the case of 
Hindustan Zinc Ltd. [2008 (228) ELT 517 (Raj.)] on the other hand  has allowed Cenvat Credit 
of duty paid on Welding Electrodes used for repairing of Capital goods. In view of the 
contrary judgments the Board may like to issue clarification regarding admissibility of 
Cenvat Credit on Welding Electrodes used for repairing and maintenance, so that a uniform 
practice is followed all over the country.

Discussion & Decision

The conference noted that there are contrary judgments on the issue by Hon’ble Allahabad 
High court in the case of M/s Upper Ganges Sugar & Industries Limited and by Hon’ble 
Rajasthan High Court in case of M/s Hindustan Zinc Limited. Further Hon’ble Supreme Court 
has referred the matter to a larger bench in case of RamalaShahkariChini Mills Ltd [2010
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers two CBIC conference decisions: (1) whether interest is chargeable on reversal of CENVAT credit for inputs/capital goods not returned from job work within 180 days, applying the General Motors India tribunal ruling and Section 11AB; (2) conflicting High Court judgments (Allahabad vs Rajasthan) on admissibility of CENVAT credit on welding electrodes used for repair/maintenance of capital goods. Both issues involve interpretive ambiguity and multi-provision reasoning, supporting HIGH-complexity practitioner queries.

questions.q: Mahindra Precision Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1Z5), a manufacturer of auto ancillaries in Pune, Maharashtra, sent capital goods — a CNC milling machine valued at Rs 48 lakhs — to their job worker, Shivam Engineering Works in Aurangabad, on 12-Sep-2024 for specialized reconditioning. The company availed full CENVAT credit on the machine at the time of receipt. Due to a labour dispute at Shivam's facility, the machine was not returned within 180 days; it was finally received back on 28-Apr-2025. Mahindra Precision reversed the credit in their March 2025 return upon realizing the 180-day window had lapsed. The department is now demanding interest on the reversed credit. The company's CA contends that interest, if at all applicable, should run only from 12-Mar-2025 (i.e., after the 180-day expiry) and not from September 2024 when the goods were initially sent out. The department insists interest runs from the date of original clearance. Who is correct, and what is the legal basis for computing the interest period?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Discussion & Decision on job-work credit reversal directly addresses this dispute — it records the conference's adoption of the General Motors India Ltd tribunal ratio that interest under Section 11AB is payable only after expiry of the 180-day period from the date of issue to the job worker, not from the date of original clearance.

questions.q: Rajendra Steels LLP, a medium-scale steel re-rolling mill in Bhilwara, Rajasthan, regularly uses welding electrodes for two purposes: (a) fabricating new material-handling structures within the factory, and (b) repairing and maintaining their existing rolling mill machinery, which qualifies as capital goods. They availed CENVAT credit on all welding electrodes purchased during FY 2023-24, totalling Rs 7.2 lakhs in duty. The Central Excise audit team has issued a show cause notice proposing to deny the entire credit, relying on the Allahabad High Court ruling in Upper Ganga Sugar. Rajendra Steels' advocate argues that at least the portion used for capital goods repair is squarely covered by the Rajasthan High Court's favourable ruling in Hindustan Zinc Ltd, and since the assessee is located within Rajasthan jurisdiction, that ruling is binding. Given the conflicting High Court positions noted by the Board and the pending Supreme Court reference in Ramala Shahkari Chini Mills, how should Rajendra Steels approach this dispute — can they sustain credit for the repair-use electrodes, and what is the procedural risk of the department pursuing recovery pending the Supreme Court's larger bench decision?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk documents the exact conflict between the Allahabad HC (Upper Ganga Sugar, denying credit) and Rajasthan HC (Hindustan Zinc, allowing credit on electrodes for capital goods repair), and notes the Supreme Court's referral to a larger bench in Ramala Shahkari Chini Mills — all three references are indispensable to advising on jurisdictional applicability and litigation risk.

title: Seeks to impose provisional anti dumping duty on imports of  "Low Ash Metallurgical Coke" originating in or exported from Australia, China PR, Colombia, Indonesia, Japan and Russia.
text:
Note: Customs classification of the subject goods is only indicative, and the determination of 
anti-dumping duty shall be made as per the description of the subject goods.

2.  
The provisional anti-dumping duty imposed under this notification shall be effective

for a period of six months, unless revoked, amended or superseded earlier, from the date of 
publication of this notification in the Official Gazette and shall be payable in Indian currency.

Explanation- For the purposes of this notification, rate of exchange applicable for the purposes 
of calculation of the anti-dumping duty shall be the rate which is specified in the notification 
of the Government of India, in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), issued from 
time to time, in exercise of the powers conferred by section 14 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 
of 1962), and the relevant date for the determination of the rate of exchange shall be the date 
of presentation of the bill of entry under section 46 of the said Customs Act.

[F. No. CBIC- 190349/76/2025-TRU]

(Dheeraj Sharma)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the provisional anti-dumping duty on Low Ash Metallurgical Coke from six specified countries, its six-month validity, payment in INR, and critically the exchange rate mechanism tied to Section 14 of Customs Act and the date of presentation of bill of entry under Section 46. This supports HIGH-complexity questions involving exchange rate determination timing, interaction between anti-dumping notifications and Customs Act provisions, and multi-jurisdiction sourcing scenarios.

questions.q: Bharat Metalloys LLP, based in Visakhapatnam, contracted to import 12,000 MT of Low Ash Metallurgical Coke from PT Bumi Resources in Indonesia at USD 285/MT CFR. The vessel arrived at Gangavaram Port on 10-May-2025, but due to port congestion the importer could not file the bill of entry until 18-May-2025. Between these two dates, the Ministry of Finance revised the customs exchange rate for USD from ₹84.50 to ₹85.20 via a fresh notification under Section 14 of the Customs Act. The importer's customs broker argues that the exchange rate prevailing on vessel arrival should apply for computing the provisional anti-dumping duty, since that is when the goods entered Indian territorial waters. Is this position correct, and at what rate should the anti-dumping duty be calculated in Indian currency?
questions.why_this_chunk: The Explanation in this notification explicitly prescribes that the relevant date for exchange rate determination is the date of presentation of the bill of entry under Section 46, not the date of vessel arrival — directly resolving the broker's incorrect claim.

questions.q: Steelworth Pvt Ltd in Jamshedpur imports Low Ash Metallurgical Coke partly from a trading house in Singapore that sources the material from a Colombian mine, and partly from a Russian producer shipping directly. For the Singapore consignment, the certificate of origin shows Colombia as country of origin, but the goods were transshipped and invoiced through Singapore. The importer contends that since Singapore is not among the countries listed in the anti-dumping notification, no provisional anti-dumping duty should apply to the Singapore-invoiced consignment. Meanwhile, the notification's validity is approaching the end of its six-month window and the importer is considering delaying the bill of entry filing to fall beyond the expiry date. Are either of these positions — that transshipment through a non-subject country avoids the duty, or that delaying filing past the six-month period eliminates liability — legally sustainable?
questions.why_this_chunk: The notification imposes duty on goods 'originating in or exported from' the listed countries (Colombia and Russia both listed), and the note clarifies classification is per description not tariff heading alone. The six-month validity clause and the bill-of-entry date as the relevant date for duty determination are both directly from this chunk and are indispensable to resolving both contentions.

claude_reasoning: This chunk contains specific conditional exemption requirements under a Customs Tariff notification — TRQ allocation certificates, end-use declarations, tourism ministry certificates, vanaspati registration, concessional duty manufacture rules, sacramental wine undertakings, LNG terminal commissioning certificates, life-saving drug exemption procedures, and atomic energy certificates. These conditions are rich enough to anchor multi-entity, cross-act, scenario-based practitioner questions.

questions.q: Meridian Hospitality Pvt Ltd, a boutique hotel in Jaipur approved by the Ministry of Tourism, is importing commercial-grade kitchen equipment (industrial ovens and refrigeration units) from Italy worth CIF INR 1.8 crore. Their customs broker has advised them to claim concessional duty under the relevant customs tariff notification by producing a Ministry of Tourism certificate. However, the certificate they hold is signed by an Under Secretary in the Ministry of Tourism. Separately, the company also wants to import 200 bottles of French sacramental wine for their in-house chapel, and their director has given a personal undertaking to the Assistant Commissioner of Customs that the wine will be used exclusively for sacramental purposes. Will Meridian Hospitality qualify for the concessional rate on either import, and what specific procedural deficiencies, if any, need to be cured before clearance?
questions.why_this_chunk: Condition 3(b) specifically requires the certificate to be from an officer 'not below the rank of a Deputy Secretary' in the Ministry of Tourism certifying the goods are essential to hotel/tourism industry — an Under Secretary is below that rank, so the condition fails. Condition 6 requires the undertaking for sacramental wine to be furnished to the Deputy/Assistant Commissioner of Customs, which is met here. Both conditions are directly in this chunk.

questions.q: Dr. Ananya Reddy, a resident of Hyderabad, has been prescribed a rare immunotherapy drug (Pembrolizumab) not commercially available in India for her Stage IV cancer treatment. Her oncologist has obtained a certificate from the Assistant Director General, Health Services, New Delhi confirming it is a life-saving medicine. However, due to the urgency, the drug shipment has already arrived at Chennai Customs and Dr. Reddy's family could not produce the certificate at the time of clearance — they have instead given an undertaking to the Assistant Commissioner of Customs to furnish it within 30 days. Meanwhile, the Department of Atomic Energy has separately arranged import of certain laser components through the same port under the same notification, and they are questioning whether the life-saving drug exemption procedure (specifically the undertaking mechanism) sets a precedent for their own delayed-certificate situation. Does the undertaking mechanism available for life-saving drugs also extend to imports requiring Department of Atomic Energy certification, and is Dr. Reddy's family's undertaking to the Assistant Commissioner valid for claiming the exemption?
questions.why_this_chunk: Condition 8 for life-saving drugs explicitly permits an undertaking 'as acceptable to the Deputy Commissioner or the Assistant Commissioner' to furnish the DGHS certificate within a specified period, failing which duty becomes payable — this validates the undertaking route for Dr. Reddy. Condition 9 (atomic energy) requires the certificate to be furnished 'at the time of import' with no analogous undertaking provision mentioned in this chunk, creating the interpretive contrast. Both conditions are squarely in this chunk.

title: Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
text:
1 [Section 38. Communication of details of inward supplies and input tax credit. *

(1) The details of outward supplies furnished by the registered persons under sub-section (1) of section 37 and of such other
supplies as may be prescribed, and an auto-generated statement containing the details of input tax credit shall be made
available electronically to the recipients of such supplies in such form and manner, within such time, and subject to such
conditions and restrictions as may be prescribed.

(2) The auto-generated statement under sub-section (1) shall consist of––

(a) details of inward supplies in respect of which credit of input tax may be available to the recipient; and

(b) details of supplies in respect of which such credit cannot be availed, whether wholly or partly, by the recipient, on account of
the details of the said supplies being furnished under sub-section (1) of section 37 ,––

(i) by any registered person within such period of taking registration as may be prescribed; or

(ii) by any registered person, who has defaulted in payment of tax and where such default has continued for such period as may
be prescribed; or

(iii) by any registered person, the output tax payable by whom in accordance with the statement of outward supplies furnished by
him under the said sub-section during such period, as may be prescribed, exceeds the output tax paid by him during the said
period by such limit as may be prescribed; or

(iv) by any registered person who, during such period as may be prescribed, has availed credit of input tax of an amount that
exceeds the credit that can be availed by him in accordance with clause (a), by such limit as may be prescribed; or

(v) by any registered person, who has defaulted in discharging his tax liability in accordance with the provisions of sub-section
(12) of section 49 subject to such conditions and restrictions as may be prescribed; or

(vi) by such other class of persons as may be prescribed.]

​ * Enforced w.e.f. 22nd June, 2017. ​

1. Substituted (​ w.e.f. 1st October, 2022 vide Notification No. 18/2022 - CT dated 28.09.2022. ) by s. 104 of The Finance Act 2022 (No. 6 of 2022) for

Section 38. Furnishing details of inward supplies. -

(1) Every registered person, other than an Input Service Distributor or a non-resident taxable person or a person paying tax under the provisions of section 10 or
section 51 or section 52 , shall verify, validate, modify or delete, if required, the details relating to outward supplies and credit or debit notes communicated under
sub-section (1) of section 37 to prepare the details of his inward supplies and credit or debit notes and may include therein, the details of inward supplies and
credit or debit notes received by him in respect of such supplies that have not been declared by the supplier under sub-section (1) of section 37 .

(2) Every registered person, other than an Input Service Distributor or a nonresident taxable person or a person paying tax under the provisions of section 10 or
section 51 or section 52 , shall furnish, electronically, the details of inward supplies of taxable goods or services or both, including inward supplies of goods or
services or both on which the tax is payable on reverse charge basis under this Act and inward supplies of goods or services or both taxable under the Integrated
Goods and Services Tax Act or on which integrated goods and services tax is payable under section 3 of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), and credit or
debit notes received in respect of such supplies during a tax period after the tenth day but on or before the fifteenth day of the month succeeding the tax period in
such form and manner as may be prescribed:
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Section 38 of the CGST Act 2017 (as substituted w.e.f. 1-Oct-2022), which governs the auto-generated statement of inward supplies and the conditions under which ITC availability is restricted to recipients — specifically when their suppliers have defaulted in tax payment, exceeded output tax limits, or over-claimed ITC. This supports HIGH-complexity questions involving ITC denial scenarios linked to supplier non-compliance.

questions.q: Meghna Polymers LLP (GSTIN 24AABFM1234K1ZP, Ahmedabad) purchased raw HDPE granules worth ₹47 lakh from Shivam Plastics Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AADCS5678L1ZR, Pune) during Q3 FY 2025-26. Meghna's accountant noticed that while the invoices appear in GSTR-2B, the ITC on roughly ₹12 lakh of those purchases is flagged as 'not available' in the auto-generated statement. On inquiry, they learn that Shivam Plastics has been consistently under-reporting output tax — specifically, the output tax payable per Shivam's GSTR-1 filings significantly exceeds the output tax actually paid in their GSTR-3B returns over the preceding two quarters. Meghna has all valid tax invoices and has made payments through banking channels. Can Meghna still claim the blocked ITC by any alternative mechanism, and what recourse does Meghna have against the restriction imposed in the auto-generated statement, given that from the recipient's side every compliance condition has been met?
questions.why_this_chunk: Section 38(2)(b)(iii) specifically provides that ITC shall be shown as unavailable when the supplier's output tax payable per GSTR-1 exceeds the output tax actually paid beyond a prescribed limit — this is the exact statutory basis for the restriction Meghna faces and is indispensable to advising on the legality and scope of the block.

questions.q: Aadhya Engineering Works, a proprietorship in Jaipur (GSTIN 08BZTPA9012R1ZX), receives fabrication services from two vendors: (1) Rudra Steels Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 08AABCR3456D1Z5), which took GST registration only 38 days ago and has filed its first GSTR-1, and (2) Kailash Industrial Supplies (GSTIN 08DEXPK7890M1ZQ), which has accumulated ITC claims in its own returns that substantially exceed what it is entitled to based on its own inward supply auto-generated statement for the last two prescribed periods. When Aadhya's tax team downloads the auto-generated inward supply statement for the current period, they find ITC from both vendors flagged as restricted — one due to the vendor's recent registration and the other due to the vendor's excess ITC availment. Aadhya's CA wants to understand: under what distinct statutory grounds has the credit been restricted for each vendor, whether the two restrictions operate identically or have different prescribed thresholds and cure mechanisms, and whether Aadhya can provisionally avail any portion of this blocked credit while the vendors regularise their compliance?
questions.why_this_chunk: The question turns on two separate clauses of Section 38(2)(b) — clause (i) restricting ITC when the supplier is within the prescribed period after taking registration, and clause (iv) restricting ITC when the supplier has availed excess input tax credit beyond prescribed limits. Both clauses are enumerated in this chunk and are indispensable to distinguishing the two restriction grounds and advising on their respective remedies.

title: Supersession of the Notification No. 33/2002-Customs (N.T.), dated the 29th May, 2002
text:
8.         The expression "when CENVAT facility has not been availed", used in the said Table, shall mean that the exporter has
to satisfy the following conditions, namely :-

(i)         The exporter declares, and if necessary, establishes to the satisfaction of the Assistant Commissioner of Customs or
Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or Deputy Commissioner of Customs or Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise,
as the case may be, that no CENVAT facility has been availed for any of the inputs used in the manufacture of the export
product;

(ii)         if the goods are exported under bond or claim for rebate of duty of central excise, a certificate from the Superintendent
of Customs or Superintendent of Central Excise in-charge of the factory of production, to the effect that no CENVAT facility has
been availed for the goods under export, is produced:

Provided that in the case of exports of handloom products or handicrafts(including handicrafts of brass artware) or finished
leather and other export products which are unconditionally exempt from the duty of central excise, the certificate regarding
non-availment of CENVAT facility shall not be required.

9.         In a case where electric fans, falling under serial or sub-serial nos. 84.13 to 84.15 of the said Table, are exported
alongwith regulators falling under serial or sub-serial nos. 84.16 or 84.17 of the said Table, then additional drawback shall be
payable at the rate specified against serial or sub-serial nos. 84.16 or 84.17 of the said Table.

10.         If bicycles or cycle rickshaws, assembled or unassembled falling under serial or sub-serial 87.44, 87.45 and 87.46 of
the said Table, are exported alongwith extra accessories as specified under serial or sub-serial numbers 40.01, 40.02, 40.03,
70.01, 73.08, 73.16, 73.18, 82.02, 85.22, and 87.47 to 87.114 of the said Table, then additional drawback shall be payable at
the rate specified against the serial or sub-serial number relating to such accessories.

11.         Whenever a composite article is exported for which any specific rate has not been provided in the Table , the rates of
drawback applicable to various constituent materials can be extended to the composite article according to net content of such
materials on the basis of a self-declaration to be furnished by the exporter to this effect. In cases of doubt or where there is any
information contrary to the declarations, the proper officer of customs shall cause a verification of such declarations.

2.         All claims for duty drawback shall be filed with reference to serial or sub-serial number shown in column 1 only.

3. This Notification shall come into effect from 7 th April, 2003.

F.No.609/40/2003-DBK
claude_reasoning: This chunk from Notification 26/2003-Custom covers duty drawback conditions: the definition of 'CENVAT facility not availed' (declaration + superintendent certificate, with a proviso exempting handloom/handicraft/finished leather), additional drawback for composite exports (fans+regulators, bicycles+accessories), and the self-declaration mechanism for composite articles without a specific drawback rate. These provisions support HIGH-complexity queries involving CENVAT-drawback interplay, composite article classification disputes, and the handloom/handicraft proviso.

questions.q: Our client, Meenakshi Handicrafts LLP based in Moradabad, UP (IEC 0503XXXXXX), exports brass artware items that are unconditionally exempt from central excise duty. They have been exporting under claim for rebate and the Customs officer at ICD Tughlakabad is insisting that they produce a certificate from the Superintendent of Central Excise confirming non-availment of CENVAT credit on inputs, before sanctioning the higher drawback rate (where CENVAT facility has not been availed). The officer's position is that since the exporter also procures some semi-finished castings from a job worker registered under Central Excise, the exemption from producing the certificate does not apply. The drawback claim is for Rs. 14.7 lakhs across 23 shipping bills filed between January and March 2025. Is the officer's insistence legally correct, and can the LLP claim the higher drawback rate without the superintendent's certificate?
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso to para 8(ii) specifically exempts 'handicrafts (including handicrafts of brass artware)' that are unconditionally exempt from excise duty from the requirement of producing the Superintendent's certificate regarding non-availment of CENVAT facility — this proviso is indispensable to resolving whether the officer's demand is legally sustainable.

questions.q: Pinnacle Engineering Pvt Ltd in Hyderabad manufactures and exports industrial cooling systems. A recent export consignment to a buyer in Nigeria consisted of a custom-built evaporative cooling unit that combines components falling under multiple tariff headings — an electric fan assembly (heading 84.14 of the drawback table), a voltage regulator (heading 84.17), copper tubing (heading 74.xx), and a fabricated steel housing (heading 73.xx). No composite drawback rate exists for this assembled unit. The company filed a drawback claim of Rs. 8.3 lakhs by applying the individual constituent material rates based on a self-declaration of net material content. Customs has rejected the claim, stating that since the electric fan and regulator have a specific additional drawback provision when exported together, the company cannot simultaneously use the self-declaration route under the composite article rule for those same components and must split the claim. Is Customs correct in requiring bifurcation, and how should the drawback claim be structured to maximize the eligible amount?
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 11 (composite article self-declaration based on constituent material rates) and para 9 (specific additional drawback for electric fans exported with regulators) both apply and potentially conflict — the question turns on whether the specific provision in para 9 overrides the general composite-article mechanism in para 11 for the fan+regulator portion of the unit.

title: In- bond Manufacture- New Guidelines
text:
Circular No. 132/95-Cus.

dated 22/12/95

F.No. 473/61/94-LC

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue, New Delhi

Subject: Warehousing -Grant of in-bond Manufacture facility under section 65 of the Customs Act, 1962- Instruction regarding.

I am directed to state that a review of the existing policy with regard to grant of in-bond Manufacture facility under section 65 of
the Customs Act, 1962 has been conducted by the Board. Consequent to the review, it has been decided that the requests for
grant of in-bond Manufacture facility under section 65 of the Act will henceforth be considered and decided at the level of the
Commissioners without making any reference to the Board. The following guidelines may, however, be kept in view while
deciding such requests.

2.    The Board's policy has been to extend the facility mainly to export oriented units so that unnecessary difficulties to pay duty
and later claim drawback can be avoided. In order to whether an Industrial unit is export-oriented or not, the following figures
may be compared-

a)    Total value of goods manufactured by using imported raw materials;

b)    Total value of such finished goods exported; and

c)    Total value of such finished goods retained for domestic sale.

3.    Flexible approach may, however, be adopted in the matter so that there is no instance that the exports have to be less than
50% of imported materials. further the Manufacture-in-bond operations are required to be carried out under customs
supervision on cost recovery basis. The Commissioner may carefully securities the application for grant of in-bond manufacture
facility and if the applicant is financially secured, has goods credibility, and has not been involved in Customs or Excise duty
evasion in the preceding 5 years in the normal course, an in-bond manufacture licence under section 65 may be granted. This
would, of courser, be subject to the condition that the promises are suitable and adequately secured. While granting the
request, the provisions of the Manufacture and other operations in Warehouse Regulations, 1966 have also to be complied
with.

Sd/-

(K. Chopra)

Under Secretary to the Govt. of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Circular 132/95-Cus providing guidelines for granting in-bond manufacture facility under Section 65 of the Customs Act, 1962. It specifies Commissioner-level approval authority, the export-orientation test (comparing total manufactured value, exported value, and domestic sale value), the flexible 50% threshold, cost-recovery customs supervision, and eligibility criteria (financial soundness, good credibility, no duty evasion in preceding 5 years). These layered conditions support HIGH-complexity practitioner scenarios involving eligibility disputes and cross-act interactions.

questions.q: Pinnacle Polymers Pvt Ltd, based in Dahej SEZ (Gujarat, GSTIN 24AABCP9912R1ZP), imports specialty polymer resins from a German supplier under bond and manufactures finished plastic automotive components in its bonded warehouse. In FY 2024-25, the total value of goods manufactured from imported raw materials was ₹18.4 crore, of which ₹8.8 crore worth was exported to a buyer in Thailand and ₹9.6 crore was sold domestically (including ₹2.1 crore cleared to another SEZ unit in Kandla). Pinnacle's Commissioner is now reviewing renewal of their in-bond manufacture licence. Separately, in 2021, the firm paid a voluntary settlement of ₹4.2 lakh under the Sabka Vishwas (Legacy Dispute Resolution) Scheme for a disputed Central Excise demand from 2019. The Commissioner is asking whether Pinnacle still qualifies for the in-bond manufacture facility, and specifically: (a) does the clearance to the Kandla SEZ unit count as 'export' for the purpose of evaluating export-orientation, and (b) does the settled Central Excise dispute constitute 'involvement in duty evasion' that would disqualify the firm under the eligibility criteria?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's para 2 prescribes the exact three-value comparison test (total manufactured value vs exported vs domestic sale) for export-orientation, and para 3 sets the flexible 50% threshold and the 'no Customs or Excise duty evasion in preceding 5 years' eligibility condition — both are indispensable to resolving whether Pinnacle qualifies.

questions.q: Sarthi Engineering LLP (Pune, Maharashtra) has applied to the Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva, for a fresh in-bond manufacture licence to import titanium alloy billets from Japan, manufacture aerospace-grade fasteners in a bonded warehouse at Chakan, and export finished goods to Airbus SE in France. The LLP was incorporated only 14 months ago with a paid-up capital of ₹12 lakh, has no prior import-export track record, and its designated premises is a rented 4,000 sq ft unit with basic perimeter fencing but no CCTV or electronic access control. The Commissioner has flagged two concerns: first, whether a newly incorporated entity with limited financial history and modest capitalisation satisfies the 'financially secured' and 'good credibility' requirements; and second, whether the premises meet the 'suitable and adequately secured' condition, given that the Manufacture and Other Operations in Warehouse Regulations, 1966 require specific physical security standards. Meanwhile, Sarthi argues that the facility is purely export-oriented (projected 100% export) and that the cost-recovery customs supervision itself provides adequate oversight. How should the Commissioner evaluate this application, and can the licence be conditionally granted with enhanced security stipulations rather than outright denial?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's para 3 sets out the specific eligibility criteria the Commissioner must evaluate — financial security, good credibility, no duty evasion history, premises suitability, and compliance with the 1966 Warehouse Regulations — and confirms Commissioner-level discretion (para 1) and the cost-recovery supervision requirement, all of which are directly at issue in this application.

title: Restoration of provisionally attached property / bank account under section83
text:
1. Reference No. includes BRN (Bank Reference Number), debit entry no., order no., if any, and acknowledgment No. of return in case of TDS & TCS

credit.

2. Tax period, if applicable, for any debit will be recorded, otherwise it will be left blank. 
3. GSTIN of deductor or tax collector at source, Challan Identification Number (CIN) of the challan against which deposit has been made,and type of

liability for which any debit has been made will also recorded under the head―description‖.

4. Application no., if any, Show Cause Notice Number, Demand ID, pre-deposit for appealor any other liability for which payment is being made will

also be recorded under the head―description‖.

5. Refund claimed from the ledger or any other debits made against any liability will be recorded accordingly. 
6. Date and time of deposit is the date and time of generation of CIN as reported by bank. 
7. ‗Central Tax‘ stands for Central Goods and Services Tax; ‗State Tax‘ stands for State Goods and Services Tax; ‗UT Tax‘ stands for Union territory

Goods and Services Tax; ‗Integrated Tax‘ stands for Integrated Goods and Services Tax and ‗Cess‘ stands for Goods and Services 
Tax(Compensation to States)

244
claude_reasoning: This chunk details the recording mechanics of the Electronic Cash Ledger — how CIN, BRN, TDS/TCS credits, demand IDs, SCN numbers, pre-deposit for appeals, and refund debits are recorded, plus tax-head definitions. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving reconciliation disputes, multi-party TDS/TCS credit issues, and appeal pre-deposit mechanics tied to cash ledger entries.

questions.q: Prism Autoparts Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234R1ZQ, Maharashtra) received supplies from three vendors during Q3 2025-26, all of whom deducted TDS under GST. When Prism's accountant checks the Electronic Cash Ledger on the portal, she finds that two TDS credits from Bharat Heavy Electricals (GSTIN 09AAACB5678M1Z5, UP) and one from Maharashtra State Road Transport Corporation show CIN entries but no corresponding acknowledgment numbers against the TDS return period. Simultaneously, Prism has paid Rs 12,40,000 via a single challan toward a demand raised under a Show Cause Notice (SCN No. DGGI/MUM/2025/1847), but the ledger shows the amount split across Central Tax and Integrated Tax heads with no Demand ID linkage visible. How should Prism's CA reconcile these cash ledger entries — specifically, what information should appear against each debit under the description field for TDS credits versus SCN-related payments, and what recourse does Prism have if the ledger entries are incomplete or incorrectly mapped?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies exactly what must be recorded under 'description' — GSTIN of deductor and acknowledgment number for TDS credits (Note 1 & 3), and SCN Number plus Demand ID for liability payments (Note 4). It also defines how CIN and tax heads (Central Tax, Integrated Tax) are recorded (Notes 3, 6, 7), which is indispensable for diagnosing Prism's reconciliation problem.

questions.q: Ganesh Trading LLP (GSTIN 33AABFG9876K1ZP, Tamil Nadu) lost an adjudication case and must make a mandatory pre-deposit to file an appeal before the Appellate Authority. The disputed demand of Rs 28,00,000 covers both CGST and SGST components. Ganesh's advocate deposits Rs 3,50,000 (being 10% rounded up) via two separate challans — one for CGST and one for SGST — on 18-Mar-2026 at 11:42 PM, but only the CGST challan's CIN appears in the Electronic Cash Ledger the next morning, while the SGST challan shows as 'processed by bank' in the bank statement but has no CIN generated. Meanwhile, Ganesh also has an unutilized refund debit of Rs 1,20,000 from an earlier IGST export refund claim that was partially sanctioned. The appeal filing deadline is 20-Mar-2026. How should the cash ledger reflect each of these three transactions (CGST pre-deposit, SGST pre-deposit, and the earlier refund debit), what specific identifiers and descriptions should appear for each, and can Ganesh rely on the bank reference number alone to demonstrate the SGST pre-deposit was made in time if the CIN is not yet generated?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is indispensable because Note 4 specifies that pre-deposit for appeal must be recorded with application number under 'description'; Note 6 defines that date/time of deposit equals date/time of CIN generation by bank (critical to whether SGST deposit is 'made' without CIN); Note 1 clarifies BRN as an alternate reference number; and Note 5 governs how refund debits are recorded — all four notes must be applied together to resolve this scenario.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GSTR-9 annual return instructions: (a) ITC availed in April-September of the next FY for supplies received in the prior FY, with reversal/reclaim rules under section 16(2) second proviso, and (b) Part VI tables 15A-15G on reporting refunds (claimed/sanctioned/rejected/pending) and confirmed demand orders. These instructions support high-complexity queries about cross-year ITC timing, reversal-reclaim reporting across annual returns, and reconciliation of refund/demand figures.

questions.q: Pinnacle Agro Industries LLP (GSTIN 06AABFP1234R1Z5, Haryana) received capital goods from a Rajasthan supplier in March 2020 worth ₹48 lakh plus ₹8.64 lakh IGST. The invoice was booked in FY 2019-20 but Pinnacle could not claim ITC in its March 2020 GSTR-3B because the supplier had not uploaded the invoice. The supplier finally uploaded it in May 2020, and Pinnacle claimed the ITC in its July 2020 GSTR-3B. Meanwhile, during an internal audit in January 2020, Pinnacle had reversed ₹3.2 lakh of ITC on certain FY 2019-20 inward supplies because payment was not made within 180 days as required, but the payment was eventually made in November 2020 and Pinnacle reclaimed that ITC in its November 2020 GSTR-3B. When Pinnacle is preparing its GSTR-9 annual return for FY 2019-20, in which specific table and in which financial year's annual return should each of these two ITC amounts — the ₹8.64 lakh claimed late and the ₹3.2 lakh reversed-then-reclaimed — be reported, and can Pinnacle exercise the option to leave any of these tables blank?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that ITC for goods received in FY 2019-20 but availed in April 2020 to September 2020 returns must be declared in the GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20, and that ITC reversed in FY 2019-20 per second proviso to section 16(2) but reclaimed in FY 2020-21 must be furnished in the annual return for FY 2020-21 — both rules are indispensable to correctly placing these two ITC amounts. The optional-fill relaxation for FY 2019-20 is also directly from this chunk.

questions.q: Sarvottam Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 24AADCS5678M1ZP, Gujarat) filed three GST refund applications during FY 2019-20: (i) ₹12 lakh for inverted duty structure in August 2019 — sanctioned for ₹9.5 lakh in October 2019 with ₹2.5 lakh rejected; (ii) ₹7.4 lakh for zero-rated exports in January 2020 — acknowledgement received but still pending as of March 2020, though a provisional refund of ₹5.55 lakh (75%) was credited; and (iii) ₹4 lakh for deemed exports in March 2020 — acknowledgement received, fully pending. Simultaneously, the jurisdictional officer issued a demand order in February 2020 confirming ₹6.8 lakh of CGST+SGST demand on alleged wrongful ITC, of which Sarvottam paid ₹2 lakh under protest. When filling Tables 15A through 15G in the GSTR-9 annual return for FY 2019-20, what exact aggregate figures should Sarvottam declare for refund claimed, refund sanctioned, refund pending, and confirmed demand/tax paid — and does the provisional refund of ₹5.55 lakh reduce the 'pending' figure? Also, can Sarvottam simply skip Part VI entirely given the relaxation available for that financial year?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's instructions for Tables 15A-15G define how to compute aggregate refund claimed, sanctioned, rejected, and pending (explicitly stating pending excludes provisional refunds received), and Tables 15E-15G for confirmed demands and taxes paid. The chunk also specifies the optional-fill relaxation inserted by Notification 56/2019 and extended to FY 2019-20 by Notification 79/2020, which is needed to answer whether Part VI can be skipped.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the Show Cause Notice form for cancellation of provisional registration under Section 18(2), including the 30-day cure period for furnishing returns to deem the order withdrawn, recovery proceedings for outstanding dues, and the note that Place of Supply (POS) details are required only when demand is created under the IGST Act. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving the interplay between IGST/CGST demand creation, the cure-period mechanism, and multi-jurisdiction supply scenarios.

questions.q: Prashant Textiles LLP, a provisional registrant in Surat, Gujarat (GSTIN 24BBB…), received a cancellation order on 10-Mar-2026 for non-filing of returns for Oct-2025 through Jan-2026. During this period, they made inter-state supplies worth ₹18.4 lakhs to Deepak Traders in Mumbai, Maharashtra, and intra-state supplies of ₹7.2 lakhs within Gujarat. The LLP's counsel filed all pending GSTR-3B returns on 06-Apr-2026 — 27 days after service of the order. The proper officer is now insisting that the cancellation still stands because the IGST liability on inter-state supplies was short-paid by ₹42,000 due to an incorrect Place of Supply declaration, and that the 30-day cure window only applies if all dues including correctly computed IGST are fully discharged. Is the officer's position legally sustainable, or does the deemed withdrawal operate automatically once returns are furnished within 30 days regardless of a POS dispute on the IGST component?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies the 30-day cure mechanism ('if you furnish the return within 30 days… the order shall be deemed to have been withdrawn') and the note that POS details are required only when demand is created under the IGST Act — both provisions are indispensable to resolving whether furnishing returns alone triggers deemed withdrawal or whether correct POS-based IGST computation is a precondition.

questions.q: Kaveri Engineering Pvt Ltd, a company with provisional registration in Bengaluru, Karnataka (GSTIN 29CCC…), was issued a cancellation order with a demand for ₹3.8 lakhs covering CGST and IGST on supplies made to three parties: (i) a bill-to-ship-to transaction where the buyer is in Tamil Nadu but goods were shipped to an SEZ unit in Kochi, Kerala, (ii) a direct supply to a government department in Karnataka, and (iii) export supplies under LUT to a buyer in Singapore. Kaveri's CA wants to file all overdue returns within the 30-day window to get the order deemed withdrawn, but is uncertain whether the demand table in the notice — which the officer filled only with CGST figures and left the IGST and tax-rate columns blank citing 'not mandatory' — can later be used to raise a fresh IGST demand for the SEZ and export supplies after the deemed withdrawal takes effect. Can the officer reopen the IGST demand post-withdrawal, and does the 'not mandatory' nature of certain columns in the notice form prejudice or protect Kaveri?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's notes that 'tax rate, turnover and tax period are not mandatory' columns and that 'POS details shall be required only if demand is created under IGST Act' are directly at issue — the question turns on whether leaving IGST/POS fields blank in the prescribed form bars the officer from subsequently raising an IGST demand after the deemed withdrawal under the 30-day cure provision.

title: Consolidated RG 23A Part II-Maintenance of
text:
CBEC Circular No. 221/55/96-CX

dated 4/6/96

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue), New Delhi

Subejct : Consolidated RG 23A Part II - Maintenance of

Consolidated maintenance of RG-23A Part II is permitted where the inputs used are common in the manufacture of dutiable
and exempted final product. However, the assessee is required to submit a statement giving details of the inputs used and the
quantity of the exempted and dutiable final product manufactured, thereof alongwith credit availment/ reversal particulars at the
end of the every month.

2.    A point was raised in the Central Advisory Council meeting held on 4.11.1995 that maintenance of consolidated RG-23A
Part-II register should be permitted in view of Notification NO. 11/95-CE(NT) dated 16.3.1995 wherein credit of duty paid on
inputs can be utilised for payment of duty on any other final product whether or not such inputs have actually been utilised in the
manufacture of the said final product.

3.    According, it has been requested that maintenance of consolidate RG-23A Part-II register should be allowed with respect
to all inputs and all final products in view of the aforesaid amendment.

4.    Matter has been examined by the Board. It has been decided that prima facie except where reversal of Modvat credit
taken is required to be done prior to clearance of goods in terms of the conditions of VABAL Notification No. 203/92-Customs
dated 14.5.1992 (in accordance with the principles laid down by Orissa High Court, Cuttack 2022/ 1995 in NALCO case
decided on 1.3.1996), consolidated maintenance of RG-23A Part-II would continue to be permitted.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers CBEC Circular 221/55/96-CX permitting consolidated RG-23A Part II register maintenance for common inputs used in both dutiable and exempted final products, with the exception where MODVAT credit reversal is required prior to clearance under VABAL Notification 203/92-Customs. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving the interplay between Central Excise MODVAT credit rules, Customs notifications (VABAL scheme), and the conditions for consolidated register maintenance vs. mandatory credit reversal.

questions.q: Durgapur Alloys & Metals Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer in Odisha, procures aluminium ingots and caustic soda as common inputs for producing both aluminium wire rods (dutiable) and aluminium dross (exempted). They have been maintaining a single consolidated RG-23A Part II register for all these inputs. The company also imports some raw materials under the VABAL scheme (Notification 203/92-Customs) and avails MODVAT credit on those imported inputs. Their internal auditor has flagged that consolidating the register for VABAL-imported inputs alongside domestically-procured inputs may not be permissible, especially after the NALCO ruling by the Orissa High Court. The company wants to know: can they continue with a single consolidated RG-23A Part II for all inputs — both domestic and VABAL-imported — or must they segregate the VABAL inputs into a separate register and reverse credit before each clearance? What monthly compliance obligations apply if consolidation is partially allowed?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifically carves out an exception to consolidated RG-23A Part II maintenance where reversal of MODVAT credit is required prior to clearance under VABAL Notification 203/92-Customs, citing the NALCO case principle from Orissa High Court — this is the precise legal basis needed to answer whether VABAL inputs must be segregated.

questions.q: Shivam Chemicals LLP in West Bengal manufactures three final products: Product A (dutiable at 16%), Product B (fully exempt under a specific Central Excise notification), and Product C (cleared for export under bond). All three products use a common set of five inputs on which the LLP avails MODVAT credit. Some of these common inputs were imported duty-free under the VABAL scheme (Notification 203/92-Customs) with the condition that credit must be reversed before clearance. The LLP's consultant advises maintaining one consolidated RG-23A Part II register and submitting a single monthly statement covering input usage, credit availment, and reversals across all three product lines. However, the jurisdictional Superintendent insists that at minimum the VABAL-imported inputs require separate tracking and pre-clearance reversal, and that the monthly statement must separately break down quantities attributable to the exempted Product B. Is the Superintendent's position legally correct, and what is the LLP's obligation regarding consolidated vs. segregated register maintenance given these mixed clearance scenarios?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes the general permission for consolidated RG-23A Part II with monthly statements detailing input usage and credit reversal particulars, while specifically excepting VABAL Notification 203/92-Customs cases requiring pre-clearance reversal per the NALCO principle — both the general rule and the exception are indispensable to resolving this mixed-clearance scenario.

title: Seeks to amend notification No. 08/2017- Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28.06.2017 so as to to extend last date for exercise of option  by GTA to pay GST under forward charge.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-
SECTION (i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 05/2023- Integrated Tax (Rate)

New Delhi, the 9th May, 2023

G.S.R......(E).-In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-sections (1), (3) and (4) of section 5, subsection

(1) of section 6 and clauses (iii), (iv) and (xxv) of section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (13 
of 2017), read with sub-section (5) of section 15, sub-section (1) of section 16 and section 148 of the Central Goods 
and Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017), the Central Government, on the recommendations of the Council, and on 
being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby makes the following further amendments in 
the notification of the Government of India, in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 8/2017-
Integrated Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, 
Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 683(E), dated the 28th June, 2017, namely:-

In the said notification, in the Table, against serial number 9, in item (iii), in sub-item (b), in the entries under 
column (5), in condition (2), after the second proviso, the following provisos shall be inserted, namely:-

“Provided also that the option for the Financial Year 2023-2024 shall be exercised on or before the 31st 
May, 2023: 
 
Provided also that a GTA who commences new business or crosses threshold for registration during any 
Financial Year, may exercise the option to itself pay GST on the services supplied by it during that 
Financial Year by making a declaration in Annexure V before the expiry of forty-five days from the date of 
applying for GST registration or one month from the date of obtaining registration whichever is later.”.

[F. No. -CBIC-190354/63/2023-TO (TRU-II)-CBEC]

(Rajeev Ranjan)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note: - The principal notification number 08/2017 - Integrated Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017 was published 
in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, vide number G.S.R. 683 (E), dated the 28th June, 2017 and last amended vide 
notification number 03/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate), dated the 13th July, 2022 published in the official gazette vide 
number G.S.R. 542(E), dated the 13th July, 2022.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Notification 05/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate) which extends the deadline for GTAs to opt for forward charge GST payment for FY 2023-24 (by 31 May 2023) and introduces a new proviso allowing newly registered GTAs to exercise the option within 45 days of applying for registration or 1 month from obtaining registration, whichever is later. These time-bound options and their interplay with IGST/CGST provisions and the reverse charge default for GTAs support high-complexity practitioner queries.

questions.q: Bharat Freight Carriers LLP, a goods transport agency based in Pune, Maharashtra, applied for GST registration on 12th April 2023 after crossing the threshold turnover during FY 2023-24. They received their registration certificate on 28th April 2023. The partners want to charge GST on their GTA services under forward charge instead of the default reverse charge mechanism applicable to their recipients (all registered factories). The LLP filed its Annexure V declaration on 10th June 2023. One of their clients, Steelmax Industries Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234M1Z5) in Nagpur, is now contesting an invoice dated 15th June 2023 where Bharat Freight charged 12% IGST on an inter-state consignment to Raipur, arguing the GTA had no authority to collect GST under forward charge since the option window had lapsed. Is the Annexure V declaration by Bharat Freight valid, and was the LLP entitled to charge IGST on the Steelmax consignment?
questions.why_this_chunk: The fourth proviso inserted by this notification specifically allows a GTA that crosses the registration threshold during a financial year to exercise the forward charge option within 45 days from the date of applying for registration or 1 month from the date of obtaining registration, whichever is later. Here, 45 days from 12 Apr = 27 May, and 1 month from 28 Apr = 28 May — the later is 28 May, so the 10 June filing would be beyond the deadline, making this chunk indispensable to determining validity.

questions.q: TransQuick Logistics Pvt Ltd, a GTA registered in Karnataka (GSTIN 29AABCT5678N1Z2), had been operating under the reverse charge mechanism throughout FY 2022-23. For FY 2023-24, the company's board decided on 20th May 2023 to switch to forward charge and pay GST itself at 12%. The accounts team filed the Annexure V declaration on 29th May 2023. However, between 1st April and 28th May 2023, TransQuick had already rendered inter-state GTA services worth ₹48 lakhs to three recipients across Tamil Nadu and Telangana, on which the recipients had discharged GST under reverse charge. Now the company wants to issue credit notes for the reverse charge component and re-invoice those services under forward charge for the entire financial year. Their CA is asking: (a) was the 29th May declaration within the permissible window for an existing GTA for FY 2023-24, and (b) can the forward charge option operate retrospectively from 1st April 2023 to cover services already supplied under reverse charge before the declaration date?
questions.why_this_chunk: The third proviso inserted by this notification sets the deadline for existing GTAs to exercise the forward charge option for FY 2023-24 as 31st May 2023. The 29 May filing is within that window, but the chunk is silent on retrospective application, making it the indispensable anchor for both the validity question and the interpretive question of whether the option covers services supplied before the declaration date.

title: amendment in the Notification No 105/99-Customs, dated the 10th August, 1999
text:
31st December, 2002

Notification No.145/2002- Customs

In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 25 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Central
Government, being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby makes the following amendment in the
notification of the Government of India in the erstwhile Ministry of Finance ( Department of Revenue), No 105/99-Customs,
dated the 10 th August, 1999, namely:-

In the said notification, after the proviso, the following shall be inserted, namely:-

"Provided further that the exemption contained in this notification shall not be applicable in the case of (i)         vanaspati,

(ii)         acrylic yarn,

(iii)         copper products falling under Chapter 74 and heading 85.44 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 and

(iv)         zinc oxide ,

when imported from Nepal in excess of the quantities specified in Condition No .3 of the Annexure to notification 40/2002-
Customs , dated the 12 th April, 2002 .

Vivek Prasad

Under Secretary to the Government of India

F.No 354/228/2002-TRU

Note:

1) Notification No 105/99-Customs dated 10 th August 1999 was published in the Gazette of India Extraordinary vide G.S.R
582 (E) dated the 10 th August 1999 and was last amended by notification No 85/2002-Customs dated the 20th August 2002
(G.S.R 585 (E) dated the 20th August 2002).

2) Notification No 40/2002-Customs dated 12 th April 2002 was published in the Gazette of India Extraordinary vide G.S.R 281
(E) dated the 12 th April 2002.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Notification 145/2002-Customs which restricts the customs duty exemption under Notification 105/99-Customs for four specific product categories (vanaspati, acrylic yarn, copper products under Chapter 74/heading 85.44, and zinc oxide) when imported from Nepal beyond quantity limits set in Notification 40/2002-Customs. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving cross-notification interplay, Nepal-India trade treaty exemptions, tariff classification, and quantity-based exemption thresholds.

questions.q: Himalayan Conductors LLP, based in Lucknow, has been importing insulated copper cables (classified under heading 85.44 of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975) from a Kathmandu-based supplier under the India-Nepal trade concession route. In Q3 2002, they imported 180 MT and claimed full exemption from basic customs duty. Now in Q4 2002, they have a fresh order for 95 MT of the same cables plus 40 MT of copper tubes falling under Chapter 74. Their customs broker says the exemption still applies because the goods originate in Nepal and are covered under the bilateral trade notification. However, the Deputy Commissioner at ICD Lucknow has issued a query asking them to demonstrate compliance with quantitative ceilings. Is the customs broker's advice correct, and what is the firm's exposure if cumulative imports of copper products from Nepal across the fiscal year have already crossed the quantity thresholds prescribed in the relevant annexure?
questions.why_this_chunk: This chunk specifically carves out copper products under Chapter 74 and heading 85.44 from the Nepal trade exemption (Notification 105/99) when imported beyond quantities specified in Condition No. 3 of Annexure to Notification 40/2002-Customs — the exact legal basis the Deputy Commissioner would rely on to deny the exemption.

questions.q: Gurkha Agri Imports, a proprietorship firm in Bihar, imports vanaspati (hydrogenated vegetable oil) and zinc oxide from Nepal for its edible oil refinery and paint manufacturing unit respectively. For the period October–December 2002, they imported 500 MT of vanaspati and 120 MT of zinc oxide, claiming nil duty under the India-Nepal preferential customs exemption notification originally issued in August 1999. The assessing officer has proposed to deny the exemption on both items citing a late-2002 amendment, but the importer argues that the amendment only restricts quantities beyond a ceiling and their imports fall within the ceiling specified in a separate April 2002 notification. If the quantity ceiling in Condition No. 3 of the Annexure to Notification 40/2002-Customs for vanaspati is 400 MT and for zinc oxide is 100 MT per the relevant period, what is the correct duty treatment for the entire consignment, and can the importer claim proportional exemption for the within-ceiling quantity while paying duty only on the excess?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's inserted second proviso explicitly lists vanaspati and zinc oxide as items excluded from the Notification 105/99 exemption when imported from Nepal in excess of quantities in Notification 40/2002 — making it indispensable for determining whether partial/proportional exemption is legally permissible or the entire consignment loses exemption once the ceiling is breached.

title: The Taxation and Other Laws (Relaxation and Amendment of Certain Provisions) Act, 2020 (38 of 2020)
text:
SEC. 1]
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINARY
19

information relating to said donation
furnished by the institution or fund to the
prescribed income-tax authority or the
person authorised by such authority,
subject to verification in accordance with
the risk management strategy formulated
by the Board from time to time.";
(XIII) in section 92CA, after sub-section (7), the following sub-sections shall be
inserted with effect from the 1st day of November, 2020, namely:—
"(8) The Central Government may make a scheme, by notification in the
Official Gazette, for the purposes of determination of the arm's length price under
sub-section (3), so as to impart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability
by—
(a) eliminating the interface between the Transfer Pricing Officer
and the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;

(b) optimising utilisation of the resources through economies of
scale and functional specialisation;
(c) introducing a team-based determination of arm's length price
with dynamic jurisdiction.
(9) The Central Government may, for the purpose of giving effect to the
scheme made under sub-section (8), by notification in the Official Gazette, direct
that any of the provisions of this Act shall not apply or shall apply with such
exceptions, modifications and adaptations as may be specified in the notification:
Provided that no direction shall be issued after the 31st day of
March, 2022.

(10) Every notification issued under sub-section (8) and sub-section (9)
shall, as soon as may be after the notification is issued, be laid before each
House of Parliament.";

(XIV) in section 115AD, with effect from the 1st day of April, 2021,—
(a) in sub-section (1),—

(i) in the opening portion, for the words "Foreign Institutional
Investor", the words "specified fund or Foreign Institutional Investor"
shall be substituted;
(ii) for clause (i), the following clause shall be substituted, namely:—

"(i) the amount of income-tax calculated on the income in
respect of securities referred to in clause (a), if any, included in the
total income,—

(A) at the rate of twenty per cent. in case of Foreign
Institutional Investor;
(B) at the rate of ten per cent. in case of specified fund;";

(iii) in clause (iv), for the words "Foreign Institutional Investor",
the words "specified fund or Foreign Institutional Investor" shall be
substituted;

(b) after sub-section (1), the following sub-section shall be inserted,
namely:—

"(1A) Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section (1), in
case of specified fund, the provision of this section shall apply only to the
extent of income that is attributable to units held by non-resident (not
being a permanent establishment of a non-resident in India) calculated in
the prescribed manner.";

(c) in sub-section (2), for the words "Foreign Institutional Investor", at
both the places where they occur, the words "specified Fund or Foreign
Institutional Investor" shall be substituted;
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers amendments to the Income Tax Act via the Taxation and Other Laws (Relaxation and Amendment of Certain Provisions) Act, 2020 — specifically transfer pricing scheme provisions (section 92CA) and tax rates for specified funds/FIIs under section 115AD. While this is a direct tax/income tax amendment rather than indirect tax (GST/Customs), it can anchor HIGH-complexity questions involving cross-act interplay between direct tax transfer pricing and GST valuation for related-party transactions, and fund taxation where GST on financial services intersects with FII/specified fund treatment.

questions.q: Our client, Meridian Capital Advisors LLP (a Category II AIF registered with SEBI, based in Mumbai), manages a specified fund that holds units on behalf of several non-resident investors with no permanent establishment in India. The fund earns income from Indian securities and also charges a 2% management fee to the non-resident unitholders. The Transfer Pricing Officer has initiated proceedings to determine the arm's length price of the management fee under the new faceless assessment scheme, claiming the fee is below market rate. Simultaneously, the GST department is asserting that the management fee constitutes an 'export of service' only if consideration is received in convertible foreign exchange, and is otherwise taxable as an intermediary service with place of supply in India. The non-residents argue that the income attributable to their units in the specified fund should be taxed at the concessional 10% rate, not the 20% FII rate. How should Meridian structure its response addressing (a) the applicability and procedural constraints of the faceless transfer pricing scheme including its sunset date, (b) the correct tax rate on securities income attributable to non-resident unitholders of a specified fund versus an FII, and (c) the GST treatment of the management fee?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is indispensable for the faceless TP scheme provisions (sub-sections 8-10 of section 92CA) including the March 2022 sunset proviso, and the specific 10% vs 20% rate distinction for specified funds vs FIIs under section 115AD(1)(i)(A)/(B), plus the sub-section 1A attribution rule for non-resident unitholders.

questions.q: Vanguard India Securities Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCV1234R1ZP, Maharashtra) acts as a sub-advisor to a foreign specified fund registered under IFSCA regulations. The Transfer Pricing Officer has proposed an adjustment to the sub-advisory fee paid by the specified fund to Vanguard, determining it is not at arm's length. Vanguard contends that the new team-based faceless TP determination scheme should apply and that the TPO cannot exercise jurisdiction through the traditional interface-based method. Meanwhile, Vanguard has been paying 18% GST on the sub-advisory fee treating it as an export of service (zero-rated under IGST Act), but the department argues that since the specified fund's income attributable to non-resident unitholders is taxed in India at 10% (not exempt), the service recipient 'benefits' in India, making it an intra-state supply. If the TP adjustment increases the deemed consideration, does Vanguard need to pay additional GST on the enhanced arm's length price, and can it rely on the faceless scheme's procedural safeguards — particularly the elimination of TPO-assessee interface and the parliamentary oversight requirement — to challenge the adjustment methodology?
questions.why_this_chunk: This chunk provides the specific statutory basis for the faceless TP scheme's three operational features (elimination of interface, economies of scale, team-based jurisdiction in sub-section 8), the sunset clause in the proviso to sub-section 9 (31 March 2022), the parliamentary laying requirement in sub-section 10, and the 10% concessional rate for specified funds under section 115AD(1)(i)(B) with the attribution rule in sub-section 1A — all of which are directly contested in the scenario.
