claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Plant Quarantine Order fee schedules and permit-issuing authority jurisdictions for importing plants, planting materials, soil, growing media, sand, peat, sphagnum moss, and related articles into India — directly relevant to customs clearance complexity for agricultural/horticultural importers. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-jurisdiction permit authority, fee computation, and interplay with Customs Act import procedures.

questions.q: GreenRoots Horticulture Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCG1234F1ZX), based in Bengaluru, Karnataka, imports a consignment of 1,200 kgs of sphagnum moss and 800 kgs of Perlite (declared for agricultural use) arriving at New Delhi's IGI Airport in March 2025. The company's customs broker insists that two separate Plant Quarantine Import Permits are required — one from the Officer-In-Charge at the National Plant Quarantine Station, New Delhi for the sphagnum moss, and another from the Plant Protection Adviser for the Perlite. Additionally, fumigation is to be arranged by the importer at his cost under supervision. What is the correct Plant Quarantine fee liability for the sphagnum moss consignment, which authority has jurisdiction to issue the permit for each commodity given the port of entry, and does the fumigation supervision obligation under Schedule IX apply to both commodities or only to the planting materials?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk provides the exact fee slab for sphagnum moss above 1000 kgs (Rs. 2300/- plus Rs. 50/- per additional tonne), the Schedule-X jurisdiction table showing NPQS New Delhi covers sphagnum moss at New Delhi Airport, and the Schedule IX fumigation/supervision obligation language specifying the importer bears the cost — all three are indispensable to answering the question.

questions.q: FloraImex LLP, a Delhi-based importer (IEC: AABFF2345Q), sources a mixed shipment arriving at Amritsar Airport containing: (a) 95 kgs of fresh fruit (mangoes) from Pakistan, (b) 500 kgs of pure Kaolin clay declared for non-agricultural industrial ceramic use, and (c) 50 kgs of biocontrol agent cultures. The RPQS Amritsar officer raises a query on whether he has jurisdiction to clear all three items or whether the biocontrol agent cultures require a separate permit from the Plant Protection Adviser at a different notified entry point, and also whether the Kaolin clay attracts any Plant Quarantine fee or supervision charge given its declared non-agricultural purpose. Advise FloraImex LLP on (i) the correct permit-issuing authority for each of the three commodities at Amritsar, (ii) the applicable fee computation for the fresh fruit consignment, and (iii) whether the Schedule IX fumigation/disinfestation obligation is triggered for the Kaolin clay.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Schedule-X entry for RPQS Amritsar explicitly lists its jurisdiction (Amritsar Airport, Punjab/Chandigarh border points) and the commodities it can permit including biocontrol agents and growing media; the fee table for fresh fruits and the Schedule IX fumigation language for 'Fresh fruits/Vegetables' vs. inorganic materials like Kaolin (listed under sand/similar materials exempt category) are all drawn directly from this chunk.

title: The Finance Act, 2024
text:
4	
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINARY	
[Part II— 
4

(i) having a total income exceeding fifty lakh rupees but

not exceeding one crore rupees, at the rate of ten per cent. 
of such income-tax;

(ii) having a total income exceeding one crore rupees, 
but not exceeding two crore rupees, at the rate of fifteen     
per cent. of such income-tax;

(iii) having a total income exceeding two crore rupees,

but not exceeding five crore rupees, at the rate of          
twenty-five per cent. of such income-tax; and

(iv) having a total income exceeding five crore rupees, 
at the rate of thirty-seven per cent. of such income-tax;

(b) in the case of every individual or association of persons, 
except in a case of an association of persons consisting of only 
companies as its members or body of individuals, whether 
incorporated or not, or every artificial juridical person referred 
to in sub-clause (vii) of clause (31) of section 2 of the Income-tax 
Act, having income under section 115AD of the Income-tax Act, 
and not having any income chargeable to tax under sub-section (1A) 
of section 115BAC of the Income-tax Act,—

(i) having a total income exceeding fifty lakh rupees but 
not exceeding one crore rupees, at the rate of ten per cent. 
of such income-tax;

(ii) having a total income exceeding one crore rupees, 
but not exceeding two crore rupees, at the rate of fifteen      
per cent. of such income-tax;

(iii) having a total income [excluding the income by way 
of dividend or income of the nature referred to in clause (b) of 
sub-section (1) of section 115AD of the Income-tax Act] 
exceeding two crore rupees but not exceeding five crore 
rupees, at the rate of twenty-five per cent. of such income-tax;

(iv) having a total income [excluding the income by way 
of dividend or income of the nature referred to in clause (b) 
of sub-section (1) of section 115AD of the Income-tax Act] 
exceeding five crore rupees, at the rate of thirty-seven         
per cent. of such income-tax; and

(v) having a total income [including the income by way 
of dividend or income of the nature referred to in clause (b) 
of sub-section (1) of section 115AD of the Income-tax Act] 
exceeding two crore rupees, but is not covered in                
sub-clauses (iii) and (iv), at the rate of fifteen per cent. of 
such income-tax:

Provided that in case where the total income includes any 
income by way of dividend or income chargeable under clause (b) 
of sub-section (1) of section 115AD of the Income-tax Act, the 
rate of surcharge on the income-tax calculated on that part of 
income shall not exceed fifteen per cent.:

Provided further that where the total income of a person, 
being a specified fund referred to in clause (c) of the Explanation 
to clause (4D) of section 10 of the Income-tax Act, includes any  
income  under  clause (a)  of  sub-section (1)  of section 115AD
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the surcharge rate schedule for individuals and AOPs under the Finance Act 2024, including the critical proviso that caps surcharge at 15% on dividend income and income under section 115AD(1)(b), and the special multi-slab computation required when a taxpayer's income straddles both 115AD-type income and other income. These carve-outs and provisos support multi-entity, legal-interpretation, and cross-act complexity.

questions.q: Silvergate Capital Advisors LLP, a SEBI-registered Category-III AIF structured as an AOP (members are all individuals, not companies), has total income of ₹6.2 crore for AY 2025-26. Of this, ₹2.8 crore is short-term capital gains on listed equity derivatives covered under section 115AD(1)(b), and ₹3.4 crore is business income from advisory fees. The LLP's tax counsel is computing surcharge liability. She notes that the blended income exceeds ₹5 crore, which ordinarily attracts 37% surcharge, but argues that the proviso capping surcharge at 15% on 115AD(1)(b) income must be applied separately to that slice. The client disputes this, contending that surcharge should be computed on total income-tax as a single figure. Which position is correct, how should the surcharge be bifurcated across the two income heads, and does the 37% rate apply at all to any portion of this taxpayer's income?
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso to sub-clause (b) explicitly caps the surcharge rate on income-tax attributable to 115AD(1)(b) income at 15%, while sub-clause (iv) imposes 37% on total income exceeding ₹5 crore — the tension between these two provisions and how to bifurcate the computation is directly answered only by this chunk.

questions.q: Meridian Global Opportunities Fund, a Mauritius-resident FPI registered with SEBI, invests through an AOP structure with Indian resident co-investors. For AY 2025-26, the AOP's total income is ₹3.6 crore, comprising ₹1.9 crore of dividend income and ₹1.7 crore of gains on listed securities — both falling under section 115AD. The AOP does not opt for section 115BAC. The tax officer proposes surcharge at 25% on total income-tax since total income exceeds ₹2 crore but not ₹5 crore. The AOP's counsel contends this is wrong: the dividend and 115AD(1)(b) gains must be excluded from the ₹2 crore threshold test under sub-clauses (iii) and (iv), and separately taxed with a 15% surcharge cap under the proviso. Critically, after the exclusion, the remaining income is only ₹1.7 crore, which falls in the 15% surcharge slab — but the total including excluded income is ₹3.6 crore, potentially triggering sub-clause (v) at 15%. Walk through the correct surcharge computation step by step, identifying which sub-clause governs each income stream and whether sub-clause (v) applies as a residual catch-all.
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-clauses (iii), (iv), and (v) of section (b) — which exclude dividend/115AD(1)(b) income from the slab threshold tests but then impose a 15% surcharge via sub-clause (v) when total income including those heads exceeds ₹2 crore — are the precise provisions needed, along with the proviso capping surcharge on that income at 15%.

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers post-submission obligations upon cancellation of GST registration: no fee payable, mandatory final return, payment of outstanding dues, and contact-update rights. These procedural rules interact with ITC reversal, pending refund claims, and compliance timelines, making them the indispensable anchor for complex cancellation-scenario questions.

questions.q: Prakash Textiles LLP (GSTIN 27AABFP3321K1ZT, Maharashtra) voluntarily applied for cancellation of GST registration on 10-March-2025 after winding down its B2B fabric supply business. At the time of filing the cancellation application, the firm had ₹4.2 lakh in net ITC lying in its electronic credit ledger, ₹1.8 lakh in output tax liability for February-2025 still unpaid, and a pending IGST refund claim of ₹2.6 lakh on zero-rated exports filed in January-2025. The partners are now asking: (a) what payment, if any, must be made before or after the cancellation application is accepted; (b) is the ITC of ₹4.2 lakh available to offset the ₹1.8 lakh liability, or must it be reversed; and (c) can the IGST refund claim still be processed after the final return is filed? Walk through the sequence of obligations the LLP must discharge under the cancellation regime, including what the final return must capture and whether failure to file it blocks the refund.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly mandates that after submitting a cancellation application the registered person 'shall make payment, if not made at the time of this application, and shall furnish final return' — this is the direct statutory basis for obligations (a) and the sequencing question in (c); without this provision the payment-and-final-return linkage cannot be established.

questions.q: Rajeshwari Cold Chain Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 36AABCR4892M1ZQ, Telangana) filed for voluntary cancellation of its GST registration on 28-February-2025, having ceased all refrigerated logistics operations. Three weeks after submission, the proprietor realized the firm's mobile number registered on the GST portal was linked to a deactivated SIM, and all OTP-based communications regarding the cancellation status and any SCN issued by the officer were going undelivered. Additionally, the firm's CA discovered that the final return had not yet been filed and ₹3.1 lakh of tax paid via DRC-03 during the cancellation process was double-counted in the application. Given that the application is already submitted: (a) can the firm now update its mobile number and email on the portal without withdrawing the cancellation application; (b) how should it track the current status of its application given the communication gap; and (c) what are the consequences if the final return is not filed within the prescribed period — does the officer's order of cancellation remain valid, and does the double-payment error need to be corrected before or after the final return?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the direct authority for two of the three sub-questions: it explicitly states the registered person 'may also update his contact address and update his mobile number and e mail address' post-submission, and that 'Status of the Application may be tracked on the common portal' — both facts are indispensable to resolving the communication-gap scenario in (a) and (b).

title: Amendment of notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax dated 19.06.2017 for notifying e-way bill website
text:
[To be published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section 
(i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise and Customs

Notification No. 9/2018 – Central Tax

New Delhi, the 23rd January, 2018

G.S.R....(E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by section 146 of the Central Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) read with section 20 of the Integrated Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017 (13 of 2017), and in supersession  of the notification of the 
Government of India in the Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue No. 4/2017 - 
Central Tax dated 19th June, 2017, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, 
Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G.S.R. 606 (E), dated the 19th June, 2017, except as 
respects things done or omitted to be done before such supersession, the Central Government 
hereby notifies www.gst.gov.in as the Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal for 
facilitating registration, payment of tax, furnishing of returns and computation and settlement 
of integrated tax and www.ewaybillgst.gov.in as the Common Goods and Services Tax 
Electronic Portal for furnishing electronic way bill.

Explanation.-

(1) For the purposes of this notification, “www.gst.gov.in” means the website managed

by the Goods and Services Tax Network, a company incorporated under the 
provisions of section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013 (18 of 2013); and

(2) For the purposes of this notification, “www.ewaybillgst.gov.in” means the website

managed by the National Informatics Centre, Ministry of Electronics & Information 
Technology, Government of India.

2.  
This notification shall be deemed to have come into force with effect from the 16th

day of January, 2018.

[F. No.349/58/2017-GST(Pt.)]

(Dr. Sreeparvathy S.L.)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively establishes (a) the legal identity of www.ewaybillgst.gov.in as the sole notified portal for e-way bills under section 146 CGST read with section 20 IGST, (b) its retrospective effect from 16-Jan-2018 superseding Notification 4/2017-CT, and (c) that NIC manages the e-way bill portal while GSTN manages the GST portal — facts critical to disputes about e-way bill validity, penalty liability, and portal-generated errors in the gap period 16-Jan to 23-Jan 2018.

questions.q: Rajashri Petrochemicals Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCR1234F1ZP, Maharashtra) was transporting a consignment of HDPE granules worth ₹38 lakh from Nagpur to a buyer in Hyderabad (Telangana) on 19-Jan-2018. The e-way bill generated on that date was challenged by a GST officer at a Vidarbha check-post on the ground that no portal had been legally notified for e-way bill generation as of that date — the officer contended that the notification was published only on 23-Jan-2018 and therefore the e-way bill issued on 19-Jan-2018 lacked a valid legal basis, exposing the consignment to detention and penalty under the relevant CGST provision. Rajashri's counsel argues the e-way bill was valid. Who is correct, and what is the precise legal basis — including the interplay between the notification's publication date and its deemed commencement clause — that determines whether an e-way bill generated on 19-Jan-2018 on www.ewaybillgst.gov.in is legally valid for an inter-state supply subject to IGST?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause that 'this notification shall be deemed to have come into force with effect from the 16th day of January, 2018' is indispensable — it is the only basis on which an e-way bill generated between 16-Jan and 22-Jan-2018 can be defended as legally valid despite the notification being Gazetted on 23-Jan-2018.

questions.q: Meridian Logistics LLP, a GTA registered in Karnataka (GSTIN 29AABFM5678K1ZQ), was hired by Coimbatore Textiles Pvt Ltd (Tamil Nadu) to transport polyester yarn from Tiruppur to a warehouse in Pune on 20-Jan-2018. The e-way bill was generated on www.ewaybillgst.gov.in by Coimbatore Textiles. En route, a GST inspector in Maharashtra issued a show-cause notice to Meridian Logistics alleging (i) the e-way bill was generated on a portal not validly notified as of the transport date, and (ii) even if the portal were valid, the liability for any penalty falls on the transporter under the CGST Act since the consignor is in a different state, making this an IGST supply. Meridian Logistics now seeks your opinion: (a) was www.ewaybillgst.gov.in a legally notified portal on 20-Jan-2018, and under which provision of which Act was it so notified; (b) does the supersession of Notification 4/2017-CT affect any obligations arising before 16-Jan-2018; and (c) given that this is an inter-state supply, does section 20 of the IGST Act independently validate the portal notification for IGST purposes, or does the CGST notification alone suffice?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the sole source for (a) the deemed-effect date of 16-Jan-2018 making the portal valid on 20-Jan-2018, (b) the 'except as respects things done or omitted before supersession' savings clause governing pre-16-Jan obligations, and (c) the explicit invocation of section 20 IGST alongside section 146 CGST as the dual statutory basis for notifying the portal for inter-state supplies.

title: Advance Intermediate Licence for iron & steel under Engg. goods export scheme - Customs instructions
text:
Circular No. 19/95-Cus. (DBK)

dated 8/3/95

F.No. 605/198/94-DBK

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue, New Delhi

Subject : Value Based Advance Intermediate Licence for import of Iron & Steel Intermediates - Engineering Products Export
(Replenishment of Iron & Steel Intermediates) Scheme

Ministry of Commerce vide their Notification No. 53/ RE/ 92-97 dated the 1st March, 1995 have notified Engineering Products
Export (Replenishment of Iron and Steel Intermediates) Scheme. To give effect to this scheme which permits duty free import of
inputs required by Iron and Steel Producers to manufacture Iron and Steel Intermediates for supply to Engineering Exporters,
Notification No. 10/ 95-Customs dated the 7th March; 1995 has been issued. The following aspects should be kept in view
while permitting duty free import against Value Based Advance Intermediate Licences issued under this Scheme.

(i) Items permissible for import.

The licence issued under the scheme shall be based on value based norms prescribed in the Handbook of Procedure, Vol. II
for the sub-group "Iron, Steel, Ferro Alloys and Refractories" under the export product group "Engineering Product". The
licence besides specifying the quantity and value of such Iron and Steel Intermediates required to be supplied to Engineering
Exporters will also indicate the inputs permissible for import which have to be raw materials, components, intermediates and
consumables specified as import item in the Input Output - Norms for the manufacture of such Iron and Steel Intermediates.
ONly such inputs should be permitted duty free clearance which are usable in the manufacture of specified Iron and Steel
Intermediates keeping in view their grade, quality and specifications. Since the licences issued under the Scheme will be
Value Based Advance Intermediate Licences, quantity / value restrictions only in respect of sensitive items as specified in the
licence or Handbook of Procedure, Vol. II shall be applicable.

(ii) Execution of Bond with the Licensing Authority.

Under the scheme the importer is required to execute a bond/LUT with the Licensing Authority and produce evidence of the
same before clearance of any inputs against the said licence.

(iii) Execution of Bond with Customs Authorities .

The notification requires the importer to execute a bond with such surety or security with the Assistant Collector of Customs
binding himself to fulfill the obligation in terms of the said notification and to pay duty in case of default. This bond should
provide for production of evidence of having supplied Iron and Steel Intermediates to Engineering Products exporter in the form
of a certificate from the Licencing Authority. The bond should also bind the importer with the actual user condition as contained
in clause (v) of the notification. The said condition makes it obligatory for the licence holder to use the inputs imported in his
own factory and in on case the licence or the inputs imported are transferable. For discharge of this obligation, a certificate of
such use of inputs by Central Excise Superintendent incharge of the factory should be insisted upon. The said certificate should
also confirm that no MODVAT was availed in respect of iron and steel intermediates supplied to Engineering Product Exporter.
The extent of security or surety to be taken can be decided by the Custom House depending upon the status of the importer i.e.
whether Trading House, Export House, etc.

(iv) Applicability of other conditions relating to VABAL Scheme.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Value Based Advance Intermediate Licence scheme for duty-free import of iron & steel inputs, specifying bond execution requirements with both Licensing Authority and Customs, actual-user conditions, non-transferability of licence/inputs, and the MODVAT non-availing certificate requirement — all highly specific procedural and legal obligations that anchor complex practitioner scenarios.

questions.q: Tata Structurals Pvt Ltd, an iron & steel producer in Jharkhand (GSTIN 20AABCT1234A1Z5), holds a Value Based Advance Intermediate Licence issued under the Engineering Products Export (Replenishment of Iron and Steel Intermediates) Scheme. They imported billets duty-free against this licence and subsequently supplied hot-rolled sections to Bharat Engineering Exports LLP in Maharashtra. Customs audit at Nhava Sheva now alleges two defaults: (a) the inputs were used partly at a job-work facility in Odisha and not exclusively in Tata Structurals' own Jharkhand factory, and (b) the Central Excise Superintendent's certificate produced does not explicitly confirm MODVAT non-availment on the iron & steel intermediates supplied to the engineering exporter. Can Tata Structurals defend on the ground that the Odisha facility is a captive unit, and is the absence of the MODVAT confirmation language alone sufficient to trigger duty demand and bond encashment by Customs?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly imposes the actual-user condition (inputs must be used in the importer's own factory, not transferable), requires a CE Superintendent certificate confirming use AND confirming no MODVAT was availed on intermediates supplied to the engineering exporter — both conditions are directly at issue in this scenario.

questions.q: Precision Alloys LLP in Tamil Nadu obtained a Value Based Advance Intermediate Licence for importing ferro-alloys under the Engineering Products Export Replenishment Scheme. Before any clearance, they executed a bond/LUT with the DGFT Licensing Authority but failed to produce evidence of this bond at the time of duty-free clearance of the first consignment at Chennai Customs. They later produced the bond document three days after clearance. Simultaneously, the Assistant Collector of Customs is demanding a separate surety bond from Precision Alloys LLP at 100% CIF value, whereas Precision Alloys holds Star Export House status. Is the post-facto production of the Licensing Authority bond a curable procedural defect, and can Precision Alloys LLP resist the 100% surety demand from Customs on the basis of their export house status, given the scheme's instructions on security quantum?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that evidence of bond/LUT with the Licensing Authority must be produced before clearance (not after), and separately that the quantum of security/surety for the Customs bond can be decided by the Custom House depending on the importer's status as Trading House, Export House, etc. — both these specific rules are indispensable to resolving the scenario.

title: Specified Goods (Prevention of Illegal Export) Rules, 1969
text:
Rule 4. Form, etc. of accounts to be maintained under section 11L. -

(1) The accounts required to be maintained under Section 11L shall contain the following particulars in respect of each
acquisition, sale or disposal of the specified goods:-

(i) name of the specified goods;

(ii) name and full business address of the person from whom the specified goods have been acquired or in whose favour the
specified goods have been parted with;

(iii) net weight of the specified goods;

1 [(iv)* * * *]

(v) time and date of acquisition, or parting with, of the specified goods.

(2) At the end of each day, the person concerned shall total the acquisitions, sales and disposals of the specified goods and
enter the closing balance in the accounts.

(3) All particulars referred to in this rule shall be entered in a register:

Provided that particulars in respect of each sale or disposal of specified goods may be entered in a book of sale or disposal
memos in duplicate.

(4) The pages of the register and the book of sale or disposal memos, shall have consecutive serial numbers stamped thereon
and the entries in respect of each acquisition, sale or disposal shall be made immediately after the acquisition, sale or disposal
as the case may be.

1 [* * *]

1. Omitted by M.F. (D.R.) Notification No. 31/90 (N.T.)-Cus., dated 8-6-1990.

Rule 5. Reasonable steps to be taken under section 11M. -

The reasonable steps to be taken under section 11M by a person selling or transferring any specified goods shall be the
following, namely :-

He shall satisfy himself about the identity and address of the purchaser or transferee, as the case may be, either -

(a) from his personal knowledge; or

(b) on the strength of a certificate given by a person personally known to the seller or transferee, as the case may be, and
with whose handwriting and signature such seller or transferor is familiar; or

(c) on the strength of a certificate issued under the Gold Control Act, 1968 (45 of 1968) for recognition as a goldsmith or an
identity card issued under that Act to an artisan; or

(d) on the strength of a certificate issued to the purchaser or transferee, as the case may be, by the Inspector of Central
Excise within whose jurisdiction such purchaser or transferee has his place of business :

Provided that -

(i) where the identity and address of the purchaser or transferee, as the case may be, is satisfied on the strength of a
certificate referred to in (b) above, the seller or transferor, as the case may be, shall retain such certificate for production
before the proper officer; and

(ii) where the identity and address of the purchaser or transferee, as the case may be, is to be satisfied on the strength of
a certificate referred to in (d) above, such certificate shall be got countersigned by the seller or transferor, as the case
may be, from the Superintendent of Central Excise within whose jurisdiction he has his place of business.

(2) The certificate of identity referred to in sub-rule (1)(b) shall be in the following form and shall be written in manuscript by the
person issuing it:

"I, Shri........residing at.......do hereby certify that Shri......... residing at.......is personally known to me.

His signature is given below. He is not a dealer in/ manufacturer of silver..........
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers two procedural compliance obligations under the Specified Goods (Prevention of Illegal Export) Rules, 1969: Rule 4 mandates a specific register format with consecutively stamped page numbers and same-day entry requirements for acquisitions/disposals of specified goods, and Rule 5 prescribes the exact 'reasonable steps' for identity verification of purchasers — including the countersignature requirement from a Superintendent of Central Excise. These are narrow, operationally specific rules that support high-complexity questions involving multi-party transactions and legal interpretation of what constitutes compliance.

questions.q: Ratlam Silver House, a proprietorship in Madhya Pradesh (GSTIN 23AABFR1234A1ZP) dealing in silver articles, sold 8.2 kg of silver granules on 18-March-2025 to Mehta Bullion Traders LLP in Rajasthan. The purchaser's identity was vouched for by a mutual acquaintance's handwritten certificate, but Ratlam Silver House's proprietor did not get the certificate countersigned by any Central Excise authority since the certificate came from a personal acquaintance, not from the Central Excise Inspector. During a customs inspection on 22-April-2025, the officer alleges that the 'reasonable steps' requirement was not met. Ratlam Silver House argues that a personal-acquaintance certificate requires no countersignature. Which sub-rule provision specifically distinguishes between certificates from personal acquaintances versus certificates from the Central Excise Inspector in terms of countersignature obligations, and has Ratlam Silver House correctly interpreted its duty?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 5 proviso (i) and (ii) draw a precise distinction: certificates from a personal acquaintance under sub-rule (1)(b) need only be retained by the seller, while certificates from the Central Excise Inspector under sub-rule (1)(d) must be countersigned by the Superintendent of Central Excise. The answer turns entirely on which sub-clause applies and the proviso language in this chunk.

questions.q: Silverline Crafts Pvt Ltd, a registered manufacturer of silver articles in Jaipur with multiple shop floors, maintains a single combined register for all acquisitions and disposals of specified goods across floors. On a particular day in February 2025, a floor supervisor recorded 14 disposal entries in the register the following morning rather than on the day of disposal itself, citing operational workload. Additionally, the company had been using an unnumbered loose-leaf binder for sale memos instead of a pre-stamped duplicate book. A customs audit team flagged both practices as non-compliant. The company's CA contends that the loose-leaf binder is effectively a 'book of sale or disposal memos in duplicate' and that next-day entry is a minor procedural lapse with no export-evasion intent. Evaluate whether each of the two practices — next-day entries and unnumbered loose-leaf memos — constitutes a violation of the prescribed account-maintenance requirements, and what specific formal elements the rules mandate for the register and the duplicate memo book.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 4(3) proviso permits a separate book of sale/disposal memos in duplicate as an alternative to register entries, but Rule 4(4) requires consecutive serial numbers stamped on both the register and that book, and entries must be made 'immediately after' each transaction. Both the timing violation and the unnumbered-binder issue are resolved exclusively by Rule 4(3)–(4) in this chunk.

title: Central Excise Act, 1944
text:
(2) Every proceeding which is pending immediately before the appointed day before the Central Government under section 36,
as it stood immediately before that day, and any matter arising out of or connected with such proceeding and which is so
pending shall stand transferred on that day to the Appellate Tribunal and the Appellate Tribunal may proceed with such
proceeding or matter from the stage at which it was on that day as if such proceeding or matter were an appeal filed before it :

Provided that if  any such proceeding or matter relates to an order where -

(a) in any disputed case, other than a case where the determination of any question having a relation to the rate of duty of excise
or to the value of goods for purposes of assessment is in issue or is one of the points in issue, the difference in duty involved or
the duty involved; or

(b) the amount of fine or penalty determined by such order, does not exceed ten thousand rupees, such proceeding or matter
shall continue to be dealt with by the Central Government as if the said section 36 had not been substituted :

Provided further that the applicant or the other party may make a demand to the Appellate Tribunal that before proceeding
further with that proceeding or matter, he may be re-heard.

(3) Every proceeding which is pending immediately before the appointed day before the Board or the 1 [Principal Commissioner
of Central Excise or Commissioner of Central Excise] under section 35A, as it stood immediately before that day, and any
matter arising out of or connected with such proceeding and which is so pending shall continue to be dealt with by the Board or
the 2 [Principal Commissioner of Central Excise or Commissioner of Central Excise], as the case may be, as if the said section
had not been substituted.

(4) Any person who immediately before the appointed day was authorised to appear in any appeal or proceeding transferred
under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) shall, notwithstanding anything contained in section 35Q, have the right to appear before
the Appellate Tribunal in relation to such appeal or proceeding.

1. Substituted (w.e.f. 6-8-2014) words "Commissioner of Central Excise" by s. 95 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2014 (25 of 2014):

2. Substituted (w.e.f. 6-8-2014) words "Commissioner of Central Excise" by s. 95 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2014 (25 of 2014).

Section 35Q. Appearance by authorised representative. -

(1) Any person who is entitled or required to appear before a Central Excise Officer or the Appellate Tribunal in connection with
any proceedings under this Act, otherwise than when required under this Act to appear personally for examination on oath or
affirmation, may, subject to the other provisions of this section, appear by an authorised representative.

(2) For the purposes of this section, " authorised representative" means a person authorised by the person referred to in sub-
section (1) to appear on his behalf, being -

(a) his relative or regular employee; or

(b) any legal practitioner who is entitled to practise in any civil court in India; or

(c) any person who has acquired such qualifications as the Central Government may prescribe for this purpose.

(3) Not withstanding anything contained in this section, no person who was a member of the Indian Customs and Central Excise
Service - Group A and has retired or resigned from such Service after having served for not less than three years in any capacity
in that Service, shall be entitled to appear as an authorised representative in any proceedings before a Central Excise Officer for
a period of two years from the date of his retirement or resignation, as the case maybe.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs (a) the transfer of pre-'appointed day' pending proceedings to CESTAT with the critical Rs. 10,000 carve-out and the rate-of-duty exception, and (b) who qualifies as an 'authorised representative' under s.35Q including the cooling-off bar on retired Group-A officers. Both rules are specific enough to anchor hard practitioner scenarios involving transition-era appeals and representation eligibility disputes.

questions.q: Precision Alloys Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, GSTIN registration pre-GST, Central Excise assessee) had a show-cause notice adjudicated against it in 2014 involving two distinct issues: (i) a disputed classification of a ferro-alloy that affected the rate of duty — duty differential of Rs. 8,40,000 — and (ii) a penalty of Rs. 9,200 for a procedural lapse in maintaining RG-1 register. The order was challenged by the company before the Central Government under the old revisionary jurisdiction immediately before the CESTAT amendment came into force ('appointed day'). On the appointed day the file was transferred to CESTAT. The company's advocate argues the entire proceeding — including the penalty limb — should now be heard by CESTAT. The department contends the penalty limb, being below Rs. 10,000, must be severed and returned to the Central Government. (a) Which limb(s) of the proceeding correctly vest with CESTAT and which, if any, remain with the Central Government after transfer, and on what basis? (b) If the penalty limb stays with the Central Government, can Precision Alloys still demand a re-hearing, and before which forum?
questions.why_this_chunk: The first proviso to sub-section (2) creates a conjunctive/disjunctive split: where the disputed case involves a rate-of-duty question (clause a) the Rs. 10,000 floor does not apply and the matter goes to CESTAT; but the penalty limb under clause (b) independently stays if it does not exceed Rs. 10,000. The second proviso's re-hearing right is also squarely in this chunk.

questions.q: Shri Ramakant Desai retired from the Indian Customs and Central Excise Service (Group A) in March 2012 after 27 years of service, and has since been practising as a consultant. He was authorised by Sunrise Chemicals LLP (Surat) to appear in an excise appeal pending before the Central Government under old section 36. On the appointed day, that appeal stood transferred to CESTAT. The department raises a preliminary objection: (a) that Desai cannot appear before CESTAT because he falls within the bar on retired Group-A officers under s.35Q, and (b) that even if he could appear, his original authorisation was for the Central Government forum and does not carry over to CESTAT. Sunrise Chemicals counters that sub-section (4) of the transition provision expressly preserves the right of anyone 'authorised to appear' in a transferred proceeding, and that this overrides s.35Q. Analyse the interplay between the two provisions and advise Sunrise Chemicals on whether Desai may continue as its representative before CESTAT in this transferred matter.
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-section (4) of 35P explicitly provides that a person authorised before the appointed day in a transferred appeal has the right to appear 'notwithstanding anything contained in section 35Q' — that non-obstante clause is the precise textual fulcrum of the dispute, and s.35Q(3)'s bar on retired Group-A officers is also defined in this chunk.

title: Implementation of E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 in supersession of E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016 – reg.
text:
extended producer responsibility certificate shall have a unique number containing year of

generation, code of end product, recycler code and a unique code and the extended producer 
responsibility certificates shall be in the denominations of 100, 200, 500 and 1000 kg or such 
other denominations as may be laid down by the Central Pollution Control Board with the 
approval of the Steering Committee.

(2) Refurbishing.  (i) The e-waste shall also be allowed for refurbishing and refurbisher shall have to 
get registered on the portal and based on the data provided, refurbishing certificate shall be generated in 
favour of a registered refurbisher in the format laid down by it in this regard.

27

Generated from eOffice by MANISH KUMAR CHOUDHARY, CBIC-OSD(MKC)-CUSTOM POLICY WING, OSD, CBEC on 07/01/2023 01:58 PM
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) certificate structure under E-Waste (Management) Rules 2022 — specifically denomination requirements (100/200/500/1000 kg), unique numbering format, and the refurbishing registration/certificate regime. These are indispensable for customs compliance questions involving import of refurbished electronics or EPR certificate trading across entities.

questions.q: Nexora Electronics Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) imports a consignment of 3,800 kg of used laptops from a Singapore-based refurbisher, Greenloop Pte Ltd, on 18-March-2025 under the Electronics category. At the time of import, Nexora presents EPR certificates totaling 3,800 kg but the denominations submitted are: two certificates of 1,500 kg each and one of 800 kg. The Customs officer at JNCH raises an objection that the certificates are not in permissible denominations and proposes to detain the consignment. Nexora's counsel argues that the CPCB may notify other denominations and that the total quantity is covered. Who is correct, and what documentary evidence would Nexora need to produce to clear the consignment without re-processing the EPR certificates?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly limits EPR certificate denominations to 100, 200, 500 and 1000 kg 'or such other denominations as may be laid down by the Central Pollution Control Board with the approval of the Steering Committee' — this proviso is the exact legal hook Nexora's counsel is invoking, and the chunk is the authoritative source for whether 1,500 kg and 800 kg denominations are permissible.

questions.q: Reborn Devices LLP (Karnataka, GSTIN 29AABCR5678G1ZY) operates as a registered refurbisher of mobile phones. It enters into a back-to-back arrangement with Vridhi Recyclers Pvt Ltd (Tamil Nadu) whereby Vridhi processes the e-waste and generates EPR certificates in its own name, which it then endorses/transfers to Reborn for use against Reborn's EPR obligation under a Producer Responsibility Agreement. During a customs audit of Reborn's import files for 2024-25, the auditor questions whether refurbishing certificates generated on the portal in Vridhi's name can be used by Reborn to satisfy its own compliance, or whether Reborn must independently be registered and have certificates generated in its own name. Advise on the validity of the cross-entity certificate transfer and the specific portal registration requirement.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk states that 'refurbishing certificate shall be generated in favour of a registered refurbisher' — the word 'in favour of' and the mandatory portal registration requirement for the refurbisher are the specific textual anchors that determine whether Reborn (as the party using the certificate) must itself be the registered refurbisher or can rely on Vridhi's certificate.

title: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) to be followed by exporters -reg.
text:
Circular No.131/1/2020-GST

CBEC-20/16/07/2020-GST

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs

GST Policy Wing

*****

New Delhi, Dated the  23rd January, 2020

To

The 
Principal 
Chief 
Commissioners/Chief 
Commissioners/Principal 
Commissioners/

Commissioners (All)  
 
Madam/Sir,

Subject: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) to be followed by exporters– regarding

As you are aware, several cases of monetisation of credit fraudulently obtained or ineligible

credit through refund of Integrated Goods & Service Tax (IGST) on exports of goods have been 
detected in past few months. On verification, several such exporters were found to be non-existent 
in a number of cases. In all these cases it has been found that the Input Tax Credit (ITC) was taken 
by the exporters on the basis of fake invoices and IGST on exports was paid using such ITC.

2. 
To mitigate the risk, the Board has taken measures to apply stringent risk parameters-based

checks driven by rigorous data analytics and Artificial Intelligence tools based on which certain 
exporters are taken up for further verification. Overall, in a broader time frame the percentage of 
such exporters selected for verification is a small fraction of the total number of exporters claiming 
refunds. The refund scrolls in such cases are kept in abeyance till the verification report in respect 
of such cases is received from the field formations. Further, the export consignments/shipments 
of concerned exporters are subjected to 100 % examination at the customs port.

3. 
While the verifications are caused to mitigate risk, it is necessary that genuine exporters do

not face any hardship.  In this context it is advised that exporters whose scrolls have been kept in 
abeyance for verification would be informed at the earliest possible either by the jurisdictional 
CGST or by Customs. To expedite the verification, the exporters on being informed in this regard 
or on their own volition should fill in information in the format attached as Annexure ‘A’ to this 
Circular and submit the same to their jurisdictional CGST authorities for verification by them. If 
required, the jurisdictional authority may seek further additional information for verification. 
However, the jurisdictional authorities must adhere to timelines prescribed for verification.

3.1 
Verification shall be completed by jurisdiction CGST office within 14 working days of

furnishing of information in proforma by the exporter.  If the verification is not completed within 
this period, the jurisdiction officer will bring it the notice of a nodal cell to be constituted in the 
jurisdictional Pr. Chief Commissioner/Chief Commissioner Office.

3.2 
After a period of 14 working days from the date of submission of details in the prescribed

format, the exporter may also escalate the matter to the Jurisdictional Pr. Chief

Page 1 of 5
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the SOP for IGST refund verification for exporters flagged by risk-based analytics, including the 14-working-day timeline, abeyance of refund scrolls, 100% customs examination, and the escalation mechanism. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving exporters whose refunds are withheld and who must navigate both CGST and Customs simultaneously.

questions.q: Zenith Pharma Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCZ1234F1ZK, Mumbai) shipped a consignment of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients worth ₹4.2 crore to a buyer in Germany in November 2024. They paid IGST of ₹75.6 lakh on exports using ITC accumulated on domestic purchases from three different suppliers in Maharashtra. The IGST refund scroll was filed in December 2024, but as of mid-February 2025, no refund has been credited and their shipment at JNPT was subjected to 100% physical examination. The company has received no formal communication from either Customs or the CGST Commissionerate. The CFO wants to know: (a) under what authority can the department keep the refund scroll in abeyance without notifying the exporter, (b) what is the prescribed timeline within which the jurisdictional CGST office must complete verification once Zenith submits the prescribed information format, and (c) if the 14-working-day period lapses without any outcome, what escalation mechanism is available and to which authority?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly prescribes the abeyance mechanism for refund scrolls (para 2), the mandatory 14-working-day verification timeline (para 3.1), the requirement to inform exporters at the earliest (para 3), and the escalation to the nodal cell in the Pr. Chief Commissioner/Chief Commissioner office (para 3.1–3.2) — all of which are indispensable to answering all three sub-questions.

questions.q: Arjun Textiles LLP (GSTIN 29AABFA5678G1ZP, Bengaluru) exports readymade garments to a buyer in UAE under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT). In January 2025 they also have a pending IGST refund claim of ₹38 lakh for a separate shipment where IGST was paid in cash (not via ITC). The department's AI-driven risk engine has flagged Arjun Textiles, and (i) their refund scroll has been kept in abeyance and (ii) all their export consignments at Bengaluru Air Cargo are now under 100% examination. Their advocate argues that since the IGST on this particular claim was paid in cash and not via ITC, there is no fraud risk and the abeyance is unjustified. The company also has a new shipment worth ₹1.8 crore ready at the warehouse. Advise: (a) does the SOP distinguish between ITC-funded and cash-funded IGST payments when applying abeyance and 100% examination, (b) what proactive step can Arjun Textiles take immediately to initiate the verification clock, and (c) what is the outermost deadline before they can escalate to the Pr. Chief Commissioner level?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's para 2 applies abeyance and 100% examination based on risk parameters without carving out cash-paid IGST from ITC-fraud cases — a legal interpretation question. Para 3 and 3.2 prescribe the proactive submission of Annexure A and the 14-working-day clock that triggers the escalation right, both of which are the specific operative facts needed to answer parts (b) and (c).

title: Related to the Courier Imports and Exports(Clearance)(Amendment) Regulations
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-
SECTION (i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

(Central Board of Excise and Customs)

Notification

No.   66 /2017-Customs (N.T.)

New Delhi, the 30th June, 2017

G.S.R.      (E).- In exercise of powers conferred by section 157 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the

Board hereby makes the following regulations further to amend the Courier Imports and Exports (Clearance) 
Regulations, 1998, namely:-

1. 
(1) These regulations may be called the Courier Imports and Exports (Clearance) (Amendment) Regulations,

2017.

(2)  They shall come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette. 
 
2.  
In the Courier Imports and Exports (Clearance) Regulations, 1998, (hereinafter referred to as the said

regulations); in regulation 3, in sub-regulation (1),

after clause (da), the following clause shall be inserted, namely:-

‘(db) “Goods and Services Tax Identification Number” (GSTIN) means the number assigned under rule 3 of 
the Goods and Services Tax Registration Rules, 2017;’
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the insertion of GSTIN as a defined term within the Courier Imports and Exports (Clearance) Regulations, 1998, creating a formal cross-act linkage between Customs courier clearance regulations and the GST registration framework. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving the intersection of courier import/export compliance and GST identification requirements.

questions.q: Zephyr Lifestyle Imports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFZ1234A1ZK, registered in Maharashtra) regularly receives commercial shipments of branded apparel from a supplier in Hong Kong via DHL courier. During a customs examination on 18-Mar-2025 at the Mumbai air cargo facility, the assessing officer raised an objection that the GSTIN declared on the courier bill of entry does not correspond to any recognized definition within the Courier Imports and Exports (Clearance) Regulations, since the firm's GST registration was obtained under the composition scheme and the officer argues that 'GSTIN' was never formally defined within the courier regulations framework. Can Zephyr Lifestyle Imports LLP successfully contest this objection, and what is the precise regulatory basis — including the effective date of any amendment — that establishes GSTIN as a defined term within the courier clearance regulations?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the sole source establishing that 'GSTIN' (defined by reference to rule 3 of GST Registration Rules, 2017) was formally inserted into the Courier Imports and Exports (Clearance) Regulations, 1998 via Notification 66/2017-Customs NT dated 30-Jun-2017, which directly resolves the officer's objection about definitional authority.

questions.q: Radiance Electronics Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AAACR5678B1Z3, Karnataka) exports refurbished mobile phone components to a buyer in Singapore through a courier aggregator. The company's shipping documentation shows a GSTIN, but the courier aggregator's system was configured before July 2017 and its pre-clearance software does not capture GSTIN as a mandatory field, resulting in the field being left blank on the courier shipping bill filed at Bengaluru airport. The customs officer proposes to treat this as a mis-declaration under the Customs Act and initiate penal proceedings. The exporter argues that (a) GSTIN was not always a required defined term under the courier export regulations, and (b) the aggregator's non-capture of GSTIN predates any formal amendment. Advise Radiance Electronics Pvt Ltd on the precise cut-off date after which omission of GSTIN in courier export documentation would constitute a regulatory violation, and whether the aggregator's legacy system configuration provides any mitigation, referencing the specific amendment that introduced the GSTIN definition into the courier regulations.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes the exact notification (No. 66/2017-Customs NT, effective date of Gazette publication, i.e., 30-Jun-2017) that introduced GSTIN into the courier regulations, which is indispensable for determining the temporal cut-off date after which GSTIN omission becomes a regulatory non-compliance.

title: Friction cloth coming into existence at intermediary stage of the process of the manufacture of transmission of TR Beltings/V Belts and Conveyer Belts is a marketable commodity
text:
Union of India & Other
... Appellants

Versus
M/s Punjab Rubber & Allied Industries & Others... Respondents

ORDER

The respondent-industries filed a Writ Petition in the High Court of Punjab & Haryana, Civil Writ No. 4459 of 1980, seeking an
appropriate writ for declaring, that friction cloth and intermediate products produced by them in the process of manufacture of
T.R. Beltings/ V. Belts and Conveyer Belts were not liable to excise duty under item 19(1)(b) of the Central Excise Tariff and for
certain other incidental and consequential relief"s. Entry 19 (1) came to be amended by the Central Excise and Salt
(Amendment) Act, 1980 (Act 6 of 1980) whereby the said Entry was amended and rubberised cotton fabrics were specifically
included as a product eligible to excise duty under item 19(1)(b) of the Excise Tariff. After the amendment of this Entry, notices
were issued by the Excise Department requiring them to take out L.4 licence for the said intermediate product. On receipt of
these notices the Writ Petition aforementioned came to be filed. The said Writ Petition was heard and disposed of by a
Division Bench of the High Court by the impugned judgement dated 2nd September, 1981.

It may here be mentioned that the learned counsel appearing for the Department conceded before the Division Bench that no
duty would be leviable on the intermediary product if the process of manufacture of the end-product in composite, integrated
and uninterrupted. At the same time he also contended that the intermediary product manufactured by the concerned
industries, namely, friction cloth was a marketable commodity and hence it attracted excise duty. The Division Bench of the
High Court in Paragraph 7 of the impugned judgement recorded a finding to the effect: "there is no manner of doubt that the
process of manufacture of transmission of T.R. Beltings/ V. shapped Belts and process of manufacture of transmission of T.R.
Beltings/ V. shapped Belts and conveyor Belts, produced by the petitioners, is a composite, integrated and uninterrupted
process and even if some article styled as "Friction Cloth" comes into existence at an intermediary stage, the Department is
not justified in demanding excise duty on the said product, especially when it is nobody"s case that this intermediary product is
used or sold by the petitioners in the marker. It is obvious from the afore-quoted finding recorded by the Division Bench of the
High Court that it overlooked the fact that it was the Department"s contention that the it was sold in the market or was having a
market. It is, therefore, obvious that the Division Bench of the high Court proceeded on an erroneous assumption that it was
nobody"s case that this intermediary product was used or sold in the market. when it was contended by the Department that the
product was a marketable commodity it was intended to convey that it had a market and was capable of being sold in the
market. Unless there was a clear finding recorded on this aspect we don"t think that the Division Bench was, justified in
allowing the petition and preventing the Department form requiring the industries to take out a L. 4 licence.

In the result, we allow this appeal, set aside the impunged order of the Division Bench of the High Court and direct that the Writ
Petition will stend dismissed with no order as to costs.

Sd/- CJI

Sd/- J

(J.S. Verma)

Sd/- J

(B.N. Kirpal)

New Delhi, 
November 5, 1994
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a Supreme Court order (CJI bench, 1994) on whether 'friction cloth' — an intermediate product in the manufacture of TR Beltings/V-Belts/Conveyor Belts — attracts Central Excise duty. It authoritatively establishes the 'marketability test': even in a composite, integrated, uninterrupted manufacturing process, an intermediate product is dutiable if it is a marketable commodity capable of being sold in the market. This supports HIGH-complexity questions involving manufacturers who produce captively-consumed intermediates but where marketability is disputed.

questions.q: Narayani Polymers Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer registered in Ludhiana (Punjab), produces conveyor belts in a continuous line where rubberised cotton fabric (friction cloth) emerges at an intermediate stage before being immediately laminated into the final belt. The company has never sold this friction cloth externally and argues the entire process is composite and uninterrupted. During an audit for the period April 2004 to March 2006, the Central Excise Range Officer issued a show-cause notice demanding duty on the friction cloth under the relevant Excise Tariff entry, on the ground that identical friction cloth is commercially available in the open market from third-party suppliers. The company's advocate relies on the Punjab & Haryana High Court's earlier ruling in their favour, arguing no duty can arise on a captively-consumed intermediate. The Department says the High Court ruling was set aside. Who is correct, and on what precise legal basis should the duty demand be sustained or dropped — specifically, does the availability of equivalent fabric in the open market constitute sufficient proof of marketability even absent actual sales by Narayani Polymers?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the Supreme Court order that expressly set aside the Punjab & Haryana HC ruling the company's advocate is relying on, and articulates the controlling legal test: marketability (capability of being sold in the market) is the determinative criterion, separate from whether the manufacturer itself sells the product — this exact distinction is indispensable to resolving the dispute.

questions.q: Speedflex Belting LLP operates two manufacturing units — Unit A in Haryana (making V-Belts) and Unit B in Rajasthan (making Conveyor Belts) — both registered separately under Central Excise. Friction cloth produced at Unit A is transferred to Unit B at cost under an inter-unit challan and then consumed in Conveyor Belt manufacture at Unit B; none of the friction cloth ever reaches the open market. For the financial year 2003-04, the Department proposes to levy excise duty on the friction cloth transferred from Unit A to Unit B, treating the inter-unit transfer price as the assessable value, on the ground that friction cloth is a marketable commodity. Speedflex argues: (i) the integrated-process exemption applies because the final article is a belt; (ii) even if duty is leviable, the assessable value should be nil since there is no 'price' — only an internal cost allocation. Evaluate both contentions in light of the Supreme Court's ruling on the marketability of friction cloth and the valuation rules for captive-consumption transfers under the Central Excise Act.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's core holding — that the Division Bench erred by ignoring the Department's marketability contention and that a finding on marketability is a prerequisite before the composite-process exemption can be granted — directly controls contention (i); the chunk also implicitly raises the valuation question by establishing that once marketability is found, duty attaches, making the assessable-value question live.

title: Sale Certificate
text:
11. Verification

I hereby solemnly affirm and declare that the information given hereinabove is true and 
correct to the best of my knowledge and belief and nothing has been concealed therefrom.

Signature of authorized signatory _______________________________________

Name _______________________________________

Designation/Status ____________________________

Date -  dd/mm/yyyy

Instructions:

1. This form is not required to be filed by taxpayers or persons who are registered as :-

(i) 
Input Service Distributors;

(ii) Persons paying tax under section 10; 
(iii) Non-resident taxable person;  
(iv) Persons required to deduct tax at source under section 51; and  
(v) 
Persons required to collect tax at source under section 52.

2. Details of stock of inputs, inputs contained in semi-finished or finished goods and

stock of capital goods/plant and machinery on which input tax credit has been 
availed.

3. Following points need to be taken care of while providing details of stock at Sl.

No.8:

(i) 
where the tax invoices related to the inputs held in stock or inputs contained

in semi-finished or finished goods held in stock are not available, the 
registered person shall estimate the amount under sub-rule (3) of rule 44 
based on prevailing market price of the goods;

(ii) 
i

n case of capital goods/ plant and machinery, the value should be the invoice 
value reduced by 1/60th per month or part thereof from the date of 
invoice/purchase taking useful life as five years.

The details furnished in accordance with sub-rule (3) of rule 44 in the Table at Sl. No. 8 
(against entry 8 (d)) shall be duly certified by a practicing chartered accountant or cost 
accountant. Copy of the certificate shall be uploaded while filing the details.

217
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs the ITC reversal stock-valuation methodology under rule 44(3) — including the market-price estimation fallback when tax invoices are unavailable and the 1/60th per-month capital-goods depreciation formula — and mandates CA/cost-accountant certification for such estimates. These rules are indispensable in complex cancellation/transition scenarios involving mixed stock and capital goods.

questions.q: Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZP, Maharashtra) had its GST registration cancelled with effect from 10-March-2025 after a suo-motu cancellation order. At the time of cancellation, it held: (a) raw polymer pellets worth ₹18 lakh for which original tax invoices from 2022 are untraceable due to a server crash, (b) semi-finished extruded sheets containing embedded inputs with traceable invoices of ₹9 lakh, and (c) a plastic extrusion machine purchased in July 2022 for ₹40 lakh (invoice available). The company's CA is preparing the final ITC reversal statement. What is the correct valuation methodology for each category of stock, which items require CA/cost-accountant certification to be uploaded, and does the cancellation-triggered obligation apply given that Meridian was previously paying tax under the composition scheme for FY 2023-24 before switching to regular registration in April 2024?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies: (i) market-price estimation under rule 44(3) when invoices are unavailable (pellets scenario), (ii) the 1/60th per-month capital-goods depreciation formula from invoice date assuming 5-year useful life (machine valuation), and (iii) the mandatory CA/cost-accountant certification + upload requirement for rule 44(3) estimates — all three are directly operative here. The composition-scheme exclusion in instruction 1(ii) adds the cross-provision complexity.

questions.q: Arkwright Engineering LLP (GSTIN 29AABFA5678K1ZR, Karnataka) is a regular taxpayer that received an order converting it to a non-resident taxable person status from 1-April-2025 due to a change in its constitution. On the effective date it holds: ₹22 lakh of CNC machine tooling (capital goods, invoiced October 2021, ITC fully availed), and ₹6 lakh of cutting-oil consumables (inputs, invoices partially available covering only ₹3.8 lakh). Its statutory auditor — a practicing cost accountant — is asked to certify the ITC reversal workings. A dispute arises internally: one partner argues the LLP need not file the final ITC reversal form at all given the new NRTP status; another insists it must, and that the cost accountant's certificate covers only the items without invoices. Who is correct on the filing obligation, what is the exact ITC reversal amount for the capital goods tooling, and is the cost accountant's certification sufficient or must it be a chartered accountant specifically?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's instruction 1(iii) explicitly excludes non-resident taxable persons from the filing obligation — resolving the partners' dispute. The 1/60th depreciation formula (54 months elapsed Oct-2021 to Apr-2025, capped at 60) determines the tooling reversal. The chunk also states certification must be by 'a practicing chartered accountant or cost accountant', confirming the cost accountant suffices — all three determinations are directly sourced from this chunk.

title: CE - Introduction of New Excise Control Code (ECC) Number based on Permanent Account Number (PAN) allotted by the Income Tax Department - Instructions - RE
text:
having PAN as aforesaid, them the New
ECC Number will be:

ABCDE1234H XM 001 (for Factory) 
ABCDE1234H XD 001 (for Dealer)

Since each factory will be applying for allotment of New ECC Number, a centralised allotment will be done at the level of
Division (where all the factories fall within the jurisdiction of the same Division) or at the level of Commissionerate (where all the
factories fall within the jurisdiction of the same Commissionerate) or at the level of Director General of Inspection, Customs &
Central Excise, New Delhi (where the factories fall within the jurisdiction of different Commissionerates). The same principle
will be applied to the allotment of New ECC Number to Dealers.

3. Procedure for allotment of New ECC
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the structural format and centralized allotment procedure for the New Excise Control Code (ECC) Number under Central Excise, where the code is PAN-based with XM/XD suffixes for factories/dealers respectively, and allotment authority depends on whether establishments fall under one Division, one Commissionerate, or multiple Commissionerates. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving multi-entity registration scenarios and cross-jurisdictional allotment authority questions.

questions.q: Bharat Polymers Pvt Ltd (PAN: BFGPH7823K) operates three manufacturing units — one in Silvassa (Union Territory), one in Vapi (Gujarat), and one in Daman — all registered under Central Excise separately. When they applied for migration to the new PAN-based Excise Control Code system, the Silvassa unit received ECC Number BFGPH7823KXM001, while the Vapi unit was told it would receive BFGPH7823KXM002. The company's CFO is now asking: (a) which authority — Division, Commissionerate, or DG Inspection — has jurisdiction to centrally allot the New ECC Numbers across all three units given they span different Commissionerates, and (b) if their trading arm (Bharat Polymers Trading, same PAN) also needs an ECC, would it receive BFGPH7823KXD001 or share the XM series?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly specifies the XM/XD suffix distinction for factories vs dealers, the sequential numbering format, and the three-tier allotment authority hierarchy (Division → Commissionerate → DG Inspection) based on jurisdictional spread — all indispensable to answering both sub-questions.

questions.q: Sterling Forge LLP (PAN: AACFS4491P) has a factory in Ludhiana (Punjab) and a depot registered as a dealer in Chandigarh (UT). Both fall under different Commissionerates. During the ECC migration exercise, the Ludhiana factory self-applied and got AACFS4491PXM001 from the Ludhiana Division office. The Chandigarh dealer unit simultaneously applied to the Chandigarh Commissionerate and was issued AACFS4491PXD001. A show-cause notice has now been issued alleging irregular allotment since, given cross-Commissionerate presence, allotment should have been centralized at the DG Inspection level rather than handled independently by each office. The partner of Sterling Forge LLP seeks your opinion: is the allotment procedurally defective, and if so, what corrective action and which authority should have originally handled the centralized allotment?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's explicit rule that factories falling under different Commissionerates must have their New ECC Numbers allotted centrally at the level of DG Inspection (not individual Commissionerates) is the specific procedural fact that determines whether the allotment was irregular and what remedy applies.

title: Exemptions for  certain taxable service of execution of a works contract referred to in sub-clause (zzzza) of clause (105) of section 65
text:
Notification                                                                                              New Delhi, the 1st March, 2011 
No. 6/2011 – Service Tax

G.S.R. (E) - In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 93 of the

Finance Act, 1994 (32 of 1994) (hereinafter referred to as the Finance Act), the Central Government,

on being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby exempts the taxable service

of execution of a works contract referred to in sub-clause (zzzza) of clause (105) of section 65 of the

Finance Act, when provided for the purpose of carrying out,-

(a) construction of new residential complex or part thereof; or

(b) completion and finishing services of new residential complex or part thereof ,

under Jawaharlal Nehru National Urban Renewal Mission and Rajiv Awaas Yojana, from the whole

of the service tax leviable thereon under section 66 of the Finance Act.

8
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers a specific Service Tax exemption (No. 6/2011) for works contract services executed under JNNURM and Rajiv Awaas Yojana for new residential complex construction/finishing — a narrow but litigable provision with real practitioner complexity around eligibility, contractor/sub-contractor chains, and the transition to GST.

questions.q: Shree Nirman Construction Pvt Ltd (Ahmedabad, Gujarat) was awarded a sub-contract in 2013 by the main contractor Mehta Infraprojects LLP to execute civil and finishing works for a new residential complex being built under the Rajiv Awaas Yojana scheme in Surat. Shree Nirman raised invoices totalling ₹4.2 crore to Mehta Infraprojects between April 2013 and March 2014 for works including plastering, flooring, and electrical finishing. The department has now issued a show-cause notice claiming that the service tax exemption for works contracts under government housing missions is available only to the primary contractor directly engaged by the government authority, not to sub-contractors. Shree Nirman's advocate argues that the exemption applies to any works contract 'provided for the purpose of carrying out' the qualifying construction. Which position is legally tenable, and what is the risk exposure if the department's interpretation prevails?
questions.why_this_chunk: The exemption language 'when provided for the purpose of carrying out' construction under JNNURM/Rajiv Awaas Yojana is the precise operative text in dispute — the chunk does not restrict the exemption to primary contractors, making the scope of 'provided for the purpose of' the central interpretive question.

questions.q: Prakash Urban Developers Pvt Ltd (Nagpur, Maharashtra) entered into a composite works contract in December 2012 with the Nagpur Municipal Corporation for construction of a new residential complex under JNNURM. The contract value was ₹18 crore covering both civil construction and post-construction completion/finishing services (painting, tiling, plumbing fixtures). The company availed full service tax exemption on the entire contract value. During a CERA audit in 2015, the auditors contend that (i) the exemption for 'completion and finishing services' is a separate limb under clause (b) of the notification and applies only if those services are rendered as a standalone engagement, not bundled into a composite works contract; and (ii) in any event, because the underlying works contract also involved commercial spaces (a community hall and a management office within the complex), the entire contract loses eligibility. Advise on both contentions — are they legally sustainable, and if the second contention partially succeeds, how should the taxable value be determined?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's two-limb structure — clause (a) for construction and clause (b) for completion/finishing — and the phrase 'new residential complex or part thereof' are directly at the centre of both audit contentions; the scope of 'part thereof' and whether the exemption covers bundled composite contracts are answerable only by reading this notification text carefully.

title: Restoration of provisionally attached property / bank account under section83
text:
UNDERTAKING

I hereby undertake to deposit to the Government the amount of refund sanctioned along with 
interest in case of non-receipt of foreign exchange remittances as per the proviso to section 
16 of the IGST Act, 201s7 read with rule 96B of the CGST Rules 2017. 
 
Signature-  
Name –

Designation / Status

]180

UNDERTAKING

I hereby undertake to pay back to the Government the amount of refund sanctioned along 
with interest in case it is found subsequently that the requirements of clause (c) of sub-
section (2) of section 16 read with sub-section (2) of section 42 of the CGST/SGST Act have 
not been complied with in respect of the amount refunded.   
 
Signature  
Name –

Designation / Status

SELF- DECLARATION [rule 89(2)(l)]

I ____________________ (Applicant) having GSTIN/ temporary Id -------, solemnly 
affirm and certify that in respect of the refund amounting to Rs. ---/ with respect to 
the tax, interest, or any other amount for the period from---to----, claimed in the 
refund application, the incidence of such tax and interest has not been passed on to 
any other person.  
 
Signature 
Name –

Designation / Status

(This Declaration is not required to be furnished by applicants, who are claiming refund 
under clause (a) or clause (b) or clause (c) or clause (d) or clause (f) of sub-section (8) of 
section 54.)

180Inserted vide Notf no. 16/2020-CT  dt. 23.03.2020

256
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers two specific undertakings and a self-declaration required for GST refund claims: (1) the foreign exchange remittance undertaking under proviso to IGST Act s.16 read with CGST Rule 96B, and (2) the ITC matching undertaking under CGST/SGST Act s.16(2)(c) read with s.42(2), plus the 'incidence not passed on' self-declaration under Rule 89(2)(l) with its carve-out for s.54(8)(a)-(f) claimants. These are indispensable procedural anchors for export refund and unjust-enrichment scenarios.

questions.q: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM1234C1Z5, Maharashtra) exported custom-fabricated industrial valves to a buyer in Germany in March 2024 under a Letter of Undertaking, claiming IGST refund of ₹38.6 lakhs on accumulated ITC. The foreign buyer remitted payment in Euros, but due to a banking intermediary issue, the equivalent INR credit hit Meridian's account only 18 months after the export date — well beyond the RBI-prescribed realization period. The GST officer is now issuing a notice demanding recovery of the entire sanctioned refund with interest. Meridian's CFO argues that the money did eventually arrive and no loss occurred to the exchequer. (a) What specific undertaking was Meridian required to furnish at the time of filing the refund application that now becomes the operative legal instrument for recovery? (b) Does late realization of foreign exchange automatically trigger recovery, or is there a curative mechanism? (c) What is the interplay between the IGST Act provision on zero-rated supplies and the CGST Rule that governs this recovery, and how should Meridian structure its reply to the notice?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the exact text of the undertaking ('deposit to the Government the amount of refund sanctioned along with interest in case of non-receipt of foreign exchange remittances as per the proviso to section 16 of the IGST Act read with rule 96B of the CGST Rules 2017') — this is the operative document the officer will rely on, and its precise wording (non-receipt vs. late receipt) is central to Meridian's defence.

questions.q: Pinnacle Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AADCP9876B1ZQ, Karnataka) filed a refund claim of ₹52 lakhs under the 'inverted duty structure' category for the quarter April–June 2024, attaching the self-declaration that the incidence of tax has not been passed on to any other person. Post-sanction, a GST audit team finds that Pinnacle had raised credit notes to its domestic distributors adjusting prices downward for the same period, and argues this proves the tax incidence was in fact passed on, warranting full recovery. Pinnacle counters that credit notes relate to commercial discounts unrelated to tax incidence. Simultaneously, the audit team questions whether Pinnacle even needed to furnish this declaration at all given the nature of its refund category. (a) Under exactly which rule and sub-clause is the self-declaration mandated, and which refund categories are explicitly exempt from furnishing it? (b) Does the inverted duty structure refund fall within the exempt categories listed in section 54(8)? (c) How should Pinnacle's advocate argue that commercial credit notes do not constitute 'passing on' of tax incidence for the purpose of this declaration, and what is the evidentiary standard the department must meet to rebut a validly furnished self-declaration?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the verbatim text of the Rule 89(2)(l) self-declaration and its critical carve-out ('not required to be furnished by applicants claiming refund under clause (a) or (b) or (c) or (d) or (f) of sub-section (8) of section 54') — determining whether inverted duty structure refund under s.54(3) falls outside this exemption list is the pivotal legal question, answerable only by reading this exact provision.

title: Regarding excise duty on Flavoured milk of animal origin and Jute twine
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA,  EXTRAORDINARY, PART-II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION(i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

NEW DELHI, THE 27 th MARCH, 2008

7- Chaitra , 1930(SAKA)

Notification No. 17 /2008 Central Excise (N.T.)
