GSR......... (E)Whereas the Central Government is satisfied that a practice was generally prevalent regarding levy of duty of
excise (including non-levy thereof) under section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944) (hereinafter referred to as the
said Act), on goods of the description given in table below, and that such goods were liable to duty of excise which was not
being levied under section 3 of the said Act according to the said practice, during the period as specified below:

"TABLE

Description

Tariff subheading

Period

1

2

3

Flavoured milk of animal origin2202 90 30
28 th Feb, 2005 to 14 th June, 2007

Jute twine
5607 90 90
1 st Jan, 2007 to 14 th June, 2007 "

2.             Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by section 11C of the said Act, the Central Government hereby
directs that the whole of duty of excise leviable under the said Act on such goods falling under such tariff sub-heading  as
described above but for the said practice, shall not be required to be paid for the period detailed in column 3 above, subject to
fulfilment of condition that the benefit under this notification shall not be admissible unless the unit claiming benefit in terms of
this notification  reverse the input credit, if any, taken in respect of inputs used in manufacture of such goods on which the said
duty of excise was not levied during the aforesaid period in accordance with the said practice.

[F. No. 19/02/07-CX1,  F.No.03/01/2007-CX1]

(Ashima Bansal)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a Section 11C Central Excise notification that retroactively waives excise duty on Flavoured milk (2202 90 30) and Jute twine (5607 90 90) for specific historical periods, subject to mandatory input credit reversal. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because it involves temporal scope of duty waiver, the condition precedent of Cenvat credit reversal, and its interaction with contemporaneous GST transition demands.

questions.q: Kalyan Dairy Products Pvt Ltd (Ahmedabad, Gujarat) was manufacturing flavoured milk under tariff heading 2202 90 30 between March 2006 and May 2007 and had availed Cenvat input credit of ₹18.4 lakhs on inputs (milk, flavouring agents, packaging) used in manufacture during that period. A Central Excise audit party issued a show-cause notice in FY 2009-10 demanding duty of ₹46 lakhs for the said period plus interest and penalty, claiming the practice-based exemption is not available because the company never reversed the Cenvat credit. The company argues it reversed only ₹6 lakhs (partial reversal) before filing its reply. Can Kalyan Dairy claim the full benefit of the duty waiver notification for the entire period, or does the partial Cenvat reversal disqualify them entirely, and how should the quantum of admissible waiver be computed?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly conditions the duty waiver on reversal of 'the input credit, if any, taken in respect of inputs used in manufacture of such goods' — the question directly turns on whether partial reversal satisfies this condition precedent and whether the benefit is all-or-nothing or proportionate, which only this notification's specific conditionality can resolve.

questions.q: Sundaram Jute Mills LLP (Kolkata, West Bengal) manufactured jute twine (tariff sub-heading 5607 90 90) from January 2007 through June 2007, supplying to agricultural commodity traders in Uttar Pradesh and Rajasthan. During this period, the mill had not paid Central Excise duty on these goods consistent with prevailing trade practice, and had accumulated Cenvat credit of ₹9.2 lakhs on inputs including raw jute and packing materials. In 2010, the company underwent a corporate restructuring whereby the LLP's manufacturing unit was transferred as a going concern to Sundaram Agro Pvt Ltd. The successor entity now seeks to claim the benefit of the excise duty waiver for the pre-transfer period, while the Revenue argues that (a) the waiver condition requires credit reversal by 'the unit claiming benefit', and (b) the successor cannot reverse credit belonging to a predecessor entity. How should the successor entity establish its entitlement, what is its obligation regarding the Cenvat credit reversal, and does the narrow time window (1 Jan 2007 to 14 June 2007) affect the computation of admissible credit to be reversed?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's phrase 'the unit claiming benefit' reversing 'input credit taken in respect of inputs used in manufacture of such goods' during the specific period '1st Jan 2007 to 14th June 2007' is directly indispensable — the question requires interpreting whether a successor-unit satisfies the identity requirement, and the exact period boundary governs which credit is in scope for reversal.

title: Seeks to impose definitive anti-dumping duty on "Purified Terephthalic Acid" including its variants Medium Quality Terephthalic Acid (MTA) and Qualified Terephthalic Acid (QTA), originating in or exported from China PR, Iran, Indonesia, Mal
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i) OF THE GAZETTE OF 
INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification

No. 28/2016-Customs (ADD)

New Delhi, the 5th July, 2016

G.S.R.       (E). – Whereas, in the matter of “Purified Terephthalic Acid” including its 
variants “Medium Quality Terephthalic Acid” and  “Qualified Terephthalic Acid” (hereinafter 
referred to as the subject goods), falling under tariff item 2917 36 00  of the First Schedule to the 
Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) (hereinafter referred to as the Customs Tariff Act), 
originating in, or exported from, the People’s Republic of China, Iran, Indonesia, Malaysia and 
Taiwan (hereinafter referred to as the subject countries) and imported into India, the designated 
authority in its preliminary findings vide notification No. 14/8/2015-DGAD, dated the 12th 
November, 2015, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 
12th November, 2015, had recommended imposition of provisional anti-dumping duty on the 
imports of subject goods, originating in, or exported from the subject countries;  
  
            And, whereas, on the basis of the aforesaid findings of the designated authority, the 
Central Government had imposed provisional anti-dumping duty on the subject goods with effect 
from 10th December, 2015 vide notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of 
Finance (Department of Revenue), No.60/2015-Customs (ADD), dated the 10th December, 
2015, published in the Gazette of India Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section 
(i) vide number G.S.R. 952 (E), dated the 10th December, 2015;  
  
            And, whereas, the designated authority in its final findings vide notification 
No.14/8/2015-DGAD, dated the 9th June, 2016, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, 
Part I, Section 1, dated the  9th June, 2016, while confirming the preliminary findings dated the 
12th November, 2015, has come to the conclusion that-

(i) 
the subject goods have been exported to India from subject countries below its normal 
value, resulting in dumping;

(ii) 
the domestic industry has suffered material injury due to dumping of the subject goods 
from the subject countries;

(iii) 
material injury has been caused by the dumped imports of subject goods from the subject 
countries,

and has recommended the imposition of definitive anti-dumping duty on  imports of the

subject goods  originating in, or exported from the subject countries;
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the definitive anti-dumping duty (ADD) notification No. 28/2016-Customs (ADD) on Purified Terephthalic Acid (PTA/MTA/QTA) under tariff item 2917 36 00, originating from China, Iran, Indonesia, Malaysia, and Taiwan, including the injury findings and the transition from provisional to definitive ADD. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving customs duty classification, ADD applicability across multiple origin countries, and interaction with GST on imports.

questions.q: Polyfab Industries Pvt Ltd, a Surat-based manufacturer of PET bottles (GSTIN 24AABCP1234F1Z5), imported 500 MT of Purified Terephthalic Acid from a Taiwanese supplier in August 2016, but the Bill of Lading shows the goods were shipped from an Iranian port after transshipment. The customs authority at Nhava Sheva is proposing to levy anti-dumping duty treating the origin as Iran (which attracts a different ADD rate than Taiwan). Polyfab's counsel argues the goods are of Taiwanese origin based on the certificate of origin issued by the Taiwan authority. Simultaneously, a sister concern — Polyfab Chemicals LLP — imported QTA (Qualified Terephthalic Acid) from a Chinese producer in the same month and is contesting whether QTA is a separate product attracting a separate ADD rate or is covered under the same notification as PTA. Advise on (a) how origin is to be determined for ADD purposes where transshipment through a third subject country occurred, (b) whether the definitive ADD notification covers both PTA and QTA as the same subject goods or separately, and (c) the interaction between the definitive ADD under this notification and the earlier provisional ADD duty paid by the sister concern — whether any differential is recoverable or refundable.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly covers PTA including its variants MTA and QTA under tariff item 2917 36 00, lists Taiwan, Iran, China, Indonesia, and Malaysia as subject countries, and establishes the transition from provisional ADD (No. 60/2015-Customs ADD dated 10-Dec-2015) to definitive ADD — all of which are indispensable to resolving the transshipment-origin dispute, the QTA classification question, and the provisional-to-definitive duty differential.

questions.q: Reliance Packaging Ltd, a Gujarat-based manufacturer exporting PET resin to the EU, imports Medium Quality Terephthalic Acid (MTA) from an Indonesian producer under a long-term contract signed in January 2016 (i.e., before the provisional ADD was imposed in December 2015). The company's CFO argues that since the contract predates both the provisional and the definitive ADD notification, the ADD should not apply to shipments under this pre-existing contract, especially given that MTA is an intermediate product used solely for manufacture of export goods, and Reliance holds an Advance Authorization under the Foreign Trade Policy. The customs officer at Mundra port has assessed ADD under the definitive notification and also applied IGST on the assessable value including ADD. Advise Reliance on (a) whether the pre-existing contract provides any exemption or abatement from definitive ADD under Indian anti-dumping law, (b) whether goods imported under an Advance Authorization are exempt from ADD, and (c) how IGST is computed on imports subject to ADD — specifically whether ADD forms part of the value for IGST computation under the Customs Tariff Act read with the IGST Act.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that definitive ADD is imposed on MTA originating from Indonesia under tariff item 2917 36 00, which is the predicate for all three sub-questions — the pre-contract defense is only meaningful if the notification applies, the Advance Authorization exemption question is directly triggered by the ADD levy, and the IGST-on-ADD computation requires knowing that ADD has been validly imposed under this notification.

title: Seeks to notify the India-ASEAN Trade in Goods Agreement (Safeguard Measures) Rules, 2016
text:
unwilling either to make the information public or to authorise its disclosure in a generalised 
or summary form, he may disregard such information unless it is demonstrated to his 
satisfaction from appropriate sources that such information is correct.

7. 
Determination of serious injury or threat of serious injury.– 
The 
Director

General shall determine serious injury or threat of serious injury to the domestic industry 
taking into account, inter alia, the following principles, namely :-

(a) 
the Director General shall evaluate all relevant factors of an objective and 
quantifiable nature having a bearing on the situation of that industry, in 
particular, the rate and amount of the increase in imports of the good 
concerned in absolute and relative terms, the share of the domestic market 
taken by increased imports, changes in the level of sales, production, 
productivity, capacity utilisation, profits and losses and employment; and

(b) 
the determination referred in clause (a) shall not be made unless the 
investigation demonstrates, on the basis of objective evidence, the existence of 
the causal link between increased imports of the good concerned under the 
Trade Agreement and serious injury or threat thereof and when factors other 
than increased imports are causing injury to the domestic industry at the same 
time, such injury shall not be attributed to increased imports.

8. 
Preliminary findings.– (1)  
The 
Director 
General 
shall 
proceed

expeditiously with the conduct of the investigation and in critical circumstances may record a 
preliminary finding regarding serious injury or threat of serious injury.

(2) 
The Director General shall issue a public notice regarding his preliminary findings

and send a copy of the public notice to -

(a) 
the Central Government in the Ministry of Commerce and in the Ministry of 
Finance;

(b) 
the Governments of the exporting State and all other member States of the 
Association of Southeast Asian Nations.

9. 
Application of provisional safeguard measures.– (1) 
The Central Government,

on the basis of the preliminary findings of the Director General, may -

(a) 
suspend further reduction of any rate of customs duty on the good provided for 
under the Trade Agreement; or

(b) 
increase the rate of customs duty on the good concerned to a level not to 
exceed the lesser of the Most Favoured Nation applied rate on the good in 
effect at the time when the safeguard measure is taken, or the Most Favoured
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the evidentiary and procedural rules for determining 'serious injury' under ASEAN safeguard proceedings (Rule 7's causal-link test, Rule 8's preliminary-finding notice obligation, and Rule 9's provisional measure ceiling tied to the MFN applied rate). These are directly relevant to importers and domestic producers navigating ASEAN-origin goods that attract a provisional safeguard duty, supporting multi-entity and cross-act HIGH-complexity questions.

questions.q: Precision Steels Pvt Ltd, a domestic flat-rolled steel manufacturer in Chhattisgarh, has filed a safeguard petition before the Director General (Safeguards) alleging that HR Coil (HS 7208) originating from Vietnam and Malaysia (both ASEAN members) is causing serious injury. During the investigation, the Vietnamese exporter, Nam Viet Steel JSC, submits confidential cost-of-production data but refuses to authorise even a summarised disclosure. Simultaneously, the domestic industry's own data reveals that capacity utilisation fell from 84% to 67% over 2023–2025, but an independent consultant's report shows that a parallel surge in Chinese imports (under a separate MFN tariff line) accounts for roughly 40% of the market-share erosion. The Director General is now deliberating whether (a) the Vietnamese confidential data can be relied upon, (b) Chinese-import-driven injury can be attributed to ASEAN imports for purposes of the causal-link test, and (c) if a preliminary finding is recorded, what is the maximum provisional safeguard duty that can be imposed given the current MFN applied rate of 12.5% and the ASEAN preferential rate of 0%? Advise the Director General on all three issues under the applicable safeguard rules.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 7(b)'s express requirement that injury caused by 'factors other than increased imports' (i.e., Chinese surge) must not be attributed to ASEAN imports is the dispositive legal test for issue (b); Rule 9(1) and its MFN-ceiling proviso directly answer the provisional duty cap question; and the confidential-information disregard standard in the opening paragraph of the chunk governs issue (a).

questions.q: Ratan Textiles LLP, a yarn spinner in Coimbatore, is the petitioner in an ongoing ASEAN safeguard investigation into polyester staple fibre (PSF, HS 5503.20) imported from Thailand and Indonesia. The Director General has just recorded a preliminary finding of 'threat of serious injury' based on a projected 35% year-on-year rise in ASEAN-origin PSF shipments. The Thai exporter, Indorama Ventures Public Company Ltd, challenges the preliminary finding on two grounds: first, that the Director General failed to send the public notice of preliminary findings to the Thai Government before applying provisional measures, as required; and second, that the duty increase proposed (from 5% ASEAN preferential rate to 15%) exceeds the MFN applied rate of 14% currently in force for PSF. Ratan Textiles LLP contests the second point, arguing that because the investigation covers a 'threat' (not actual injury), a higher provisional rate is justified. Analyse the validity of both exporter challenges and advise whether the Central Government's provisional safeguard notification is legally sustainable under the applicable rules.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 8(2)(b) expressly mandates sending the public-notice copy to 'the Governments of the exporting State and all other member States' before or concurrently with provisional measures — this is the procedural ground for challenge 1; Rule 9(1)(b)'s ceiling of 'the lesser of the MFN applied rate … or …' directly invalidates a 15% provisional duty where the MFN rate is 14%, defeating Ratan's 'threat' argument — these two specific sub-rules in this chunk are indispensable to resolving both issues.

title: seeks to increase tariff rate of BCD on populated, loaded or stuffed printed circuit boards, falling under tariff item 8517 70 10, of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 from Nil to 10%.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, 
SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No. 36/2018 – Customs

New Delhi, the 2nd April, 2018

G.S.R.     (E).-  WHEREAS the Central Government on being satisfied that the import duty 
leviable on populated, loaded or stuffed printed circuit boards, falling under tariff item 8517 
70 10 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), should be increased 
and that circumstances exist which render it necessary to take immediate action.

NOW, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 8A 
of the said Customs Tariff Act, the Central Government, hereby directs that the First 
Schedule to the said Customs Tariff Act, shall be amended in the following manner, namely:-

In the First Schedule to the said Customs Tariff Act, in Section XVI, in Chapter 85, 
against tariff item 8517 70 10, for the entry in column (4), the entry "10%" shall be 
substituted.

[F.No.354/20/2017-TRU]

(Ruchi Bisht)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the emergency tariff amendment under Section 8A of the Customs Tariff Act 1975, raising BCD on populated/loaded/stuffed PCBs (CTH 8517 70 10) from Nil to 10% effective 2 April 2018. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving the transition from Nil to 10% BCD, its interaction with IGST on imports, and classification disputes for multi-component electronics imports.

questions.q: Nexus Electronics Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, IEC holder) had a purchase order for 5,000 units of populated PCBs (CTH 8517 70 10) from a Taiwanese supplier, with the Bill of Lading dated 30 March 2018 and the vessel arriving at JNPT on 4 April 2018. The Out of Charge was granted on 5 April 2018. The company's customs broker filed the Bill of Entry on 3 April 2018 claiming Nil BCD based on the tariff rate prevailing on the date of B/L. The assessing officer is applying 10% BCD citing the rate on the date of arrival. Additionally, Nexus had separately imported the same PCBs for its SEZ unit in Kandla under a prior Bill of Entry dated 28 March 2018 that is still pending assessment. What is the applicable BCD rate for each of these three scenarios, and how does the emergency amendment mechanism under Section 8A interact with the general rule on the relevant date for customs duty assessment?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that the 10% BCD was imposed via Section 8A (immediate effect emergency power) on 2 April 2018, which determines whether the Nil rate or 10% applies depending on the precise assessment date — directly indispensable for resolving the date-of-levy dispute in each sub-scenario.

questions.q: Horizon Telecom Components LLP (Noida, UP) imports a consignment of telecom base station sub-assemblies from South Korea, declared under CTH 8517 70 90 as 'parts of telephony equipment'. Customs at ICD Tughlakabad proposes reclassification to CTH 8517 70 10 as 'populated printed circuit boards' after inspection reveals the goods are fully stuffed PCBs used in base stations, with the import invoice dated 10 April 2018. If reclassification is upheld, what is the differential BCD and IGST liability (assuming IGST at 18% and no exemption notification applies), and can Horizon claim credit of the differential IGST as input tax credit in its GSTR-3B given that the end-use is in manufacture of telecom infrastructure equipment supplied domestically under forward charge?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's specific amendment of the tariff entry for 8517 70 10 to 10% BCD is the pivotal fact — without it, the differential duty calculation and the consequent IGST escalation (computed on assessable value + BCD) that determines the ITC eligibility question cannot be resolved.

title: amendments in the notification . No. 21/2002 - Customs, dated the 1st March , 2002
text:
23 rd July, 2002.

Notification No. 72/2002 - Customs

In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section ( i ) of section 25 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962 ), the Central
Government, being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby makes the following further amendments
in the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue ), No. 21/2002 - Customs,
dated the 1 st March , 2002, namely :-

In the said notification ,-

( 1 ) in the Table, after S.No.167 and the entries relating thereto, the following S.No. and entries shall be inserted, namely,-

(1)
(2)
(3)
(4)(5)(6)

"167AAny

NilNil21A

(a) Buckles,'D' rings,eyelets, hooks and eyes,rivets and studs; 
(b) Buttons and fasteners of all types; 
(c)  Velcro Hook,Velco tapes and Loop taps; 
(d) Elastic Tape adhesive tape and reinforcement tape; 
(e)  Stamping foil;

(g)  Locks including magnetic locks; 
(h)  Metal handles, handle fitting,dog hooks, Logos all types, Ring binders Key hooks, Key rings, Key
holders, Push clip, Chains,pullers, hinges and magnetic snaps; 
(i)    Glow Liners; 
(j)    Loop rivets and loop oval.";

(2)              in the Annexure, -

(i)                in Condition No.21, for sub-condition (a) and (b), the following sub-conditions shall respectively by substituted,
namely,-

Condition

(ii)              after Condition No.21 and the condition relating thereto, the following Conditions No. and shall be inserted, namely,

Condition

Alok Jha

Under Secretary to the Government of India

F No. 605/201/2001 -DBK

Note : The principal notification No.21/2002 - Customs dated the 1 st March ,2002 -was published in the Gazette of India,
(Extraordinary ) vide GSR No.118(E) , dated , the 1 st March, 2002 and last amended by notification No.70/2002- Customs
dated, the 11 th July, 2002 published in the Gazette of India (Extraordinary ) vide GSR No. 490 (E) dated the 11 th July, 2002.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers Condition 21A inserted by Notification 72/2002-Customs, which grants nil customs duty on specified accessories (buckles, eyelets, velcro, sewing thread, magnetic locks, metal handles, etc.) imported by manufacturers of leather footwear, leather gloves, travel goods, and handbags — subject to a 1% FOB cap, CLE registration, and a certificate requirement. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because it involves multi-condition eligibility (registration + FOB cap + certificate), cross-notification linkage (21/2002 parent + 72/2002 amendment), and practical scenarios where manufacturers may straddle multiple product categories or exceed thresholds.

questions.q: Our client, Meridian Leather Pvt Ltd, based in Chennai (Tamil Nadu), is registered with the Council for Leather Exports and manufactures both leather handbags and leather footwear for export. During FY 2024-25, they exported leather handbags worth ₹8 crore FOB and leather footwear worth ₹12 crore FOB. In April 2025, they wish to import a combined consignment containing velcro tapes, magnetic snaps, metal handle fittings, and sewing thread — total CIF value ₹22 lakh — to be used across both product lines. Their CLE certificate covers both categories. Can they claim nil customs duty on the entire consignment under the relevant exemption, and if so, how should the 1% FOB cap be calculated — on the combined FOB of ₹20 crore, or separately per product category? What documentary precautions must they take at the time of filing the Bill of Entry?
questions.why_this_chunk: Condition 21A in this chunk specifically governs the 1% FOB cap and CLE certificate requirement for leather footwear, leather gloves, travel goods, handbags, and similar containers. The question turns on whether the FOB cap applies per-category or in aggregate — an interpretation question that is only answerable by reading the exact language of sub-conditions (a) and (b) of Condition 21A as inserted by this notification.

questions.q: Horizon Accessories LLP, a manufacturer in Agra (UP) registered with the Council for Leather Exports, exported leather gloves worth ₹5 crore FOB in FY 2024-25. In the current financial year (FY 2025-26), they have already imported goods worth ₹4.5 lakh under the relevant customs exemption notification. They now wish to import a fresh consignment in March 2026 comprising glow liners, loop rivets, D-rings, and stamping foil, total assessable value ₹55,000, to be used in manufacturing leather gloves for export. Their CLE certificate for FY 2024-25 shows exports of ₹5 crore and prior imports of ₹4.5 lakh under this scheme during FY 2025-26. A junior consultant has flagged that the ₹55,000 import will breach the permissible threshold. Is the consultant correct? Additionally, if Horizon LLP also manufactures leather travel bags (a separate product line, same factory, same CLE registration), can they claim a separate 1% entitlement for travel bags, or is the cap pooled?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-conditions (b) and (c) of Condition 21A are directly indispensable: sub-condition (b) sets the 1% FOB cap (₹5 lakh on ₹5 crore), sub-condition (c) requires the CLE certificate to certify both FY exports and the value/quantity already imported in the current year. The question also requires interpreting whether sub-condition (a)'s enumerated product list creates separate caps per product or a single pooled cap — a legal interpretation issue anchored squarely in this chunk's text.

title: Guidelines for deciding classification of Ayurvedic medicines in the light of Supreme Court judgement in the case of Shri Baidyanathh Ayurved Bhavan Limited Vs. CCE, Nagpur( 1996(83)ELT 492(SC)
text:
Circular No. 333/49/97-CX

dated 10/9/97

F.No. 102/6/97-CX.3

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs, New Delhi

Subject: Classification of Products under the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985, which are claimed by the manufacturers as
Ayurvedic medicines. - regarding.-

There have been disputes arising in the classification of products under the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985, which are being
claimed by the manufacturers as Ayurvedic medicines, and as per the contention of the department the same were cosmetics/
toilet requisites falling under Chapter 33 of the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985.

2.    There are number of judgements on the issue which have laid down certain criteria for the classification of a product as an
ayurvedic medicine or as a cosmetic/ toilet requisite.

3.    The Hon"ble Supreme Court of India in its judgement in the case of Shri Baidyanath Ayurved Bhavan Limited Vs. CCE,
Nagpur [1996 983) ELT 492(SC)] has stated that "The Tribunal rightly points out that in interpreting statutes like the Excise Act
the primary object of which is to raise revenue and for which purpose various products are differently classified, resort should
not be had to the scientific and technical meaning of the terms and expressions used but to their popular meaning, that is to
say, the meaning attached to them by those using the product. It is for this reason that the Tribunal came to the conclusion that
scientific and technical meanings would not advance the case of the appellants if the same runs counter to how the product is
understood in popular parlance. That is why the Tribunal observed in Paragraph 86 of the judgement as under:

So certificates and affidavits given by the Vaidyas do not advance the cases of Shri Baid yanath Ayurved Bhavan Limited in
the absence of any evidence on record to show and prove that the common man who uses this Dant Manjan daily to clean his
teeth considers this Dant Manjan as a medicine and not a toilet requisite".

It is this line of reasoning with which we are in agreement. The Tribunal rejected the claim of the appellant holding that ordinarily
a medicine is prescribed by a Medical Practitioner and its is used for a limited time and not every day unless it is so
prescribed to deal with a specific disease like diabetes. We are, therefore, of the opinion that the Tribunal applied the correct
principles in concluding that the product in question was not a medicinal preparation ("Ayurvedic") and, there fore, the appellant
was not entitled to the benefit of the exemption notification."

4.    The Hon"ble Tribunal in the case of M/s. Alpine Industries Vs. CCE, Delhi [1997(92) ELT 53 (Trib)] have stated that "that
fact of manufacture of an item under licence under the Drugs & Cosmetics Act is no guarantee that it is a medicament".

5.    The Hon"ble Tribunal in the case of CCE, Mumbai Vs. M/s. Muller & Phipps Ltd. have stated, while holding that Johnson
Prickly Heat Powder was a cosmetic classifiable under Chapter 33, that,

"Product is available in the market, does not required any prescription of a Doctor and the people generally use it to prevent
prickly heat and therefore in the circumstances the curative and prophylactic uses are subsidiary."

6.    Even in the case of M/s. Richardson Hindustan Limited, the Hon"ble CEGAT vide judgement date 10.02.1988 [1988 (35)
ELT 424 (Trib)] has stated while classifying Vicks Vaporub as an ayurvedic medicine;
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Central Excise classification test for Ayurvedic medicines vs. cosmetics/toilet requisites under the 1985 Tariff Act, anchoring the 'popular parlance' / 'common man understanding' doctrine from Baidyanath SC ruling plus the Drug licence irrelevance principle from Alpine Industries. These doctrines are indispensable to any classification dispute involving dual-use herbal/Ayurvedic products.

questions.q: Himalaya Herbals & Co. Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCH1234F1ZK, Karnataka) manufactures 'DantaShudhi Herbal Tooth Powder' — formulated with Triphala, Neem and Clove oil, licensed under the Drugs & Cosmetics Act as an Ayurvedic medicament (Schedule E), and sold over-the-counter at pharmacies and supermarkets nationwide without any prescription requirement at ₹85/unit MRP. The company has been classifying this under Chapter 30 (Ayurvedic medicinal preparations) claiming nil/concessional excise duty. The jurisdictional CE Commissioner, Bengaluru-II is issuing a show-cause notice proposing re-classification under Chapter 33 (cosmetics/toilet requisites) demanding differential duty of ₹2.3 crore for the period April 2022 to March 2025. The company argues that (i) the Drugs & Cosmetics licence conclusively establishes medicinal character, and (ii) Ayurvedic texts classify Triphala as a therapeutic agent. How should the company's counsel assess the sustainability of their Chapter 30 classification, and what is the governing legal test the adjudicating authority will apply to decide whether DantaShudhi is an Ayurvedic medicine or a toilet requisite for tariff purposes?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the Supreme Court's ruling in Baidyanath (1996 ELT 492) — which specifically involved Dant Manjan — holding that the 'popular parlance' / common man test governs, that a D&C licence is not conclusive (Alpine Industries principle in para 4), and that daily-use without prescription militates against medicinal classification. All three doctrines are directly on point and indispensable.

questions.q: Zandu Pharmaceutical Works Ltd, Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AAACZ9876B1ZT) manufactures 'Zandu Balm Ultra Power' — a product containing Wintergreen oil, Camphor, Menthol and Chilli extract, described on the pack as 'provides relief from headache, joint pain and cold symptoms', sold at ₹55 per 8g tube, available freely at kiranas, petrol station stalls and e-commerce platforms without prescription. The company classifies it under Chapter 30 (Ayurvedic/patent medicine), supported by a Drug Controller licence and affidavits from registered Ayurvedic practitioners (Vaidyas) certifying therapeutic utility. The CGST+CE audit team at Pune-III proposes that for the period July 2017 to March 2023 the correct classification is Chapter 33 (cosmetics/toilet preparations) as the product is used prophylactically rather than curatively by the general public, and raises a demand of ₹4.7 crore. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) what is the evidentiary weight of Vaidya affidavits in such classification disputes, (b) does the prophylactic vs. curative distinction matter legally, and (c) does the fact that it is sold without prescription and available at general trade channels affect the classification outcome under settled Central Excise / GST tariff classification principles?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses all three sub-questions: (a) Baidyanath SC ruling explicitly states Vaidya certificates/affidavits do not advance the case without evidence of common-man perception as medicine; (b) Muller & Phipps (para 5) holds that prophylactic/curative uses being subsidiary does not make a product a medicine; (c) Baidyanath doctrine that daily use without prescription by ordinary consumers indicates cosmetic, not medicinal, classification.

title: Seeks to extend the validity of notification No 120/2010- Customs (ADD) dated 01.12.2010 for a further period of one year
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i) OF THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 14/2015-Customs (ADD)

New Delhi, the 17th April, 2015

G.S.R.     (E). -Whereas, the designated authority vide notification No.15/05/2015-DGAD, dated the 9th April, 2015, published
in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 9th April, 2015, has initiated review, in terms of sub-section
(5) of section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) (hereinafter referred to as the Customs Tariff Act), and in
pursuance of rule 23 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles
and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995 (hereinafter referred to as the said rules), in the matter of continuation of anti-
dumping duty on "Phenol", falling under Tariff Item 2907 11 10 or 2707 99 00 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act,
originating in, or exported from, Thailand and Japan, imposed vide notification of the Government of India, in the Ministry of
Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 120/2010-Customs (ADD), dated the 1st December, 2010, published in the Gazette of
India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G.S.R. 933(E), dated the 1st December, 2010 and has
recommended for  extension of anti-dumping duty for a further period of one year, in terms of sub-section (5) of section 9A of
the Customs Tariff Act.

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-sections (1) and (5) of section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act and in
pursuance of rule 23 of the said rules, the Central Government hereby makes the following amendments in the notification of
the Government of India, in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 120/2010-Customs (ADD), dated the 1st
December, 2010, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G.S.R. 933
(E), dated 1st December, 2010, namely: -

In the said notification, after paragraph 2 and before the Explanation, the following paragraph shall be inserted, namely: -

"3. Notwithstanding anything contained in paragraph 2, this notification shall remain in force up to and inclusive of the 18th day
of April, 2016, unless revoked earlier.".

[F.No.354/23/2010-TRU (Part-I)]

(Akshay Joshi)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note: The principal notification was published vide number G.S.R. 933 (E), dated the 1st December, 2010.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the sunset-review extension of anti-dumping duty on Phenol (CTH 2907 11 10 / 2707 99 00) from Thailand and Japan, valid through 18-Apr-2016 under section 9A(5) of the Customs Tariff Act. It supports HIGH-complexity questions because it involves cross-act/cross-notification interaction (original ADD notification + extension notification), specific commodity classification, country-of-origin rules, and has direct commercial consequences for importers mid-review.

questions.q: Solvents & Polymers Pvt Ltd, a Mumbai-based manufacturer (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1Z5), imported 40 MT of Phenol from a Thai supplier under a CIF contract dated 20-Feb-2015 with bill of lading dated 28-March-2015, but the goods arrived at JNPT on 22-April-2015 — after the original anti-dumping duty notification No. 120/2010-Customs (ADD) had technically lapsed pending review. Customs has now raised a demand for ADD at the rate in the original 2010 notification. The importer's counsel argues no ADD was in force on the date of arrival since the extension notification was issued only on 17-Apr-2015 and the DGAD recommendation came on 9-Apr-2015. Does the extension notification cover goods arriving between 1-Dec-2014 (expiry of the original five-year period) and 18-Apr-2015, and can Customs retrospectively collect ADD for the 22-April-2015 consignment if it had already filed the bill of entry before the gazette publication of the extension?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes the exact operative date of the extension (17-Apr-2015 gazette notification), the validity ceiling (18-Apr-2016), and the mechanism under section 9A(5) + rule 23 — all indispensable to determining whether ADD was legally in force on 22-Apr-2015 and whether retroactive collection is permissible.

questions.q: Chemfab Industries LLP, a Chennai-based epoxy resin producer, sources Phenol both domestically and via imports. In Q1 FY2026, they have two open purchase orders: (a) 25 MT from a Japanese trading house (origin: Japan, invoiced by a Singapore intermediary under a back-to-back contract) and (b) 15 MT from a Thai producer routed through a Free Trade Warehousing Zone in Nhava Sheva before clearance into DTA. Their CFO wants to know: (i) whether ADD on Phenol from Japan under the extended notification would apply to goods where the country of export is Singapore but the country of origin is Japan, (ii) whether the FTWZ-to-DTA transfer triggers a fresh ADD liability at the rate applicable on the date of DTA clearance, and (iii) given that the extension was explicitly stated to last only until 18-Apr-2016, what is the ADD exposure if clearance from the FTWZ happens on 20-Apr-2016 after the sunset date without a further extension being notified?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that ADD applies to goods 'originating in, or exported from, Thailand and Japan' and fixes the hard expiry at 18-Apr-2016 — both facts are essential to resolving the origin-vs-export-country question for the Singapore-invoiced Japanese consignment and the FTWZ timing question for the 20-Apr-2016 clearance.

title: Procedure for investigation of related party import cases and other cases by the Special Valuation Branches (revised and updated on 26.08.2016)
text:
Has the price of the goods been settled in manner consistent with the way the price is 
settled by the seller with unrelated buyers?

Please provide the information under Rule 3 (3) (b) of CVR, 2007, if any.

Is the price of the imported goods determined on the basis of a price list? If yes, please 
provide copy of the price list and the basis of arriving at the invoice value.

If the price is not based upon a price list, then is the price declared a ‘Transfer Price’?

What is the basis on which the price has been settled between the buyer and seller?

Other Payments (Costs & Services under Rule 10 of CVR, 2007)

Whether the goods imported by the importer are sold under 
a trade mark, design, 
licencing/royalty agreement or patent owned or controlled by the seller of the goods or

any other person?

6.1

Whether the product manufactured by the importer using the imported goods is sold 
under a trade mark, design, licencing/royalty agreement or patent owned or controlled

by the seller of the goods or any other person?

Whether any amount or any part of the proceeds of any subsequent resale, disposal or

6.2

use of the imported goods accrues, directly or indirectly, to the seller?

Whether there are any other payments actually made or to be made as a condition of

6.3

sale of the imported goods, by the buyer to the seller, or by the buyer to a third party to

satisfy an obligation of the seller?

6.4

ls any amount paid or payable, directly or indirectly, to or on behalf of the seller of the 
imported goods in connection with the production of the imported goods? (for example,

materials, parts, components, tools, dies, moulds, engineering, development, art work, 
design work, plans or sketches undertaken elsewhere than in India)

6.5

Are any services rendered by or on behalf of the importer relatable 
to the goods 
imported or undertaken on behalf of the seller of foreign goods?

6.6

Will the price paid or payable by the importer be settled with the seller at the end of 
defined period by means of debit note / credit note (post — import price adjustment)?

Other information

Has the importer or any of its associates entered into an Advance Pricing Agreement

with the Income Tax Authorities or obtained Advance Ruling? (please enclose copy).

Note:

The replies to this questionnaire shall be signed by the Authorised Signatory of the Importer accompanied by a

self-certified copy of the statutory document as proof of such authorization.

In cases where the import takes place through CHs of Mumbai 
/ Delhi 
/ Chennai 
/ Kolkata 
/ Bangalore, the 
importer will be free to select the SVB of Customs House of Import or the Customs House most proximate to

the Corporate Office, as convenient to him.

Company Seal 
(Signature of Authorised Representative)/ Director (Finance)/CFO 
Name: 
Designation:

Tel no. / email:
claude_reasoning: This chunk is the SVB questionnaire under Customs Valuation Rules 2007, covering Rule 10 additions (royalties, assists, post-import price adjustments, APA existence) and Rule 3(3)(b) price-settlement tests for related-party imports. It authoritatively governs what disclosures and additions-to-value SVB can demand, making it indispensable for high-complexity related-party import valuation disputes.

questions.q: Luminos Semiconductors Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCL1234F1ZX, Mumbai) imports CMOS image sensor dies from its parent Luminos Japan KK under an intercompany agreement. The invoice price is USD 4.20 per die. Luminos India also pays a 3% royalty to Luminos Japan on net sales of finished camera modules that incorporate these dies, under a separate IP licensing agreement. Additionally, Luminos Japan supplies tooling dies and test jigs to the Indian plant at no charge ('assists'). The SVB at JNCH has issued a questionnaire and is proposing to load both the royalty and the fair-market value of the assists onto the customs value. Luminos India counters that (a) the royalty relates to the finished product, not the imported dies, and (b) the tooling was provided free as a condition of the supply contract, not as an independent payment. Luminos India has also entered an APA with Indian Income Tax authorities fixing the arm's-length price of the dies at USD 4.20. Can the SVB override the APA-determined transfer price, and which specific additions to transaction value is SVB legally entitled to make in this scenario?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's questionnaire items 6.1 (royalty on goods sold under IP owned by seller), 6.4 (materials/tools/dies supplied by seller — assists), 6.5 (services relatable to imported goods), and the APA disclosure note are each directly implicated; the chunk is the procedural and substantive source defining what SVB can investigate and load onto customs value.

questions.q: Praxis Healthcare LLP (Bengaluru, IEC AABCP5678Q) imports Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients from its Swiss affiliate Praxis AG under a long-term supply contract. The invoice price is fixed annually but clause 12 of the agreement provides for a year-end true-up: if Praxis AG's consolidated margin falls below 15%, Praxis India must pay a top-up via debit note within 90 days of the financial year close. For FY 2024-25, a debit note of EUR 8.2 lakh was raised by Praxis AG in July 2025 referencing API shipments made between April 2024 and March 2025, all of which have been cleared and assessed. The SVB Bengaluru had earlier accepted the transaction value on a provisional basis pending annual review. Praxis India's CFO wants to know: (i) does the post-import debit note trigger a re-assessment of customs value for all prior consignments, and which rule mandates disclosure of this price-adjustment mechanism at the time of import; and (ii) if Praxis India had disclosed this clause upfront but SVB had not flagged it, does SVB retain jurisdiction to re-open assessments after final out-of-charge?
questions.why_this_chunk: Questionnaire item 6.6 (post-import price adjustment via debit/credit note at end of a defined period) is the precise disclosure hook that governs this scenario; the chunk is the only authoritative source establishing that this mechanism must be declared to SVB and forms part of the customs value determination exercise.

title: Central Excise - Notification No.1/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 11th January, 2001 amending provisions relating to Payment of duty on fortnightly basis - Regarding
text:
Circular No.565/2/2001-CX

dated 12-01-2001

F.No.201/28/2000-CX.6

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

Subject: Central Excise - Notification No.1/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 11 th January, 2001 amending
provisions relating to Payment of duty on fortnightly basis - Regarding

I am directed to enclose copy of Notification No.1/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 11 th January, 2001, whereby clause (e) of
sub-rule (1) of rule 49 and clause (e) of sub-rule (1) of rule 173G have been amended. Instances have come to notice where
certain Central Excise assessees did not pay substantial dues relating to earlier period even after being debarred for two
months and were again eligible for the facility of fortnightly payment.

2. In order to safeguard revenue, amendments have been made in the rules mentioned above so as to provide that in case
where a manufacturer forfeits the facility to pay the dues in installments on account of sub-clauses (i) & (ii) of clause (e) of sub-
rule (1) of rule 49 or sub-clauses (i) & (ii) of clause (e) of sub-rule (1) of rule 173G, for a period of two months starting from the
date of communication of an order passed by the proper officer in this regard, and if he still fails to make full payment of the
outstanding amount before the end of this period, such manufacturer shall continue to forfeit the facility to pay the dues in
installments till the date all outstanding amounts are paid. For the sake of clarify, it is mentioned that where a manufacturer
pays the outstanding amount before the end of the said two months' period, he can resume to avail the facility immediately after
the completion of the two months period.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Central Excise fortnightly duty payment facility — specifically the rule that once a manufacturer forfeits installment payment rights due to default, the forfeiture continues beyond the initial two-month period until ALL outstanding dues are cleared. This creates complex compliance scenarios for manufacturers with legacy CE dues transitioning into GST era.

questions.q: Precision Castings Pvt Ltd, a Pune-based manufacturer (Maharashtra) with a legacy Central Excise registration under the pre-GST regime, had forfeited its fortnightly duty payment facility in March 2017 after defaulting on dues of ₹18.7 lakhs under the erstwhile Central Excise rules. The company partially cleared ₹12 lakhs before the two-month debarment period ended but left ₹6.7 lakhs unpaid. Post-GST rollout in July 2017, the company's tax counsel is now arguing before the adjudicating authority that (a) the forfeiture of the installment facility lapsed automatically with the repeal of Central Excise Rules under the GST transition, and (b) any recovery of the ₹6.7 lakhs outstanding should be governed solely by Section 142 of CGST Act read with transitional provisions, not by the pre-GST forfeiture order. The department contends the forfeiture order remains operative until full payment. Which authority's position is legally sustainable, and what is the specific rule-based mechanism that determines when the forfeiture of the installment payment facility terminates?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that where a manufacturer fails to clear the full outstanding amount before the end of the two-month period, the forfeiture continues 'till the date all outstanding amounts are paid' — directly determining whether the ₹6.7 lakh partial payment terminates or extends the forfeiture, which is the crux of the dispute.

questions.q: Bharat Alloys & Forgings LLP, operating a manufacturing unit in Raipur (Chhattisgarh), had its fortnightly Central Excise duty payment facility withdrawn in November 2016 via a proper officer's order after two successive defaults under sub-clauses (i) and (ii) of the relevant rule. The LLP made full payment of all outstanding Central Excise dues in January 2017 — within the two-month debarment window — and immediately sought to resume fortnightly payments for February 2017. The jurisdictional Superintendent denied reinstatement, arguing the two-month period must run its full course regardless of early clearance. Meanwhile, a sister concern — Bharat Alloys Trading Pvt Ltd (same promoters, separate GSTIN in Odisha) — which was availing fortnightly payment under a separate registration, is now asking whether the default and forfeiture order against the LLP has any bearing on its own eligibility for installment payment. Advise on: (i) whether the LLP can resume fortnightly payment immediately upon clearing dues within the two-month window; and (ii) whether the forfeiture under one legal entity's registration affects a separately registered related entity's entitlement.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the specific carve-out that 'where a manufacturer pays the outstanding amount before the end of the said two months' period, he can resume to avail the facility immediately after the completion of the two months period' — this precise proviso resolves question (i); and the entity-specific nature of the registration-based forfeiture mechanism addresses question (ii).

title: Levy and Collection of Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS) on imports under various schemes such as Merchandise Exports from India Scheme (MEIS), Services Exports from India Scheme (SEIS) etc
text:
Court in judgement dated 06.12.2019 (supra), it is clarified that SWS is not exempted and has to

be levied and collected on the imported goods.

10. 
It is further noted that as per past practice, SWS is being allowed to be debited in the duty

credit scrips along with Basic Customs Duty and Additional Duties of Customs. However,

keeping in view the position explained hereinabove, 
it emerges that SWS cannot be debited

through duty credit scrips and therefore has to be paid by the importer in cash. Directorate

General of Systems has been requested to make the relevant System level changes in this regard.

Li, 
With regard to the past cases of debits of SWS already made in duty credit scrips, 
it has

been decided by the Board that for ensuring ease of doing business, such past cases should not be

disturbed and the payments made through debit in duty credit scrips may be accepted as revenue

duly collected and recoveries in cash not be insisted for these cases.

12. 
A suitable Trade Notice and Standing Order may be issued for the guidance of the trade

and officers. Difficulties faced, if any, in implementation of the Circular may be immediately 
brought to the notice of the Board.

Yours faithfully,

(Jitender Kumar) 
OSD (Drawback)

Tel: 011 23341480
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the clarification that Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS) cannot be debited through duty credit scrips (MEIS/SEIS) and must be paid in cash, while also providing a transitional relief that past SWS debits via scrips will not be disturbed. This supports HIGH-complexity queries involving importers who used MEIS/SEIS scrips for SWS payment and now face compliance questions about prospective vs retrospective liability.

questions.q: Precision Composites Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) imported carbon fibre prepregs worth ₹4.2 crore CIF from Japan in March 2020 and debited Social Welfare Surcharge of ₹8.4 lakh against their MEIS scrips at JNCH, alongside BCD debit. Post a CBIC circular issued in early 2020, the customs officer at the port is now issuing a demand notice for recovery of the ₹8.4 lakh SWS in cash, arguing the scrip debit was invalid. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) whether the demand for cash recovery of SWS debited via MEIS scrips prior to the circular is legally sustainable, (b) what the precise cut-off date/event is that separates protected past cases from prospective cash-payment obligation, and (c) whether filing a writ is advisable or if the circular itself provides a complete defence against the recovery notice.
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 11 of the circular explicitly states that past SWS debits made through duty credit scrips shall not be disturbed and payments so made may be accepted as revenue duly collected — cash recovery shall not be insisted. This is the direct and indispensable statutory basis for the company's defence against the demand notice.

questions.q: Helix Agro Exports LLP, a 100% EOU unit in Tamil Nadu (GSTIN 33AABHH5678K1ZQ), imported speciality fertiliser inputs under an advance authorisation in January 2020 and simultaneously held SEIS scrips worth ₹35 lakh. At the time of clearance, their CHA debited BCD, IGST (as Additional Duty equivalent), and SWS aggregating ₹2.1 lakh from the SEIS scrips. Six months later, during an internal audit ahead of their annual performance reporting to the Development Commissioner, the company's tax counsel flags that SWS may not have been a permissible debit on the scrips per a recent Board clarification. The counsel also notes that the EOU is entitled to certain duty exemptions under the Foreign Trade Policy. The company wants a complete analysis: (i) whether the SWS debit from SEIS scrips in January 2020 creates a recoverable liability today, (ii) how the interaction between the FTP scrip mechanism, the Customs Tariff Act levy of SWS, and the Board's transitional protection clause should be read together, and (iii) whether the EOU status independently extinguishes the SWS obligation or whether the cash-payment rule applies regardless of EOU exemption notifications.
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 10 establishes that SWS cannot be debited through duty credit scrips and must be paid in cash prospectively, while Para 11 provides the transitional safe harbour for past debits — both paras are essential to resolve parts (i) and (ii) of the query. No generic GST or customs chunk can substitute for this specific Board clarification on SWS + scrip interaction.

title: Central Excise Act, 1944
text:
(a) fraud; or

(b) collusion; or

(c) any wilful mis-statement; or

(d) suppression of facts; or

(e) contravention of any of the provisions of this Act or of the rules made thereunder with intent to evade payment of duty, by any
person chargeable with the duty, the Central Excise Officer shall, within five years from the relevant date, serve notice on such
person requiring him to show cause why he should not pay the amount specified in the notice along with interest payable thereon
under section 11AA and a penalty equivalent to the duty specified in the notice.

3 [(5) to (7) * * * * * *]

4 [(7A) Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section (1) or sub-section (3) or sub-section (4) 5 [***], the Central Excise
Officer may, serve, subsequent to any notice or notices served under any of those sub-sections, as the case may be, a
statement, containing the details of duty of central excise not levied or paid or short-levied or short-paid or erroneously refunded
for the subsequent period, on the person chargeable to duty of central excise, then, service of such statement shall be deemed
to be service of notice on such person under the aforesaid sub-section (1) or sub-section (3) or sub-section (4) or sub-section
(5), subject to the condition that the grounds relied upon for the subsequent period are the same as are mentioned in the earlier
notice or notices.]

6 [(8) Where the service of notice is stayed by an order of a court or tribunal, the period of such stay shall be excluded in
computing the period of 2 [two years] referred to in clause (a) of sub-section (1) or five years referred to in sub-section (4) 5 [* * *],
as the case may be.]

(9) Where any appellate authority or tribunal or court concludes that the notice issued under sub-section (4) is not sustainable for
the reason that the charges of fraud or collusion or any wilful mis-statement or suppression of facts or contravention of any of the
provisions of this Act or of the rules made thereunder with intent to evade payment of duty has not been established against the
person to whom the notice was issued, the Central Excise Officer shall determine the duty of excise payable by such person for
the period of 2 [two years], deeming as if the notice were issued under clause (a) of sub-section (1).

(10) The Central Excise Officer shall, after allowing the concerned person an opportunity of being heard, and after considering
the representation, if any, made by such person, determine the amount of duty of excise due from such person not being in
excess of the amount specified in the notice.

(11) The Central Excise Officer shall determine the amount of duty of excise under sub-section (10) -

(a) within six months from the date of notice where it is possible to do so, in respect of cases falling under sub-section (1);

(b) within 2 [two years] from the date of notice, where it is possible to do so, in respect of cases falling under sub-section (4) 5
[***].

(12) Where the appellate authority or tribunal or court modifies the amount of duty of excise determined by the Central Excise
Officer under sub-section (10), then the amount of penalties and interest under this section shall stand modified accordingly,
taking into account the amount of duty of excise so modified.

(13) Where the amount as modified by the appellate authority or tribunal or court is more than the amount determined under sub-
section (10) by the Central Excise Officer, the time within which the interest or penalty is payable under this Act shall be counted
from the date of the order of the appellate authority or tribunal or court in respect of such increased amount.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers Section 11(4) of the Central Excise Act 1944 — the extended 5-year limitation period triggered by fraud/suppression, the deemed-notice mechanism under 11(7A) for subsequent periods, the fallback to normal 2-year period under sub-section (9) when fraud is not established, and adjudication timelines under sub-sections (10)-(13). These provisions are indispensable for HIGH-complexity disputes involving limitation, intent-to-evade allegations, and multi-round SCN scenarios.

questions.q: Arvind Alloys Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer of MS ingots in Raipur (Chhattisgarh), received a show-cause notice in March 2021 alleging suppression of production records and wilful mis-statement in ER-1 returns for the period April 2015 to March 2019, with a proposed duty demand of ₹4.2 crore and equivalent penalty. The adjudicating authority confirmed the demand in February 2024. On appeal, the CESTAT in July 2025 held that while there were discrepancies in records, the department had not discharged the burden of establishing intent to evade payment of duty — the foundational charge of fraud/suppression was therefore not proved. Arvind Alloys now argues that the entire demand must fall because the normal limitation period of 2 years would cover only April 2017 onwards, while the department contends the adjudication order survives in full. Additionally, during the pendency of the original SCN, the department had in 2022 served a supplementary statement covering April 2019 to March 2021 on the same grounds of suppression — Arvind Alloys argues that statement too must fall. What is the legal position on (a) the extent to which the demand survives after CESTAT's finding on fraud, (b) the validity and independent standing of the 2022 supplementary statement, and (c) the consequential recalculation of penalty and interest?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-section (9) directly governs what happens when the appellate authority finds fraud/suppression not established — it mandates the officer to treat the notice as if issued under the normal 2-year window, limiting the surviving demand to that period. Sub-section (7A) governs the validity of the supplementary statement (it is deemed valid only if grounds are the same as the original notice, and it rides on the parent notice). Sub-section (12) governs modification of penalties and interest on appellate modification of duty.

questions.q: Precision Components LLP, a manufacturer of automotive castings in Pune (Maharashtra) under Central Excise registration, had a show-cause notice served in January 2019 covering the period November 2013 to October 2018 (over 5 years), alleging clandestine removal and suppression of manufacturing records, with a duty demand of ₹7.8 crore. The notice was challenged before the Bombay High Court, which in April 2020 granted a stay on service/further proceedings; the stay was vacated in September 2022. The adjudicating officer issued the order-in-original only in March 2025. Precision Components now takes three grounds: (i) the 5-year extended period itself was not triggered because the SCN pre-dates clear evidence of intent; (ii) even if triggered, the adjudication in March 2025 breaches the prescribed timeline for completion; and (iii) the period of court stay should not be excluded because the stay was on 'service' not on 'proceedings'. As their tax counsel, analyse each ground in light of the applicable limitation and adjudication-timeline provisions under the Central Excise Act, identifying which grounds have statutory support and which do not.
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-section (4) sets the 5-year limitation triggered by fraud/suppression/intent-to-evade — ground (i) turns entirely on whether those conditions are met. Sub-section (8) governs exclusion of court-stay period from computing both the 2-year and 5-year windows — ground (iii) is resolved by this sub-section's wording. Sub-section (11)(b) prescribes the 2-year adjudication timeline for extended-period cases — ground (ii) depends on this provision and whether the stay-exclusion under (8) also extends the adjudication clock.

title: Seeks to amend Notification No 14/2017- Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28.06.2017.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZZETE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-
SECTION (i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No.17/2021-Integrated Tax (Rate)

New Delhi, 18th November, 2021.

G.S.R......(E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (5) of section 5 of the Integrated Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017 (13 of 2017), the Central Government, on the recommendations of the Council, hereby 
makes the following amendments further to amend the notification of the Government of India, in the Ministry of 
Finance (Department of Revenue), No.14/2017- Integrated Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017, published in 
the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 689(E) dated the 28th 
June, 2017, namely:-

1. 
In the notification,-

(i) in clause (i), for the words “and motor cycle;”, the words “, motor cycle, omnibus or  any other motor vehicle;” 
shall be substituted;

(ii) after clause (iii), the following clause shall be inserted, namely:-

“(iv) supply of “restaurant service” other than the services supplied by restaurants, eating joints etc. 
located at specified premises.”

2. 
In the said notification, in Explanation, -

(i) in item (b), for the words, brackets, numbers and figures “and “motor cycle” shall have the same meanings as 
assigned to them respectively in clauses (22), (25) and (26) of section 2 of the Motor Vehicle Act, 1988 (59 of 
1988).”, the words, brackets, numbers and figures ,“, motor cycle, motor vehicle and omnibus shall have the same 
meanings as assigned to them respectively in clauses (22), (25), (27), (28) and (29) of section 2 of the Motor 
Vehicle Act, 1988 (59 of 1988).” shall be substituted;

(ii) after item (b), the following shall be inserted namely, -

“(c) specified premises means premises providing hotel accommodation service having declared tariff of 
any unit of accommodation above seven thousand five hundred rupees per unit per day or equivalent.”

2. This notification shall come into force with effect from the 1st day of January, 2022.

[F. No. 354/207/2021-TRU]

(Rajeev Ranjan)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note:-The principal notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, vide notification No. 
14/2017 – Integrated Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017, vide number G.S.R. 689(E), dated the 28th June, 2017
claude_reasoning: This chunk amends the IGST rate notification to bring 'restaurant service' outside specified premises under the reverse charge / composition-like regime effective 1-Jan-2022, and expands the motor vehicle definition. The 'specified premises' threshold (Rs 7,500/unit/day declared tariff) and the restaurant service carve-out create rich practitioner complexity around hotel+restaurant bundling, place of supply, and inter-state service classification.

questions.q: Skyline Hospitality LLP operates a 5-star hotel in Bengaluru (GSTIN 29AABCS1234F1ZK) with rooms declared at Rs 9,000 per unit per day. The hotel's in-house restaurant — 'The Palette' — serves meals to both in-house guests and walk-in customers from the street. From 1-Jan-2022 onwards, the LLP has been charging GST at 5% without ITC on all restaurant sales, treating the restaurant as a standalone food outlet. A Maharashtra-based corporate client, Meridian Pharma Pvt Ltd, booked 10 rooms (same tariff) for a 3-night conference in March 2022 and also paid Rs 4.8 lakh for banquet/catering services billed separately on the same GSTIN. The LLP's tax team now claims the banquet supply should also be taxed at 18% with ITC as it falls under 'specified premises.' The client's CFO disagrees and wants to recover the differential GST paid under protest. Who is correct, and what is the applicable GST rate and ITC entitlement for (a) restaurant meals to walk-in guests, (b) in-room dining to hotel guests, and (c) the banquet/catering supply — given the declared tariff of the accommodation units at this property?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk defines 'specified premises' as hotel accommodation with declared tariff above Rs 7,500/unit/day and carves out 'restaurant service' supplied by restaurants at such specified premises from the 5%-no-ITC regime — making the Rs 9,000 tariff the decisive fact that determines whether all three supply scenarios attract 18% with ITC or 5% without ITC.

questions.q: Regal Wheels Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-registered company (GSTIN 07AAACR5678M1ZP), operates a B2B rental fleet. Post 1-Jan-2022, it enters into a 12-month contract with a Pune-based IT firm, Techbridge Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCT9012K1ZQ), to provide chauffeur-driven omnibuses (14-seater) for daily employee commute in Pune. The vehicles are registered in Delhi, the contract is signed in Delhi, and invoices are raised from Delhi. Regal's accounts team claims the supply is exempt from IGST under the motor vehicle reverse charge notification because omnibuses were not covered prior to the amendment and argues the old notification language controls for contracts entered into before 1-Jan-2022 but invoiced after. Techbridge's tax counsel contends IGST at 12% applies on all invoices raised post 1-Jan-2022 regardless of contract date, and that reverse charge liability falls on Techbridge as the recipient. Who bears the GST liability, at what rate, and does the expanded definition of 'omnibus' and 'motor vehicle' under the amended explanation change the place-of-supply analysis for this inter-state B2B rental?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's substitution of 'omnibus or any other motor vehicle' into clause (i) and the expanded Motor Vehicles Act definitions in the Explanation (clauses 27, 28, 29) are indispensable — omnibuses were not covered before this amendment, so the effective date of 1-Jan-2022 and the new definitional scope directly determine whether the supply falls within the amended notification and who bears reverse charge liability.

title: This Notification amends certain notifications
