claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs amended conditions for EOU/EPZ/SEZ/EHTP/STP Central Excise exemption notifications — specifically the revised NFE threshold (15% replacing 24%), the CT-3 procedure for concessional duty removal, job-worker registration requirements for export, and inter-unit transfer permissions. These provisions create highly specific compliance traps for multi-location manufacturing and export structures that practitioners regularly face.

questions.q: Kaleidoscope Garments Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 33AABCK1234M1ZP) is a 100% EOU registered in Chennai, Tamil Nadu. In FY 2025-26, it dispatches semi-processed fabric (cut-parts) without payment of Central Excise duty to Rajasthan Stitch House, a job worker located in Jaipur, Rajasthan, for embroidery work. Rajasthan Stitch House is registered under GST but has never taken Central Excise registration and does not appear as a Central Excise registrant under any rule. After embroidery, the goods are exported directly from Jaipur under a shipping bill in Kaleidoscope's name. The DRI has issued a show-cause notice alleging that export from the job worker's premises violates EOU exemption conditions because the job worker is not a Central Excise registrant. Kaleidoscope argues that post-GST, Central Excise registration for job workers is redundant and the condition has been impliedly waived. Separately, for the same FY, Kaleidoscope's NFE works out to 12% — the company argues the old 24% benchmark still governs because the amending notification was never incorporated into their Letter of Permission. Is the DRI's show-cause notice on job-worker registration legally sustainable, and does the 12% NFE trigger a duty demand or only an EXIM Policy violation?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes the job-worker export condition to explicitly require the job worker to be 'Central Excise registrant under rule 9 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002', and separately substitutes the NFE threshold from 24% to fifteen per cent — both are indispensable to resolving the dual issues in this scenario.

questions.q: Nexora Techsolutions LLP, registered as an EHTP unit in Hyderabad (GSTIN 36AAFCN5678R1ZK), holds a Central Excise exemption under the EOU/EHTP notification. It primarily exports software services to clients in the US and EU, but also manufactures and supplies embedded hardware components to domestic buyers within India (cleared on payment of duty). During an audit for FY 2024-25, the jurisdictional Central Excise superintendent issues a notice proposing to deny the entire notification benefit on the ground that Nexora is a 'service providing unit which does not export services out of India' for the months (April–July 2024) when US client contracts were suspended and all revenue came from domestic hardware sales. Nexora counters that (a) it is an EHTP unit, not purely a service unit, (b) it resumed service exports by August 2024, and (c) the exclusion clause applies only to units that never export services, not to units with temporary gaps. Additionally, Nexora transferred capital goods worth ₹45 lakhs to a co-located SEZ unit in HITEC City without payment of duty in September 2024, and the department questions whether this transfer was permissible given the disputed notification status. Advise on (i) the correct interpretation of the service-unit exclusion clause, (ii) whether the transfer of capital goods to the co-located SEZ unit was valid, and (iii) the period for which, if at all, the notification benefit can be denied.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk inserts paragraph 6 — 'Nothing contained in this notification shall be applicable to a service providing unit which does not export services out of India' — and separately permits transfer/supply of capital goods produced by a unit to another SEZ/EHTP/STP/EOU unit; both provisions are directly in issue and cannot be answered from any generic chunk.

title: Restoration of provisionally attached property / bank account under section83
text:
List of documents to be uploaded:- 
 
1. 
Photographs (wherever specified in the Application Form)

(a) Proprietary Concern – Proprietor

(b) 
Partnership 
Firm 
/ 
Limited 
Liability 
Partnership 
–

Managing/Authorised/Designated Partners (personal details of all partners are to 
be submitted but photos of only ten partners including that of Managing Partner 
are to be submitted)

(c) Hindu Undivided Family – Karta

(d) Company – Managing Director or the Authorised Person

(e) Trust – Managing Trustee

(f) Association of Persons or Body of Individuals –Members of Managing 
Committee (personal details of all members are to be submitted but photos of only 
ten members including that of Chairman are to be submitted)

(g) Local Authority – Chief Executive Officer or his equivalent

(h) Statutory Body – Chief Executive Officer or his equivalent

(i) Others – Person in Charge

2. 
Constitution of Business: Partnership Deed in case of Partnership Firm, 
Registration Certificate/Proof of Constitution in case of Society, Trust, Club, 
Government Department, Association of Persons or Body of Individuals, Local 
Authority, Statutory Body and Others etc.

3. 
Proof of Principal Place of Business:

(a) For Own premises –

Any document in support of the ownership of the premises like latest Property Tax 
Receipt or Municipal Khata copy or copy of Electricity Bill.

(b) For Rented or Leased premises –

A copy of the valid Rent / Lease Agreement with any document in support of the 
ownership of the premises of the Lessor like Latest Property Tax Receipt or 
Municipal Khata copy or copy of Electricity Bill.

(c) For premises not covered in (a) and (b) above –

A copy of the Consent Letter with any document in support of the ownership of 
the premises of the Consenter like Municipal Khata copy or Electricity Bill copy. 
For shared properties also, the same documents may be uploaded.

(d) For rented/leased premises where the Rent/lease agreement is not available, an 
affidavit to that effect along with any document in support of the possession of the 
premises like copy of Electricity Bill.

(e) If the principal place of business is located in a Special Economic Zone or the 
applicant 
is 
an 
Special 
Economic 
Zone 
developer, 
necessary

documents/certificates issued by Government of India are required to be uploaded.

21
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GST registration document requirements (photos, constitution proof, place-of-business proof) for various entity types including SEZ. While procedural, it supports HIGH-complexity questions involving SEZ registration and multi-entity/multi-jurisdiction registration scenarios where specific document gaps create legal risk.

questions.q: Nexus Infratech LLP is setting up its principal place of business inside the Kandla Special Economic Zone in Gujarat and is applying for GST registration. The LLP has 14 designated partners spread across Maharashtra, Rajasthan, and Gujarat. The premises are co-shared with another SEZ unit under an informal arrangement — no formal lease exists. The LLP's tax consultant is unsure: (a) how many partner photographs must be submitted and whose exactly, (b) what document establishes possession of the shared SEZ premises given the absence of a lease, and (c) whether the standard property-tax-receipt route for proving the principal place of business is sufficient or whether additional government-issued SEZ certificates are mandatorily required. Please advise on all three documentation gaps before the registration application is filed.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly answers all three sub-questions: for LLPs, photos of only 10 partners including the Managing/Designated Partner are required even if there are more; for shared premises without a lease, a Consent Letter plus electricity bill or municipal khata of the consenter suffices; and for SEZ premises, the chunk explicitly mandates government-issued SEZ certificates in addition to standard proof — none of these answers are derivable from a generic GST chunk.

questions.q: Arvind Textile Processors Pvt Ltd, a company in Surat (GSTIN 24AABCA1234K1ZX), was provisionally registered and is now filing its final GST registration application. The Managing Director is based in Mumbai and the company's factory is in Surat on premises owned by the MD's HUF — there is no formal lease deed, only a verbal consent arrangement. The GST officer has flagged that (i) the photograph submitted is of the CFO, not the MD or an authorised signatory, and (ii) the HUF consent letter was uploaded without any corroborating ownership document. The company argues that because the HUF's karta is the MD's father, the family connection itself establishes bona fide possession. Assess whether the officer's objection is legally sustainable and what the company must upload to cure both defects.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that for a Company, the photograph must be of the Managing Director or the Authorised Person — not any officer — making the CFO photograph non-compliant; and for consent-based premises (category (c)), the chunk requires both a Consent Letter AND a supporting ownership document (Municipal Khata or Electricity Bill) of the consenter, regardless of familial relationship, making the HUF's family-connection argument legally untenable under this specific rule.

title: Amends notification no.5/2006-CE dated 01.03.2006
text:
New Delhi, dated the 24th March, 2011

Notification 
No. 25/2011-Central Excise

G.S.R.     (E). - In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 5A

of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944), the Central Government, on being satisfied that 
it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby makes the following further amendments 
in the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of 
Revenue), No. 5/2006-Central Excise, dated the 1st March, 2006 which was published in the 
Gazette of India, Extraordinary, vide G.S.R. 95(E) dated the 1st March, 2006, namely: -  
 
In the said notification, in the Table,-

(i) in S. No. 21, in column (3), for the words ‘ from any form of gold’, the words ‘from

any form of gold other than gold ore, concentrate or dore bar’ shall be substituted;

(ii) in S. No. 23, in column (3), the words ‘ copper or’ shall be deleted.

[F. No. B-1/3/2011 –TRU]

(Sanjeev Kumar Singh)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note.- The principal notification No. 5/2006-Central Excise, dated the 1st March, 2006 was 
published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, vide number G.S.R. 95(E), dated the 1st 
March, 2006 and last amended vide notification no. 5/2011-Central Excise, dated the 1st 
March, 2011, published vide number G.S.R.120 (E), dated the 1st March, 2011.
claude_reasoning: This chunk amends two specific entries in notification 5/2006-CE: (i) it carves out gold ore, concentrate and dore bar from the exemption available on articles made 'from any form of gold' (S.No.21), and (ii) it removes copper from the materials listed in S.No.23. These targeted amendments create high-complexity queries because practitioners must track the amendment chain to determine whether a specific input form (dore bar, gold ore) falls inside or outside the exemption, requiring multi-entity and cross-notification reasoning.

questions.q: Suvarnacraft Refineries Pvt Ltd (GSTIN equivalent: ECC SUVCR1234ME001) in Mumbai imports gold dore bars and refines them into 24-karat gold coins and bars for sale to jewellers in Rajasthan. The refinery was availing Central Excise duty exemption under notification 5/2006-CE on the finished articles on the ground that they are manufactured 'from any form of gold.' A Central Excise audit team visiting in May 2011 has raised a demand contending that the exemption under S.No.21 is no longer available on goods manufactured from gold dore bars effective 24-March-2011. The refinery argues that a dore bar is simply unrefined gold and should still qualify as 'any form of gold.' Which specific amendment governs this dispute, and what is the precise scope of the carve-out introduced in March 2011 — does it cover only gold ore and dore bar, or also gold concentrate, and what is the consequence for the refinery's clearances from April 2011 onwards?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes the words in S.No.21 column(3) to exclude 'gold ore, concentrate or dore bar' from the phrase 'any form of gold,' which is the exact legal text needed to resolve whether dore-bar-origin goods retain the exemption.

questions.q: Hindalco-Bharat Copper Works LLP, a manufacturer of copper wires and copper alloy rods in Gujarat, was availing the benefit of an exemption entry under notification 5/2006-CE covering specified non-ferrous metals listed in S.No.23. Following the March 2011 budget cycle, their CA advises that the entry has been amended and copper has been deleted from that entry effective 24-March-2011. The LLP has stock of semi-finished copper rods manufactured before 24-March-2011 but cleared to buyers in Maharashtra after that date, and also a fresh batch of copper wire drawn entirely post-amendment. Advise the LLP: (a) whether clearances of pre-amendment stock made after 24-March-2011 attract duty, (b) what rate of Basic Excise Duty now applies on copper rods and wire absent the exemption, and (c) whether any other notification under the Central Excise Act could independently shelter these clearances, given that the specific deletion of 'copper' from S.No.23 by notification 25/2011-CE is now on record.
questions.why_this_chunk: The deletion of the words 'copper or' from S.No.23 column(3) of notification 5/2006-CE is exclusively effected by this chunk; without this amendment text, the practitioner cannot establish that copper was specifically removed from the exemption entry or on what date.

title: Regarding the manner of issue of duty credit for goods exported under the RoSCTL Scheme under Foreign Trade Policy, 2023
text:
nterest, 
as applicable, it shall be recovered in the manner provided in section 142 of the said Act.

5. Recovery of amount of duty credit where export proceeds are not realised.- (1) Where an amount 
of duty credit has been allowed to an exporter but the sale proceeds in respect of such export goods 
have not been realised by the exporter in India within the period allowed under the Foreign Exchange 
Management Act, 1999 (42 of 1999), the exporter shall, himself or on demand by the proper officer, 
repay the amount of duty credit, along with interest, at the rate as fixed under section 28AA of the said 
Act for the purposes of that section, within fifteen days of expiry of the said period.

(2) In case any extension of the said period for realisation of sale proceeds has been given by the 
Reserve Bank of India and the exporter produces evidence of such extension to the proper officer, and if 
the said sale proceeds are not realised in such extended period, the exporter shall repay the said amount 
of duty credit along with the said interest, within fifteen days of expiry of the said period.

(3) If a part of the sale proceeds has been realised, the amount of duty credit to be recovered shall be the 
amount equal to that portion of the amount of duty credit allowed which bears the same proportion as 
the portion of the sale proceeds not realised bears to the total amount of sale proceeds.

(4) Where the exporter fails to repay the duty credit amount within the said period of fifteen days, the 
said duty credit shall be deemed never to have been allowed and it shall be recovered, along with the 
said interest, in the manner as provided in section 142 of the said Act.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs recovery of RoSCTL duty credit when export proceeds are not realised within FEMA-prescribed periods, including partial realisation pro-rata recovery and RBI extension scenarios — highly specific rules that cannot be answered by any generic customs or GST chunk.

questions.q: Velankanni Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 33AABCV1234F1ZK, Tamil Nadu), an exporter of cotton bed-sheets (HS 6302), was granted RoSCTL duty credit of ₹18,40,000 for a shipment invoiced at USD 22,000 to a buyer in Germany on 10-March-2024. As of the normal FEMA realisation deadline, only USD 14,000 has been credited to the exporter's EEFC account; the remaining USD 8,000 is disputed by the foreign buyer. The exporter's banker confirms no RBI extension has been applied for. The proper officer has issued a demand notice. How much duty credit must Velankanni repay, at what interest rate and under which provision, and what happens to the duty credit entitlement if they miss the fifteen-day repayment window?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 5(3) provides the exact pro-rata formula: duty credit recoverable = (unrealised proceeds / total proceeds) × total duty credit allowed. Clause 5(4) specifies the consequence of missing the 15-day window — the entire credit is deemed never to have been allowed and recovered under section 142. Clause 5(1) fixes the interest rate reference at section 28AA of the Customs Tariff Act.

questions.q: Sunrise Garments LLP (Surat, Gujarat) exported embroidered sarees worth USD 50,000 under RoSCTL and received duty credit scrips totalling ₹3,20,000. The exporter realised USD 30,000 within the standard FEMA period, then obtained an RBI extension for the balance USD 20,000. Before the extended deadline expired, USD 12,000 was further realised, but USD 8,000 remained outstanding even after the extended period. The proper officer is now determining the recoverable amount and the applicable timeline for demand. Meanwhile, Sunrise has already transferred part of the scrip to a third-party buyer on the duty-credit secondary market. Advise on (a) the exact amount of duty credit to be recovered from Sunrise, (b) whether the 15-day clock runs from the expiry of the original or the extended FEMA period, and (c) the legal effect on the third-party transferee who has already utilised the transferred scrip for import duty payment.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 5(2) governs the RBI-extension scenario and anchors the 15-day window to expiry of the extended period, not the original period. Clause 5(3) provides the pro-rata calculation applied to the post-extension unrealised balance. Clause 5(4)'s 'deemed never to have been allowed' language is indispensable to analysing the third-party transferee's exposure.

title: Fruit pulp based drinks under heading 2202.30 will include frozen fruit pulp based drinks but would not include fruit juice or fruit juice concentrate based drinks( Heading 2202.91 or 2202.99)
text:
Circular No. 309/25/97-CX

dated 31/3/97

F.No. 22/1/96-CX.1

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

(Central Board of Excise and Customs), New Delhi

Subject: Classification of drinks made from frozen fruit pulp, fruit pulp concentrates and fruit pulp - Clarification regarding.

I am directed to say that a doubt has arisen on the classification of the drinks made from frozen fruit pulps, fruit pulp
concentrates and the fruit pulps. The trade has represented that the tariff entry "fruit pulp based drinks" in heading No. 2202.30
may be revised to read as "fruit pulp based drinks, fruit juices, fruit juice concentrates."

2.    The matter has since been examined by the Board. Board observed that fruit pulp fall under Ch. heading 2201 of the CET
as per HSN Explanatory Notes corresponding to Ch. heading 20.08 at page 152. Similarly, the fruit juice also falls under Ch.
heading 20.01 of CET as per the HSN explanatory notes corresponding the heading 2009 at page 148. As per HSN
Explanatory Note at page 152 & 154 it is seen that the fruit juices can be generally obtained by pressing fresh healthy and ripe
fruit which may not be preceded either by crushing or grinding (for apples in particular). Certain juices, however (in particular
those obtained from pulpy fruits such as Apricots, peaches and Tomatoes) still contain part of the pulp in finely divided form
either in suspension or as a deposit. The juices may be concentrated (whether or not frozen). Therefore, as per the HSN
Explanatory Notes the fruit juice as well as fruit concentrate can be frozen or otherwise. But a distinction has been made
between fruit pulp and fruit juice or fruit juice concentrate as fruit pulp is covered under Ch. Heading 20.08 of the HSN and fruit
juice is covered under Heading 20.09 of the HSN though in the CET fruit pulp and fruit juice are covered under heading 20.01
in the absence of separate sub-headings provided thereunder. The tariff entry against sub-headings provided thereunder. The
tariff entry against sub-hading 2202.30 of the CET covers fruit pulp based drinks. The fruit pulp shall include frozen fruit pulp but
shall not cover fruit juice (frozen or not) or fruit juice concentrate. Therefore, Board is of the view that fruit pulp based drinks
classifiable under Chapter Heading 2202.30 will include frozen fruit pulp based drinks also. However, the term "fruit pulp based
drinks" would not include fruit juice or fruit juice concentrate based drinks. The fruit juice or fruit juice concentrate based drinks
will appropriately be covered under Chapter Heading 2202.91 or 2202.99 as the case may be.

Sd/-

(S.C. Bhatia)

Under Secretary to the Govt. of India
claude_reasoning: This circular authoritatively resolves the classification boundary between fruit pulp based drinks (CET 2202.30) and fruit juice/concentrate based drinks (CET 2202.91/2202.99) under Central Excise Tariff, including the specific treatment of frozen variants. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving classification disputes, differential duty liability, and the interplay between HSN explanatory notes and CET headings.

questions.q: Refresh Beverages Pvt Ltd, a Karnataka-based manufacturer (GSTIN 29AABCR1234F1ZK), produces two product lines at its Tumkur plant: (1) 'FruzBurst' — made by diluting frozen mango pulp with water and sugar, packaged in 200ml tetra packs; and (2) 'JuicyPop' — made by reconstituting spray-dried mango juice concentrate with water. The company has been classifying both under CET heading 2202.30 and paying Central Excise duty accordingly since FY 2021-22. The jurisdictional Superintendent has issued a show cause notice proposing reclassification of 'JuicyPop' to heading 2202.99, demanding differential duty of ₹38.6 lakhs plus interest and penalty for the period April 2021 to March 2024. The company's advocate argues that since mango juice concentrate can technically contain fruit pulp solids in suspension (as noted in HSN explanatory notes for pulpy fruits), 'JuicyPop' should remain under 2202.30. Does the CBIC's clarificatory position on the distinction between fruit pulp and fruit juice concentrate support or undermine the company's classification defence, and what is the correct heading for each product?
questions.why_this_chunk: The circular explicitly states that 'fruit pulp based drinks' under 2202.30 include frozen fruit pulp drinks but expressly exclude fruit juice or fruit juice concentrate based drinks, which must go to 2202.91 or 2202.99 — directly determining whether FruzBurst (frozen pulp → stays at 2202.30) and JuicyPop (juice concentrate → moves to 2202.99) are correctly classified, and defeating the conflation argument.

questions.q: Himalayan Orchard Products LLP, an Uttarakhand manufacturer, exports 'ApriGold' drinks to the UAE. ApriGold is produced by blending apricot pulp (not juice) with water and permitted additives; however, during the manufacturing process the apricot pulp is first frozen for preservation and then thawed before blending. A Central Excise audit party at the Haridwar factory is contending that the intermediate freezing of pulp changes its character to 'fruit juice' because apricot is a pulpy fruit and post-thaw the product contains pulp in finely divided suspension — citing HSN explanatory notes for heading 20.09 — and is therefore misclassified under 2202.30 instead of 2202.99, resulting in a proposed short-levy of ₹52 lakh. Simultaneously, for the export consignments, the company is claiming a duty drawback rate linked to the 2202.30 classification. If the classification is changed to 2202.99, the drawback entitlement also shifts. Advise on (a) whether frozen-then-thawed apricot pulp retains its character as 'fruit pulp' or becomes 'fruit juice' for tariff purposes, (b) the correct CET heading for ApriGold, and (c) the drawback implications of any reclassification.
questions.why_this_chunk: The circular directly addresses the frozen fruit pulp question, stating that 'fruit pulp shall include frozen fruit pulp' under 2202.30, and separately notes the HSN distinction that pulpy fruit juices (like apricot) may contain pulp in suspension under heading 20.09 — making the chunk indispensable to resolving whether freezing transforms pulp into juice and consequently whether 2202.30 or 2202.99 applies, with cascading drawback consequences.

Note: - The principal notification No. 4/2017-Union territoryTax (Rate), dated the 28thJune, 
2017, was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i),

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively establishes RCM liability under UTGST for specific essential oils (HSN 33012400/33012510-33012540) supplied by unregistered persons to registered persons, effective 1-Oct-2021. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because answering requires linking the UTGST RCM entry to corresponding IGST/CGST RCM notifications, place-of-supply rules for goods, and input tax credit eligibility — a multi-act, multi-entity reasoning exercise.

questions.q: Aromatic India LLP (GSTIN 07AABFA1234C1ZX, registered in Delhi) purchases 200 kg of Spearmint oil (ex-Mentha spicata) worth ₹3,80,000 on 12-Nov-2021 from a small unregistered distiller based in Dadra & Nagar Haveli. The distiller dispatches the goods from Silvassa directly to Aromatic India's Delhi warehouse. The LLP's accounts team is unsure: (a) whether GST is payable by the LLP on reverse charge and under which Act(s), (b) whether IGST or CGST+UTGST applies given the origin is a Union Territory, and (c) whether the ITC of tax paid under RCM on this purchase can be used immediately to offset the LLP's output CGST liability on its own Delhi sales. Advise fully, citing the applicable notifications and place-of-supply rules.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk inserts S. No. 3A into Notification 4/2017-UTGST(Rate) placing Spearmint oil (HSN 33012520) under RCM when supplier is unregistered and recipient is registered — this is the indispensable trigger for the LLP's RCM liability question; without this entry the obligation under UTGST does not arise, and the parallel CGST/IGST analysis must be cross-checked against it.

questions.q: Vedic Essentials Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCV5678D1ZP, Maharashtra) is a manufacturer of ayurvedic cosmetics and regularly procures Peppermint oil (Mentha piperita) and Bergamot mint oil (Mentha citrata) from small tribal cooperatives that are unregistered. One of its regular suppliers — a cooperative in Puducherry — supplied 150 kg of Peppermint oil for ₹2,10,000 on 5-Oct-2021, with the bill-to being Vedic Essentials' head office in Pune and the ship-to being its contract-manufacturing unit in Pondicherry (also held in its own name under the same GSTIN via an additional place of business registration). The cooperative has never taken GST registration. Determine: (i) which RCM notification(s) govern this transaction and whether UTGST, IGST, or CGST+SGST applies; (ii) whether the ship-to/bill-to split affects the place of supply and therefore the applicable tax head; and (iii) how Vedic Essentials should self-invoice and account for the tax, given its Pondicherry unit is in a Union Territory while its GSTIN is Maharashtra-based.
questions.why_this_chunk: The insertion of S. No. 3A covering Peppermint oil (HSN 33012400) under UTGST RCM (unregistered supplier → registered recipient) is the pivot of the question: the supply originates in Puducherry (a UT), triggering potential UTGST RCM applicability under this very notification, and the cross-GSTIN/additional-place-of-business wrinkle forces the practitioner to reconcile UTGST RCM with IGST place-of-supply rules — neither question is answerable without this chunk.

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs UTGST rate amendments for specific food items — dried mangoes (HS 0804), khakhra/roti (HS 1905/2106), and unbranded namkeens/bhujia (HS 2106 90) at 2.5% UTGST — including the branded vs. unbranded carve-out with the voluntary-foregone-brand-name proviso. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because the branded/unbranded distinction, the voluntary-foregoing condition, and the interplay between CGST Rate notification and this parallel UTGST Rate notification require multi-entity and cross-act interpretation.

questions.q: Haldiram Snacks Pvt Ltd (Delhi, GSTIN 07AAACH1234F1ZX) supplies 500 kg of loose bhujia mixture packed in 1 kg pouches under the brand name 'Haldiram's' to a distributor in Lakshadweep (a Union Territory). The company's legal team is considering filing a declaration voluntarily foregoing all enforceable rights on the 'Haldiram's' brand name for this specific product line, claiming the benefit of the lower GST rate applicable to unbranded namkeens. (a) Under the applicable UTGST Rate notification as amended in October 2017, what rate of UTGST would apply to this supply if the foregoing declaration is validly made, and what are the precise conditions of that ANNEXURE that must be satisfied? (b) Does the fact that 'Haldiram's' remains a registered trademark under the Trade Marks Act — with active enforcement in other product categories — defeat the voluntary-foregoing claim for this specific SKU? (c) How does the parallel CGST Rate notification interact with this UTGST notification so that both taxes are computed at the lower rate, and what combined GST liability arises on a taxable value of ₹12,50,000?
questions.why_this_chunk: S. No. 101A inserted by this notification specifically carves out namkeens/bhujia/mixture under HS 2106 90 from the 2.5% UTGST rate where a registered brand name or actionable/enforceable brand claim exists, but restores the lower rate where the brand right has been 'voluntarily foregone subject to conditions in the ANNEXURE' — this proviso is the linchpin of the question and exists only in this amendment notification.

questions.q: Sunrise Dry Fruits LLP (registered in Maharashtra, GSTIN 27AABFS9876K1ZP) exports dried mango slices (HS 0804) to a buyer in Singapore on a CIF basis, but the physical shipment passes through and is consolidated at a warehouse in Daman (a Union Territory, GSTIN 25AABFS9876K1ZQ — a separate registration held by the same LLP). The Daman unit raises the export invoice. Customs clearance happens at Nhava Sheva (Maharashtra). The LLP's tax consultant needs to determine: (a) Prior to October 2017, what HSN was applicable to dried mango slices under the UTGST rate schedule, and how did the October 2017 amendment change the classification or rate? (b) For the Daman-registered entity, does the supply from Daman to the overseas buyer constitute an inter-state zero-rated supply under IGST (making UTGST moot), or is there a scenario — such as the goods being 'sold' from Daman warehouse to the Maharashtra exporter before export — where UTGST on an intra-UT transaction could be triggered at 2.5%? (c) If UTGST is triggered on a prior domestic leg valued at ₹8,00,000, what ITC mechanism is available to offset it against the export obligation?
questions.why_this_chunk: S. No. 30A inserted by this notification — adding 'Mangoes sliced, dried' under HS 0804 to Schedule I (2.5% UTGST) — is the direct source of the applicable rate for this product in a Union Territory context; without this amendment the classification entry did not exist in the UTGST rate schedule, making this chunk indispensable to answering part (a) and anchoring the entire rate analysis.

title: Continuation of Recovery Proceedings

2. It has been noticed that you have not filed the final return by the due date.

3. You are, therefore, requested to furnish the final return as specified under section 45 of

the Act within 15 days failing which your tax liability for the aforesaid [tax period may]37 
be determined in accordance with the provisions of the Act based on the relevant material 
available with or gathered by this office. Please note that in addition to tax so assessed, 
you will also be liable to pay interest as per provisions of the Act.

4. This notice shall be deemed to be withdrawn in case the return is filed by you before issue

[5. This is a system generated notice and does not require signature.]38

claude_reasoning: This chunk is a statutory notice template under section 45 (final return for cancelled registrations) warning of best-judgment assessment if the final return is not filed within 15 days. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving cancelled GSTINs, post-cancellation assessment timelines, and the interplay between the notice, the 15-day window, and the deemed-withdrawal proviso.

questions.q: Veritas Exports LLP, a Pune-based exporter (GSTIN 27AABFV1234Q1ZK), had its GST registration cancelled effective 31-Jan-2025 following a suo motu cancellation order by the proper officer. The LLP filed its final return (GSTR-10) on 20-Mar-2025, which was after the statutory due date. The department has now issued a notice stating that tax liability for the period ending 31-Jan-2025 will be determined based on material available with the office, and is also demanding interest. The LLP's CA argues that (a) the notice is void because no signature is present, and (b) if the LLP pays the assessed tax before the assessment order is issued, the notice should be treated as withdrawn. Is the CA's position on both counts legally sustainable, and what is the outer boundary of the LLP's exposure if the department proceeds to assess?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the exact statutory language: (a) system-generated notices do not require signature (inserted by Notf 02/2020-CT), and (b) the deemed-withdrawal proviso — 'This notice shall be deemed to be withdrawn in case the return is filed by you before issue of the assessment order' — is indispensable to evaluating the CA's second argument. The 15-day window and interest liability language are also directly quoted here.

questions.q: Meridian Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 07AABCM5678R1ZP, Delhi) surrendered its GST registration on 28-Feb-2025 after closing its Delhi branch. It failed to file GSTR-10 by the due date. On 15-Apr-2025 it received a system-generated notice (no wet signature) directing it to file the final return within 15 days or face best-judgment assessment, plus interest. On 25-Apr-2025 — before expiry of the 15-day window but after receiving the notice — the company filed GSTR-10, declaring an output tax liability of ₹18.4 lakh and an ITC reversal of ₹6.2 lakh. The proper officer, however, argues that the filing was belated and proceeds to issue an assessment order on 28-Apr-2025. Meridian's counsel contends the assessment order is bad in law. Analyse the validity of the assessment order, the effect of the deemed-withdrawal clause, and whether interest accrues on the ₹18.4 lakh for the period between the due date of GSTR-10 and the date of actual filing.
questions.why_this_chunk: The deemed-withdrawal clause ('notice shall be deemed to be withdrawn in case the return is filed before issue of the assessment order') and the 15-day cure window are the operative legal provisions. The chunk's note that interest liability continues under Act provisions is directly relevant to the interest sub-question. The amendment substituting 'tax liability will' with 'tax liability may' (Notf 02/2020-CT) also affects the discretionary vs mandatory nature of the assessment.

title: Seeks to levy definitive anti-dumping duty on imports of ' Steel and Fibre Glass Measuring tapes and their parts and components ',originating in, or exported from, People's Republic of China for a period of five years, in pursuance of final
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II,

SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification

No. 17/2020-Customs (ADD)

New Delhi, the  8th July, 2020

G.S.R…. (E). - Whereas, the designated authority, vide notification No. 7/24/2019-

DGTR dated 18th December 2019, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, 
Section 1, dated the 18th December 2019,  had initiated a review in the matter of continuation 
of anti-dumping duty on imports of “Steel and Fibre Glass Measuring tapes and their parts and 
components” (hereinafter referred to as the subject goods) falling under heading 9017 of the 
First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) (hereinafter referred to as the 
Customs Tariff Act), originating in or exported from the People's Republic of China 
(hereinafter referred to as the subject country), imposed vide notification of the Government 
of India, in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue) No. 31/2015- Customs (ADD), 
dated the 9th July, 2015, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, 
Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 549 (E), dated the 9th July, 2015;

And whereas, in the matter of review of anti-dumping duty on imports of the subject

goods, originating in or exported from the subject country, the designated authority in its final 
findings, published vide notification No. F. No. 7/24/2019-DGTR, dated the 18th June, 2020 
published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part- I, Section 1, dated the 18th June, 2020 
has come to the conclusion that: -

(i) 
there is continued dumping of the subject goods from subject country and the 
imports are likely to enter the Indian market at dumped prices in the event of expiry 
of duty;

(ii) 
the domestic industry’s performance has improved but remains vulnerable to 
dumping and consequent injury;

(iii) the information on record shows likelihood of continuation/ recurrence of dumping

and injury in case the anti-dumping duty in force is allowed to cease at this stage;

(iv) there is sufficient evidence to indicate that the cessation of anti-dumping duty at this

stage will lead to continuation of dumping and recurrence of injury to the Domestic 
Industry,

and has recommended continued imposition of the anti-dumping duty on imports of the subject 
goods, originating in or exported from the subject country;
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the definitive anti-dumping duty (ADD) on Steel and Fibre Glass Measuring Tapes (HS 9017) originating from China, including the sunset review findings and continued imposition rationale — directly relevant to import cost planning, ADD applicability disputes, and cross-act GST+Customs interaction for practitioners importing or dealing in these goods.

questions.q: Precision Tools Pvt Ltd, registered in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZK), imports a consignment of 50,000 units of fibre glass measuring tapes from Shanghai Metrology Co. Ltd. under a bill of lading dated 14-March-2025. The goods are classified under CTH 9017 and valued at USD 18,000 (CIF). The importer's customs broker argues that since the original ADD notification from 2015 has lapsed, no anti-dumping duty is payable. The company plans to sell these goods to a construction firm in Gujarat on an inter-state basis, and wants to understand: (a) whether ADD is still applicable on this consignment and on what legal basis, (b) how ADD is treated for the purpose of computing the assessable value for GST under IGST — specifically whether ADD forms part of the value of taxable supply, and (c) whether input tax credit can be claimed on the IGST paid at import if the goods are used in the course of a taxable supply.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that the 2015 ADD notification was subject to a sunset review under DGTR notification 7/24/2019, and based on findings of continued dumping and likelihood of recurrence, continued imposition was recommended — directly refuting the broker's argument that ADD lapsed. This is indispensable for part (a) of the question.

questions.q: Bharat Instruments LLP, a Rajasthan-based manufacturer (GSTIN 08AAKFB5678M1Z3) of surveying equipment, sources steel measuring tape components from two channels: (1) direct import from Guangzhou Tape Factory, China under CIF contracts, and (2) domestic purchase from a Mumbai-based trader who had earlier imported the same components from China. The LLP has received a show-cause notice from the customs department alleging short-payment of anti-dumping duty on the direct imports, claiming the ADD rate applicable to 'parts and components' of measuring tapes under HS 9017 was not correctly applied. Separately, the domestic supplier has passed on the ADD-inclusive cost in their invoice but has not charged GST on the ADD component, claiming it is a 'government levy' outside the value of supply. Advise Bharat Instruments LLP on: (a) whether 'parts and components' of steel and fibre glass measuring tapes are covered within the scope of the ADD notification and whether the SCN is sustainable, (b) whether the domestic supplier's treatment of ADD for GST valuation purposes is legally correct, and (c) the anti-dumping duty and GST implications if Bharat Instruments LLP re-exports the finished measuring tapes to a buyer in Nepal under a zero-rated supply.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that ADD applies to 'Steel and Fibre Glass Measuring tapes and their parts and components' under heading 9017 — the phrase 'parts and components' is textually present in this notification and is indispensable to determining whether the SCN on component imports is sustainable.

title: New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules
text:
186  
 THE GAZETTE OF INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
 [PART II—SEC. 3(i)]

(vii) 
all package inserts, promotional literature and patient education material subsequently produced are 
required to be consistent with the contents of the approved full prescribing information. The drafts of label and 
carton texts should comply with provisions of rule 96 and rule 97 of the Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945. After 
submission and approval by the Central Licencing Authority, no changes in the package insert shall be effected 
without such changes being approved by the Central Licencing Authority;

(viii) 
complete testing protocol for quality control testing together with a complete impurity profile and 
release specifications for the product as prescribed should be submitted as part of new drug application for 
marketing. Samples of the pure drug substance and finished product are to be submitted when desired by the 
regulatory authority;

(ix) 
if the application is for the conduct of clinical trials as a part of multi-national clinical development of 
the drug, the number of sites and patients as well as the justification for undertaking such trials in India should 
be provided to the Central Licencing Authority along with the application.

(2) 
Special situations for a new drug where relaxation, abbreviations, omission or deferment of data may be 
considered. – (i) Depending on categories and nature of new drugs to be imported or manufactured for sale or clinical 
trial to be undertaken (viz. New Chemical Entity, biological products, similar biologics, approved new drug or new 
dosage form or new indication or new route of administration or new strength of already approved drugs, etc.,) 
requirements of chemical and pharmaceutical information, animal pharmacology and toxicology data, clinical data may 
differ. The requirements may also differ depending on the specific phase of clinical trial proposed to be conducted as 
well as clinical parameters related to the specific study drug.

(ii) For drugs intended to be used in life threatening or serious disease conditions or rare diseases and for drugs intended 
to be used in the diseases of special relevance to  Indian scenario or unmet medical need in India, disaster or special 
defence use e.g. haemostatic and quick wound healing, enhancing oxygen carrying capacity, radiation safety, drugs for 
combating chemical, nuclear, biological infliction etc., following mechanism may be followed to expedite the development 
of new drug and approval process.

(A) Accelerated Approval Process: Accelerated approval process may be allowed to a new drug for a disease 
or condition, taking into account its severity, rarity, or prevalence and the availability or lack of alternative 
treatments, provided that there is a prima facie case of the product being of meaningful therapeutic benefit 
over the existing treatment.

(a) 
In such case, the approval of the new drug may be based on data generated in clinical trial 
where surrogate endpoint shall be considered rather than using standard outcome measures such as 
survival or disease progression, which are reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit, or a clinical 
endpoint. These should be measurable earlier than irreversible morbidity or mortality (IMM) and 
reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit.

(b) 
After granting accelerated approval for such drug, the post marketing trials shall be required to 
validate the anticipated clinical benefit.

(c) 
Accelerated approval may also be granted to a new drug if it is intended for the treatment of a 
serious or life-threatening condition or disease of special relevance to the country, and addresses 
unmet medical needs. This provision is intended to facilitate and expedite review of drugs so that an 
approved product can reach the therapeutic armamentarium expeditiously.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs the regulatory data requirements and accelerated approval pathway under the New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules, including the conditions for multi-national clinical trial imports and the surrogate-endpoint-based accelerated approval mechanism — both of which are prerequisites for determining eligibility for customs duty exemptions and import licensing conditions on pharmaceutical products.

questions.q: Lifecure Biopharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCL1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) is importing a biological investigational medicinal product from its Swiss parent for a Phase II multi-national clinical trial being conducted across 12 sites in India and 40 sites globally. The consignment declared under CTH 3002 arrives at Mumbai Air Cargo, and the company seeks full customs duty exemption under a relevant DGFT-linked notification applicable to drugs imported for clinical trials. Customs at JNCH has raised a query demanding (a) site-and-patient justification document submitted to the Central Licencing Authority and (b) proof that the Indian phase is part of the global multi-national development plan — arguing that without these, the import cannot be treated as a 'clinical trial import' eligible for exemption. The company contends it need only produce the CLA permission letter. Which specific regulatory condition — precisely what the importer must have submitted to the CLA at the time of application — resolves whether JNCH's documentary demand is legally sustainable, and how does this interact with the customs exemption notification's requirement that the goods be 'imported for the purpose of clinical trials'?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (ix) of the chunk expressly requires that for multi-national clinical development, the number of sites and patients AND the justification for undertaking trials in India must be provided to the CLA with the application — this is the exact document JNCH is demanding, making this chunk indispensable to adjudicating whether the customs officer's demand is legally grounded.

questions.q: Novagen Therapeutics LLP, a Hyderabad-based company (GSTIN 36AABFN5678G1ZQ), has received an Accelerated Approval from the Central Licencing Authority for its new drug targeting a rare haematological disorder (disease prevalence <1 in 10,000), with marketing approval granted on the basis of a surrogate endpoint (reduction in aberrant protein expression) rather than overall survival data. The company now imports finished formulation from its CMO in South Korea under CTH 3004. The customs valuation officer argues that since the drug lacks a 'full clinical outcome validation' and post-marketing confirmatory trials are still ongoing, the product cannot be classified as a 'drug approved for sale' and seeks to reclassify it under CTH 3006 as an investigational product, attracting a higher BCD rate. Separately, the GST authority in Telangana contends that without final clinical validation, the product does not qualify as a 'medicine' for the purpose of the GST exemption notification applicable to drugs for rare diseases. Advise on (a) whether the Accelerated Approval satisfies the 'approved for marketing' threshold for customs classification and GST exemption purposes, and (b) what post-approval obligation the company must be fulfilling to defend the classification if challenged.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk defines the Accelerated Approval mechanism — approval based on surrogate endpoints with mandatory post-marketing confirmatory trials — and explicitly states this constitutes valid drug approval. This is the authoritative source for whether surrogate-endpoint-based approval is a legally complete marketing approval, which is central to both the customs classification dispute and the GST exemption eligibility question.

title: Exempts certain goods when brought into 100% EOU / STP complex.
text:
ted 4-1-95), 10/95-Central Excise dated the 23 rd February, 1995 (G.S.R. 87(E) dated 23 rd

February, 1995), 37/99-Central Excise dated, the 15th September, 1999( G.S.R 637 (E), dated the 15 th September, 1999)
and 37/2000- Central Excise, dated the 8 th May, 2000 (G.S.R 414(E) dated the 8th May, 2000), are hereby rescinded.

13.         This notification shall come into force on the 1 st day of April, 2003.

Explanation - For the purposes of this notification,-

(i)
"Board of Approval" means the authority appointed by the Central Government in exercise of the powers conferred by
section 14 of the Industries (Development or Regulation) Act, 1951(65 of 1951) and the rules made under that Act;

(ii)
" Domestic Tariff Area" means area except special economic zone, export oriented undertakings, Software Technology
Park unit and Electronic Hardware Technology Park unit;

(iii)

"Export and Import Policy" means Export and Import Policy, 1 April 2002 - 31 March 2007, published by the Government
of India in the Ministry of Commerce and Industry vide notification No. 1 (RE-2003) /2002-07, dated 31 st March, 2003, as
amended from time to time;

(iv) "export oriented undertaking" has the same meaning as assigned to "hundred percent. export oriented undertaking" in

clause (ii) to the Explanation of sub-section (1) of section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 ( 1 of 1944);

(v)

"Electronic Hardware Technology Park (EHTP) unit" means a unit established under and in accordance with the
Electronic Hardware Technology Park Scheme notified by the Government of India in the Ministry of Commerce, vide
notification No. 5(RE-95)/92-97, dated 30 th April, 1995 and approved by the Inter Ministerial Standing Committee;

(vi)

"Handbook of Procedures, Volume I " means Handbook of Procedures Volume I, 2002-2007 published by the
Government of India, Ministry of Commerce and Industry vide Public Notice No. 1(RE-2003)/2002-07, dated 31 st March,
2003, as amended from time to time;

(vii) "Inter-Ministerial Standing Committee" means a committee appointed by the Government of India in the Ministry of

Industry (Department of Industrial Development) vide notification No. S.O. 117(E), dated the 22 nd February, 1993;

(viii)

"Software Technology Park (STP) unit" means a unit established under and in accordance with Scheme notified by the
Government of India in the Ministry of Commerce and Industry vide notification No. 4(RE-95)/92-95, dated 30 th April,
1995 and approved by the Inter- Ministerial Standing Committee;

(ix) "special economic zone" has the same meaning as assigned to it in clause (iii) to the Explanation 2 of sub-section (1) of

section 3 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 ( 1 of 1944).

ANNEXURE - I
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively defines 'Domestic Tariff Area', 'export oriented undertaking', 'STP unit', 'EHTP unit', and 'SEZ' for the purposes of Notification 22/2003-CE, and links those definitions back to specific parent statutes (Central Excise Act s.3(1) and scheme notifications). Practitioners litigating legacy Central Excise duty exemptions, EOU/STP eligibility disputes, or DTA clearance classification for periods prior to GST must rely precisely on these definitions.

questions.q: Infosys BPO Solutions Ltd operates a data-processing centre in Bengaluru that was set up under a scheme notified by the Ministry of Commerce and approved internally by the unit's own management committee — not by the Inter-Ministerial Standing Committee constituted vide S.O. 117(E) dated 22 Feb 1993. The unit had been availing Central Excise duty exemption on goods brought into the complex between April 2003 and June 2017, treating itself as an STP unit under Notification 22/2003-CE. The department has raised a demand for the entire period, arguing the unit never qualified as an 'STP unit' because IMSC approval was absent. The unit counters that it was registered with STPI and had all operational approvals. Which specific approval authority requirement, as defined in the notification, is determinative of STP-unit status, and can STPI registration substitute for IMSC approval when defending the demand covering ₹4.2 crore of accumulated exempted duty?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (viii) of the Explanation defines 'STP unit' as one established under the scheme notified vide No. 4(RE-95)/92-95 AND approved by the Inter-Ministerial Standing Committee — the chunk makes IMSC approval a conjunctive, non-substitutable condition, which is the precise legal pivot of the department's demand.

questions.q: Precision Castparts India Pvt Ltd, a 100% EOU in Pune (Maharashtra), made DTA clearances of aerospace forgings to Hindustan Aeronautics Ltd (HAL) in Bengaluru during FY 2005-06. HAL's Bengaluru facility at the time was a notified Defence Production SEZ unit. Precision Castparts treated the clearance as a sale into 'Domestic Tariff Area' and paid applicable Central Excise duty under the EOU scheme rules. HAL is now contesting that the transaction was not a 'DTA clearance' at all because the destination was an SEZ unit, and is claiming a refund of the excise duty element embedded in the invoice price, arguing it paid duty on a supply that should have been zero-rated. Which definition in the governing notification determines whether HAL's Bengaluru SEZ facility falls outside the 'Domestic Tariff Area', and does that classification affect the duty liability of the supplying EOU rather than only the recipient?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (ii) of the Explanation defines 'Domestic Tariff Area' expressly as excluding special economic zones — meaning a supply to an SEZ unit is not a DTA clearance, which directly determines whether Precision Castparts' duty payment was correctly made or constitutes excess collection, making this definition the indispensable legal hook for HAL's refund claim.

title: Application for Compounding of Offence

5L 
There may be cases where registered persons might have opted out of the 
composition scheme during the current financial year. Their turnover as per the 
audited Annual Financial Statement would include turnover both as composition 
taxpayer as well as normal taxpayer. Therefore, the turnover for which GST was paid 
under the composition scheme shall be declared here.  
[For FY 2017-18, 2018-19 and 2019-20]156, the registered person shall have an option 
to not fill this table. If there are any adjustments required to be reported then the same 
may be reported in Table 5O.]157

5M 
There may be cases where the taxable value and the invoice value differ due to 
valuation principles under section 15 of the CGST Act, 2017 and rules thereunder. 
Therefore, any difference between the turnover reported in the Annual Return (GSTR

in valuation of supplies shall be declared here. 
[For FY 2017-18, 2018-19 and 2019-20]158, the registered person shall have an option

[For FY 2017-18, 2018-19 and 2019-20]160, the registered person shall have an option 
to not fill this table. If there are any adjustments required to be reported then the same 
may be reported in Table 5O.]161

5O 
Any difference between the turnover reported in the Annual Return (GSTR9) and 
turnover reported in the audited Annual Financial Statement due to reasons not listed 
above shall be declared here.

5Q 
Annual turnover as declared in the Annual Return (GSTR 9) shall be declared here. 
This turnover may be derived from Sr. No. 5N, 10 and 11 of Annual Return (GSTR 
9).

6 
Reasons for non-reconciliation between the annual turnover declared in the audited 
Annual Financial Statement and turnover as declared in the Annual Return (GSTR 9) 
shall be specified here.

7 
The table provides for reconciliation of taxable turnover from the audited annual 
turnover after adjustments with the taxable turnover declared in annual return 
(GSTR-9).

7A 
Annual turnover as derived in Table 5P above would be auto-populated here.

7B 
Value of exempted, nil rated, non-GST and no-supply turnover shall be declared here. 
This shall be reported net of credit notes, debit notes and amendments if any.

7C 
Value of zero rated supplies (including supplies to SEZs) on which tax is not paid 
shall be declared here. This shall be reported net of credit notes, debit notes and 
amendments if any.
