claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers GSTR-9C reconciliation tables (5L through 7C) explaining how taxpayers must reconcile turnover differences between audited financial statements and GSTR-9, including composition scheme transitions, valuation differences under section 15 CGST, foreign exchange fluctuations, and zero-rated/SEZ supplies. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving multi-year reconciliation with composition-to-regular transitions and valuation disputes.

questions.q: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) was under the composition scheme for April 2019 to September 2019, then opted out and registered as a normal taxpayer from October 2019. Their audited financial statement for FY 2019-20 shows total turnover of ₹4.2 crore. They also have ₹18 lakh in export invoices where the INR equivalent shifted due to USD/INR movement between invoice date and payment receipt, and ₹9 lakh in supplies to an SEZ unit in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AABCS5678G1ZY) on which IGST was not charged. When filing GSTR-9C for FY 2019-20, across which reconciliation tables should each of these three turnover components be declared, and does the taxpayer have an option to leave any of these tables blank given the year in question?
questions.why_this_chunk: Tables 5L (composition-to-normal transition turnover split), 5N (forex fluctuation differences), and 7C (zero-rated supplies including SEZ without tax payment) are each explicitly defined in this chunk, including the FY 2019-20 optional-filing relaxation under Notf 79/2020 which is indispensable to answering whether 5L and 5N can be left blank.

questions.q: Aravind Spices LLP (GSTIN 32AACFA9876B1ZM, Kerala) manufactures masalas and supplies to both domestic buyers and a 100% Export Oriented Unit (EOU) in Tamil Nadu. For FY 2018-19, the audited P&L shows revenue of ₹6.8 crore, but GSTR-9 declares ₹6.5 crore. The ₹30 lakh gap arises from: (a) ₹12 lakh difference because the LLP values certain inter-state supplies at cost-plus-10% per their internal policy rather than the transaction value principle under section 15 CGST read with the valuation rules, and (b) ₹18 lakh in supplies to the EOU on which they charged IGST at zero rate. The department is questioning whether these differences were correctly reported or concealed. As their advocate, in which specific GSTR-9C reconciliation tables should these two items have been disclosed, and given that FY 2018-19 falls in the relaxation window under Notification 79/2020, what is the legal consequence of the taxpayer having left those tables blank versus having reported adjustments in Table 5O instead?
questions.why_this_chunk: Table 5M (valuation differences under section 15 CGST), Table 7C (zero-rated EOU supplies without tax), Table 5O (catch-all for unexplained differences), and the Notf 79/2020 optional-filing relaxation for FY 2018-19 are all explicitly defined in this chunk and are indispensable to determining disclosure obligations and the legal defensibility of blank tables vs. 5O reporting.

title: amendments in the No.130/2004-NT-Customs [S.O.1299(E)] dated 24th November, 2004 (export goods)
text:
23rd December, 2004

Notification N.140/2004-NT-Customs

In pursuance of sub-clause (i) of clause (a) of sub-section (3) of section 14 of Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Board
hereby makes the following amendments in the Notification of the Government of India, Ministry of Finance, Department of
Revenue No.130/2004-NT-CUSTOMS [S.O.1299(E)] dated 24 th November, 2004 ( export goods ), namely:-

In the Schedule-I of the said Notification for serial No.2 and the entries relating thereto, the following shall be substituted,
namely:-

S.No.Foreign CurrencyRate of exchange of one unit of foreign currency equivalent to Indian rupees
(1)
(2)
(3)

2.
Canadian Dollar
35.60

The rates will be effective from 23 rd December, 2004.

F.No.468/15/2004-Cus.V

S.P. Rao

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a narrow foreign exchange rate notification amending Schedule-I of Notification 130/2004-NT-Customs, fixing the Canadian Dollar rate at Rs.35.60 for export goods valuation under Section 14(3)(a)(i) of the Customs Act, 1962. It can anchor HIGH-complexity questions involving export invoice valuation disputes where the declared rate differs from the notified rate, especially in multi-currency or cross-border supply chains.

questions.q: Meridian Exports Pvt Ltd, based in Chennai (GSTIN 33AABCM1234F1Z5), shipped a consignment of processed granite tiles to a buyer in Ontario, Canada on 23rd December 2004. The export invoice was denominated in Canadian Dollars at CAD 18,500. The shipping bill was filed the same day. The exporter's bank converted the invoice at an interbank rate of Rs.36.10 per CAD, and Meridian declared this rate in the shipping bill. The assessing officer at Chennai Customs is proposing to revalue the shipment using a different rate, contending that the declared rate is inconsistent with the rate notified by the Board for that date. Meridian's consultant argues that the bank rate should prevail for determining transaction value. Which rate governs for the purpose of converting the export value into Indian rupees for this shipment, and does the Board-notified rate for 23rd December 2004 override the actual bank conversion rate used by the exporter?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk fixes the Board-notified Canadian Dollar exchange rate at Rs.35.60 per unit effective 23rd December 2004 under Section 14(3)(a)(i) of the Customs Act — directly determining which rate governs the customs valuation of this export shipment on that specific date.

questions.q: Coastal Minerals LLP, an EOU registered in Vishakhapatnam, exported iron ore fines to a Canadian importer under a contract priced in CAD on 23rd December 2004. The consignment was also partly funded by an advance received in USD, and the LLP's accountant blended the two currency receipts to arrive at a composite rupee realization figure for export proceeds reporting under FEMA. The Customs officer at Vizag port is computing the assessable value of the export for the purposes of drawback entitlement and wants to know whether the exchange rate for Canadian Dollars notified under the Customs Act for that date should be used for the CAD-denominated portion, or whether the blended FEMA-rate should apply. Advise on the correct valuation methodology, particularly whether the Customs-notified rate for CAD on 23rd December 2004 is binding for export drawback computation or whether the RBI/FEMA rate can be substituted.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk provides the specific Board-notified rate for Canadian Dollar (Rs.35.60) effective 23rd December 2004 for export goods under the Customs Act, which is indispensable to determining whether this rate binds the drawback valuation or whether a FEMA blended rate may substitute.

title: Order For re-credit of the amount to electronic credit ledger
text:
UNDERTAKING

I hereby undertake to deposit to the Government the amount of refund sanctioned along with 
interest in case of non-receipt of foreign exchange remittances as per the proviso to section 
16 of the IGST Act, 201s7 read with rule 96B of the CGST Rules 2017. 
 
Signature-  
Name –

Designation / Status

]180

UNDERTAKING

I hereby undertake to pay back to the Government the amount of refund sanctioned along 
with interest in case it is found subsequently that the requirements of clause (c) of sub-
section (2) of section 16 read with sub-section (2) of section 42 of the CGST/SGST Act have 
not been complied with in respect of the amount refunded.   
 
Signature  
Name –

Designation / Status

SELF- DECLARATION [rule 89(2)(l)]

I ____________________ (Applicant) having GSTIN/ temporary Id -------, solemnly 
affirm and certify that in respect of the refund amounting to Rs. ---/ with respect to 
the tax, interest, or any other amount for the period from---to----, claimed in the 
refund application, the incidence of such tax and interest has not been passed on to 
any other person.  
 
Signature 
Name –

Designation / Status

(This Declaration is not required to be furnished by applicants, who are claiming refund 
under clause (a) or clause (b) or clause (c) or clause (d) or clause (f) of sub-section (8) of 
section 54.)

180Inserted vide Notf no. 16/2020-CT  dt. 23.03.2020

256
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the statutory undertakings and self-declaration required for GST refund applications — specifically the foreign-exchange-remittance undertaking under IGST s.16 proviso + Rule 96B, the ITC reversal undertaking under CGST/SGST s.42(2), and the unjust-enrichment self-declaration under Rule 89(2)(l). These are indispensable to export refund claims and create HIGH-complexity scenarios when multiple conditions, entities, and cross-act provisions interact.

questions.q: Nexus Pharma Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) shipped a consignment of API intermediates to a buyer in Germany on 20-Jan-2025 under a Letter of Undertaking and claimed IGST refund of ₹38 lakhs in GSTR-RFD-01 for the period Oct–Dec 2024. The foreign buyer subsequently restructured the payment: 60% was received in USD within 90 days, but the remaining 40% (≈₹15.2 lakhs) was to be paid via a barter offset against a counter-import of German machinery, with no actual foreign-exchange remittance expected for that portion. The jurisdictional refund officer is asking whether the undertaking submitted at the time of refund sanction creates a mandatory obligation to deposit the ₹15.2 lakh portion along with interest, and if so, from which date interest runs. Additionally, the company's CA argues that since the barter offset is a commercially recognised settlement, Rule 96B should not apply. Advise on the legal exposure and what the undertaking text itself commits the exporter to.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the exact text of the foreign-exchange-remittance undertaking ('I hereby undertake to deposit to the Government the amount of refund sanctioned along with interest in case of non-receipt of foreign exchange remittances as per the proviso to section 16 of the IGST Act read with rule 96B of the CGST Rules 2017') — the legal obligation and its trigger condition are defined verbatim here, making this chunk indispensable to determine whether a barter offset qualifies as 'receipt of foreign exchange' and what the undertaking commits the exporter to.

questions.q: Kiran Textiles LLP (GSTIN 24AABFK5678G1ZQ, Gujarat) filed a refund claim of ₹22 lakhs for zero-rated supply of embroidered fabrics to a SEZ unit in Kandla for the quarter Jul–Sep 2024. The LLP furnished the Rule 89(2)(l) self-declaration stating that the incidence of tax has not been passed on to any other person. Post-sanction, the GST audit wing found that the LLP had invoiced the SEZ unit at a price inclusive of GST and the SEZ unit had in turn claimed the amount as part of its input costs in its books — effectively passing on the tax burden. The department now seeks recovery of the ₹22 lakh refund plus interest on the ground that the self-declaration was false. The LLP's counsel argues (a) that the self-declaration requirement under Rule 89(2)(l) does not apply to supplies to SEZ units since such supplies fall under clause (a) of s.54(8) of CGST Act, and therefore the declaration was given voluntarily and cannot create a legal estoppel; and (b) that even if the incidence was passed on commercially, the legal incidence of GST was always on the supplier, not the SEZ unit. Analyse the validity of both arguments with reference to the specific carve-out in the declaration form and the cross-act provisions involved.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the precise text of the Rule 89(2)(l) self-declaration and, critically, the parenthetical carve-out that explicitly states 'This Declaration is not required to be furnished by applicants claiming refund under clause (a) or (b) or (c) or (d) or (f) of sub-section (8) of section 54' — the entire first legal argument in the question turns on whether SEZ supplies under s.54(8)(a) are covered by this carve-out, which is answerable only by reading this specific declaration text.

title: Admissibility of Duty Drawback in Respect of Supplies Effected by DTA Units to Special Economic Zones - reg.
text:
and processed in the Customs Section of
the SEZ. The jurisdictional Commissioner of Customs in consultation with the Pay & Accounts Officer shall make arrangements
for issue of authorisation and drawback cheque books (wherever EDI facilities are not available for directly crediting the said
amount to the Bank Accounts of the exporters) with immediate effect. It should be ensured that the claims are processed and
amounts disbursed within the time limit prescribed by CBEC. In case of EDI Ports, the drawback amounts should be
sanctioned within 3 working days and in case of such locations where the Shipping Bills are manually processed, drawback
amount should be sanctioned within 5 working days.

4.Suitable Public Notices for information of the trade and Standing Orders for guidance of the staff may kindly be issued
accordingly. Difficulties faced, if any, in implementation of this Circular may be brought to the notice of the Board.

5. Receipt of this Circular may kindly be acknowledged.

Yours faithfully,

Sd/-

(P.K. Mohanty)

Joint Secretary to the Government of India

Telefax No.23341079
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the procedural and timeline mandates for drawback disbursement on DTA-to-SEZ supplies — specifically the 3-working-day EDI vs 5-working-day manual processing deadlines, the role of the jurisdictional Commissioner of Customs and Pay & Accounts Officer, and the cheque-book/EDI credit mechanism. These specifics anchor high-complexity queries about delayed drawback, inter-agency coordination failures, and multi-party supply chains involving DTA exporters and SEZ developers.

questions.q: Aravali Fabrics Pvt Ltd, a DTA unit in Bhiwandi (Maharashtra), supplied 12,000 metres of technical textile to Mundra Special Economic Zone unit of Adani Ports SEZ Ltd in April 2025, under a shipping bill processed manually at the Nhava Sheva Customs House. The drawback claim was filed on 5-Apr-2025. As of 18-Apr-2025 — 13 calendar days later — no disbursement has been made. The exporter's bank account is registered on ICEGATE and EDI credit should theoretically be available, but the jurisdictional Deputy Commissioner says the Shipping Bill was treated as 'manual' because the SEZ customs section processed the document offline. The Pay & Accounts Officer disputes whether his office is even required to issue a cheque book for this category of claim. Can Aravali Fabrics invoke the prescribed processing deadline against the department, and which authority — the Commissioner of Customs or the PAO — bears the statutory obligation to ensure disbursement? Does the EDI vs manual classification at the SEZ customs section override the ICEGATE registration of the exporter for timeline purposes?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies the exact 3-working-day (EDI) vs 5-working-day (manual) deadlines, assigns joint responsibility to the jurisdictional Commissioner and the Pay & Accounts Officer, and mandates cheque-book issuance where EDI credit is unavailable — all of which are directly contested in this scenario.

questions.q: Precision Alloys LLP, a DTA manufacturer in Coimbatore (Tamil Nadu), entered into a back-to-back arrangement in March 2025 where it supplies copper rods to two SEZ units simultaneously: one unit of TIDEL Park SEZ Chennai (processed through EDI at Chennai Customs) and another unit of Cochin SEZ (shipping bills manually processed at Cochin Customs). Both shipping bills were filed on the same date — 10-Mar-2025. The Chennai claim was credited to the LLP's bank account on 14-Mar-2025 (4 calendar days, 3 working days). The Cochin claim remained unpaid as of 20-Mar-2025 (10 calendar days). The LLP's consultant argues that since the CBEC-prescribed limit was breached for the Cochin claim, the LLP is entitled to interest under the Customs Act and should also write to the Board. The Cochin Customs jurisdictional Commissioner contends that the 5-working-day clock started only after the SEZ customs section completed processing, not from the date of filing. Who is correct on when the clock starts, and is there a basis for the LLP to claim interest or escalate to the Board under this circular's framework?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly mandates 5-working-day disbursement for manually processed shipping bills, requires the Commissioner to make arrangements for processing, and instructs that difficulties in implementation be reported to the Board — all of which are indispensable to resolving the dispute about clock-start timing and escalation rights.

title: Eleventh Amendment to CGST Rules, 2017
text:
Provided further that the information in Table 6A furnished under the first proviso 
shall be auto-drafted in FORM GSTR-1 for the said tax period.”;

(iv) 
in rule 96A, in sub-rule (2), the following provisos shall be inserted, namely:-

“Provided that where the date for furnishing the details of outward supplies in FORM 
GSTR-1 for a tax period has been extended in exercise of the powers conferred under 
section 37 of the Act, the supplier shall furnish the information relating to exports as 
specified in Table 6A of FORM GSTR-1 after the return in FORM GSTR-3B has 
been furnished and the same shall be transmitted electronically by the common portal 
to the system designated by the Customs: 
 
Provided further that the information in Table 6A furnished under the first proviso 
shall be auto-drafted in FORM GSTR-1 for the said tax period.”

[F. No. 349/58/2017-GST (Pt. II)]

(Dr.Sreeparvathy S.L.)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note:- The principal rules were published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, 
Section 3, sub-section (i) vide notification No. 3/2017-Central Tax, dated the 19th June, 2017, 
published vide number G.S.R 610 (E), dated the 19th June, 2017 and last amended vide 
notification No. 47/2017-Central Tax, dated the 18th October, 2017, published vide number 
G.S.R 1304 (E), dated the 18th October, 2017.
claude_reasoning: This chunk governs the sequencing of export data filing under rule 96A(2) when GSTR-1 deadlines are extended — specifically that Table 6A export details must be filed after GSTR-3B (not before) and auto-draft into GSTR-1, which is indispensable for exporters claiming LUT-based refunds or IGST refund transmission to Customs during extended filing periods.

questions.q: Nexus Pharma Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1Z5, Mumbai) filed their GSTR-3B for March 2025 on 22-Apr-2025. Their GSTR-1 due date for March 2025 had been extended by a CBIC notification to 30-Apr-2025. The company's CHA submitted the shipping bills for 14 export consignments (totalling ₹4.8 crore of zero-rated API exports under LUT) before GSTR-1 was filed. The Customs EDI system has not received the GST export data, and the IGST refund for ₹42 lakh is stuck. The company's CFO is arguing that Table 6A data should have been pushed to Customs automatically once GSTR-3B was filed. Is that position correct? What is the precise sequencing obligation on Nexus Pharma, and what should they do now to unblock the Customs transmission?
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso in rule 96A(2) inserted by this notification explicitly states that when GSTR-1 due date is extended, the supplier must furnish Table 6A *after* GSTR-3B is filed, and only then does the portal transmit to Customs — this exact sequencing rule is the crux of answering whether the CFO's position is correct.

questions.q: Clearview Textiles LLP (GSTIN 24AABCC5678G1ZQ, Surat) exports polyester fabric to a buyer in Bangladesh. For the quarter Oct–Dec 2024, CBIC extended the GSTR-1 filing deadline. Clearview filed GSTR-3B on 20-Jan-2025 but mistakenly uploaded Table 6A export data to FORM GSTR-1 on 15-Jan-2025 — i.e., before GSTR-3B was filed — thinking an early filing would speed up the Customs refund. Customs has rejected the transmission saying the data arrived out of sequence. The LLP's tax counsel now wants to know: (a) whether the Table 6A data already uploaded to GSTR-1 before GSTR-3B is legally valid or void, (b) whether the auto-draft mechanism would have covered this data anyway, and (c) what corrective steps are available to ensure the ₹18 lakh IGST paid on these exports is refunded?
questions.why_this_chunk: The second proviso — that Table 6A furnished under the first proviso (i.e., post-GSTR-3B) shall be auto-drafted into GSTR-1 — directly determines whether Clearview's out-of-sequence Table 6A upload triggers the auto-draft and Customs transmission, making this chunk the authoritative source for parts (a) and (b) of the question.

title: Anti-dumping duty on Poly-Iso-Butylene
text:
31st  October, 2002

Notification No. 123/2002-Customs

WHEREAS in the matter of import of Poly-Iso-Butylene falling under sub-heading 3902.20 of the First Schedule to the Customs
Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), originating in, or exported from, European Union, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea,
Singapore and Thailand, the designated authority in its preliminary findings vide notification No.47/1/2001-DGAD, dated the
12th December, 2001, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 12th December, 2001, had
come to the conclusion that -

(a)Poly-Iso-Butylene originating in, or exported from, European Union, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea, Singapore

and Thailand , had been exported to India below normal value, resulting in dumping;

(b)the Indian industry had suffered material injury only from exports of Poly-Iso-Butylene from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic

of Korea and Singapore;

(c)injury had been caused cumulatively by the imports from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and Singapore;

AND WHEREAS on the basis of the aforesaid findings of the designated authority, the Central Government had imposed an
anti-dumping duty on Poly-Iso-Butylene vide notification of the Government of India in the erstwhile Ministry of Finance
(Department of Revenue), No. 7/2002-Customs, dated the 16th January, 2002, [G.S.R. 32(E), dated the 16th January, 2002],
published in Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, dated the 16th January, 2002;

AND WHEREAS the designated authority, vide its final findings notification No.47/1/2001-DGAD, dated the 5th September,
2002, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 9th September, 2002 has come to the
conclusion that -

(a)Poly-Iso-Butylene originating in, or exported from, European Union, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea, Singapore

and Thailand, have been exported to India below normal value, resulting in dumping;

(b)the Indian industry has suffered material injury on account of price under-cutting, price suppression and significant increase

in the volume of dumped imports of Poly-Iso-Butylene from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and Singapore;

(c)the injury has been caused cumulatively by the dumped imports from Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and

Singapore;

and has recommended the imposition of definitive anti-dumping duty on all imports of  the said Poly-Iso-Butylene originating in,
or exported from, Brazil, Japan, People's Republic of Korea and Singapore;

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1), read with sub-section (5) of section 9A of the said
Customs Tariff Act, and rules 18 and 20 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty
on Dumped Articles and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995, the Central Government, on the basis of the aforesaid final
findings of the designated authority, hereby imposes on all imports of Poly-Iso-Butylene, falling under sub-heading 3902.20 of
the First Schedule to the said Customs Tariff Act, originating in, or exported from, the country specified in column (2) of the
Table below, when exported by the exporter or producer specified in the corresponding entry in column (3) of the said Table,
and imported into India, an anti-dumping duty at a rate which is equivalent to the difference between the amount specified in the
corresponding entry in column (4) of the said Table  and  the landed value, in US $ per metric Tonne, of such imported Poly-Iso-
Butylene.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the definitive anti-dumping duty imposed on Poly-Iso-Butylene (HS 3902.20) imported from Brazil, Japan, South Korea, and Singapore — critically distinguishing countries subject to duty (these four) from those where dumping was found but injury was NOT attributed (EU, Thailand). It supports HIGH-complexity questions because practitioners must navigate country-of-origin vs country-of-export distinctions, the preliminary vs definitive duty transition, and the interaction between the anti-dumping notification and the parent Customs Tariff Act provisions.

questions.q: Kiran Polymers Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, GSTIN 27AABCK1234F1ZX) is importing a consignment of Poly-Iso-Butylene (PIB) classified under HS 3902.20 from a Singapore-based trader, Chem-Asia Pte Ltd, but the goods were actually manufactured in Thailand and merely transshipped through Singapore. The import invoice is dated 18-March-2025 and the CIF value is USD 4,20,000. Our client wants to know: (a) whether anti-dumping duty under the definitive notification applies given that the country of origin is Thailand but the exporter is Singapore-based, (b) how the 'originating in, or exported from' language in the notification affects the duty liability when origin and export country differ, and (c) whether the earlier provisional anti-dumping duty paid under the January 2002 notification can be credited against the definitive duty now being assessed by the Custom House, Mumbai?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's operative language 'originating in, or exported from' is the precise legal text that determines whether Thailand-origin goods exported via Singapore attract duty — the chunk confirms Singapore IS a subject country but Thailand is NOT among the four injury-causing countries, making the origin/export distinction legally dispositive. The chunk also establishes the link between the provisional duty (No. 7/2002-Customs dated 16-Jan-2002) and the definitive notification, which is essential to the credit question.

questions.q: Anand Petrochemicals LLP, a manufacturer in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AAHFA5678M1ZQ), imports Poly-Iso-Butylene from two sources simultaneously: (i) 50 MT from a Korean producer, Daejin Chemical Co. Ltd, under a long-term supply agreement signed in 2001 when only preliminary anti-dumping findings existed, and (ii) 30 MT from a Brazilian trader, Petro-Sul Comercio Ltda, routed via a UAE free-trade-zone warehouse. Both consignments arrive at Mundra Port on the same date in October 2002, just after this definitive notification. The client is disputing the anti-dumping duty assessment on the following grounds: (a) the Korean supply agreement pre-dates the definitive finding and should be governed by the preliminary duty rate, (b) the Brazilian goods having entered a UAE FTZ should be treated as 'exported from UAE' rather than Brazil. Advise on the tenability of both contentions and identify which specific legal provisions govern how the designated authority's final findings interact with pre-existing contractual arrangements.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is indispensable because it explicitly records the transition from preliminary findings (DGAD notification 47/1/2001-DGAD dated 12-Dec-2001 → provisional duty 7/2002-Customs dated 16-Jan-2002) to definitive findings (DGAD final findings dated 5-Sep-2002 → this notification), which is the legal basis for assessing whether pre-existing contracts can claim the earlier rate. The 'originating in, or exported from' language is again critical to the UAE FTZ contention for Brazilian goods.

title: Exemption to goods meant for 100%EOUs.
text:
emises :

Provided the waste or scrap or remnants generated during such processes at the jobworker"s premises is either returned to
the unit or is cleared on payment of duty as if the said waste or scrap of remnants have been cleared by the said unit;

(b)   Omitted

1. permit to remove moulds/jigs, tools, fixtures, tackles/instruments, hangers and patterns or drawings from the

premises of the user industry to the premises of sub-contractors subject to the condition that the goods so removed
shall be brought back to the premises of such user industry on completion of the job work, within a period fixed by
the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or Deputy Commissionor of Central Excise.

(d) permit the goods manufactured, produced or packaged to be supplied or transferred from user-industry to another unit in a
free trade zone or special economic zone or a hundred per cent export oriented undertaking or an Electronic Hardware
Technology Park (EHTP) or Software Technology Parks(STP) unit, as the case may be, for any of the purposes as specified in
clauses (a) to (c) of the first paragraph or for export;

(e) permit the capital goods to be supplied or transferred from user-industry to another unit in the free trade zone or special
economic zone or a hundred per cent export oriented undertaking or an Electronic Hardware Technology Park (EHTP) or
Software Technology Parks (STP) unit, as the case may be, for use within the unit.";

3.    Subject to the satisfaction of the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise,
duty shall not be leviable in respect of -

(a)    the said goods used for imparting training (including commercial training) subject to the condition that the user industry
imparting such training had achieved net foreign exchange earnings as percentage of exports prescribed in the Export-Import
Policy and that no computer terminal shall be installed outside the premises of such user industry for this purpose;

(b)    the said goods or goods manufactured, produced, processed or packaged in the user industry, if such goods are
destroyed within the user industry in the presence of the Central Excise Officer;
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Central Excise exemption conditions for 100% EOUs — specifically: (i) the job-work/sub-contractor regime including waste/scrap disposal obligations, (ii) removal and return of moulds/jigs/tools to sub-contractors under AC/DC-fixed timelines, (iii) inter-unit supply/transfer of manufactured goods and capital goods to SEZ/FTZ/EHTP/STP units, and (iv) duty-free destruction and training-use carve-outs. These provisions are granular enough to anchor complex client scenarios.

questions.q: Precision Castings (India) Pvt Ltd, a 100% EOU in Coimbatore (Tamil Nadu), sent specialised titanium dies and jigs worth ₹38 lakhs to M/s Venkateswara Forgings, a domestic sub-contractor in Hosur, for job-work on aero-component housings under an order dated 10-January-2025. The AC of Central Excise had fixed a 90-day return period. Due to a labour dispute at Venkateswara's plant, the dies were returned on day 112. Additionally, metal scrap worth ₹4.2 lakhs was generated at Venkateswara's premises during the job; Venkateswara neither returned the scrap nor paid duty on it, claiming it was Precision Castings' property and therefore covered by the EOU exemption. The Range Superintendent has now issued a show-cause notice demanding duty on both the overdue dies (treating removal as permanent) and the un-cleared scrap. (a) Is the demand on the dies tenable solely on account of the 22-day delay beyond the AC-fixed period, and what is Precision Castings' best defence? (b) Who bears the duty liability on the scrap generated at the job-worker's premises — the EOU or the sub-contractor — and under what precise condition can that liability be extinguished?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause (b) under the job-work proviso explicitly states that waste/scrap at the job-worker's premises must either be returned to the unit OR cleared on payment of duty 'as if cleared by the said unit'; and clause (d) fixes the return-of-moulds/tools obligation 'within a period fixed by the AC/DC' — both conditions are directly at issue in the SCN scenario.

questions.q: Luminos Optics Pvt Ltd is a 100% EOU in Pune (Maharashtra) manufacturing precision optical lenses. It has a sister concern, Luminos SEZ Unit, co-located in the Seepz SEZ, Mumbai. Luminos Optics wants to (i) transfer a CNC grinding machine (capital goods, original value ₹1.2 crore, duty-free imported) to the SEZ unit for permanent use in lens polishing, and (ii) supply 8,000 semi-finished lens blanks (goods manufactured in the EOU) to the SEZ unit for further processing and eventual export. Simultaneously, Luminos Optics is setting up a commercial training centre for 35 trainees on optical fabrication, with two computer terminals proposed to be installed at a client's premises in Andheri for remote supervision of trainees. The company's NFE as a percentage of exports for the last financial year stands at 24%, against the EXIM Policy-prescribed threshold of 30%. A consultant has advised that all three activities — capital goods transfer, goods supply to SEZ, and training use — are duty-free under the EOU exemption notification. Critically evaluate this advice, identifying which of the three activities qualifies for duty exemption, which does not and why, and what specific remedial steps (if any) can regularise the non-qualifying activity.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause (e) permits capital goods transfer to an SEZ unit; clause (d) permits supply of manufactured goods to an SEZ unit; and clause 3(a) conditions training-use exemption on (i) NFE meeting the EXIM Policy threshold AND (ii) no terminal installed outside the user-industry's premises — both conditions are violated here, making this chunk the indispensable authority for evaluating all three activities.

title: Regarding allotment of Warehouse Code for Customs Bonded Warehouses
text:
5. 
It is proposed to publish the unique warehouse code so generated on the

ICEGATE website for the information of trade. This activity will start from the 
10thJune 2016 and shall be completed by the 15th June 2016. From 20thJune 
2016,declaring the Warehousing Code in the Bill of Entry would become mandatory 
for filing Into-Bond and Ex-Bond Bill of Entry. As Ex-Bond Bill of Entries with invalid 
warehousing code will be rejected, due care may be taken for smooth transition so 
as to avoid any hardships to the trade.

6. 
Difficulties relating to I.T. system, if any, may be brought to the notice of

National System Manager, ICES (nsm.ices@icegate.gov.in).

7. 
Hindi version follows.

(Temsunaro Jamir)

Under Secretary to the Govt. of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the mandatory warehousing code requirement for Bill of Entry filing under Customs Bonded Warehouse procedures, specifically the June 2016 transition timeline and the rejection consequence for invalid codes in Ex-Bond Bills of Entry.

questions.q: Our client, Vega Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCV1234F1Z5, Mumbai), operates a customs bonded warehouse and had imported a consignment of specialty resins from Germany in May 2016. The goods were warehoused under an Into-Bond Bill of Entry filed before the ICEGATE warehousing code system went live. When their CHA attempted to file an Ex-Bond Bill of Entry on 22nd June 2016 to clear goods worth ₹1.4 crore for their Pune manufacturing unit, the system rejected the Ex-Bond entry citing an invalid warehousing code. The warehouse operator claims they never received the unique code from ICEGATE. Who bears responsibility for the rejection — the importer, the warehouse licensee, or the customs system — and what is the prescribed remedy or escalation path to avoid demurrage and interest liability under the bond?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that from 20th June 2016 Ex-Bond Bills of Entry with invalid warehousing codes will be rejected, and directs IT difficulties to nsm.ices@icegate.gov.in — both facts are indispensable to determining the consequence and the escalation mechanism.

questions.q: Arjun Exports LLP, an EOU unit in Chennai, had goods lying in a third-party customs bonded warehouse (licensed under Section 57/58 of the Customs Act) as of early June 2016. The warehouse operator published its ICEGATE-assigned unique warehouse code on 14th June 2016 on a notice board but did not formally communicate it to all depositors. Arjun Exports LLP's clearing agent filed an Ex-Bond Bill of Entry on 21st June 2016 using the old internal reference number instead of the new ICEGATE warehouse code, and the entry was rejected. The goods are temperature-sensitive pharma intermediates and every day of delay causes product degradation. Does the mandatory warehousing code requirement apply equally to EOU-linked bonded warehouses, and what is the fastest permissible procedural route — including any ICEGATE system complaint mechanism — to get the Ex-Bond entry accepted without re-filing from scratch?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's statement that the mandatory code requirement starts 20th June 2016 for all Ex-Bond filings, combined with the IT escalation contact (nsm.ices@icegate.gov.in) and the transition timeline (codes published 10th–15th June), are the specific facts needed to assess whether the filing was timely, who erred, and what the remedy is.

title: Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017
text:
1 [(b) issues invoice or bill without supply of goods or services 2 [or both] in violation of the provisions of this Act, or the rules
made thereunder; or

(c) violates the provisions of section 171 of the Act or the rules made thereunder].

3 [(d) violates the provision of rule 10A ]

4 [(e) avails input tax credit in violation of the provisions of section 16 of the Act or the rules made thereunder; or

(f) furnishes the details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1 under section 37 for one or more tax periods which is in excess of
the outward supplies declared by him in his valid return under section 39 for the said tax periods; or

(g) violates the provision of rule 86B .]

5 [(h) being a registered person required to file return under subsection (1) of section 39 for each month or part thereof, has not
furnished returns for a continuous period of six months;

(i) being a registered person required to file return under proviso to subsection (1) of section 39 for each quarter or part thereof,
has not furnished returns for a continuous period of two tax periods.]

1. Substituted (w.e.f. 22.06.2017) vide Notification No. 07/2017 -CT dated 27.06.2017 for "(b) issues invoice or bill without supply of goods or services in violation
of the provisions of this Act, or the rules made thereunder."

2. Inserted vide Notification No.94/2020 - CT dated 22.12.2020.

3. Inserted vide Notification No.31/2019 - CT dated 28.06.2019.

4. Inserted vide Notification No.94/2020 - CT dated 22.12.2020.

5. Inserted (w.e.f. 01.10.2022) vide Notification No. 19/2022 - CT dated 28.09.2022.

1 [ Rule 21A. Suspension of registration. -

(1) Where a registered person has applied for cancellation of registration under rule 20 , the registration shall be deemed to be
suspended from the date of submission of the application or the date from which the cancellation is sought, whichever is later,
pending the completion of proceedings for cancellation of registration under rule 22 .

(2) Where the proper officer has reasons to believe that the registration of a person is liable to be cancelled under section 29 or
under rule 21 , he may, 2 [****] suspend the registration of such person with effect from a date to be determined by him, pending
the completion of the proceedings for cancellation of registration under rule 22 .

3 [(2A) Where, a comparison of the returns furnished by a registered person under section 39 with

(a) the details of outward supplies furnished in FORM GSTR-1 ; or

(b) the details of inward supplies derived based on the details of outward supplies furnished by his suppliers in their FORM
GSTR-1 ,

or such other analysis, as may be carried out on the recommendations of the Council, show that there are significant
differences or anomalies indicating contravention of the provisions of the Act or the rules made thereunder, leading to
cancellation of registration of the said person, his registration shall be suspended and the said person shall be intimated in
FORM GST REG-31 , electronically, on the common portal, or by sending a communication to his e-mail address provided
at the time of registration or as amended from time to time, highlighting the said differences and anomalies and asking him
to explain, within a period of thirty days, as to why his registration shall not be cancelled.]

(3) A registered person, whose registration has been suspended under sub-rule (1) or sub-rule (2) 3 [or sub-rule (2A)], shall not
make any taxable supply during the period of suspension and shall not be required to furnish any return under section 39 .
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Rule 21A (suspension of registration) and the grounds for cancellation under Rule 21, including fake invoicing, ITC violations, GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B mismatches, Rule 86B violations, and non-filing — all of which are highly litigated practitioner issues involving multi-provision interplay.

questions.q: Precision Alloys LLP (GSTIN 27AABFP1234C1ZX, Maharashtra) has been regularly filing GSTR-1 showing outward supplies of ₹4.2 crore per month, but its GSTR-3B for the same periods consistently declares only ₹1.8–2.1 crore. The GST department has now issued a communication in FORM GST REG-31 asking the LLP to explain within 30 days why its registration should not be cancelled. Simultaneously, the LLP's CFO informs us that they have several pending B2B supply contracts worth ₹90 lakh to be invoiced in the next fortnight. Can the LLP legally issue these tax invoices and collect GST during the 30-day explanation window, or does the suspension triggered by the REG-31 notice bar all taxable supplies — and what are the legal consequences if they proceed anyway?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 21A(2A) specifies that a mismatch between GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B triggering REG-31 suspends registration, while Rule 21A(3) explicitly bars taxable supply during suspension — both sub-rules are indispensable to answering whether the LLP can invoice during the 30-day window.

questions.q: Sunrise Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AAECS5678K1ZP, Karnataka) filed an application for voluntary cancellation of its GST registration effective 31-March-2025, citing business restructuring. However, before the cancellation proceedings under rule 22 were completed, the proper officer independently discovered that the company had been availing ITC aggregating ₹78 lakh on invoices from two suppliers whose own registrations were retrospectively cancelled — a violation of section 16 conditions — and has now separately initiated cancellation proceedings on that ground, also suspending the registration. The company argues that since the registration is already 'deemed suspended' from the date of its voluntary cancellation application, the officer cannot impose a second, independently-grounded suspension. As counsel, advise on: (a) whether the deemed suspension under sub-rule (1) and the officer-initiated suspension under sub-rule (2) can coexist or one subsumes the other; (b) whether the ITC reversal demand can be raised during the suspension period; and (c) what the effective date of suspension is for each limb.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains both Rule 21A(1) (deemed suspension from date of voluntary cancellation application) and Rule 21A(2) (officer-initiated suspension for rule 21 grounds including section 16 ITC violations), and their interaction — plus Rule 21(e) on ITC violations — is the precise legal issue; no generic chunk addresses the coexistence of dual suspension grounds.

title: Central Excise Act, 1944
text:

opinion of the majority of the Judges who have heard the case including those who first heard it.

(9) Save as otherwise provided in this Act, the provisions of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908), relating to appeals
to the High Court shall, as far as may be, apply in the case of appeals under this section.]

1. Substituted (w.e.f. 14-5-2003) by s. 144 of the Finance Act, 2003 (32 of 2003).

2. Substituted (w.e.f. 6-8-2014) words "Commissioner of Central Excise" by s. 95 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2014 (25 of 2014).

3. Substituted (w.e.f. 6-8-2014) words "Commissioner of Central Excise" by s. 95 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2014 (25 of 2014).

4. Inserted (w.e.f. 1-7-2003) by s. 108 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2009 (33 of 2009).

Section 1 [35H. Application to High Court. -

(1) The 2 [Principal Commissioner of Central Excise or Commissioner of Central Excise] or the other party may, within one
hundred and eighty days of the date upon which he is served with notice of an order under section 35C passed 3 [before the1st
day of July, 2003] (not being an order relating, among other things, to the determination of any question having a relation to the
rate of duty of excise or to the value of goods for purposes of assessment), by application in the prescribed form, accompanied,
where the application is made by the other party, by a fee of two hundred rupees, apply to the High Court to direct the Appellate
Tribunal to refer to the High Court any question of law arising from such order of the Tribunal.

(2) The 4 [Principal Commissioner of Central Excise or Commissioner of Central Excise] or the other party applying to the High
Court under sub-section (1) shall clearly state the question of law which he seeks to be referred to the High Court and shall also
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the procedural mechanism under Section 35H of the Central Excise Act 1944 for filing a reference application to the High Court against CESTAT orders passed before 1-Jul-2003, including the 180-day limitation, fee, exclusion of rate/valuation questions, and applicability of CPC appeal provisions. It can anchor HIGH-complexity questions involving appellate strategy, limitation periods, and cross-act procedural interplay.

questions.q: Varun Steels Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, Maharashtra) received a CESTAT order dated 15-Mar-2003 confirming a demand of ₹2.8 crore on classification of their hot-rolled coils under Central Excise. The order was served on 20-Mar-2003. The company's advocate now wants to challenge a pure question of law — whether the Tribunal correctly applied the relevant exemption notification — before the High Court, but the Revenue also intends to file a cross-reference on the valuation methodology adopted by the Tribunal. The in-house counsel asks: (a) what is the last date by which Varun Steels must file its reference application, (b) can Revenue's valuation ground be entertained by the High Court in a reference application, and (c) what procedural rules govern the High Court's hearing once the reference is admitted?
questions.why_this_chunk: Section 35H(1) fixes the 180-day limitation running from date of service of the CESTAT order (giving the cut-off date calculation), expressly excludes questions relating to rate of duty or value of goods from the reference jurisdiction (answering Revenue's valuation ground), and sub-section (9) applies CPC High Court appeal provisions to the reference proceedings — all three answers are indispensable and come exclusively from this chunk.

questions.q: Precision Forgings LLP (Pune, Maharashtra) lost before CESTAT in an order dated 10-Feb-2003 (served 28-Feb-2003) on two issues: (i) whether a particular job-work activity amounted to 'manufacture' under Central Excise, and (ii) the assessable value of captive-consumed intermediates. They want to file a reference application to the Bombay High Court on both issues simultaneously. Their advocate needs to know: which of the two issues is legally maintainable as a reference question, what fee is payable given that Precision Forgings is the applicant (not the Revenue), and whether the 180-day window has already closed as of today (22-Apr-2026)?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Section 35H(1) is the sole authority for: (a) the express carve-out barring reference on 'determination of any question having a relation to the value of goods for purposes of assessment' (eliminating the valuation issue), (b) the ₹200 fee applicable when the 'other party' — i.e., the assessee — files the application, and (c) the 180-day period from date of service (28-Feb-2003 + 180 days = 27-Aug-2003), making the window long closed — facts answerable only with this chunk's specific provisions.

title: Guidelines for verifying the Transitional Credit in light of the order of the Hon’ble Supreme Court in the Union of India vs. Filco Trade Centre Pvt. Ltd., SLP(C) No. 32709-32710/2018, order dated 22.07.2022 & 02.09.2022
text:
has component of central tax also.  
 
5.3.3 The jurisdictional tax officer shall, in parallel, continue the verification of the remaining 
portion of the transitional credit at his end. 
 
5.3.4 The jurisdictional tax officer and the counterpart tax officer shall verify the transitional credit 
claimed under the CGST or the SGST head, as the case may be, by referring to the guidelines 
detailed in Annexure I to this circular for transitional credit pertaining to central tax and the 
guidelines issued by the relevant state/UT for verification of transitional credit pertaining to the State 
Tax/Union territory Tax, as applicable. While conducting the verification, the officer must also check

Page 3 of 14
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the procedural mechanics of transitional credit (Tran-1/Tran-2) verification under Circular 182/14/2022, specifically the parallel verification protocol between jurisdictional and counterpart tax officers for CGST/SGST heads following the Supreme Court's Filco Trade Centre order. It supports HIGH-complexity questions because it governs a cross-authority, dual-officer verification process with distinct central/state tax components that a practitioner must navigate post-SC order.

questions.q: Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) had migrated to GST in July 2017 with transitional credit of ₹48 lakhs — ₹31 lakhs attributable to central excise duties and ₹17 lakhs to Maharashtra VAT. After the Supreme Court's order in the Filco matter reopened the Tran-1 filing window, Meridian re-filed its TRAN-1 in October 2022. The Maharashtra SGST officer is refusing to proceed with verification, arguing the CGST wing must act first. Meanwhile the CGST division officer says he will only verify the central tax component and expects the state to independently verify the VAT credit. Meridian's counsel wants to know: what is the legally mandated verification protocol — must the two wings act sequentially or in parallel, which officer is responsible for which head of credit, and what guidelines govern each officer's verification exercise?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly mandates that the jurisdictional tax officer 'shall, in parallel, continue the verification of the remaining portion' and assigns CGST/SGST verification to the respective officer using Annexure I for central tax and state-issued guidelines for state tax — directly resolving the dispute about sequential vs. parallel action and the split-head responsibility.

questions.q: Apex Ceramics LLP (GSTIN 24AADFA5678K1ZQ, Gujarat) operated both a factory (central excise registered) and a trading arm (VAT registered) under the same PAN before GST. On migration, it claimed ₹22 lakhs in TRAN-1 — a blended credit with ₹14 lakhs from central excise input tax credit and ₹8 lakhs from Gujarat VAT. Following the Filco Supreme Court order, the GST department has reopened verification. The CGST Ahmedabad officer is now asking Apex to produce source documents for the full ₹22 lakhs, while the Gujarat SGST officer has separately issued a notice demanding the same documents. Apex's CFO is concerned about duplicate disallowances and wants clarity: can the CGST officer's findings on the central tax portion bind the SGST officer, or must Apex defend the same credit independently before both authorities, and which specific verification framework applies to each head?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's direction that each officer verify credit 'under the CGST or the SGST head, as the case may be' using Annexure I for central tax and separate state guidelines for state tax establishes that the two verification exercises are legally distinct and parallel — meaning Apex faces independent proceedings before each authority, which is the crux of the CFO's concern.

title: Medical Devices Rules
text:
ce of Class A, data on clinical investigation 
may not be required, except in cases, where  depending on the nature of the medical device, the Central Licensing 
Authority, for reason to be recorded in writing, considers such data necessary: 
                     Provided also that subject to other provisions of these rules, in case of medical device of which drugs are 
also a part, the submission of requirements relating to animal toxicology, reproduction studies, teratogenic studies, 
perinatal studies, mutagenicity and carcinogenicity may be relaxed in case of drugs already approved and marketed in 
India and supported by adequate published evidence regarding safety of the drug. 
        Provided also that, the results of clinical investigation  may not be required to be submitted where the 
investigational medical device is approved by the regulatory authorities of either the United Kingdom or the United 
States of America or Australia or Canada or Japan and the said device has been marketed for at least two years in that 
country and the Central Licencing Authority is satisfied with the data of safety, performance and pharmacovigilance 
of the device, and,- 
(a)  there is no evidence or theoretical possibility, on the basis of existing knowledge, of any difference in the 
behavior and performance in Indian population; 
(b)  the applicant has given an undertaking in writing to conduct post marketing clinical investigation with the 
objective of safety and performance of such investigational medical device as per protocol approved by the 
Central Licensing Authority.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers provisos under Medical Devices Rules regarding relaxation of clinical investigation data requirements — specifically for Class A devices, combination drug-device products, and devices already approved in reference countries (UK/USA/Australia/Canada/Japan) with 2+ years marketing history. These provisos create complex eligibility conditions that interact with Customs import licensing, CDSCO approvals, and GST classification of medical devices, supporting HIGH-complexity practitioner questions.

questions.q: Medivance Diagnostics Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) is importing a Class IIb continuous glucose monitor from its UK parent company, BioSync Medical Ltd. The device has been approved by the MHRA and marketed in the UK for 3 years. Medivance claims exemption from submitting full clinical investigation data to CLA, relying on the reference-country proviso. However, the customs assessable value is ₹4.2 crore and the importer is also claiming a concessional BCD rate under a specific Customs notification applicable to 'approved medical devices.' The CBIC field formation at JNCH is questioning whether the CLA's satisfaction under the reference-country proviso is a pre-condition to the Customs benefit, or whether these are independent tracks. Can Medivance clear the goods under the concessional notification without first obtaining CLA's written satisfaction on safety, performance and pharmacovigilance, given that the post-marketing clinical investigation undertaking has been submitted but CLA approval of the protocol is still pending?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's proviso on reference-country approval explicitly requires (a) no evidence of difference in Indian population behaviour AND (b) a written undertaking to conduct post-marketing clinical investigation 'as per protocol approved by the Central Licensing Authority' — the pending CLA protocol approval is the crux, and this specific two-limb condition is indispensable to determining whether the import licensing prerequisite for the Customs notification is satisfied.

questions.q: Orthex Solutions LLP, a Bengaluru-based manufacturer (GSTIN 29AABFO5678G1Z3), is seeking Central Licensing Authority approval for a Class A electrosurgical generator that incorporates a drug-eluting coating (the drug itself — rapamycin — is already approved and marketed in India with published safety literature). The LLP's regulatory counsel is advising that (i) clinical investigation data can be waived entirely because it is Class A, and separately (ii) animal toxicology and reproduction studies can be relaxed because the drug component is already approved. A competitor has filed a representation before the CLA arguing that the Class A waiver applies only where the Authority does not find the nature of the device to require such data — and that a combination drug-device product's 'nature' necessarily triggers that requirement, overriding the blanket Class A relaxation. Assess which proviso takes precedence and whether both relaxations can be claimed simultaneously for this combination device, and advise on the written-reasons requirement the CLA must satisfy if it decides to demand clinical data despite the Class A status.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains two sequential provisos that directly conflict in application to a combination drug-device Class A product: the first permits waiver of clinical investigation data for Class A 'except where the CLA for reasons recorded in writing considers it necessary,' and the second permits relaxation of animal toxicology etc. for already-approved drug components. The interaction and precedence of these two provisos, and the written-reasons obligation on CLA, are entirely derived from this chunk.

title: Rebate of the whole of the service tax and cess paid on all taxable services exported in terms of rule 3 of the said rules, to any country other than Nepal and Bhutan
text:

exported .............................................service (name of the taxable service) under rule 5 of the Export of Service Rules, 2005 to

claude_reasoning: This chunk is a prescribed declaration form (Part A and Part B) for claiming service tax rebate on exported services under rule 3 of Export of Service Rules, 2005, per Notification 11/2005-ST. It authoritatively governs the procedural requirements — payment receipt declaration, enclosure of documents, and the 15-day processing timeline by the sanctioning authority — making it indispensable for questions about rebate claim mechanics, delays, and cross-border payment receipt conditions.

questions.q: Meridian Consulting LLP (registered in Mumbai, Maharashtra) provided IT-enabled business process outsourcing services to a client in Germany during FY 2005-06, paying service tax of Rs. 4,80,000 and education cess of Rs. 9,600 on these exports. The payment from Germany was received partly in foreign currency (Rs. 3,20,000 equivalent) in September 2005 and the balance Rs. 1,60,000 was received in January 2006. Meridian filed a rebate claim in February 2006 enclosing the bank realisation certificates for both tranches. The Assistant Commissioner's office has not responded for 22 days. (a) Can Meridian declare in Part A of the rebate claim form that payment has been received 'in full' given the staggered receipts, and what documentary specifics must be furnished under the 'details of receipt of payment' field? (b) What legal recourse does Meridian have given the sanctioning authority has exceeded the prescribed processing period, and does the delay affect the rebate entitlement under rule 3 of the Export of Service Rules, 2005?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the exact Part A declaration language requiring the exporter to confirm payment 'has already been received in India in full' with details, and Part B's explicit 15-day processing benchmark — both are directly in issue when payment arrived in tranches and the authority is overdue.

questions.q: Skybridge Events Pvt Ltd, a Bengaluru-based company with service tax registration, organized a large-scale product launch event for a US-based multinational in Dubai during March 2006. Skybridge paid service tax of Rs. 7,20,000 and education cess of Rs. 14,400 in India on the said services. The contract stipulated payment in USD, but the US parent company routed the payment through its Indian subsidiary (Skybridge's holding company) in INR as an intercompany settlement, and the INR amount was credited to Skybridge's account in April 2006. The jurisdictional Deputy Commissioner rejected the rebate claim on two grounds: (i) the service was performed in Dubai, not exported 'from India', and (ii) payment was not received from outside India in foreign exchange. Advise Skybridge on whether the rejection is sustainable under rule 3 of the Export of Service Rules, 2005, specifically addressing whether the INR intercompany receipt satisfies the 'payment received in India' declaration required in the rebate claim form, and whether the Dubai performance location disqualifies the export characterisation.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Part A declaration — requiring the service provider to declare export under rule 5 of Export of Service Rules 2005 AND confirm payment receipt 'in India in full' — is the precise evidentiary standard the Deputy Commissioner applied to reject the claim; the chunk's form language is the operative test being disputed.

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the nil-rate GST exemption for millet flour food preparations (HS 1901, ≥70% millet by weight, non-pre-packaged/non-labelled) effective 20-Oct-2023. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because the exemption hinges on two precise conditions (composition threshold + packaging status) that interact with place-of-supply, invoicing, and input-tax-credit reversal rules.

questions.q: Annapurna Agro Foods Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 36AABCA1234F1ZK, Telangana) manufactures a millet-based weaning food under HSN 1901 containing 72% finger millet and 28% wheat flour, sold in 25 kg HDPE bags without any printed MRP or consumer label. They supply 500 bags worth ₹4,50,000 to a mid-day meal NGO registered in Maharashtra on 5-Nov-2023. Their procurement team has claimed full ITC on millet grain purchases. The CFO now asks: (a) does this supply attract nil CGST/SGST or standard rate, (b) does the inter-state nature of the supply change the analysis given the corresponding IGST notification, and (c) if a portion of the same batch was repackaged into 500 g retail pouches with printed labels for a supermarket chain, what is the GST liability on that portion and must ITC already availed be reversed proportionately?
questions.why_this_chunk: The nil-rate entry at S. No. 94A inserted by this notification is the pivotal source — it sets the twin conditions (≥70% millet by weight AND 'other than pre-packaged and labelled') that determine whether each of the two supply streams (bulk unbranded vs retail-labelled) qualifies for exemption, directly driving the ITC reversal analysis under Rule 42.

questions.q: Millex Nutritionals LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM5678G1Z3, Maharashtra) exports millet flour preparations (HSN 1901, 75% bajra content) in 50 kg jute bags to a buyer in UAE under a Letter of Undertaking. The goods are manufactured at their Pune plant and physically loaded at Nhava Sheva port on 12-Nov-2023. A separate domestic arm of the same LLP also supplies the identical product — same batch, same composition — to a government school canteen in Rajasthan under a state tender, delivered in 20 kg bags pre-printed with the school's name and nutritional information as mandated by the tender. The tax manager needs to determine: (i) the GST treatment of the export leg (zero-rated vs exempt, and whether LUT covers it), (ii) the correct classification and rate for the domestic school supply given the mandatory pre-printing required by the tender, and (iii) whether 'pre-packaged and labelled' for purposes of the exemption carve-out should be interpreted by reference to the Legal Metrology Act definition or solely by the plain language of the notification, especially where labelling is contractually compelled rather than voluntary.
questions.why_this_chunk: The carve-out phrase 'other than pre-packaged and labelled' in the S. No. 94A entry is indispensable: whether mandatory tender-driven labelling triggers the carve-out (and thus standard GST rate) on the domestic school supply is a direct legal-interpretation question anchored to the exact wording inserted by this notification, which no generic chunk on exports or zero-rating addresses.

title: Makes Pan Masala Packing Machines (Capacity Determination And Collection of Duty) Rules, 2008
text:
ication, approve the declaration and determine and pass order concerning the annual capacity of production of the factory
within five working days in accordance with the provisions of these rules:

Provided that the Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise or the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise, as the case may
be, may direct for modifications in the plan or details of the part or section of the factory premises intended to be used by the
manufacturer for manufacture of notified goods of different retail sale prices, as he thinks proper, for effective segregation of
the parts or sections of the premises and the machines to be used in such parts or sections before granting the approval:

Provided further that if the manufacturer does not receive the approval in respect of his declaration within the said period of five
working days, the approval shall be deemed to have been granted subject to the modifications, if any, which the Deputy
Commissioner of Central Excise or the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise, as the case may be, may communicate
later on but not later than thirty days of filing of the declaration.

(3)        The annual capacity of production shall be calculated by application of the appropriate quantity that is deemed to be
produced by use of one operating packing machine as specified in rule 5 to the number of operating packing machines in the
factory during the month beginning which the capacity is being determined.

(4)        The number of operating packing machines during any month shall be equal to the number of packing machines
installed in the factory during that month.

(5)        The machines which the manufacturer does not intend to operate shall be uninstalled and sealed by the Superintendent
of Central Excise and removed from the factory premises under his physical supervision:

Provided that in case it is not feasible to remove such packing machine out of the factory premises, it shall be uninstalled and
sealed by the Superintendent of Central Excise in such a manner that it cannot be operated.

(6)        In case a manufacturer wishes to make any subsequent changes with respect to any of the parameters which has been
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the Pan Masala Packing Machines Rules 2008 — specifically the deemed-capacity determination procedure: the 5-working-day approval window, deemed approval on lapse, calculation of annual capacity from operating machines, and the sealing/removal of non-operating machines. These procedural and capacity-computation rules support HIGH-complexity questions involving disputed capacity orders, multi-machine factory configurations, and the interplay between the deemed-approval provision and subsequent modification communications.

questions.q: Aroha Flavours Pvt Ltd, a pan masala manufacturer in Kanpur (UP), operates a factory with 12 packing machines. On 3-March-2025 they filed their declaration under the Packing Machines Capacity Rules, segregating 8 machines for 'Royal Zarda' pouches (MRP ₹5) and 4 machines for 'Aroha Gold' pouches (MRP ₹10). The Deputy Commissioner neither approved nor communicated any modifications by 10-March-2025 (5 working days). On 28-March-2025 — 25 days after filing — the DC issued an order directing Aroha to restructure the factory layout, reduce the 'Royal Zarda' section to 6 machines, and recalculate capacity accordingly. Aroha contests this, arguing deemed approval had already crystallised on 10-March and the DC's 28-March order is time-barred. The DC counters that deemed approval is subject to modifications he can communicate up to 30 days. Who is correct, and what is Aroha's annual assessed capacity for each product line — and does the DC's modification order legally override the already-deemed approval?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's second proviso to the approval procedure directly governs this dispute: deemed approval attaches after 5 working days but remains subject to modifications communicated 'not later than thirty days of filing.' The chunk also provides the capacity-calculation formula (operating machines × deemed output per Rule 5), making it indispensable to computing whether 8 or 6 machines form the base.

questions.q: Mirchi Masala LLP in Indore (MP) has 10 packing machines. In January 2025 they declared 7 as operating and requested the Superintendent to unseal and remove the remaining 3 — however, the factory layout makes it physically impossible to move those machines out of the premises. The Superintendent sealed them in-situ. In February 2025, during a surprise visit, the Range Officer found one of the 3 sealed machines running — the seal had apparently been broken — and raised a demand computing duty on all 10 machines for February, arguing that once a sealed machine is found operated, the entire month's capacity must be reassessed treating all 10 as 'installed.' The LLP argues duty should be on 7 machines for the month plus a penalty only for the specific machine found running. Analyse the legal merits: does the 'installed = operating' deeming under these Rules support the Department's all-10-machine demand for February, and what is the significance of the in-situ sealing (as opposed to physical removal) to the LLP's liability?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's sub-rules (4) and (5) are the direct authority: sub-rule (4) equates installed machines to operating machines for capacity purposes, while sub-rule (5)'s proviso permitting in-situ sealing when removal is infeasible — and the legal status of that sealed machine — is central to whether the Department can treat the broken-sealed machine as 'installed' retroactively for the full month.

title: Cosmetics Rules, 2020
text:
[भागII—खण्‍ड‍3(i)]
भारत‍का‍राजपत्र‍:‍असाधारण‍
85

cosmetic in which the cosmetic is packed and every other outer covering in which the container is 
packed:﹘

(a) name of the cosmetic;

(b) the distinctive batch number or lot number or serial number preceded by the word ―Lot No.‖ 
or ―Lot‖ or ―Batch No.‖ or ―B. No.‖ or ―Serial No.‖ or ―B‖;

(c) use before or date of expiry, if any;

(d) the name and address of manufacturer and address of actual premises where the cosmetic has 
been manufactured;

(e) licence number preceded by letters ―Licence No. or Lic. No.‖;

(f) internationally recognised symbols in lieu of text, wherever required:

Provided that where a cosmetic is required by the consignee to be not labelled with the name and 
address of the manufacturer, the labels on packages or containers shall bear a code number as approved 
by the state Licensing Authority.

35.  Prohibition against altering inscription on containers, labels or wrappers of cosmetic.﹘ No person

shall alter, obliterate or deface any inscription or mark made or recorded by the manufacturer on the 
container, label or wrapper of any cosmetic:

Provided that nothing in this rule shall apply to any alteration, inscription or mark made on the

container, label or wrapper of any cosmetic at the instance or direction or with the permission of the 
Central Licensing Authority.

36.  Prohibition against false or misleading claims.﹘ No cosmetic may purport or claim to purport or

convey any idea which is false or misleading to the intending user.

37.  Labelling of hair dyes containing dyes, colours and pigments. ﹘ Hair dyes containing Para-

phenylenediamine or other dyes, colours and pigments shall be labelled with the following legend in 
English and local languages and these shall appear on both the inner and the outer labels:

―Caution.﹘ This product contains ingredients which may cause skin irritation in certain cases and so a 
preliminary test according to the accompanying directions should first be made. This product should not 
be used for dyeing the eyelashes or eyebrows; as such a use may cause blindness.‖

Each package shall also contain instructions in English and local languages on the following lines for 
carrying out the test:

―This preparation may cause serious inflammation of the skin in some cases and so a preliminary test 
should always be carried out to determine whether or not special sensitivity exists. To make the test, 
cleanse a small area of skin behind the ear or upon the inner surface of the forearm, using either soap 
and water or alcohol. Apply a small quantity of the hair dye as prepared for use to the area and allow it 
to dry. After twenty-four hours, wash the area gently with soap and water. If no irritation or 
inflammation is apparent, it may be assumed that no hypersensitivity to the dye exists. The test should, 
however, be carried out before each and every application. This preparation should on no account be 
used for dyeing eyebrows or eyelashes as severe inflammation of the eye or even blindness may result.‖

38.  Special provisions relating to toothpaste containing fluoride.﹘

(i) Fluoride content in toothpaste shall not be more than 1000 ppm and the content of fluoride in terms

of ppm shall be mentioned on the tube and carton.

(ii) Date of expiry should be mentioned on tube and carton.

39.  Standards of cosmetics.﹘(1) No cosmetic shall be imported or manufactured unless it complies with

the specifications prescribed under the Ninth Schedule or any other standards of quality and safety, 
applicable to it, and other provisions under the rules.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs mandatory labeling requirements for imported cosmetics under the Cosmetics Rules 2020, including the specific proviso allowing code-number substitution for manufacturer identity, the Rule 35 prohibition on label alteration, the Rule 37 bilingual caution mandate for para-phenylenediamine hair dyes, and the Rule 38 fluoride-ppm disclosure requirement for toothpaste — all of which create real compliance pinch-points at customs clearance and domestic distribution.

questions.q: Luminova Beauty Imports LLP (IEC: 0523XXXXX, GSTIN: 27AADFL1234C1ZM, Mumbai) has imported a consignment of Para-phenylenediamine-based permanent hair colour kits from a South Korean OEM under a white-label arrangement. The South Korean manufacturer, at the specific written request of Luminova, has omitted its own name and address from both inner and outer labels and instead printed the code 'LB-KR-2024' approved by the Maharashtra State Licensing Authority. Customs officers at JNCH have detained the consignment under Section 111(d) of the Customs Act, alleging that the goods are 'not labelled in accordance with the requirements of any law for the time being in force.' Simultaneously, the CDSCO inspector notes that the English caution text appears only on the outer carton and not on the inner tube, and that no local-language caution text is present anywhere. Can Luminova (a) defend the omission of the manufacturer's name against the customs seizure notice, (b) quantify the specific labeling gaps that independently constitute a violation under the Cosmetics Rules 2020, and (c) assess whether the defect in (b) is compoundable or requires re-export/destruction of the ₹38 lakh shipment?
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso to Rule 34 expressly permits omission of manufacturer name/address when the consignee so requires and a SLA-approved code number is substituted — this is the precise statutory defence for point (a). Rule 37 requires the caution legend on BOTH inner and outer labels and in English AND local languages — the dual-surface and bilingual requirement directly establishes the violations in point (b). No generic chunk covers both the white-label proviso and the hair-dye bilingual dual-label mandate together.

questions.q: Dent-Fresh Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN: 07AABCD5678E1ZK, Delhi) is an authorised distributor of an imported toothpaste brand manufactured in Germany. The imported tubes, cleared through ICD Tughlakabad, carry the fluoride content stated as '0.76% w/w sodium monofluorophosphate' on the carton but the inner aluminium tube bears no fluoride figure at all. After post-clearance audit, the customs department proposes to re-assess the goods under Section 17(5) of the Customs Act on the ground that the goods were 'not in accordance with the import declaration' (which listed them as standards-compliant cosmetics), and additionally the CDSCO regional office in Delhi has initiated show-cause proceedings for marketing a non-conforming cosmetic. A Delhi-based retailer, Glow Mart, has already purchased ₹12 lakh worth of stock and relabelled the tubes with a sticker showing '1000 ppm fluoride' before Dent-Fresh could recall them — thereby triggering a potential Rule 35 violation by Glow Mart. Advise: (i) whether the original import labeling (ppm absent on tube, present only on carton) violates the Cosmetics Rules 2020; (ii) whether Dent-Fresh faces customs duty re-assessment and on what legal basis; and (iii) whether Glow Mart's remedial sticker itself constitutes an independent offence, and under what exception, if any, it could be saved.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 38(i) explicitly requires fluoride content in ppm on BOTH the tube and the carton — the absence on the tube is a direct, specific violation that anchors point (i). Rule 35 prohibits any person from altering inscriptions on the container/label/wrapper, and its proviso carving out alterations made 'at the instance or direction or with the permission of the Central Licensing Authority' is the only route to saving Glow Mart's sticker — this specific proviso is indispensable to point (iii).

title: Seeks to amend the Customs notification No. 84/2007-Customs (N.T.) dated 17th August, 2007 to give effect to the amendments to Rules of Origin of India-Chile PTA
text:
possession of the issuing authority which issued the certificate of origin under 
verification, which may be deemed necessary for verifying the authenticity of the 
said certificate of origin and the veracity of the information contained therein and in 
such a request, the number and the date of the issue of the certificate of origin under 
verification shall be indicated; 
 
(b) 
for the purposes of verification of the contents of the local or regional added

value, the producer or exporter shall facilitate the access to any information or
