documentation necessary for establishing the CIF value of the non-originating goods 
used in the production of the goods under verification; 
 
(c) 
for the purposes of verification of the characteristics of certain production

processes, the exporter or producer shall facilitate the access to any information and 
documentation that allow the confirmation of such processes; 
 
(d) 
the customs authority of the importing Party may send a questionnaire to the

issuing authority of the exporting Party which shall be passed on to the exporter or 
producer, indicating the certificate of origin under verification; 
 
(e) 
when the customs authority of the importing Party is not satisfied with the

results of the verification of origin pursuant to clauses (a) to (d), it shall deliver a 
written request to the issuing authority of the exporting Party to facilitate visits to the 
premises of the producer, with a view to examining the production processes, as well 
as the equipment and tools utilised in the manufacture of the product under 
verification; 
 
(f) 
the issuing authority of the exporting Party will provide the requisitioned

documents, statement and cost sheet of materials to the customs authority of the 
importing Party at least fifteen days before the date of visit; 
 
(g) 
the issuing authority of the exporting Party shall accompany the authorities of

the importing Party in their above-mentioned visit, which may include the 
participation of specialists who shall act as observers: 
 
 
Provided that, each Party may designate specialists, who shall be neutral

and have no interest whatsoever in the verification:

Provided further that, each Party may deny the participation of such

specialists whenever the latter represent the interests of the companies involved in 
the verification; 
 
(h) 
once the visit is concluded, the participants shall subscribe the minutes of it, in

which it shall be indicated that it was carried out according to the conditions 
established in these rules and the said minutes shall contain, in addition, the

8
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the verification procedure for certificates of origin under the India-Chile PTA Rules of Origin, specifically the multi-step process customs authorities must follow when questioning the authenticity of an origin certificate — including document requests, questionnaires, factory visits, and the role of neutral specialists. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving cross-border preferential tariff claims where origin authenticity is disputed.

questions.q: Sunrise Spices Pvt Ltd, a Chennai-based exporter (GSTIN 33AABCS1234F1Z5) shipped 50 MT of chilli oleoresin (HS 1302.19) to Santiago, Chile, claiming preferential tariff under the India-Chile PTA with a certificate of origin issued by the Export Inspection Council on 12-Jan-2026. Chilean customs has initiated a verification, sent a questionnaire to EIC, and is now unsatisfied with the responses — they are demanding a factory visit to Sunrise's Erode processing unit. EIC has asked Sunrise to prepare for this visit, scheduled for 10-Mar-2026. What documents must EIC provide to Chilean customs BEFORE the visit, by what deadline, and what are the rules governing the participation of industry specialists who accompany the visiting team — specifically can Chilean customs exclude a specialist if that specialist is on retainer with a competing Chilean importer?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (f) specifies the 15-day advance document requirement before a visit; clause (g) and its provisos directly govern specialist participation, neutrality, and the right to deny participation when specialists represent company interests — all indispensable to answering this question.

questions.q: Bharat Herbal Extracts LLP, based in Hyderabad, exported 12 tonnes of Andrographis paniculata dry extract (HS 1302.19) to a Chilean pharmaceutical company in Valparaíso under the India-Chile PTA, with preferential duty claimed on the basis of a Certificate of Origin issued by APEDA on 5-Nov-2025. The Chilean customs authority suspects that non-originating solvent (procured from China) accounts for more than the permissible non-originating content and has (i) sent a written request to APEDA for records, (ii) issued a questionnaire to APEDA to forward to Bharat Herbal, and (iii) after receiving unsatisfactory answers, formally requested a premises visit. Bharat Herbal's production manager is worried about three things: how the CIF value of the Chinese solvent must be demonstrated to verify local value addition, whether Chilean customs can bring an industry chemist as a 'specialist observer' who coincidentally consults for a competing Chilean buyer, and what happens procedurally once the factory visit is concluded. Walk through the applicable verification framework step by step.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (b) directly governs the obligation to facilitate access to CIF value information for non-originating inputs; clause (g)'s second proviso addresses denial of specialist participation on conflict-of-interest grounds; clause (h) covers the minutes-signing requirement at visit conclusion — all three are in this chunk and are each independently necessary to answer the three sub-questions.

title: Regularizing payment of GST on co-insurance premium apportioned by the lead insurer to the co-insurer and on ceding /re-insurance commission deducted from the reinsurance premium paid by the insurer to the reinsurer.
text:
subject to the condition that the Central tax, the State tax, the Union territory tax and the 
integrated tax is paid by the reinsurer on the gross reinsurance premium payable by the 
insurer to the reinsurer, inclusive of the said ceding commission or the reinsurance 
commission.

The above provisions were enacted vide Finance (No. 2) Act, 2024 and have been brought into 
force on 01.11.2024 vide Notification No. 17/2024-Central Tax dated 27.09.2024. 
 
3. 
In its 53rd meeting, the GST Council further recommended that the payment of GST on the 
activities or transactions, as specified in paragraph 2 above, may be regularized for the past period, 
i.e. from 01.07.2017 to the effective date of amendments in the CGST Act, , on ‘as is where is’ 
basis. 
 
4. 
Thus, as recommended by the 53rd GST Council, the payment of GST on the activities or 
transactions specified in paragraph 2 above is regularized for the period 01.07.2017 to 31.10.2024, 
on ‘as is where is’ basis. 
 
5. 
Difficulties, if any, in the implementation of this circular may be brought to the notice of 
the Board.

Yours sincerely,

(Sachin Jain)

Joint Secretary, TRU-II

Page 2 of 2
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the retrospective regularization ('as is where is') of GST on co-insurance premium apportionments and ceding/re-insurance commissions for the period 01.07.2017 to 31.10.2024, enacted via Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 and brought into force 01.11.2024 — a highly specific amnesty/regularization window that is indispensable to advising clients facing GST demands or ITC mismatches on these transactions for that historical period.

questions.q: New India Assurance Co. Ltd. (lead insurer, GSTIN 27AAAAA0000A1Z5, Maharashtra) co-insured a large infrastructure project with Oriental Insurance (co-insurer, GSTIN 07BBBBB0000B1Z3, Delhi) during FY 2019-20, apportioning ₹4.2 crore of premium to Oriental. The GST department has now issued a SCN to Oriental demanding GST on the co-insurance premium received, on the ground that the apportionment constitutes a separate taxable supply by New India to Oriental. Oriental's counsel argues no GST liability arose. Simultaneously, Oriental's ITC claims on that transaction were denied. For the period prior to November 2024, what is the correct legal position — does Oriental have a GST liability on the apportioned co-insurance premium, and can the department sustain the demand, given subsequent legislative and administrative developments?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly regularizes GST on co-insurance premium apportionments by lead insurers to co-insurers for 01.07.2017 to 31.10.2024 on 'as is where is' basis, which directly determines whether Oriental's past non-payment can be sustained as a demand or is extinguished by the regularization.

questions.q: Bajaj Allianz General Insurance (insurer, GSTIN 27CCCCC0000C1Z1, Pune) entered into a reinsurance treaty with Munich Re India (reinsurer, GSTIN 27DDDDD0000D1Z9, Mumbai) for FY 2021-22 and FY 2022-23. Munich Re deducted a ceding commission of ₹1.8 crore from the gross reinsurance premium before remitting the net amount to Bajaj Allianz. Bajaj Allianz paid GST only on the net reinsurance premium received, while Munich Re paid GST only on the net reinsurance premium payable (i.e., after deducting the ceding commission). A GST audit for FY 2022-23 has flagged this arrangement, contending that the ceding commission should have been taxed as a separate supply. Bajaj Allianz's in-house counsel needs to know: (a) whether GST is payable on the gross reinsurance premium inclusive of ceding commission or only on the net amount, (b) who bears that liability — the insurer or the reinsurer, and (c) whether any past shortfall for FY 2021-22 and FY 2022-23 is still actionable by the department.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies the exact condition: GST must be paid by the reinsurer on the gross reinsurance premium inclusive of ceding/reinsurance commission, and regularizes any past non-compliance for 01.07.2017–31.10.2024 on 'as is where is' basis — both limbs are required to answer parts (b) and (c) of this question.

title: rescinds the notification No.74/2000-Customs, dated the 22nd day of May, 2000
text:
7th September, 2001

Notification No. 92 /2001-Customs

In exercise of powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), read with rules
18 and 20 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles and for
Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995, the Central Government hereby rescinds the notification of the Government of India in the
Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No.74/2000-Customs, dated the 22 nd day of May, 2000 [G.S.R.478(E), dated
the 22 nd May, 2000].

G. D. Lohani

Under Secretary to the Government of India

F. No. 354/131/2001-TRU
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a rescission notification under the anti-dumping duty framework (Section 9A, Customs Tariff Act 1975 read with 1995 AD Rules), specifically revoking Notification 74/2000-Customs dated 22-May-2000. It authoritatively establishes the exact date anti-dumping duty under that earlier notification ceased to have legal force, which is indispensable for determining duty liability on imports straddling the rescission date.

questions.q: Precision Metals Pvt Ltd, an importer based in Mumbai (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1Z5), had placed a purchase order with a South Korean supplier in July 2001 for a consignment of goods that were subject to anti-dumping duty under Notification 74/2000-Customs dated 22-May-2000. The vessel departed the Korean port on 5-September-2001 and the bill of lading is dated that day, but the goods arrived at JNPT and the bill of entry was filed on 10-September-2001. Customs has issued a demand notice asserting anti-dumping duty is payable. Precision Metals contends no anti-dumping duty applies. Which notification date controls the levy, what is the precise legal basis on which the duty obligation was extinguished, and does the date-of-filing-of-bill-of-entry versus date-of-arrival analysis change the outcome given the rescission came into force on 7-September-2001?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the operative rescission notification itself — it establishes 7-September-2001 as the exact date Notification 74/2000-Customs ceased to have effect, which is the linchpin fact for determining whether the bill of entry filed on 10-September-2001 attracts anti-dumping duty or not.

questions.q: Bharat Alloys LLP, a trader in Ahmedabad, imported a consignment under an advance licence and also holds a separate bonded warehouse licence. Part of the goods (cleared ex-bond on 20-August-2001) were subject to anti-dumping duty under Notification 74/2000-Customs, and the duty was provisionally assessed and a bank guarantee furnished. The remaining goods were cleared ex-bond on 12-September-2001. The customs department now seeks to finalise the provisional assessment and encash the bank guarantee for the August clearance, while Bharat Alloys argues the rescission of the parent anti-dumping notification on 7-September-2001 should also extinguish liability for the earlier provisional assessment. Advise on: (a) whether a rescission of an anti-dumping notification under Section 9A operates prospectively or also wipes out crystallised provisional duty liabilities; and (b) the interaction between Rules 18 and 20 of the 1995 AD Rules and the effect of rescission on pending finalisation proceedings.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk invokes Section 9A(1) read with Rules 18 and 20 of the 1995 AD Rules as the source of power for the rescission — the specific rules cited are indispensable to analysing whether the rescission affects pending provisional assessments or only future imports.

title: Warehousing - Grant of extension of warehousing period by the Chief Commissioners under section 61 of the Customs Act, 1962-regarding
text:
7. Chief Commissioner of Customs are, therefore, requested to decide the requests for grant of extension of warehousing
period after taking into consideration all aspects referred to above. Issues other than those related to extension of warehousing
period, as contained in the Board's instructions referred in para 2 above, shall continue to be governed by the said instructions.
Board's Circular No. 12/98-Cus., dated 06.03.1998, may be treated as subsumed in the present instructions. The contents of
this Circular may also be brought to the notice of the field formations and the trade under your jurisdiction suitably.

8. It may be clarified here that though the powers under statute now vest with Chief Commissioner of Customs, the above
guidelines are warranted in order to have uniform approach throughout the country.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the delegation of warehousing period extension powers to Chief Commissioners under section 61 of the Customs Act, 1962, and the supersession of Circular 12/98-Cus — it authoritatively governs who decides extension requests, on what uniform criteria, and the circulars that no longer independently apply. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving multi-party warehousing scenarios where the correct authority, governing circular, and interplay with bonding/duty deferral obligations must all be identified.

questions.q: Arjun Exports Pvt Ltd (IEC: AABCA1234C) has warehoused 800 MT of crude palm oil at a customs bonded warehouse in Kandla since January 2024, with the initial two-year warehousing period expiring in January 2026. The company has filed a request for a further 12-month extension citing global commodity price depression. The Deputy Commissioner at Kandla has returned the application saying it is beyond his sanctioning powers. Arjun Exports' counsel argues that Circular 12/98-Cus grants the Commissioner the authority to grant such extensions. Who is the correct authority to decide this extension request as of today, does Circular 12/98-Cus still independently govern this, and what factors must be weighed before the competent authority grants or refuses the extension?
questions.why_this_chunk: Paragraph 8 expressly states that statutory powers now vest with the Chief Commissioner (not the Commissioner or Deputy Commissioner), and paragraph 7 states that Circular 12/98-Cus is subsumed in the present instructions — both facts are indispensable to correctly advising on authority and the continued relevance of the earlier circular.

questions.q: Meridian Petrochemicals LLP imported a consignment of specialty chemical intermediates from Germany (invoice value EUR 2.4 million, CIF Mumbai) in March 2023 and placed them in a private bonded warehouse in Nhava Sheva under a bond. The initial warehousing period has been exhausted and two successive extensions were granted by the jurisdictional Commissioner. The LLP now seeks a third extension of 18 months, arguing downstream processing delays caused by force majeure. A field officer has flagged that the Chief Commissioner's office has applied different criteria at Nhava Sheva versus JNCH for similar requests, leading to inconsistent outcomes. (a) Under the current circular framework, is the Chief Commissioner the sole competent authority for this third extension, or can the Commissioner grant it? (b) What is the precise rationale behind mandating uniform guidelines across all formations despite the statutory power vesting at Chief Commissioner level, and how should the Chief Commissioner weigh force majeure against duty-deferral risk when deciding?
questions.why_this_chunk: Paragraph 8 directly addresses the rationale for uniform guidelines ('warranted in order to have uniform approach throughout the country') even after statutory power vests with the Chief Commissioner — this is the only authoritative source explaining why field-level inconsistency is impermissible and what principle overrides it. Paragraph 7's instruction to consider 'all aspects' links to the multi-factor evaluation required.

title: Appointment of CAA by PrDGRI
text:
[To be published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part-II, section 3, sub-section (ii)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs

Directorate of Revenue Intelligence

Notification No. 20/2021-Customs (N.T./CAA/DRI)

New Delhi, dated 23.02.2021

S.O. 
 (E). - In pursuance of notification No. 60/2015-Customs (N.T.), published vide number

G.S.R. 453(E), dated 4thJune 2015 in Gazette of India, Extra-ordinary, Part-II, section 3, sub-section

(i), and as amended by notification No. 133/2015-Customs (N.T.), published vide number G.S.R.

916(E) dated 30thNovember 2015 in Gazette of India, Extra-ordinary, Part-II, section 3, sub-section

(i), issued by the Government of India, Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue, under clause (a)

of section 152 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Principal Director General, Revenue

Intelligence, hereby makes the following amendment in the Notification No.50/2020-Customs

(N.T./CAA/DRI) dated 08.10.2020 published vide S.O. 3563(E) dated 12.10.2020.

In the said notification, against serial number 1, in column 5 for the existing words,

“Joint/Additional Commissioner of Customs (Drawback), Nhava Sheva-II, Jawaharlal Nehru Custom

House, Raigad” shall be substituted.

[F. No. DRI/HQ-CI/50D/CAA-32/2020]

(DEEPAK KUMAR)

ADDITIONAL DIRECTOR

Copy forwarded for information and updation of record to

1. Joint/Additional Commissioner of Customs (Drawback), Nhava Sheva-II, Jawaharlal Nehru

Custom House, Raigad.

2. Joint/Additional Commissioner of Customs, New Custom House, Near Balaji Temple, Kandla. 
3. Pr. Additional Director General, Directorate of Revenue Intelligence, Mumbai with respect to his

letter dated20.01.2021 and with the request to inform the noticees and co-noticees to the show 
cause notice dated 10.07.2020 in this regard.
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a DRI notification amending the designated Customs Adjudicating Authority (CAA) for a specific show cause notice at Nhava Sheva-II/JNCH, replacing the adjudicating officer mid-proceeding. It authoritatively covers the jurisdictional assignment of adjudication power under Section 152 of the Customs Act for DRI-initiated SCNs at JNCH Drawback, making it indispensable for questions about mid-proceeding authority substitution and its legal consequences.

questions.q: Globex Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCG1234F1Z5, Mumbai) received a DRI show cause notice dated 10-Jul-2020 at Jawaharlal Nehru Custom House alleging fraudulent drawback claims of ₹4.2 crore on synthetic textile shipments. The original adjudicating authority named in the SCN was the Joint Commissioner of Customs, New Custom House, Kandla. In February 2021, Globex received an intimation stating that adjudication will now be conducted by the Joint/Additional Commissioner of Customs (Drawback), Nhava Sheva-II, JNCH, Raigad. Globex's counsel argues this mid-proceeding substitution of the adjudicating authority is void ab initio — that the company was denied natural justice because the new officer has no nexus to the port of export or the original investigation. Does the substitution have a valid legal basis under the Customs Act, and can Globex challenge the new officer's jurisdiction before the CESTAT?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the exact notification (20/2021-Customs NT/CAA/DRI amending 50/2020) that substitutes the adjudicating authority for serial number 1 — directly covering the Nhava Sheva-II JNCH Drawback officer appointment and its linkage to SCN dated 10.07.2020, which is indispensable to evaluating the legality of the substitution.

questions.q: Sunrise Trading LLP (proprietor based in Raigad, Maharashtra) is a co-noticee in a DRI investigation arising from a show cause notice dated 10-Jul-2020 issued out of JNCH, alleging mis-declaration of country of origin on electronics to inflate IGST-paid drawback refunds totalling ₹78 lakhs. The principal noticee is a Delhi-based exporter. Following a CAA amendment notification in February 2021, Sunrise's advocate discovers that the adjudicating authority for the SCN has been changed to the Joint/Additional Commissioner (Drawback), Nhava Sheva-II. Sunrise argues: (a) as a co-noticee it was never separately informed of the change as required, and (b) since its alleged role relates only to the GST/IGST drawback component, adjudication should lie with the GST authorities under CGST Act and not Customs. Analyse whether the CAA change binds Sunrise as a co-noticee, whether notice to the principal noticee suffices, and whether the drawback fraud allegation falls exclusively within Customs Act adjudication or can attract concurrent GST jurisdiction.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly records that the Pr. ADG DRI Mumbai was requested to inform 'noticees and co-noticees' to the SCN dated 10.07.2020, making the notification the direct authority on the co-noticee notification obligation and the specific CAA change to Nhava Sheva-II — both facts are indispensable to resolving points (a) and (b).

title: The Finance Act (No. 2), 2019 (23 of 2019)
text:
SEC. 1]
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINARY
53

other than the Supreme Court or the High Court, then, notwithstanding anything contained
in any other provisions of any law for the time being in force, such appeal or reference or
reply shall be deemed to have been withdrawn.

(7) Where the declarant has filed a writ petition or appeal or reference before any High
Court or the Supreme Court against any order in respect of the tax dues, the declarant shall
file an application before such High Court or the Supreme Court for withdrawing such writ
petition, appeal or reference and after withdrawal of such writ petition, appeal or reference
with the leave of the Court, he shall furnish proof of such withdrawal to the designated
committee, in such manner as may be prescribed, along with the proof of payment referred to
in sub-section (5).

(8) On payment of the amount indicated in the statement of the designated committee
and production of proof of withdrawal of appeal, wherever applicable, the designated
committee shall issue a discharge certificate in electronic form, within thirty days of the said
payment and production of proof.

Rectification
of errors.

128. Within thirty days of the date of issue of a statement indicating the amount
payable by the declarant, the designated committee may modify its order only to correct an
arithmetical error or clerical error, which is apparent on the face of record, on such error being
pointed out by the declarant or suo motu, by the designated committee.

129. (1) Every discharge certificate issued under section 126 with respect to the amount
payable under this Scheme shall be conclusive as to the matter and time period stated
therein, and—

Issue of
discharge
certificate to
be conclusive
of matter and
time period.

(a) the declarant shall not be liable to pay any further duty, interest, or penalty
with respect to the matter and time period covered in the declaration;

(b) the declarant shall not be liable to be prosecuted under the indirect tax
enactment with respect to the matter and time period covered in the declaration;

(c) no matter and time period covered by such declaration shall be reopened in
any other proceeding under the indirect tax enactment.

(2) Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section (1),—

(a) no person being a party in appeal, application, revision or reference shall
contend that the central excise officer has acquiesced in the decision on the disputed
issue by issuing the discharge certificate under this scheme;

(b) the issue of the discharge certificate with respect to a matter for a time period
shall not preclude the issue of a show cause notice,—

(i) for the same matter for a subsequent time period; or

(ii) for a different matter for the same time period;

(c) in a case of voluntary disclosure where any material particular furnished in
the declaration is subsequently found to be false, within a period of one year of issue
of the discharge certificate,  it shall be presumed as if the declaration was never made
and proceedings under the applicable indirect tax enactment shall be instituted.

130. (1) Any amount paid under this Scheme,—

Restrictions of
Scheme.

(a) shall not be paid through the input tax credit account under the indirect tax
enactment or any other Act;

(b) shall not be refundable under any circumstances;

(c) shall not, under the indirect tax enactment or under any other Act,—

(i) be taken as input tax credit; or
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the Sabka Vishwas (Legacy Dispute Resolution) Scheme 2019 under Finance Act (No. 2) 2019 — specifically discharge certificate finality (s.129), appeal withdrawal requirements (s.127), rectification limits (s.128), and scheme restrictions (s.130). It supports HIGH-complexity queries because practitioners must navigate the interplay between scheme finality, carve-outs for subsequent periods/different matters, the false-declaration unwind clause, and the ITC prohibition — all in real litigation-exit scenarios.

questions.q: Annapurna Ceramics Pvt Ltd (Morbi, Gujarat, GSTIN 24AABCA1234F1ZX) had a central excise dispute on classification of vitrified tiles for the period Apr 2014–Mar 2017, where the department alleged duty short-payment of ₹3.8 crore plus interest. The company filed a declaration under the legacy dispute resolution scheme, received a statement from the designated committee for ₹1.1 crore, and paid the full amount in December 2019. The discharge certificate was issued in January 2020. In March 2024, the department issued a show cause notice for the same classification issue but for the period Apr 2017–Jun 2017 (just before GST rollout). Simultaneously, an audit party has flagged that Annapurna had incorrectly disclosed the quantum of cenvat credit availed in its original declaration, and the department claims this constitutes a false material particular. Annapurna's counsel argues: (a) the discharge certificate is conclusive and bars any further proceedings; (b) the one-year window for invoking the false-declaration clause has long expired. Can the department proceed on either ground — the subsequent-period SCN and the false-declaration allegation — and what are the precise legal limits on each?
questions.why_this_chunk: Section 129(2)(b)(i) explicitly carves out that a discharge certificate does not bar a SCN for the same matter for a subsequent time period; section 129(2)(c) sets the one-year clock for false-declaration proceedings from the date of discharge certificate — both are indispensable to resolving whether either departmental action survives the scheme.

questions.q: Meridian Pharma LLP (Hyderabad, Telangana) resolved a service tax dispute under the Sabka Vishwas scheme for alleged short-payment on contract research services rendered to overseas clients (Oct 2013–Jun 2017). The designated committee issued a statement for ₹62 lakh; Meridian paid and also withdrew a pending writ petition before the Telangana High Court, submitting proof of withdrawal to the designated committee within the prescribed time. The discharge certificate was issued in February 2020. In FY 2021-22, Meridian's GST auditor proposed to avail transitional credit (TRAN-1) for the service tax amount paid under the scheme, arguing it constitutes 'tax paid' eligible as opening credit. The CFO also wants to know whether, in a parallel customs dispute for the same underlying import transactions (for lab reagents) covering the same 2013–2017 period but on a different legal issue (mis-classification of goods), the department can still issue proceedings. Advise on (i) whether the SVLDRS payment can be treated as input tax credit or taken into TRAN-1, and (ii) whether the discharge certificate under the service tax stream shields Meridian from the customs mis-classification proceedings for the overlapping period.
questions.why_this_chunk: Section 130(1)(a) and (c) expressly prohibit the scheme payment from being taken as input tax credit or credited through any ITC account under any Act — directly answering the TRAN-1 question; section 129(2)(b)(ii) clarifies that a discharge certificate for one matter does not preclude proceedings for a different matter (customs mis-classification) for the same period — both provisions are essential and non-generic.

title: Appoints Common Adjudicator
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (ii) ]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification No.122 / 2008-Customs (N.T.)

New Delhi, the   12 th November, 2008

S.O.          (E). - In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 4 and sub-section (1) of section 5 of the
Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Central Board of Excise and Customs hereby appoints the Commissioner of Customs,
Nhava Sheva, Mumbai, to act as a common adjudicating authority to exercise the powers and discharge the duties conferred
or imposed on the Commissioner of Customs, Inter Container Depot, Tughlakabad, New Delhi, for the purpose of adjudicating
the matters relating to show cause notice pertaining to M/s. Vishal Hira Merchants Private Limited, 5/11, WEA Saraswati
Marg, Karol Bagh, New Delhi 110025 and others vide, DRI F.No. 50D/92/2006-CI, dated the 25 th April, 2008, by the
Additional Director General, Directorate General of Revenue Intelligence, 7 th Floor, I.P.Bhavan, I.P.Estate, New Delhi.

[F.No. 437/33/2008-Cus.IV]

(Aseem Kumar)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a specific common adjudicating authority appointment notification under Customs Act ss.4 and 5, designating the Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva to adjudicate a DRI show cause notice issued against M/s. Vishal Hira Merchants Pvt Ltd at Tughlakabad ICD. It authoritatively covers jurisdictional transfer of adjudication powers and the procedural legitimacy of cross-jurisdictional SCN adjudication — directly relevant to challenges on adjudicating authority competence.

questions.q: Our client M/s. Arjun Impex LLP, an importer operating through Tughlakabad ICD (New Delhi), has received a show cause notice issued by DRI New Delhi. The adjudication proceedings have now been transferred and the matter is being heard by the Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva, Mumbai — a port thousands of kilometres away from where the goods were assessed and the ICD where the client operated. The client's advocate wants to challenge the territorial jurisdiction of the Nhava Sheva Commissioner to adjudicate this SCN, arguing that only the jurisdictional Commissioner at Tughlakabad ICD has the power to hear the matter. Under what statutory mechanism can the Central Board appoint a Commissioner from a completely different Customs zone as a common adjudicating authority for a DRI-issued SCN, and does such an appointment validly override the ordinary territorial jurisdiction of the Commissioner at the ICD where the import transactions occurred?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly invokes sub-section (1) of section 4 and sub-section (1) of section 5 of the Customs Act as the statutory basis for appointing a cross-jurisdictional common adjudicating authority — exactly the mechanism whose validity the client's advocate is challenging. The specific appointment of Nhava Sheva Commissioner over Tughlakabad ICD matters is the factual and legal template for this question.

questions.q: M/s. Celestial Gems Pvt Ltd, GSTIN 07AABCC1234F1ZX, a diamond and precious stone dealer in Karol Bagh, New Delhi, imported rough diamonds through Tughlakabad ICD. DRI has issued a show cause notice alleging misdeclaration of value and quantity, naming Celestial Gems and three associated trading entities as noticees. The SCN was issued by DRI's New Delhi office but CBIC has appointed a Commissioner of Customs at a major western seaport as the common adjudicating authority to hear all four noticees together. Celestial Gems disputes this arrangement on two grounds: (a) the appointed Commissioner has no territorial nexus to Delhi-based ICD transactions, and (b) consolidating all four noticees before a single authority outside the originating jurisdiction violates natural justice since each noticee's facts differ materially. Analyse the statutory basis and limits of CBIC's power to make such a common adjudicator appointment, and whether the consolidation of multiple noticees before a single out-of-jurisdiction Commissioner can be challenged on natural justice or ultra vires grounds.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the direct precedent: it shows CBIC doing exactly this — appointing Nhava Sheva Commissioner over a Tughlakabad ICD DRI SCN involving multiple noticees (M/s. Vishal Hira Merchants and 'others'), under ss.4 and 5 of the Customs Act. Both the multi-noticee consolidation pattern and the cross-jurisdictional appointment are drawn verbatim from this notification.

title: Regarding Authorized Economic Operator (AEO) Programme
text:
Circular No. 21/2015

F.No. 450/179/2009-Cus IV (Pt)

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

(Central Board of Excise and Customs)

New Delhi, dated: 19.08.2015

To,

(i)             All  Chief Commissioners of Customs/ Customs (Prev.)

(ii)            All Chief Commissioners of Customs and Central Excise

(iii)           All Commissioners of Customs/ Customs (Prev.)

(iv)          All Commissioners of Customs and Central  Excise

(v)           All Director Generals under CBEC

Sir/Madam,

Subject: Authorized Economic Operator (AEO) Programme -regarding

Reference is invited to Board Circular No 28/2012-Customs dated 16.11.2012 which lays down detailed guidelines on
processing, validation and certification under the Authorized Economic Operator (AEO) Programme.

2.         Para 15.5 of the Board Circular No. 28/2012- Customs dated 16.11.2012 stipulates that the validity of certificate
of AEO status holders shall be for three years.
3.         References have been received in the Board requesting therein that in case of validity of certificates being valid
for three years, the fresh application for AEO and causing fresh verification as per laid down standards conditions should
not be insisted upon as periodical post certification review indicate their continued adherence to the laid down
guidelines.
4.         The matter has been examined. In this regard, it is seen that as per the Board's guidelines, the post certification
review is done on yearly basis for AEO status holders. Therefore, in order to reduce transaction cost and for ease of
doing business, Board has decided that validity of AEO certificate shall be normally for a period of five year or for further
period as extended by DGICCE, subject to yearly review of the same by AEO Programme Manager as this will obviate
the necessity of filing fresh applications and causing fresh verifications after three years in respect of the AEO status
holders.
5.         Para 15.5 of Board Circular No. 28/2012-Customs dated 16.11.2012
6.         Difficulty faced, if any, may be brought to the notice of the Board.

Yours faithfully

(Pawan Khetan)

OSD Cus-IV
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the extension of AEO certificate validity from 3 years to 5 years (extendable by DGICCE) with yearly review, under the AEO Programme. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving AEO status continuity, customs clearance benefits, and procedural implications for importers/exporters with time-sensitive operations.

questions.q: Nexus Pharma Exports Pvt Ltd, based in Ahmedabad (Gujarat), holds AEO-T2 certification that was issued on 10-March-2022 under the AEO Programme. The company is negotiating a 3-year export contract with a buyer in Germany starting April 2026, and their customs broker has advised that the AEO certificate will expire in March 2025 — requiring a fresh application with full re-verification before the contract period begins. The CFO is alarmed because a previous re-verification took 8 months and disrupted shipments. A legal consultant has now raised a counter-view that a 2015 circular superseded the 3-year validity rule and the certificate may actually remain valid through 2027 subject to yearly review, eliminating the need for fresh filing. Which position is correct, what is the operative validity period, who has authority to extend it further, and what periodic compliance obligation must Nexus Pharma satisfy to keep the certificate alive during the contract period?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the direct authority that changed AEO certificate validity from 3 years (Circular 28/2012 Para 15.5) to 5 years extendable by DGICCE with yearly review — the core dispute in the question turns entirely on this amendment and the yearly review obligation it imposes.

questions.q: Brightline Logistics LLP, an AEO-LO certified entity in Chennai operating as a customs broker, was granted its AEO certificate on 01-June-2021. In February 2026, a major FMCG client — Sunfresh Foods Pvt Ltd — is evaluating whether to route all its import clearances exclusively through Brightline, partly on the assurance that Brightline's AEO-LO status guarantees faster examination-free clearances. Sunfresh's in-house counsel has flagged two concerns: (a) Brightline's certificate will allegedly expire in June 2024 under the 3-year rule and they may be operating with a lapsed certificate, and (b) even if valid, whether yearly reviews constitute a condition precedent such that a missed review would retroactively invalidate clearances already done under the AEO benefits. Advise Sunfresh on the validity status of Brightline's certificate as of February 2026, and whether a missed or delayed yearly review by the AEO Programme Manager would void the certificate or merely trigger a show-cause / suspension process.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes the 5-year validity (overriding the earlier 3-year rule) and specifies that continued validity is 'subject to yearly review by AEO Programme Manager' — both the validity period calculation and the legal effect of a missed review depend directly on the language of this circular.

title: Handling of Cargo in Customs Areas Regulations, 2009
text:
with the permission in writing of the proper officer;

(g)               not permit any export cargo to enter the customs area without a shipping bill or a bill of export having been filed with
the proper officer;

(h)               not permit any import cargo to enter the customs area or be unloaded therein without the import report or the import
manifest having been filed with the proper officer;

(i)                 be responsible for the safety and security of imported and export goods under its custody;

(j)                  be liable to pay duty on goods pilfered after entry thereof in the customs area;

(k)               be responsible for the secure transit of the goods from the said customs area to any other customs area at the
same or any other customs station in accordance with the permission granted by the proper officer;

(l)                  subject to any other law for the time being in force, shall not charge any rent or demurrage on the goods seized or
detained or confiscated by the proper officer;

(m)       dispose off in the manner specified and within a time limit of ninety days, the imported or export goods lying unclaimed,
uncleared or abandoned:

Provided that the period of ninety days may be extended by the Commissioner of Customs by such further period as may be
allowed, on sufficient cause being shown for delay in the disposal;

(n)        not make any alteration in the entry or exit points or boundary wall without the permission of the Commissioner of
Customs;

(o)       shall bear the cost of the customs officers posted by the Commissioner of Customs on cost recovery basis and shall
make payments at such rates and in the manner specified by the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance unless
specifically exempted by an order of the said Ministry;

(p)      shall observe the Central Government holidays as followed by the jurisdictional Customs formations and in case of any
variation in the working days, intimate the same to Commissioner of Customs and the trade, at least seven days in advance,
and

(q)       abide by all the provisions of the Act and the rules, regulations, notifications and orders issued thereunder.

(2)       The Customs Cargo Service provider approved for custody of imported or export goods and for handling of such goods
shall not lease, gift, sell or sublet or in any other manner transfer any of the premises in a customs area; or sub contract or
outsource functions permitted or required to be carried out by him in terms of these regulations to any other person, without the
written permission of the Commissioner of Customs.

(3)       The Customs Cargo Service provider shall publish and display the schedule of charges for the various services
provided by him in relation to the imported goods or export goods in the customs area.

7.   Power to relax and regulate : (1) if the Commissioner of Customs is satisfied that in relation to the custody and handling
of imported or export goods in a customs area, the Customs Cargo Service provider, for reasons beyond his control, is unable
to comply with any of the conditions of regulation 5, he may for reasons to be recorded in writing, exempt such Customs Cargo
Service provider from any of the conditions of regulation 5.

(2)       The Commissioner of Customs may regulate the entry of goods in a customs area for efficient handling of such goods.

8.         Commencement of operations :   The Customs Cargo Service provider approved for custody of imported or export
goods and for handling of such goods shall not commence any operations in a customs area for the first time unless the
Commissioner of Customs is satisfied that all the requirements of the Act have been fulfilled and grants permission to
commence the operations by an order in writing.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively governs the statutory obligations of Customs Cargo Service Providers (CCSPs) under the Handling of Cargo in Customs Areas Regulations, 2009 — specifically duties around pilferage liability, unclaimed goods disposal timelines, prohibition on rent/demurrage on seized goods, and prohibition on subletting/outsourcing without Commissioner approval. These provisions are indispensable in multi-party logistics disputes and compliance scenarios.

questions.q: Maersk Logistics India Pvt Ltd operates a CFS at Nhava Sheva (JNCH) under a CCSP licence. A consignment of 200 MT of steel coils imported by Tata International Ltd (IEC: 0312045678) was cleared by Customs on 14-Jan-2025, but Tata International failed to take physical delivery. Maersk has been storing the goods since then and, as of 15-Apr-2025, the goods remain unclaimed. Maersk wants to (a) auction the goods to recover demurrage charges accumulated since the date of customs clearance, and (b) subcontract the auction and disposal process to a third-party warehousing firm, QuickStore Logistics LLP, without approaching the Commissioner. A customs officer has also formally seized an adjacent consignment of electronics in the same shed under Section 110 of the Customs Act. Advise Maersk on: (i) whether it can charge demurrage on the seized electronics consignment; (ii) the mandatory timeline and approval requirements before it can dispose of the unclaimed steel coils; and (iii) whether it can subcontract the disposal to QuickStore without Commissioner approval, citing the applicable regulatory bar.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (l) directly bars the CCSP from charging rent/demurrage on seized/detained goods; clause (m) sets the 90-day disposal window with Commissioner extension proviso for unclaimed/abandoned goods; Regulation 7(2) sub-regulation on subletting/outsourcing without written Commissioner permission — all three are specific, operative rules in this chunk that are indispensable to the answer.

questions.q: Blue Anchor Freight Pvt Ltd, a CCSP operating the Air Cargo Complex at Kempegowda International Airport, Bengaluru, has entered into a revenue-sharing arrangement with SkyHandle Solutions LLP, whereby SkyHandle physically handles all export cargo entering the customs area on behalf of Blue Anchor. On 22-Mar-2025, a consignment of garments meant for export by Karnataka Exports Ltd (GSTIN: 29AABCK1234R1Z5, Shipping Bill No. 8876543) entered the air cargo complex but was later found short by 15 cartons after unloading from the export shed — the missing cartons were traced to SkyHandle's staff. The jurisdictional AC Customs has issued a show-cause notice to Blue Anchor holding it liable for duty on the pilfered goods and also flagging the revenue-sharing arrangement with SkyHandle as an unauthorized subletting of CCSP functions. Blue Anchor contends that SkyHandle is merely a 'service contractor' and not a sublettee, and that since the goods were export cargo, no duty is payable on pilferage. Analyse Blue Anchor's twin defences under the applicable customs cargo handling regulations.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (j) imposes duty liability on the CCSP for goods pilfered after entry into the customs area (covering export goods, not just imports); Regulation 7(2) prohibits subletting or outsourcing CCSP functions without written Commissioner permission, making the 'service contractor' characterisation legally vulnerable — both provisions are directly in this chunk and are the core of the legal analysis.

title: Classification of bura, makhana, mishri, hardas and battasas (patashas)
text:
a.           1996 (84) E.L.T. 221 (T) D.C.M. Shriram Industries Vs C CEx, Meerut

b.           2006 (203) E.L.T. 232 (T) C C. EX Aurangabad Vs. Shrijee Heavy Projects Works Ltd

c.           2007 (210) E.L.T. 681 (T) Atul Industries Vs. C C. Ex., Pune

9.          However, Tribunal has in case of S M Confectionery (1993 (66) ELT 469) decided that items like Chironjidana, Mishri,
Rewadi, Batasha are more appropriately classifiable as sugar confectionary under tariff heading 1704.90. But the dispute
before the Tribunal in this case was whether these sugar items are prasadam falling under chapter 21 or are  sugar
confectionary classifiable under chapter 17. Moreover since this decision failed to consider the implication of chapter notes
referred above and the decision of the Apex Court in case of Sakarwala, the decision is per incuriam and cannot be taken as
binding precedence.

10.         The HSN explanatory notes provide that:

a.            Refined sugar can be produced as crystalline substance in the form of small cube, loaves, slabs or sticks or regularly
moulded sawn or cut pieces. [Hardas are moulded products].

b.            Heading 1701 covers items like sugar candy consisting of large crystal produced by slow crystallization of
concentrated solutions of sugar. [Mishri is a type of candy manufactured by this process].

11.           In view of above discussion, it is evident that these products namely Ghadi Sakar (Mishri), Batasha (Pattasha),
Illichidana (Chironjidana), Makhana etc., are a form of sugar having more than 90% sucrose and therefore correctly classified
under item no 17019100 under the entry refined sugar.

12.          Now, therefore, following instructions/ directions are issued:

a.             The process of making bura, makhana, mishri, hardas and battasas (patashas) from sugar shall amount to
manufacture in terms of provisions of Section 2(f) of the Central Excise Act, 1944 and will be liable to pay excise duty.

b.             These products shall be classified under heading no 17019100, provided that they fulfil the conditions of sub-heading
note 1 and 2.

13.          Based on the above clarifications pending cases may be disposed of.

14.          Receipt of this order may please be acknowledged.

15.          Hindi version will follow.

(Ashima Bansal)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively establishes that bura, makhana, mishri, hardas, and battasas (patashas) constitute 'manufacture' under Section 2(f) of the Central Excise Act 1944 and are classifiable under heading 17019100 as refined sugar — directly resolving classification disputes and excise duty liability for these specific sugar confectionery items.

questions.q: Shree Prasad Mithai Bhandar, a proprietorship firm in Varanasi (UP) with Central Excise registration, manufactures mishri and battasas (patashas) from raw sugar purchased from a local mill. A show-cause notice has been issued demanding excise duty of ₹18,40,000 for the period April 2005 to March 2007, on the ground that the process of converting sugar into these products constitutes 'manufacture' under the Central Excise Act. The firm's advocate argues that since mishri and battasas are traditional religious/prasadam items, they should either be exempt as prasadam under Chapter 21 or, alternatively, that the process is not 'manufacture' at all because no new distinct article emerges. Given the Tribunal's conflicting decisions — including S M Confectionery (1993) classifying batasha as sugar confectionery under 1704.90, and the Apex Court's ruling in Sakarwala — how should the adjudicating authority rule on (i) whether the process amounts to manufacture, (ii) the correct tariff heading, and (iii) whether the S M Confectionery precedent can be relied upon by the noticee?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses all three issues: paragraph 12(a) settles that the process amounts to manufacture under Section 2(f); paragraph 12(b) settles the classification as 17019100; and paragraph 9 explicitly declares S M Confectionery per incuriam because it failed to consider Chapter Notes and the Sakarwala Apex Court decision — making the chunk the sole authoritative source for rejecting that precedent.

questions.q: Dhanvantari Confections Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer based in Pune (Maharashtra) with excise registration, produces makhana and hardas from refined sugar sourced from a supplier in Kolhapur. During an audit for FY 2006–07, the excise department contends that (a) these products fall under heading 17019100 as refined sugar (not 1704.90 as sugar confectionery claimed by the company), and (b) since the company had been clearing these goods without paying excise duty by classifying them under a nil-duty entry applicable to 'sugar confectionery not elsewhere specified,' the entire clearance of ₹42,15,000 is liable to duty along with interest and penalty under Rule 25. The company's consultant argues that hardas are moulded products distinct from sugar and should be classified as confectionery under 1704, and further that makhana's sucrose content was never tested. The department counter-argues using HSN explanatory notes. Advise Dhanvantari Confections on (i) the correct classification of hardas and makhana, (ii) whether HSN notes are binding in Indian tariff disputes, and (iii) the exposure to penalty given the company genuinely relied on a prior Tribunal ruling in its favour.
questions.why_this_chunk: Paragraph 10(a) of the chunk specifically cites HSN explanatory notes to establish that hardas are moulded products covered under heading 1701, and paragraph 10(b) establishes that mishri/candy-type items including makhana belong under 1701 — these HSN references are indispensable to resolving the classification dispute and are unique to this circular. Paragraph 11 further anchors liability by confirming >90% sucrose content as the determinative criterion.

title: Exempts certain materials required for the manufacture of the final goods from additional duty, safeguard duty and anti-dumping duty
claude_reasoning: This chunk from Notification 112/2009-Custom authoritatively covers (a) deemed export supply categories exempt from additional/safeguard/anti-dumping duty — specifically fertilizer plant capital goods (clause f), ICB-procured power/refinery project goods (clause h), Marine Freight Containers by EOUs (clause i), UN-funded project supplies (clause j), nuclear power project supplies (clause k) — and (b) key definitions of 'Materials' and 'Specified Chartered Accountant' under the notification. These provisions are specific enough to anchor HIGH-complexity practitioner questions.

questions.q: Bharat Fertilizers & Chemicals Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCB1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) is supplying a disassembled heat-exchanger assembly (CIF value ₹18 crore) and associated commissioning spares worth ₹2.2 crore to a new urea plant being set up by Krishnapatnam Agro Industries Ltd in Andhra Pradesh. The supply is being made through an International Competitive Bidding process, but the bid documents included Indian customs duties in the price build-up because the procurement team believed ICB exemptions only apply to imports. The plant developer is claiming deemed-export benefits and wants to know: (1) whether the spares at ₹2.2 crore qualify for the same duty exemptions as the capital goods, given the FoR value of the capital goods is ₹15.8 crore ex-works, and (2) whether the erroneous inclusion of customs duties in the ICB bid price disqualifies the supply from the exemption altogether. Please advise with reference to the applicable notification conditions.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (f) of the notification is directly indispensable: it caps spares eligibility at 10% of Free on Rail value of capital goods (10% of ₹15.8 cr = ₹1.58 cr, meaning ₹2.2 cr spares partially exceed the cap), and it mandates ICB 'without including the duties of customs' — the erroneous inclusion in bid price is a direct clause (f) compliance failure.

questions.q: Sea-Tainer Fabricators EOU Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 33AACTS5678G1ZK, Tamil Nadu), a domestic freight container manufacturer registered as an Export Oriented Unit, supplies 120 marine freight containers worth ₹9.6 crore to a shipping line in JNPT (Maharashtra) in January 2026. As per the contract, the containers are to be loaded onto vessels and exported by the shipping line within 5 months. However, due to port congestion and a vessel scheduling dispute, 18 containers are still lying at JNPT in June 2026 — beyond the standard 6-month window. The shipping line approaches the Deputy Commissioner of Customs, JNPT, for an extension, but the DC refuses on the ground that the containers were supplied by the EOU to a domestic shipping line (not directly exported by the EOU), and therefore Sea-Tainer cannot claim the deemed export exemption at all. Advise Sea-Tainer on (a) whether the DC's jurisdictional objection on 'who must export' is sustainable under the notification, (b) the correct procedure to seek extension of the 6-month period, and (c) the duty consequences if the extension is denied and the containers are not exported.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (i) is the controlling provision: it specifies that the EOU (domestic freight container manufacturer) supplies the containers and they must be 'exported out of India within six months or such further period as may be permitted by the Deputy Commissioner of Customs or the Assistant Commissioner of Customs' — the DC's authority to grant or deny extension, who bears the export obligation, and the 6-month trigger are all exclusively located in this clause.

title: This Notification imposes Anti-Dumping duty on Optical Fibre originating in, or exported from Korea RP.
text:
ther exporters
8.96

2.    The anti-dumping duty imposed under this notification shall be levied with effect from the date of imposition of the
provisional anti-dumping duty i.e. the 29th December, 1999 and shall be payable in Indian currency.

Explanation - For the purposes of conversion of the amount of duty specified in column (2) of the Table above into Indian
currency, the "rate of exchange" applicable shall be the rate notified by the Central Government under sub-clause (i) of clause
(a) of sub-section (3) of section 14 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962) and in force on the date on which the bill of entry is
presented under section 46 of the said Customs Act.

(Prashant Kumar Sinha)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

F.NO.354/176/99-TRU

Index
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the retroactive levy of anti-dumping duty on Optical Fibre from Korea RP (effective 29-Dec-1999) and the specific exchange-rate mechanism under Customs Act s.14(3)(a)(i) applied at date of bill-of-entry presentation — two highly specific rules that drive HIGH-complexity queries on duty liability computation and retrospective demand scenarios.

questions.q: Fibrelink Communications Pvt Ltd (Chennai, GSTIN 33AABCF1234A1Z5) imported 500 MT of single-mode optical fibre from a Korean manufacturer (origin: Korea RP) under a CIF contract signed in October 1999. The goods arrived at Chennai Port and the bill of entry was filed on 18-February-2000. The Korean USD invoice shows USD 8.50/km. Between the date the provisional anti-dumping duty was imposed and the date of filing the bill of entry, the RBI reference rate moved from ₹43.50 to ₹44.80 per USD. The importer's customs broker argues that (a) the anti-dumping duty should be computed at the exchange rate prevailing on the date provisional duty was imposed (29-Dec-1999), not on the date of bill-of-entry presentation, and (b) since the final notification came after the goods arrived, no retrospective demand can stand. Is the broker's position correct? Which exchange rate applies, and what is the precise legal basis for computing the anti-dumping duty amount in Indian rupees?
questions.why_this_chunk: The Explanation in the chunk explicitly mandates that the exchange rate for converting anti-dumping duty into Indian currency is the rate notified under s.14(3)(a)(i) of the Customs Act as in force on the date the bill of entry is presented under s.46 — directly refuting the broker's claim about the provisional-duty date rate. The chunk also establishes retroactive levy from 29-Dec-1999, making the retrospective-demand argument answerable only by reading this specific provision.

questions.q: Datalink Infrastructure LLP (Mumbai) is contesting a Show Cause Notice from the Customs department demanding differential anti-dumping duty on optical fibre imported from Korea RP across three separate consignments — bills of entry filed on 05-Jan-2000, 22-Mar-2000, and 10-Jun-2000 — totalling ₹1.8 crore in dispute. The LLP's argument is two-fold: first, that the anti-dumping duty notification was issued only in 2000 and cannot attach liability to goods cleared on 05-Jan-2000 when only the provisional order existed; second, that for the March and June 2000 bills, the exchange rate used by the department is incorrect because it applied the rate on the date of the notification gazette rather than the bill-of-entry date. The LLP also imports a mix of fibre from China and Korea RP on the same bills of entry and contends the department cannot apportion the anti-dumping duty selectively to the Korean-origin fibre without re-examining the entire consignment. Advise on (i) the validity of the retroactive demand for the January 2000 consignment, (ii) the correct exchange-rate anchor for the March and June consignments, and (iii) whether country-of-origin segregation on a mixed bill of entry affects anti-dumping duty liability.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's explicit retroactivity clause ('levied with effect from the date of imposition of the provisional anti-dumping duty i.e. the 29th December, 1999') is the sole legal basis for answering point (i). The Explanation tying exchange rate to the bill-of-entry presentation date under s.14(3)(a)(i)/s.46 of the Customs Act is indispensable for point (ii). Both facts are uniquely in this chunk and not derivable from any generic customs provision.

title: AmendsNotification No. 57/2002-Central Excise, dated 14th November, 2002
text:
Tariff Act, 1985 (5 of 1986) as in corresponding entry in column (2) of the said Table, and on finalization of the special rate,
necessary adjustments be made in the subsequent refunds admissible to the manufacturer in the month following the fixation of
such special rate.

(4) Where the Central Government considers it necessary so to do, it may-

(a) revoke the special rate or amount of refund as determined under sub-paragraph (2) by the Commissioner of Central Excise
or the Commissioner of Customs and Central Excise, as the case may be, or

(b) direct the Commissioner of Central Excise or the Commissioner of Customs and Central Excise, as the case may be, to
withdraw the rate so fixed.

Explanation :            For the purpose of this paragraph, the actual value addition in respect of said goods shall be calculated on
the basis of the financial records of the preceding financial year, taking into account the following:

(i)                  Sale value of the said goods excluding excise duty, Value Added Tax and other indirect taxes, if any, paid on the
goods;

(ii)                Less: Cost of raw materials and packing material consumed in the said goods;

(iii)               Less: Cost of fuel consumed if eligible for input credit under CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004;

(iv)              Plus: Value of said goods available as inventory in the unit but not cleared, at the end of the financial year;

(v)                Less: Value of said goods available as inventory in the unit but not cleared, at the end of the financial year
preceding that under consideration.

Special rate would be the ratio of actual value addition in the production or manufacture of the said goods to the sale value of
the said goods excluding excise duty, Value Added Tax and other indirect taxes, if any, paid on the goods.

(5)            The manufacturer shall be entitled to refund at the special rate fixed under sub-paragraph (2) in respect of all
clearances of excisable goods manufactured and cleared under this notification with effect from the date on which the
application referred to at sub-paragraph (1) was filed with the Commissioner of Central Excise or Commissioner of Central
Excise and Customs, as the case may be.

(6)            Where a special rate is fixed under sub-paragraph (2), the refund payable in a month shall be equivalent to the
amount calculated as a percentage of the total duty payable on such excisable goods, at the rate so fixed:

Provided that the refund shall not exceed the amount of duty paid on such goods, other than by utilization of CENVAT credit.'

2.            This notification shall come into force with effect from the 1 st day of April, 2008.

[F.No. 334/1/2008-TRU]

(S.Bajaj)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note:- The principal notification No. 57/2002-Central Excise, dated 14 th November, 2002 was published in the Gazette of
India, Extraordinary, vide number G.S.R. 765 (E), dated the 14 th November, 2002 and was last amended vide notification no.
11/2004-Central  Excise, dated 29 th January, 2004 published vide number G.S.R.  84 (E), dated the 29 th January, 2004.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the special rate mechanism for Central Excise refunds under Notification 57/2002 (as amended by 22/2008), specifically: how actual value addition is calculated from financial records, how special rates are fixed/revoked by Commissioners, the retroactivity of refund entitlement from application date, and the cap that refunds cannot exceed duty paid excluding CENVAT credit utilization. These rules are specific enough to anchor complex practitioner scenarios involving multi-year refund disputes and CENVAT interaction.

questions.q: Ganesh Organics Pvt Ltd, a small-scale manufacturer in Silvassa (UT of Dadra & Nagar Haveli) producing specialty pigments classifiable under Chapter 32 of the Central Excise Tariff, had been availing refund under Notification 57/2002-CE at the standard rate. In FY 2007-08, the company's actual value addition (computed from audited financials) worked out to 38%, significantly higher than the standard rate applicable. The company filed an application with the Commissioner of Central Excise, Daman in May 2008 seeking fixation of a special rate. The Commissioner fixed the special rate at 36% (not 38%, citing one adjustment). However, before disbursement of arrear refunds, the Central Government issued a direction to the Commissioner to withdraw the rate so fixed. Ganesh Organics contends: (a) refunds at the special rate are due from May 2008 (application date) retroactively for all clearances; (b) the Government's withdrawal direction is without jurisdiction since the rate was already fixed; and (c) the value addition calculation used by the Commissioner wrongly excluded closing inventory adjustments. Which of these three contentions is legally tenable under the amended notification, and how should the value addition formula be correctly applied — specifically, how are opening and closing inventory of uncleared goods treated in the formula?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-paragraphs (4)(b), (5), and the Explanation clauses (iv) and (v) are directly indispensable: (5) establishes retroactivity from application date; (4)(b) confirms the Central Government's power to direct withdrawal even after fixation; and the Explanation's inventory adjustment formula (add closing inventory, deduct preceding year's closing inventory) resolves the value-addition dispute.

questions.q: Malabar Cashew Processing LLP, a manufacturer in Kollam, Kerala, cleared excisable goods (roasted cashew kernels) under Notification 57/2002-CE and had been receiving monthly refunds at the standard rate. For the month of March 2008 (i.e., pre-April 2008), the total excise duty payable was ₹18,40,000, of which ₹11,20,000 was discharged by utilizing CENVAT credit on input packaging material and ₹7,20,000 was paid in cash. The special rate subsequently fixed for the LLP was 40%. The jurisdictional AC computed the March 2008 refund as 40% of ₹18,40,000 = ₹7,36,000. The LLP's consultant argues the refund should be capped at ₹7,20,000 (cash duty only), while the department argues the cap does not apply because the notification was amended only from 1 April 2008. Additionally, the LLP consumed HSD (high-speed diesel) as fuel — the department says HSD is ineligible for input credit under CENVAT Credit Rules 2004 and therefore its cost cannot be deducted in the value-addition formula. Advise on: (i) the correct refund cap for March 2008 clearances; and (ii) whether HSD cost should be excluded from the fuel-cost deduction in the value-addition formula.
questions.why_this_chunk: The proviso to sub-paragraph (6) — 'refund shall not exceed the amount of duty paid on such goods, other than by utilization of CENVAT credit' — is the precise cap rule resolving contention (i). The Explanation clause (iii) — 'cost of fuel consumed if eligible for input credit under CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004' — directly governs whether HSD (ineligible for CENVAT) can be deducted, resolving contention (ii). Both answers hinge exclusively on language in this chunk.

title: Ad-hoc exemption order for taxable service provided by the hotel or restaurant in the flood affected State of Uttarakhand.
text:
F.No.354/182/2013-TRU

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Tax Research Unit

*****

New Delhi, 17 th September, 2013

AD-HOC EXEMPTION ORDER NO.1/1/2013

Whereas the recent floods and landslides has caused extensive damage in the State of Uttarakhand and has adversely
affected the life of the common man in the state. There is a need to provide support to ensure sustenance for the local
population by revival of the hospitality industry;

And whereas taxable services provided in the State of Uttarakhand are chargeable to service tax;

Now therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 93 of the Finance Act, 1994 (32 of 1994), the
Central Government, on being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do and that there are circumstances of
exceptional nature as mentioned above, hereby exempts the following taxable service provided to any person in the State of
Uttarakhand, from the whole of service tax leviable thereon under section 66B of the Finance Act, 1994 (32 of 1994), namely:-

i.        Services by way of renting of a room in a hotel, inn, guest house, club, campsite or other commercial place meant for
residential or lodging purposes;

ii. Services provided in relation to serving of food or beverages by a restaurant, eating joint or mess

This exemption order is applicable for the above mentioned taxable services provided during the period 17 th September,
2013 to 31 st March, 2014.

(Raj Kumar Digvijay)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk is an ad-hoc service tax exemption order covering hotel/lodging and restaurant/food services in Uttarakhand for a specific period (17-Sep-2013 to 31-Mar-2014), grounded in the exceptional-circumstances power under section 93(2) of the Finance Act 1994. It supports HIGH-complexity questions because practitioners must reason about temporal applicability, the statutory basis for ad-hoc exemption vs. standard exemption notifications, and cross-period/cross-jurisdiction billing scenarios.

questions.q: Himalayan Retreats Pvt Ltd, a hotel chain with properties in Rishikesh (Uttarakhand) and Haridwar (Uttarakhand), entered into a corporate billing arrangement with a Delhi-based IT firm, TechAxis Solutions Pvt Ltd, under which TechAxis had pre-booked 20 rooms for its employees during October–November 2013 as part of a project near Tehri dam. The invoice was raised on 15-December-2013 after the employees checked out in November 2013. Himalayan Retreats' service tax consultant is now questioning whether the exemption from service tax on renting of rooms applies here given: (a) the period of stay falls within the exemption window but invoicing falls outside; and (b) TechAxis is a registered entity in Delhi, not Uttarakhand. Advise on the availability of the service tax exemption, specifically whether the 'provided in the State of Uttarakhand' condition is satisfied when the recipient is a Delhi company, and whether the point-of-taxation rules affect the exemption entitlement.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that the exemption applies to taxable services 'provided to any person in the State of Uttarakhand' during 17-Sep-2013 to 31-Mar-2014 — the interpretation of 'provided in the State' versus the recipient's location, and the temporal boundary of the order, are both directly anchored in this order's text.

questions.q: Devbhoomi Hospitality LLP operates a chain of dhabas and a mid-segment hotel under the same GSTIN (pre-GST: single service tax registration) in Dehradun, Uttarakhand. During January–March 2014, the LLP provided both lodging services (rooms rented at Rs. 2,800/night) and food/beverage services (restaurant billing of Rs. 14 lakhs). Post a service tax audit in 2016, the department contended that: (i) the ad-hoc exemption order was ultra vires because the LLP did not fall within 'hotel, inn, guest house, club, campsite' but was a roadside dhaba; and (ii) the exemption order, being ad-hoc under section 93(2), cannot override the charging section 66B and hence service tax demand must be confirmed for the full period. The LLP's advocate is preparing submissions. Analyse the validity of the department's contention on both grounds — specifically whether a dhaba qualifies as 'eating joint or mess' under the order, and whether an ad-hoc exemption under section 93(2) is legally competent to exempt a service chargeable under section 66B.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's operative text enumerates the exempted categories ('restaurant, eating joint or mess') and explicitly invokes section 93(2) of the Finance Act 1994 as the source of the ad-hoc power to exempt services chargeable under section 66B — both the statutory basis and the category definitions are indispensable to the LLP's defence.

title: Classification of Digital Still Image Video Camera - Admissibility of Notification benefits
text:
Circular No. 32/2007-Cus.

F. No. 528/96/2001-Cus.(TU)

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

159A, North Block, New Delhi-1.

Dated 10 th September, 2007.

To

All Chief Commissioners of Customs.

All Chief Commissioners of Customs & Central Excise.

Chief Departmental Representatives, CESTAT.

All Commissioners of Customs.

All Commissioners of Customs & Central Excise.

Subject:  Classification of Digital Still Image Video Camera - Admissibility of Notification benefits - reg.

***

It has come to the notice of the Board that divergent practices are being followed by field formations regarding classification of
'Digital Still Image Video Camera' and consequent import duty exemption benefits.  The doubt raised in the field formations
was whether 'digital camera with still image as well as moving image capability' would be eligible for exemption benefit under
the entry as 'Digital Still Image Video camera'.

2.         Prior to 1.1.2007, tariff Item 8525 40 00 of the Customs Tariff Act (CTA) 1975, covered "still image video cameras and
other video camera recorders; digital cameras". According to Harmonized System (HS) Explanatory Notes (Third edition,
2002), the apparatus of sub-heading 8525.40 consisted of a combination of video camera and a video recording or
reproducing apparatus; these apparatus record the images taken by the camera. All types of digital cameras perform these
functions.  With effect from 1.1.2007, based on HS 2007 changes, all digital cameras have been grouped under tariff item
8525 80 20 of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 as 'digital cameras'. However, 'Video Camera Recorders' are separately
classifiable under Tariff Item 8525 80 30.

3.         Import duty exemption was extended to 'Digital Still Image Video camera' falling under sub-heading 8525.40, vide entry
at Serial Number 314 of Notification No.21/2002-Customs dated 1.3.2002.  This exemption is presently covered under Serial
Number 13 of Notification No. 25/2005-Customs dated 1.3.2005. Since the notification entry at Sl. No. 314 of Notification
No.21/2002-Cus. dated 1.3.2002 used the words 'video' as well as 'still image', there was a doubt in the field formations
whether this entry covers such digital cameras which are capable of both still image photography as well as video film shooting.

4.         This issue was deliberated at the Conference of Chief Commissioners' on Tariff and Allied Matters in 2006, wherein it
was decided that only those digital cameras that  record still images on Video format are covered under the description of the
exemption entry i.e. 'digital still image video cameras'. However, to ensure uniformity, it was decided that the Board would
issue a circular in this regard.  The minutes of this Conference were circulated to the field formations.

5.         The WCO has clearly outlined the features of the Digital Video camera. Such cameras have been described as Digital
cameras that capture light images, convert them to digital electronic signals and then record such digital image data on internal
or removable media (built-in memory or diskettes). These images can be reproduced by connecting the camera to a video
monitor (or TV), an ADP machine, or by inserting the media in the ADP machine. This type of digital cameras can produce
both video signals (e.g., NTSC, PAL, SECAM or other similar video format) and computer readable image data like MPEG.

6.         The term 'Video' relates to recording, reproducing or broadcasting of visual images. It is a technology of electronically
capturing, recording, processing, storing, transmitting, and reconstructing a sequence of still images representing scenes in
motion. The term "Still image video" camera covers the types of camera that take still images and stores them as single frame
of video. These single video frames, when reproduced at a specified frame rate achieve an illusion of a moving image. The
minimum frame rate is about fifteen frames per second. The term 'digital still image video camera' covers only digital cameras
that have the capability of taking still images. This would also include digital cameras that take moving images for limited
period of time although they are primarily still image cameras. Such cameras fall under tariff item 8525 80 20. However, digital
cameras that can take both still images and moving images like Camcorder or video recorder falling under Tariff item 8525 80
30 shall not be covered under the said entry.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the classification of Digital Still Image Video Cameras under the Customs Tariff Act 1975 and the admissibility of import duty exemption under Notification No. 21/2002-Customs / Notification No. 25/2005-Customs, including the specific interpretive ruling that only cameras recording still images in Video format qualify — making it ideal for HIGH-complexity classification-cum-exemption disputes.

questions.q: Precision Optics Imports Pvt Ltd, an IEC holder in Mumbai (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZX), imported a consignment of 500 units of Sony DSC-HX series cameras in March 2025, classifying them under CTH 8525 80 20 and claiming full exemption under the current Notification No. 25/2005-Customs Sl. No. 13 on the ground that these cameras are 'Digital Still Image Video Cameras'. The Mumbai Customs house has issued a SCN denying the exemption, contending that post-HS 2007 reclassification, only cameras that exclusively record still images in a video format qualify, not dual-mode cameras capable of shooting both still photographs and MP4 video clips. The importer argues that since the cameras can output NTSC/PAL video signals and store images on removable media viewable on a TV monitor, they squarely fall within the WCO description of a 'Digital Still Image Video Camera'.
