Which category of cameras actually qualifies for the exemption benefit, and does the post-2007 tariff restructuring affect the exemption eligibility of a dual-mode camera that also produces MPEG/computer-readable image data?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk provides the specific interpretive ruling from the Chief Commissioners' Conference 2006 — that only cameras recording still images in Video format qualify — and the WCO description requiring NTSC/PAL/SECAM video signal output alongside MPEG capability, both of which are indispensable to resolving whether the dual-mode Sony cameras qualify.

questions.q: Digicam Distributors LLP, registered in Hyderabad (GSTIN 36AABFD5678G1ZY), imported 1,200 units of a Chinese OEM camera brand in January 2025. These cameras record still images internally in JPEG format and can also capture 1080p MP4 video clips, but they do NOT output NTSC, PAL, or SECAM signals and cannot be directly connected to a conventional TV monitor — they only connect to computers via USB or stream via Wi-Fi to smartphones. The Hyderabad Customs Commissionerate classified them under CTH 8525 80 20 ('digital cameras') but denied the import duty exemption under Notification No. 25/2005-Customs Sl. No. 13, arguing these are not 'Digital Still Image Video Cameras' since they lack video signal output capability. The LLP's advocate contends that since these cameras produce MPEG (a computer-readable image data format), they satisfy the WCO's own definition and should get the exemption. The department's CDR at CESTAT is relying on the Board's 2007 circular to oppose the claim. How should CESTAT weigh the WCO description against the Board's interpretive ruling, and does absence of NTSC/PAL/SECAM output disqualify a camera that produces MPEG from claiming the exemption benefit?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the WCO's precise technical criteria — NTSC/PAL/SECAM video signal output AND MPEG/computer-readable data — and the Board's ruling limiting eligibility to cameras recording still images in Video format, both of which are central to adjudicating whether MPEG-only output suffices for the exemption.

title: The Finance Act, 2020 (12 of 2020)
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Finance Act 2020 amendments to the Customs Tariff Act First Schedule, substituting specific basic customs duty rates for footwear (Ch.64), household articles (Ch.69, 70, 73, 74, 76), compressors/fans (Ch.84), and other goods — making it the definitive source for post-Budget 2020 BCD rates on these items, which directly affect import cost calculations, SVB assessments, and IGST on imports.

questions.q: Stride Footgear Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1ZX, Mumbai) imports a consignment of waterproof rubber-soled boots (HS 6401 10 10) and leather dress shoes (HS 6403 59 10) from a Chinese supplier under an FOB contract dated 20-Mar-2020, with the Bill of Lading dated 12-Apr-2020 and the Bill of Entry filed on 18-Apr-2020. The supplier's invoice shows USD 85,000 for the entire lot. The company's customs consultant is claiming the pre-Budget BCD rate of 25% applies because the purchase order was raised before the Finance Act received Presidential assent. The DRI officer contends the amended 35% rate applies. Separately, the company also imports shoe parts (HS 6406 10 90) for its domestic assembly unit and argues those should attract only 20%. Please advise on (a) which BCD rate applies to each HS code given the date of filing, (b) the consequential IGST on imports calculation under the IGST Act read with the Customs Tariff Act, and (c) whether the increased BCD forms part of the assessable value for levy of Social Welfare Surcharge.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes the BCD rate to 35% for all tariff items under headings 6401–6405 and 20% for heading 6406 — these exact amended rates and the heading-level specificity are indispensable to determining the correct applicable duty and resolving the rate-change timing dispute.

questions.q: Bharat Kitchenware LLP, a Chennai-based importer (GSTIN 33AABFB5678G1ZY), clears a mixed consignment from a Vietnamese exporter at Chennai Sea Port on 02-May-2020. The consignment includes: (i) stainless steel kitchen articles — ladles, skimmers, and tongs (HS 7323 93 00) worth INR 12,00,000 CIF; (ii) copper table and kitchen articles under sub-heading 7418 10 (HS 7418 10 10) worth INR 8,50,000 CIF; and (iii) aluminium cooking pots under sub-heading 7615 10 (HS 7615 10 10) worth INR 6,20,000 CIF. The importer holds an Advance Authorisation for the copper articles and claims BCD exemption under Customs Notification 18/2015 for that portion. The assessing officer disputes whether the pre-amendment or post-amendment BCD rate should form the 'normal rate' for the purpose of computing the differential duty under the Advance Authorisation condition. Additionally, Bharat Kitchenware's parent company in Delhi wishes to transfer the aluminium goods on stock transfer to its Tamil Nadu warehouse — advise on the customs duty liability on each product line, the correct 'standard rate' to use for AA differential duty computation on the copper articles, and the GST implications of the subsequent stock transfer.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes BCD to 20% for all items of heading 7323, sub-heading 7418 10, and sub-heading 7615 10 — these three specific rate changes are each individually necessary to compute the duty liability on the three product lines and to determine the 'normal rate' baseline for the Advance Authorisation differential duty question.

title: Seeks to amend notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 36/2012-Service Tax dated the 20th June, 2012
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

CORRIGENDUM

New Delhi, the 2 nd July, 2012

G.S.R......(E) .- In the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 36/2012-
Service Tax dated the 20 th June, 2012 published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide
number G.S.R.478 (E), dated the 20 th June 2012,in rule 6A, in sub-rule (1), in clause (f),-

for "Explanation 2", read "Explanation 3".

[F. No. 334 /01/2012- TRU]

(Vikas)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a corrigendum to Notification 36/2012-ST, correcting a cross-reference in Rule 6A(1)(f) from 'Explanation 2' to 'Explanation 3' — a narrow typographical fix in the export-of-services rule. While it can anchor questions about export of services under the pre-GST regime and the effect of corrigendum corrections on compliance, it is extremely narrow and procedural, with no substantive rule content beyond a single cross-reference correction.

questions.q: Meridian Consulting LLP (registered in Mumbai under pre-GST Service Tax, ST registration MH-2011-99887) provided management consultancy services to a Singapore-incorporated client, Nexus Holdings Pte Ltd, during May–June 2012. The consideration was USD 50,000 remitted via wire transfer. The LLP's CA is now responding to a show-cause notice issued in 2015 alleging that the services did not qualify as 'export of services' because the place of performance was India and the conditions under Rule 6A(1) were allegedly not met — specifically, the SCN cites clause (f) read with 'Explanation 2' as defining 'payment in convertible foreign exchange.' The LLP's CA argues the SCN itself contains an error because the Gazette text of Notification 36/2012-ST was corrected by a subsequent corrigendum. Which Explanation actually governs clause (f) of Rule 6A(1) as corrected, and how does this affect whether the remittance condition is satisfied and the zero-rate benefit under Rule 6A applies to Meridian's invoices?
questions.why_this_chunk: The corrigendum in this chunk specifically corrects the cross-reference in Rule 6A(1)(f) from 'Explanation 2' to 'Explanation 3' — the SCN's reliance on 'Explanation 2' is precisely the error this chunk rectifies, making it indispensable to the CA's defense.

questions.q: Apex IT Solutions Pvt Ltd (Chennai, pre-GST ST registration TN-2012-44521) exported software testing services to a US-based client, TechVentures Inc., invoiced in USD in June 2012. During a Service Tax audit in 2016, the auditor disallowed the export exemption on the ground that the original Notification 36/2012-ST (G.S.R.478(E) dated 20-Jun-2012) referenced 'Explanation 2' in Rule 6A(1)(f), and Apex's foreign exchange receipt documentation matched Explanation 3, not Explanation 2 — arguing the company relied on the wrong explanation. Apex's counsel contends that a corrigendum issued on 2nd July 2012 retroactively corrected this very cross-reference. Does the corrigendum operate retrospectively from the date of the original notification (20-Jun-2012), and if so, was Apex's June 2012 export invoice correctly zero-rated from inception, defeating the audit objection entirely?
questions.why_this_chunk: This chunk is the corrigendum itself — issued 2-Jul-2012 correcting G.S.R.478(E) of 20-Jun-2012 — and the precise legal question of its retrospective operation and which Explanation applies to clause (f) is only answerable by reference to this corrigendum's text and date.

title: Certificate action under clause (e) of sub-section (1) section 79
text:
2. It may be noted that additional liability for the FY 2017-18 not declared in FORM GSTR-4 
may be declared in this return.

3. Part I consists of basic details of taxpayer. The instructions to fill Part I are as follows :

Table No. 
Instructions

5 
Aggregate turnover for the previous financial year is the turnover of the financial year 
previous to the year for which the return is being filed. For example for the annual 
return for FY 2017-18, the aggregate turnover of FY 2016-17 shall be entered into 
this table. It is the sum total of turnover of all taxpayers registered on the same PAN.

4. Part II consists of the details of all outward and inward supplies in the financial year for 
which the annual return is filed. The instructions to fill Part II are as follows:

Table No. 
Instructions

6A 
Aggregate value of all outward supplies net of debit notes / credit notes, net of 
advances and net of goods returned for the entire financial year shall be declared 
here. Table 6 and Table 7 of FORM GSTR-4 may be used for filling up these details.

6B 
Aggregate value of exempted, Nil Rated and Non-GST supplies shall be declared 
here.

7A 
Aggregate value of all inward supplies received from registered persons on which tax 
is payable on reverse charge basis shall be declared here. Table 4B, Table 5 and 
Table 8A of FORM GSTR-4 may be used for filling up these details.

7B 
Aggregate value of all inward supplies received from unregistered persons (other than 
import of services) on which tax is payable on reverse charge basis shall be declared 
here. Table 4C, Table 5 and Table 8A of FORM GSTR-4 may be used for filling up 
these details.

7C 
Aggregate value of all services imported during the financial year shall be declared 
here. Table 4D and Table 5 of FORM GSTR-4 may be used for filling up these 
details.

8A 
Aggregate value of all inward supplies received from registered persons on which tax 
is payable by the supplier shall be declared here. Table 4A and Table 5 of FORM 
GSTR-4 may be used for filling up these details.

8B 
Aggregate value of all goods imported during the financial year shall be declared 
here.

5. Part IV consists of the details of amendments made for the supplies of the previous financial 
year in the returns of April to September of the current FY or date of filing of Annual Return for 
previous financial year (for example in the annual return for the FY 2017-18, the transactions 
declared in April to September 2018 for the FY 2017-18 shall be declared), whichever is earlier. 
The 
instructions 
to 
fill 
Part 
V 
are 
as 
follows:

Table No. 
Instructions

10,11,12,13 
and 14

Details of additions or amendments to any of the supplies already declared in the 
returns of the previous financial year but such amendments were furnished in Table 
5 (relating to inward supplies) or Table 7 (relating to outward supplies) of FORM 
GSTR- 4 of April to September of the current financial year or upto the date of 
filing of Annual Return for the previous financial year, whichever is earlier shall be 
declared here.

198
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the GSTR-9A annual return filing instructions for composition scheme taxpayers, specifically how to report aggregate turnover (using prior-year PAN-consolidated figures), reverse-charge inward supplies from registered/unregistered persons, imported goods/services, and late amendments declared in April–September of the subsequent year. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving composition dealers who also have RCM obligations or import transactions, and reconciliation of GSTR-4 data with the annual return.

questions.q: Shree Balaji Traders, a proprietorship registered under the GST composition scheme in Rajasthan (GSTIN 08AABCS1234A1Z5), had an aggregate turnover of ₹1.2 crore in FY 2016-17 across two firms on the same PAN. In FY 2017-18, one of those firms de-registered mid-year. While filing the GSTR-9A for FY 2017-18, the accountant is confused about (a) which turnover figure to enter in the 'aggregate turnover for the previous financial year' field — whether it should be only the surviving firm's FY 2016-17 turnover or the combined PAN-level figure — and (b) how to report ₹3.8 lakh worth of legal consultancy services sourced from an unregistered advocate in Mumbai on which reverse charge applies, since those entries were omitted from the quarterly GSTR-4 filings but discovered during annual reconciliation. Can the additional RCM liability be declared in GSTR-9A itself, and if so, under which table?
questions.why_this_chunk: Table 5 instruction explicitly states aggregate turnover must be the PAN-level sum of all registered taxpayers for the previous FY; para 2 explicitly permits additional liability for FY 2017-18 not declared in GSTR-4 to be declared in the annual return; Table 7B covers RCM on inward supplies from unregistered persons.

questions.q: Meridian Handicrafts LLP, a composition taxpayer in Uttar Pradesh (GSTIN 09AABFM4567B1ZQ), imported raw silk fabric worth USD 18,000 (CIF) from China in November 2017 and also received IT support services from a Singapore-based vendor invoiced at USD 4,200 in January 2018 — both transactions were reported in the respective GSTR-4 quarterly returns for those periods. However, in July 2018 the LLP's CA discovered that a credit note received from a registered domestic supplier in March 2018 (relating to FY 2017-18 outward supply adjustments) was erroneously entered in the October 2018 GSTR-4 instead of being captured in the annual return for FY 2017-18. When filing GSTR-9A for FY 2017-18, how should the LLP (i) report the imported goods and imported services in the correct tables, (ii) treat the net outward supply figure given the misallocated credit note, and (iii) determine the cut-off date for including the amendment — specifically whether the July 2018 discovery window is still open or whether the October 2018 GSTR-4 entry governs?
questions.why_this_chunk: Table 8B governs reporting of imported goods; Table 7C governs imported services; Table 6A covers net outward supplies after credit notes; Part IV / Tables 10-14 govern amendments declared in GSTR-4 of April–September of the current FY or date of filing of annual return, whichever is earlier — directly resolving the cut-off date dispute.

title: Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
text:
unless recovered under the existing law, be recovered as an arrear of tax under this Act and the amount so recovered shall not
be admissible as input tax credit under this Act;

(b) where in pursuance of an assessment or adjudication proceedings instituted, whether before, on or after the appointed day,
under the existing law, any amount of tax, interest, fine or penalty becomes refundable to the taxable person, the same shall be
refunded to him in cash under the said law, notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in the said law other than the
provisions of sub-section (2) of section 11B of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944) and the amount rejected, if any, shall not
be admissible as input tax credit under this Act.

(9) (a) where any return, furnished under the existing law, is revised after the appointed day and if, pursuant to such revision, any
amount is found to be recoverable or any amount of CENVAT credit is found to be inadmissible, the same shall, unless
recovered under the existing law, be recovered as an arrear of tax under this Act and the amount so recovered shall not be
admissible as input tax credit under this Act;

(b) where any return, furnished under the existing law, is revised after the appointed day but within the time limit specified for
such revision under the existing law and if, pursuant to such revision, any amount is found to be refundable or CENVAT credit is
found to be admissible to any taxable person, the same shall be refunded to him in cash under the existing law, notwithstanding
anything to the contrary contained in the said law other than the provisions of sub-section (2) of section 11B of the Central Excise
Act, 1944 (1 of 1944) and the amount rejected, if any, shall not be admissible as input tax credit under this Act.

(10) Save as otherwise provided in this Chapter, the goods or services or both supplied on or after the appointed day in
pursuance of a contract entered into prior to the appointed day shall be liable to tax under the provisions of this Act.

(11) (a) notwithstanding anything contained in section 12 , no tax shall be payable on goods under this Act to the extent the tax
was leviable on the said goods under the Value Added Tax Act of the State;

(b) notwithstanding anything contained in section 13 , no tax shall be payable on services under this Act to the extent the tax was
leviable on the said services under Chapter V of the Finance Act, 1994 (32 of 1994);

(c) where tax was paid on any supply both under the Value Added Tax Act and under Chapter V of the Finance Act, 1994 (32 of
1994), tax shall be leviable under this Act and the taxable person shall be entitled to take credit of value added tax or service tax
paid under the existing law to the extent of supplies made after the appointed day and such credit shall be calculated in such
manner as may be prescribed.

(12) Where any goods sent on approval basis, not earlier than six months before the appointed day, are rejected or not
approved by the buyer and returned to the seller on or after the appointed day, no tax shall be payable thereon if such goods are
returned within six months from the appointed day:

Provided that the said period of six months may, on sufficient cause being shown, be extended by the Commissioner for a
further period not exceeding two months:

Provided further that the tax shall be payable by the person returning the goods if such goods are liable to tax under this Act,
and are returned after a period specified in this sub-section:
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers CGST Act transitional provisions (Sections dealing with the appointed day): recovery of pre-GST dues, refunds under existing law, revised returns post-appointed day, double taxation relief (VAT + Service Tax), contracts straddling the appointed day, and goods sent on approval basis. These provisions are highly specific to the GST transition and support complex practitioner scenarios involving pre-GST assessments, cross-act interactions (Central Excise/VAT/Service Tax vs CGST), and time-sensitive approval-basis goods.

questions.q: Precision Castings Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234Q1ZK, Maharashtra) had a Central Excise assessment pending for FY 2015-16 which was adjudicated in September 2025, resulting in a demand of ₹18.5 lakhs in excise duty plus ₹3.2 lakhs interest. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) under which law and mechanism will this amount be collected — will it appear as a GST demand or a Central Excise demand, (b) once paid, can this ₹18.5 lakhs be claimed as input tax credit under CGST to offset current output tax liability, and (c) if the adjudication had instead resulted in a refund of ₹6 lakhs wrongly paid excise duty, in what form would Precision Castings receive that refund — cash or credit ledger — and would the rejected portion of the refund claim be available as ITC?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-sections 8(a) and 8(b) directly answer all three parts: amounts recoverable from pre-GST adjudications are recovered as arrears under CGST Act but explicitly cannot be claimed as ITC; refunds from pre-GST adjudications must be paid in cash under the existing law (subject to section 11B of Central Excise Act 1944 carve-out), and rejected refund amounts are also not admissible as ITC.

questions.q: Bhavani Textiles LLP (GSTIN 33AABFB5678M1ZP, Tamil Nadu) entered into a long-term supply contract in January 2017 with a Delhi-based buyer for supply of cotton yarn. Under that contract, goods worth ₹40 lakhs were dispatched on approval basis in March 2017 — i.e., before GST's appointed day of 1-July-2017 — and VAT at 5% (₹2 lakhs) was paid by Bhavani. The buyer rejected the entire consignment and returned the goods to Bhavani in August 2017. Simultaneously, Bhavani's accountant discovered that the March 2017 VAT return had understated turnover, and after revision of that return in September 2017, an additional ₹80,000 CENVAT credit was found admissible. Advise Bhavani on: (i) whether GST is payable on the returned goods, (ii) what is the last permissible date for the goods to be returned to avoid GST liability, (iii) whether the Commissioner can grant any extension and on what grounds, and (iv) how the ₹80,000 additional CENVAT credit from the revised return will be treated — cash refund or ITC under CGST?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-section 12 and its provisos govern the approval-basis goods scenario (6-month window from appointed day = 31-Dec-2017, with up to 2-month Commissioner extension on sufficient cause); sub-section 9(b) governs the revised return scenario — additional CENVAT credit found admissible on a return revised within time limit must be refunded in cash under the existing law, not as ITC under CGST.

title: Implementation of automation in the Customs (Import of Goods at Concessional Rate of Duty) Rules, 2017 with effect from 01.03.2022.
text:
Annexure-II

BOND ADDENDUM WITH PROVISION FOR ADDITION OF SECURITY

KNOW ALL MEN BY THESE PRESENTS THAT the Proper Officer of Customs has been

pleased to accept on behalf of the President of India the bond for the purposes of  import or 
export goods in accordance with notifications under section 25 of the Customs Act, 1962 read 
with the Customs (Import of Goods at Concessional Rate) Rules, 2017, executed by us vide 
request dated...........and assigned the Bond Number ..........

And

WHEREAS

I/We, the importer/exporter, [hereinafter called the obligor(s)], has/have deposited with the 
Principal Commissioner of Customs or Commissioner of Customs, as the case may be, 
at……… (location),  a security/bank guarantee of amount Rs............. for IEC No................ 
with reference No....... issued by ................ in favour of O/O Commissioner of 
Customs............  for the due observance of and performance of the terms and undertakings on 
my part and for being applied in part or full towards the indemnity hereby given in terms of the 
Bond aforementioned, or otherwise according to law. Further, the obligor undertakes to deposit 
the balance security, if required, as and when he desires to import or export under the above 
provisions.

.

NOW, IT IS HEREBY AGREED AND DECLARED that -

i. 
The President or the Proper Officer, without prejudice to any other mode of recovery 
may, inter alia, adjust the security deposit / securities in part or full towards the dues 
arising out of non-compliance of conditions in afore stated bond.

ii. 
In case the proceedings are not final, the obligor(s)…………..agree to renew the 
security/bank guarantee before 15 days of expiry of the security/bank guarantee, 
failing which, the bank guarantee shall be encashed by the department.

The Schedule of Securities above referred to

[Particulars of the amount deposited, along with consignments, if any]

Page 10 of 11
claude_reasoning: This chunk is the Bond Addendum (Annexure-II) under CIGCR Rules 2017, governing the security/bank guarantee mechanism for concessional duty imports. It authoritatively covers the obligor's undertaking to renew bank guarantees 15 days before expiry, the department's right to encash or adjust security against dues, and the bond's linkage to section 25 notifications — making it indispensable for questions about lapsed guarantees, duty recovery, and multi-consignment bond management.

questions.q: Precision Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1Z5, IEC: 0512034567) in Pune imports CNC machine parts from Germany under an end-use notification (Customs Notification No. 50/2017) with a bond and Rs. 25 lakh bank guarantee issued by HDFC Bank, valid until 30-Apr-2026. The company's procurement team forgot to renew the guarantee and on 15-Apr-2026 the Assistant Commissioner of Customs, Mumbai port, initiated encashment of the bank guarantee citing non-renewal. Meanwhile, a fresh consignment of Rs. 18 lakh CIF value is held up at JNPT. The CFO asks: (a) Was the department legally entitled to encash the bank guarantee before its actual expiry date, given that only 15 days' notice of non-renewal had passed? (b) Can the company simultaneously request release of the held consignment by depositing fresh security, and will that cure the default under the original bond? (c) What is the interplay between the bond conditions, section 25 of the Customs Act, and the CIGCR Rules 2017 in determining whether the encashment extinguishes the company's concessional duty entitlement entirely or only for that consignment?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (ii) of the Bond Addendum explicitly states the obligor must renew the bank guarantee '15 days before expiry' failing which 'the bank guarantee shall be encashed by the department' — this is the direct legal basis for the department's action and is indispensable to answering whether the encashment was premature or lawful.

questions.q: Aero Precision LLP (IEC: 0319045678), an EOU in Hyderabad, executes a single consolidated bond with a Rs. 40 lakh bank guarantee (SBI BG No. HYD/2024/00882) before the Principal Commissioner of Customs, Hyderabad, covering concessional duty imports of aerospace-grade aluminium alloy billets across multiple consignments during FY 2025-26. In March 2026, a customs audit flags that two consignments valued at Rs. 31 lakh (aggregated duty foregone Rs. 6.8 lakh) were diverted to non-EOU use, constituting non-compliance. Simultaneously the LLP applies for a new consignment clearance. The department proposes to: (i) adjust Rs. 6.8 lakh from the existing BG towards duty recovery, and (ii) demand a 'top-up' security for the fresh consignment before clearance. The LLP's advocate contends that adjusting the BG extinguishes the security available for pending proceedings and the department cannot simultaneously demand additional security. Analyze the legal validity of both department actions under the bond conditions, and advise whether the LLP must comply with the top-up demand even while contesting the duty recovery in adjudication.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (i) of the Bond Addendum grants the President/Proper Officer the right to 'adjust the security deposit/securities in part or full towards dues arising out of non-compliance,' while clause (ii) and the main operative paragraph together establish the obligor's continuing obligation to maintain adequate security 'as and when he desires to import' — both clauses are directly needed to resolve the LLP's dual legal challenge.

title: Amendments to the Central Excise (Compounding of Offences) Rules, 2005
text:
Circular No. 862 /20/2007-CX

F. No. 267/22/05-CX-8

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

***

New Delhi dated the December 27, 2007

To

The Chief Commissioners of Central Excise (All)

The Chief Commissioner of Customs (All)

The Commissioners of Central Excise (All)

The Commissioners of Customs (All)

The Director Generals of Custom and Central Excise (All)

Subject:  Amendments to the Central Excise (Compounding of Offences) Rules, 2005

***

Sir/ Madam,

The undersigned is directed to refer to notification No.42/2007-C.E.(N.T) dated 27.12.2007, wherein  amendments have been
carried out in the Central Excise (Compounding of Offences) Rules, 2005.

2.         Rule 5 of the said Rules has been amended to revise the compounding amount prescribed under the rules. Further, a
proviso has been inserted in this rule, which provides that if a person has, in respect of same goods, committed offences falling
under more than one category specified in this rule and where amount of duty evasion or amount of CENVAT Credit wrongly
taken or utilized is same for all such offences, the compounding amount, in such cases, shall be the amount determined for the
offence for which a higher compounding amount has been prescribed. The interpretation and scope of this proviso is explained
in the following illustrations;

a)      A non-registered person removes goods without payment of duty which results into committing of offences specified in
sub-section (1)(a), (1)(b) and (1)(bb) of section 9 of the Act. In this case, the offence specified under section 9 (1)(b) attracts
compounding amount, which may extend up to 50 % of the amount of duty evasion (specified at Sl. No. 2 in the table given in
the Notification) and this amount is higher than or equal to those prescribed for other two  offences. Therefore, the
compounding amount for all the said offences shall be determined under the said category of one offence [under section 9(1)
(b)] only.

b)      A person takes or utilizes CENVAT credit wrongly and also furnishes false information.  He, therefore, commits offence
specified under sub-sections (1)(bbbb) and (1)(c) of Section 9 of the Act. In this case, the offence specified under section 9 (1)
(bbbb) attracts compounding amount, which may extend up to 50% of the amount of CENVAT Credit wrongly taken or utilized.
This amount is higher than prescribed for other offence. Therefore, the compounding amount for all the said offences shall be
determined under the said category of one offence [under section 9(1) (bbbb)] only.

c)      A person removes goods without payment of duty of Rs. 10 lakh and also takes the CENVAT credit of Rs. 20 lakh
wrongly, thus committing offence under sub sections (1)(b), (1)(bb) and (1)(bbbb) of Section 9 of the Act. In this case, the
compounding amount shall be determined separately for,-

(i)    Removal of goods without payment of duty; and

(ii)   Wrong availment of CENVAT Credit,

as the goods involved and duty evasion amount are different for these offences.

3.         Further, in rule 4, a new provision has been added, whereby it has been provided that an applicant should pay duty,
penalty, and interest before submission of application for compounding. Correspondingly, the Application Form has also been
amended.

4.         In view of reduction of compounding amount, the assessees may be persuaded to opt for compounding route in more
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Central Excise compounding of offences rules, specifically the proviso in Rule 5 that governs how compounding amounts are calculated when a person commits multiple overlapping offences involving the same goods and same duty evasion amount — a nuanced rule that requires multi-provision reasoning and careful interpretation of the proviso's scope. It also covers the pre-payment requirement under Rule 4 before filing a compounding application.

questions.q: Precision Metals Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer in Pune (Maharashtra), was found by the Central Excise officers to have removed stainless steel sheets worth Rs. 45 lakh without payment of duty, resulting in duty evasion of Rs. 8.1 lakh. During the same audit, it was established that the company had also wrongly availed CENVAT credit of Rs. 8.1 lakh on inputs used in the manufacture of these very same sheets, and had simultaneously furnished false declarations to the Range Superintendent. The company now wants to compound all three offences and avoid prosecution. Their CFO is asking: (a) whether the compounding amount for all three overlapping offences will be consolidated or calculated separately, (b) what the maximum compounding amount exposure is, and (c) whether they must clear the outstanding duty, penalty, and interest before even filing the compounding application — or can they file first and pay later?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's illustration (a) and (b) directly govern the consolidation question: where duty evasion and CENVAT credit amounts are the same and relate to the same goods, only the higher compounding amount applies. Rule 4's pre-payment requirement (paragraph 3 of the circular) answers the procedural question about payment before application — both are indispensable to answering this multi-issue query.

questions.q: Apex Polymers LLP, a non-registered dealer operating out of Surat (Gujarat), clandestinely cleared HDPE granules on three separate occasions in March 2007, evading duty of Rs. 12 lakh in total. Separately, in the same period, the firm also wrongly availed and utilized CENVAT credit of Rs. 22 lakh on inputs attributable to a different product line, and furnished fabricated invoices to support the credit claim. The firm is now considering compounding under the Central Excise (Compounding of Offences) Rules, 2005 to avoid criminal prosecution. Their advocate needs to advise: (i) given that the duty evasion (Rs. 12 lakh) and CENVAT misuse (Rs. 22 lakh) involve different amounts and different goods, will the compounding amount be computed as a single consolidated figure or separately for each limb; (ii) which offence category will govern the CENVAT limb given the false invoices; and (iii) can the firm apply for compounding before arranging funds to pay the confirmed demand, interest, and penalty, since they are currently cash-strapped?
questions.why_this_chunk: Illustration (c) in the chunk is specifically on point for question (i) — where duty evasion and CENVAT credit amounts differ and involve different goods, compounding amounts must be determined separately for each limb. Illustration (b) addresses question (ii) — the false furnishing offence under s.9(1)(c) is subsumed into the higher CENVAT offence under s.9(1)(bbbb). Paragraph 3 (Rule 4 pre-payment requirement) directly answers question (iii).

title: Clarification on refund related issues. Rescinded vide Circular No. 125/44/2019 - GST dated 18.11.2019.
text:
Circular No. 79/53/2018-GST

the application till the date on which the amount is credited to the bank account of the

claimant. Accordingly, all tax authorities are advised to issue the final sanction orders in

FORM GST RFD-06 within 45 days of the date of generation of ARN, so that the

disbursement is completed within 60 days by both Central and State Tax Authorities for

CGST / IGST / UTGST / Compensation Cess and SGST respectively.

Refund applications that have been generated on the portal but not physically received

in the jurisdictional tax offices:

6. 
There are a large number of applications for refund in FORM GST RFD-01A which

have been generated on the common portal but have not yet been physically received in the

jurisdictional tax offices. With the implementation of electronic submission of refund

application, as detailed in para 2 above, this problem is expected to reduce. However, for the

applications (except those relating to refund of excess balance in the electronic cash ledger)

which have been generated on the common portal before the issuance of this Circular and

which have not yet been physically received in the jurisdictional offices (list of all

applications pertaining to a particular jurisdictional office which have been generated on the

common portal, if not already available, may be obtained from DG-Systems), the following

guidelines are laid down:

a) All refund applications in which the amount claimed is less than the statutory limit of

Rs. 1,000/- should be rejected and the amount re-credited to the electronic credit

ledger of the applicant through the issuance of FORM GST RFD-01B.

b) For all applications wherein an amount greater than Rs. 1000/- has been claimed, a

list of applications which have not been received in the jurisdictional tax office within

a period of 60 days starting from the date of generation of ARN may be compiled. A

communication may be sent to all such claimants on their registered email ids,

informing that the application needs to be physical submitted to the jurisdictional tax

office within 15 days of the date of the email. The contact details and the address of

the jurisdictional officer may also be provided in the said communication. The

claimant may be further informed that if he/she fails to physically submit the

application within 15 days of the date of the email, the application shall be summarily

rejected and the debited amount, if any, shall be re-credited to the electronic credit

ledger.

7. 
For the applications generated on the common portal before the issuance of this

Circular in relation to refund of excess balance from the electronic cash ledger which have

not yet been received in the jurisdictional office, the amount debited in the electronic cash

Page 5 of 10
claude_reasoning: This chunk from Circular 79/53/2018-GST authoritatively covers procedural timelines for refund sanction (45-day order + 60-day disbursement), handling of RFD-01A applications not physically received, the Rs. 1,000 statutory minimum threshold for refund claims, and the 15-day cure window before summary rejection. These specific procedural rules and thresholds anchor HIGH-complexity practitioner queries about refund timelines and legacy application handling.

questions.q: Nexgen Pharma Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFN1234M1Z5, Maharashtra) filed a refund application under IGST paid on zero-rated exports in FORM GST RFD-01A on the common portal on 10-March-2018 (ARN generated that day) claiming Rs. 48,00,000. The application was never physically submitted to the jurisdictional Commissionerate at Pune. The jurisdictional officer now (post-issuance of Circular 79/53) sends an email on 01-Nov-2018 asking the company to submit physically within 15 days. The company's CFO argues that (a) interest under Section 56 of the CGST Act should accrue from the date of ARN generation since the delay in processing is attributable to the department's failure to collect the physical application, and (b) the 45-day sanction timeline has already been breached, so the refund should be deemed sanctioned. Advise Nexgen Pharma on the merits of both arguments and the correct procedure from this point.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly prescribes: sanction orders must issue within 45 days of ARN generation; the 15-day physical submission cure window triggered by email before summary rejection; and that interest under Section 56 runs from the date of application till credit to bank account — all three are indispensable to evaluating both of Nexgen's arguments.

questions.q: Suncrest Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 24AABCS5678K1ZQ, Gujarat) had filed 12 separate refund applications in FORM GST RFD-01A on the GST common portal between January and August 2018 — 3 applications each claim Rs. 800 (CGST + SGST split), 7 applications claim between Rs. 1,200 and Rs. 4,50,000, and 2 applications relate solely to excess balance in the electronic cash ledger. None of the 12 applications were physically received by the jurisdictional CGST Division, Surat. The company's tax consultant now wants to know: which applications face mandatory rejection without further opportunity, which must go through the 15-day email-cure process, which are governed by a separate procedure entirely, and whether the interest clock under the refund provisions has been running since ARN generation across all 12 applications — and if so, from which date does it stop for each category.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes three distinct procedural tracks (sub-Rs. 1,000 → mandatory rejection via RFD-01B; above Rs. 1,000 → 60-day compile + 15-day email cure; electronic cash ledger → separate paragraph 7 procedure) and the interest accrual rule (ARN date to bank credit), all of which are necessary to correctly classify and advise on all 12 applications.

3. 
Proof of Principal Place of Business:

(a) For Own premises –

Any document in support of the ownership of the premises like latest Property Tax

applicant 
is 
an
