developer, 
necessary

documents/certificates issued by Government of India are required to be uploaded.

21
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GST registration document requirements for various entity types and premises types, including SEZ-specific requirements. It can anchor HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-entity registration scenarios (e.g., companies with SEZ units, LLPs with consent-based premises) where the document checklist interacts with entity constitution and place-of-supply rules.

questions.q: Nexus Pharma Pvt Ltd, incorporated in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1Z5), is setting up a new principal place of business in a Special Economic Zone in Kandla, Gujarat. The premises are leased from the SEZ developer, but the lease deed has not yet been executed — only a Letter of Approval (LOA) from the Development Commissioner is available. The company's CFO wants to apply for fresh GST registration for the Gujarat unit immediately to avoid disrupting input tax credit flow on capital goods imports. What documents must Nexus Pharma upload as proof of principal place of business for the Kandla SEZ unit, and does the absence of a registered lease deed trigger the affidavit route or the SEZ-specific government certificate route — and can both be submitted simultaneously as a belt-and-suspenders approach?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk expressly requires 'documents/certificates issued by Government of India' for SEZ premises and separately provides an affidavit route for rented/leased premises where a rent/lease agreement is not available — the question turns on which sub-clause applies when an LOA exists but no executed lease deed.

questions.q: Triton Logistics LLP, registered in Tamil Nadu, operates out of a warehouse in Chennai that is owned by a third-party (M/s. Sri Murugan Properties) but is neither formally rented nor leased — Triton uses it under a verbal consent arrangement. The LLP has four designated partners, one of whom (Mr. Harish Iyer) is also the authorised signatory. While applying for GST registration, the LLP's CA is uncertain: (a) whose photograph must be submitted — all four designated partners or only those ten including the managing partner, (b) whether a consent letter from Sri Murugan Properties along with the property owner's electricity bill suffices as proof of principal place of business, and (c) whether the absence of a formal partnership deed (since LLPs file an LLP Agreement, not a partnership deed) means the constitution-of-business document requirement is satisfied by the LLP incorporation certificate alone. Advise on all three document-related questions.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly specifies (i) photo requirements for LLPs — personal details of all partners but photos of only ten including managing/designated partner, (ii) the consent letter route with ownership proof for premises not covered by own/rented categories, and (iii) that constitution proof for LLPs is the Registration Certificate/Proof of Constitution, distinguishing it from partnership deeds.

title: General Bond (Form B-17) to be executed by the EOUs
text:
4. 
AND WHEREAS we the obligors having been permitted by the Deputy or

Assistant Commissioner of Customs or Central Excise at………………..to 
procure from time to time goods not exceeding the quantity listed in the 
Annexure-I as may be required for manufacture of goods in his factory 
annually in the manner specified without payment of whole of the duty.

5. 
AND WHEREAS the said Deputy or Assistant Commissioner of

Customs or  Central Excise has permitted the obligor to clear duty free 
imported goods from Ports/Airports/Inland Container Depots or Bonded 
Warehouses, as the case may be, to the export oriented unit.

6. 
AND WHEREAS the Deputy or Assistant Commissioner of Customs or

Central Excise or such other delegated authority, as the case may be, under 
Rule  19  of the Central Excise Rules, 2017 has permitted the obligors to 
remove the said goods from the said unit without payment of duty and 
dispatch the same by air, sea, rail or road for export to foreign countries 
without payment of duty/tax and when required bona fide to do so, or other 
EOUs/Warehouses subject to the prescribed conditions set out for the due 
arrival of the said goods at the said EOUs/warehouse.

7. 
AND WHEREAS the Deputy or Assistant Commissioner of Customs or

Central Excise has permitted the obligor to remove the goods sourced 
imported/domestically or goods partially manufactured or processed 
therefrom to any other place in India without payment of duty subject to 
such conditions and limitations as may be specified by him for the purpose 
of repair, refining, processing, testing, job work or display and to be 
returned to the unit thereafter.

8. 
AND WHEREAS the Deputy or  Assistant Commissioner of Customs

or Central Excise has permitted provisional assessment of goods 
manufactured by the above obligors from time to time which could not be 
finalized for want of full information as regard to value or description or 
quality or of the proof thereof or for the non-completion of the chemical or 
other tests in respect thereof or otherwise, as per the provisions contained 
in the Central Excise Rules, 2017 as per request of the obligor.

9. 
AND WHEREAS the Commissioner of Customs or Central Excise or

such other delegated authority, as the case may be, has required the 
obligor to deposit as security for the amount of this bond, the sum of 
Rs…………(Rupees………………….) in cash (the securities as hereinafter 
mentioned 
of 
a 
total 
value 
of

Rs…………………(Rupees………………………….) endorsed in favour of the 
President of India and accepted for and on behalf of the President of India 
by the Commissioner, Deputy Commissioner, Assistant Commissioner, 
Superintendent of Customs or  Central Excise, namely………..) and 
whereas the obligor has furnished such guarantee by depositing the 
cash/securities as afore mentioned.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers specific operative clauses of the B-17 General Bond executed by Export Oriented Units (EOUs), detailing permitted activities: duty-free procurement, clearance of imported goods, removal for export under Rule 19 of Central Excise Rules 2017, job-work/repair removals, provisional assessment, and security deposit obligations. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because practitioners advising EOUs must link these bond conditions to GST/Customs interplay, multi-jurisdiction supply chains, and specific permitted-use carve-outs.

questions.q: Apex Precision Components Pvt Ltd, an EOU in Pune (GSTIN 27AABCA1234F1Z5) manufactures aerospace fasteners. In March 2025, they sent a consignment of semi-processed titanium blanks to a job-worker, Shree Metalworks LLP in Nashik, for heat treatment, without payment of duty. The blanks were sourced from a bonded warehouse in JNPT. Six weeks later, 15% of the batch was found damaged at Shree Metalworks and could not be returned. The DC Customs, Pune is now asking Apex to show cause why the B-17 bond condition has not been breached and why duty should not be crystallised on the unreturned quantity. Apex's counsel argues that the bond only covers goods 'to be returned to the unit thereafter' and that a force-majeure clause in the job-work agreement extinguishes the duty liability. Does the bond instrument itself create an absolute obligation to return goods sent for job-work, and can a private contractual force-majeure clause override that bond obligation? What is the maximum financial exposure on the bond security already deposited?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 7 of the chunk is the precise operative provision permitting removal for job-work 'to be returned to the unit thereafter' — the question turns on whether 'thereafter' creates an unconditional return obligation and whether the bond security (clause 9) gets enforced on the unreturned quantity.

questions.q: Horizon Biotech Solutions Pvt Ltd is an EOU in Hyderabad (GSTIN 36AABCH5678K1ZQ) exporting pharmaceutical intermediates. In January 2025, they cleared a shipment of 500 kg of a controlled bulk drug by road to Chennai Port for export to a buyer in Germany. En route, the consignment was detained by GST intelligence officers in Nellore on grounds that the e-way bill did not reflect 'export without payment of IGST' status and that no bond-backed permission for road removal was endorsed on the documents. Horizon contends that their B-17 bond with the AC Customs, Hyderabad already covers removal by road for export under Rule 19 of Central Excise Rules 2017, and that no separate IGST bond/LUT is required because the EOU bond subsumes that obligation. The field officers disagree, arguing the B-17 bond is a legacy Central Excise instrument with no GST force. Who is correct, and what documentary evidence must Horizon produce at the check-post to secure release of the goods without paying IGST under protest?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 6 of the chunk explicitly authorises removal 'by air, sea, rail or road for export to foreign countries without payment of duty/tax' under Rule 19, Central Excise Rules 2017 — the dispute in the question is whether this bond language ('duty/tax') extends to IGST, making this chunk indispensable to resolving the jurisdictional and documentary dispute.

title: Regarding enforcement of Intellectual Property Rights on imported goods - Clarification on the issue of parallel imports
text:
(ii)            imported goods having applied thereto any 'false trade description' within the meaning of definition provided in
clause (i), in relation to any of the matters connected to description, statement or other indication direct or indirect of the
product but not including those specified sub- clauses (ii) and (iii) of clause (za), of sub-section (1) of section 2 of the Trade
Marks Act, 1999[for para (ii)].

Thus, the prohibition under the para (i) and (ii) of aforesaid Notification No.51/2010-Customs (NT) would be applicable only
when the imported goods fall within the purview of the above referred provisions of Trade Marks Act, 1999.

3.         In this context, the issue of permitting import of original/genuine products (not counterfeit or pirated) which are sold/
acquired legally abroad and imported into the country, by persons other than the intellectual property right holder without
permission/ authorisation of the IPR holder, known in the trade as 'parallel imports' was referred to the administrative Ministry
i.e., Department of Industrial Policy and Promotion (DIP&P), Ministry of Commerce & Industries, seeking their clarification.

4.         In this regard, the Department of Industrial Policy and Promotion which is nodal authority for all matters relating to (i)
Trade Marks Act, 1999 (ii) Patents Act, 1970 and (iii) Designs Act, 2000 has, interalia , stated that:

(i) Section 107A (b) of the Patents Act, 1970 provides that importation of patented products by any person from a person who
is duly authorised under the law to produce and sell or distribute the product shall not be considered as an infringement of
patent rights. Hence, in so far as Patents are concerned, Section 107A (b) provides for parallel imports.

(ii) Section 30(3)(b) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 provides that where the goods bearing a registered Trade Mark are lawfully
acquired, further sale or other dealing in such goods by purchaser or by a person claiming to represent him is not considered
an infringement by reason only of the goods having been put on the market under the registered Trade Mark by the proprietor
or with his consent. However, such goods should not have been materially altered or impaired after they were put in the market.

(iii) In so far as designs are concerned, it is clarified that parallel imports are not allowed as indicated by Section 22 (1)(b) of
the Designs Act, 2000.

(iv) As regards geographical indications, it is stated that there are no identical or similar provisions as in Section 107A(b) of
Patents Act, 1970 on parallel imports under the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999.
The said Act does not address the issue of parallel import at all. Hence, parallel imports are not covered under this Act.

(v) As regards 'copyright' since the clarification is awaited from the nodal authority i.e., Department of Higher Education, the
field formations may follow the extant provisions of the Copyright Act, 1957 until further instructions are issued in this regard.

5.1.       In view of the above, the field formations are directed to decide cases of import of 'parallel imports' on the basis of
aforesaid legal provisions of parent Acts, the provisions of Notification No. 51/2010-Customs(N.T.) dated 30.6.2010 and the
clarification given by the administrative Ministry as detailed in para 4 above.

6.       The above instructions may be brought to the notice of all concerned immediately and wide publicity of this circular may
given through appropriate Public Notice.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the legal framework for parallel imports under Indian Customs law, specifically the interplay between Customs Notification No. 51/2010-NT and the parent IPR statutes (Patents Act s.107A(b), Trade Marks Act s.30(3)(b), Designs Act s.22(1)(b)), making it ideal for HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-act cross-referencing and legal interpretation of import permissibility.

questions.q: Technova Imports LLP (Mumbai, Maharashtra) has purchased 500 units of a patented pharmaceutical device — originally manufactured in Germany by the patent holder and legitimately sold there to a Swiss distributor — and is now importing those units into India without the Indian patent holder's authorisation. The patent holder has filed a complaint with Customs at JNPT seeking seizure under the IPR enforcement notification. Technova argues the import is lawful. Simultaneously, each device bears the patent holder's registered Indian trade mark, and Technova concedes the goods have not been materially altered. How should the Assistant Commissioner adjudicate: (a) the patent-based seizure demand, (b) the trade-mark-based seizure demand, and (c) what documentary evidence should Technova produce to establish that the Swiss distributor was 'duly authorised under the law to produce and sell or distribute' the product, and does the answer change if Technova cannot produce such evidence?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the direct source for the rule that s.107A(b) Patents Act permits parallel imports if the foreign seller is duly authorised, AND that s.30(3)(b) Trade Marks Act permits further dealing in legitimately acquired trade-marked goods provided they are not materially altered — both limbs are indispensable to adjudicating the two separate seizure demands.

questions.q: Prestige Luxe Pvt Ltd (Delhi) imports 200 handbags bearing a well-known foreign fashion house's registered design into India from a UAE retailer that lawfully purchased them from the brand's own boutique in Paris. The goods also carry the brand's registered trade mark. At IGI Airport Customs, the Indian exclusive licensee of the design demands seizure, while the trade-mark proprietor (the foreign fashion house) has given no objection. Prestige Luxe relies on the position that because the fashion house itself put the goods on the market under its registered trade mark with its consent, further dealing is permissible. Advise on: (i) whether the design-based seizure by the exclusive licensee can succeed despite the trade-mark proprietor's non-objection, (ii) whether the trade-mark proviso independently saves the import from seizure, and (iii) how the field formation should weigh the DIPP clarification vis-à-vis the Customs notification when the two IPR statutes point in opposite directions for the same consignment.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that parallel imports are NOT allowed under designs law per s.22(1)(b) Designs Act, while s.30(3)(b) Trade Marks Act permits further dealing in legitimately acquired goods — the tension between these two statutory positions for a single consignment, as clarified by DIPP and directed to field formations, is only resolvable using this circular.

title: Anti dumping duty on import of certain grades/types of alloy and non-alloy steel billets, bars and rounds of sizes 70 mm to 250 mm diameter originating in, or exported from, Russia, China and Ukraine
text:
Anti dumping duty on import of certain grades/types of alloy and non-alloy steel billets, bars and rounds of sizes 70 mm to 250
mm diameter originating in, or exported from, Russia, China and Ukraine

26 th December, 2000

Notification No. 151 /2000-Customs

Whereas in the matter of import of certain grades/types of alloy and non-alloy steel billets, bars and rounds of sizes 70 mm to
250 mm diameter conforming to Indian Standards specifications or any other international specifications equivalent to Indian
Standards specifications, which are listed in the Annexure and falling under sub-heading Nos. 7206.90, 7207.19 and 7207.20
of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975) originating in, or exported from, Russia, China and Ukraine, the Designated
Authority vide its preliminary findings, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part I, Section 1, dated the 1 st
December, 2000, has come to the conclusion that -

1. exporters from Russia and China have been exporting subject goods below normal value, resulting in dumping;

2. the Indian industry has suffered material injury;

3. injury has been caused cumulatively by the dumped imports of the subject goods from Russia and China.

and has considered it necessary to impose anti-dumping duty, provisionally, pending final determination, on all imports of the
subject goods, originating in, or exported from, Russia and China;

Now, therefore, in exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 9A of the said Customs Tariff Act, read with
rule 13 and rule 20 of the Customs Tariff (Identification, Assessment and Collection of Anti-dumping Duty on Dumped Articles
and for Determination of Injury) Rules, 1995, the Central Government on the basis of the above findings of the Designated
Authority hereby imposes on grades/types of alloy and non-alloy steel billets, bars and rounds of sizes 70 mm to 250 mm
diameter conforming to Indian Standards specifications or any other international specifications equivalent to Indian Standards
specifications, which are listed in the Annexure and falling under sub-heading Nos. 7206.90, 7207.19 and 7207.20 of the said
Customs Tariff Act, originating in, or exported from, country specified in column (2) of the Table below, when exported by the
exporters mentioned against the corresponding country in column (3) of the said Table, and imported into India, an anti-
dumping duty at the rate which is equivalent to the corresponding amount mentioned in column (4) of the said Table.

Table

S. No.
Country
Exporter
Amount of duty (US $ per metric

tonne)

(1)
(2)
(3)
(4)

1.
Russia
(i) M/s Oscol Electrometallurgical Combinat
(OEMK)
16.03

(ii) Other
exporters
148.50

2.
Peoples Republic of
China
All exporters
179.14

2. The anti-dumping duty imposed under this notification shall be effective upto and inclusive of the 25 th day of June, 2001.

Explanation.- For the purposes of this notification, the anti-dumping duty shall be paid in Indian currency and the "rate of
exchange" applicable for the purposes of calculation of such anti-dumping duty shall be the rate, which is specified in the
notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), issued from time to time, in exercise
of powers under sub-clause (i) of clause (a) of sub-section (3) of section 14 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962) and the
relevant date for determination of the "rate of exchange" shall be the date of presentation of the "bill of entry" under section 46
of the said Customs Act.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers provisional anti-dumping duty rates (exporter-specific and residual) on alloy/non-alloy steel billets, bars and rounds (70–250 mm dia) from Russia and China under Notification 151/2000-Customs, including the exchange-rate determination mechanism and the sunset date of 25 June 2001. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because practitioners must link the exporter-specific duty table, the sub-heading classification, the currency conversion rule anchored to the bill-of-entry date, and the provisional vs. final duty distinction.

questions.q: Steelcraft Industries Pvt Ltd, Mumbai (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1ZK), imports a consignment of 500 metric tonnes of alloy steel rounds of 120 mm diameter conforming to IS 2062 from M/s Oscol Electrometallurgical Combinat (OEMK), Russia. The bill of entry is presented on 10 March 2001. The CBIC exchange-rate notification on that date pegs USD at ₹46.85. The customs appraising officer proposes to levy anti-dumping duty at the residual 'other exporters' rate arguing that OEMK's exporter-specific rate applies only if the exporter's name appears on the shipping bill. Our client disputes this and also wants to know: (a) what is the exact rupee quantum of anti-dumping duty on this shipment, (b) whether the provisional duty can be finalised after 25 June 2001 and retrospectively recovered, and (c) whether the sub-heading 7207.19 classification claimed by the supplier is correct for rounds vs billets. Advise.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Table explicitly lists OEMK at USD 16.03/MT vs. residual USD 148.50/MT for Russia; the Explanation fixes the exchange rate to the bill-of-entry date; and the sub-headings 7206.90/7207.19/7207.20 and the sunset date of 25 June 2001 are all directly from this notification — none of these figures appear in any generic anti-dumping provision.

questions.q: Bharat Forge Components LLP, Pune, is negotiating a back-to-back supply contract: it will import non-alloy steel billets of 200 mm diameter from a Chinese mill (Baosteel Group, exported through a Hong Kong trading house) and re-sell them to an SEZ unit in Gujarat after minor processing. The import leg involves three questions our client is stuck on: (1) The Chinese supplier's invoice shows origin as China but shipment is transhipped via Singapore — does the anti-dumping duty still apply and at what rate, given the notification covers goods 'originating in, or exported from' China? (2) The bill of entry date falls on 20 June 2001; does the provisional duty still apply or has it lapsed, and can the customs house demand the duty on goods already warehoused before that date? (3) If the LLP pays anti-dumping duty at import and then clears goods into the SEZ, is there a refund or exemption mechanism under the SEZ Act that offsets this cost? Walk through each issue citing the applicable provision.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's operative language 'originating in, or exported from' China (covering transhipment scenarios), the single residual rate of USD 179.14/MT for all Chinese exporters, and the hard expiry date of 25 June 2001 (making the 20 June bill-of-entry date critical) are indispensable facts only found in this notification; the SEZ interaction then requires cross-act reasoning layered on top.

title: Appoints Special Adjudicators
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (ii) ]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

New  Delhi,  the  20 th July, 2007

29   Asadha, 1929  Saka

NOTIFICATION NO.  74  /2007-Customs (N.T.)

S.O.          (E). - In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1)   of    section 4 and sub-section (1) of section 5 of  the
Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Central Board of Excise and Customs hereby appoints the Commissioner of Customs,
Inland Container Depot, Tughlakabad, New Delhi  to act as a common adjudicating authority to exercise the powers and
discharge the duties conferred or imposed on the Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva, Mumbai for the purpose of
adjudicating the matters relating to Show Cause Notice pertaining to Shri Har Prasad Goyal, S/o Late Shri Narain Singh, B-
1/578, Janak Puri, New Delhi and others issued, vide, F.No. 50D/113/2006-CI, dated the 12 th February, 2007, by the
Additional Director General, Directorate General of Renenue Intelligence, D-Block, I.P. Bhavan, I.P. Estate, New Delhi.

[F.NO.437/  28  /2007-Cus.IV]

(Anupam Prakash)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a specific administrative notification appointing a common adjudicating authority (Commissioner of Customs, ICD Tughlakabad) to exercise jurisdiction originally vested in Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva — covering cross-jurisdictional adjudication of a Show Cause Notice issued by DGRI. It authoritatively covers the legal mechanism of common adjudication under Customs Act sections 4 and 5, and the transfer of adjudicatory powers across customs jurisdictions.

questions.q: Our client Mehta Exports Pvt Ltd, registered in Delhi, imported consignments through Nhava Sheva, Mumbai and has now received a Show Cause Notice issued by the Directorate of Revenue Intelligence out of Delhi. The company has been asked to appear before the Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva. However, our client's entire documentation, records, and witnesses are in Delhi. Can CBEC/CBIC appoint the Commissioner of Customs at ICD Tughlakabad, New Delhi as the adjudicating authority instead, and if so, what is the legal basis and procedure for such an appointment — and does the appointed authority have the same powers as the original jurisdictional Commissioner at Nhava Sheva?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the direct precedent showing exactly this fact pattern: CBEC exercising power under Customs Act sections 4(1) and 5(1) to appoint Commissioner ICD Tughlakabad as common adjudicating authority in place of Commissioner Nhava Sheva for a DRI-issued SCN, confirming the legal mechanism and its scope.

questions.q: Raghunath Trading LLP, a Delhi-based importer, is being investigated jointly for alleged mis-declaration of goods imported via multiple ports — Nhava Sheva (Mumbai) and Chennai. The DRI's Delhi office has issued a consolidated Show Cause Notice covering all consignments. The LLP's counsel is contesting whether the Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva has jurisdiction over Delhi-based importers and goods examined at ICD Tughlakabad, and whether CBIC can vest adjudication powers in a single authority to avoid conflicting orders from two jurisdictional commissioners. What is the statutory mechanism available to CBIC to appoint a single common adjudicating authority, and what are the limits of such authority's powers relative to the original jurisdictional commissioners at each port?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly demonstrates CBIC invoking sections 4(1) and 5(1) of the Customs Act to appoint a single common adjudicating authority cutting across port jurisdictions (Nhava Sheva and ICD Tughlakabad), which is the precise legal mechanism at issue and indispensable to advising on multi-port consolidated adjudication.

title: Contactless delivery of international courier consignments - reg.
text:
Circular No. 47/2020-Customs

F. No. 451/13/2020-Cus. V

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs

************

North Block, New Delhi

Dated 20th October 2020

To, 
All Principal Chief Commissioners/Chief Commissioners of Customs 
Principal Directors General/Directors General of Customs 
Principal Commissioners/Commissioner of Customs 
 
Subject: Contactless delivery of international courier consignments -reg. 
 
Madam/Sir,

Representations have been received by the Board from the Express Courier Industry for

allowing contactless delivery of international courier consignments in lines with the OTP based 
contactless delivery being adopted by the domestic courier companies. The said request is with a 
view to maintain the required social distancing considering the COVID-19 pandemic.

2. 
At present, the authorized courier, upon delivery of the import consignments obtains a

“proof of delivery” from the consignees by taking their physical signature.

3. 
In light of the need for contactless delivery and also considering that the OTP based

validation gives secure confirmation of delivery to the intended consignee through his registered 
mobile number, which also has the necessary KYC, Board, has decided to allow the delivery of 
international courier shipments, based on the OTP validation as follows:

(i) 
Consignee gets OTP on their mobile number which has been registered with the 
authorized courier. The authorized courier should exercise due diligence to ensure that 
the mobile number belongs to the consignee at the time of registering the phone 
number.

(ii) 
At the time of delivery, the authorised courier electronically validates the OTP from 
the consignee.

(iii) 
Upon successful validation, the shipment is delivered as per the instructions of the 
consignee. In case of failure to validate the OTP, the consignment will not be 
delivered.

3.1 
The OTP based validation will be an alternative means of obtaining proof of delivery to

the existing procedure of taking physical signatures. Thus, the authorized couriers will obtain the 
proof of delivery either by taking the physical signatures or through OTP based validation.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the CBIC-approved OTP-based contactless delivery procedure for international courier consignments under Customs, introduced during COVID-19 as an alternative to physical signature proof-of-delivery. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving courier importers disputing non-delivery, liability for mis-delivery, and the interplay between KYC obligations of the authorized courier and consignee rights.

questions.q: Nexus Pharma Pvt Ltd, Mumbai (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1Z5), imported temperature-sensitive biologics via DHL Express on 18-Mar-2021. DHL's records show OTP validation was completed and goods delivered, but Nexus Pharma claims the OTP was sent to an outdated mobile number registered years ago by their former procurement manager — not the current authorized signatory. The goods were received by an unauthorized person at a third-party warehouse in Navi Mumbai. Nexus Pharma is claiming non-delivery for customs duty drawback and insurance purposes, while DHL is asserting valid delivery under the CBIC-approved OTP procedure. Who bears liability — the authorized courier or the importer — and what due-diligence obligation does CBIC's OTP framework place on DHL at the time of mobile number registration that is critical to determining fault?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifically requires the authorized courier to 'exercise due diligence to ensure that the mobile number belongs to the consignee at the time of registering the phone number' — this precise obligation is indispensable to determining whether DHL's OTP-based proof of delivery is legally valid or defective.

questions.q: Skybridge Logistics LLP, acting as an authorized courier under the Courier Imports and Exports (Clearance) Regulations, delivered a consignment of high-value electronics (declared CIF value USD 12,000) to a consignee in Bengaluru on 5-Feb-2022. The delivery was completed via OTP validation, but the consignee — Aryan Retail Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCA5678G1ZK) — now alleges the OTP was intercepted via SIM-swap fraud and the goods were received by an impostor. Customs authorities at Bengaluru Air Cargo are questioning whether Skybridge's OTP-based delivery satisfies the legal 'proof of delivery' requirement, or whether it must revert to physical signature in disputed cases. Separately, Aryan Retail is seeking to reverse the IGST paid on import on grounds of non-receipt. Does CBIC Circular 47/2020 permit OTP as a standalone proof of delivery with full legal equivalence to physical signature, or is it merely a facilitation measure that can be challenged in a fraud scenario, and how does this affect Aryan Retail's IGST reversal claim?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause 3.1 explicitly states OTP validation is 'an alternative means of obtaining proof of delivery to the existing procedure of taking physical signatures' — whether this equivalence holds in a SIM-swap fraud scenario is a direct legal interpretation question anchored exclusively in this circular's text.

title: Seeks to amend notification No. 1/2017- Compensation Cess(Rate).
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, 
SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No.1 / 2021-Compensation Cess (Rate)

New Delhi, the 30thSeptember, 2021

G.S.R……. (E). - In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 8 of the

Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017 (15 of 2017), the Central

Government, on the recommendations of the Council, hereby makes the following further

amendments in the notification of the Government of India, in the Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue), No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017,

published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide

number G.S.R. 720(E), dated the 28th June, 2017, namely:-

In the said notification, in the Schedule, after S. No. 4A and the entries relating

thereto, the following S. No. and the entries shall be inserted, namely: -

12%";

"4B 
2202 Carbonated Beverages of Fruit Drink or Carbonated Beverages

with Fruit Juice.

2. 
This notification shall come into force on the 1st day of October, 2021.

[F.No.190354/206/2021-TRU]

(Rajeev Ranjan)

UnderSecretary to the Government of India

Note: - The principal notification No.1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated the 28th day of

June, 2017, was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-

section (i), vide number G.S.R. 720(E), dated the 28th day of June, 2017, and was last

amended vide notification No. 2/2019-Compensation Cess (Rate), dated the 30th day of

September, 2019, published vide number G.S.R.707(E), dated the 30th day of September,

2019.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively establishes that 'Carbonated Beverages of Fruit Drink or Carbonated Beverages with Fruit Juice' (HS 2202) attract 12% Compensation Cess effective 1-Oct-2021, inserted as S.No. 4B in the Compensation Cess Rate schedule — a specific rate change that is indispensable for computing total GST liability on these products post that date.

questions.q: Refreshio Beverages Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCR1234F1ZP, Maharashtra) manufactures a product marketed as 'FruitSpark' — carbonated water with 8% real orange juice, sold in 250ml PET bottles to a distributor in Gujarat (GSTIN 24XYZPQ5678G1ZR) at ₹18 per bottle ex-factory. The company had been paying GST at 28% + 12% Cess up to September 2021 under the plain aerated drinks entry. From October 2021, their tax consultant argues that since the product now has fruit juice it should be reclassified to a lower Cess bracket, while their auditor insists a new specific entry was inserted in October 2021 that captures exactly this category and the Cess rate is 12% regardless. Who is correct, what is the applicable Cess rate from 1-Oct-2021, and how does the IGST (Maharashtra → Gujarat interstate supply) stack up against the total tax per bottle?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk inserts S.No. 4B specifically for 'Carbonated Beverages of Fruit Drink or Carbonated Beverages with Fruit Juice' under HS 2202 at 12% Cess effective 1-Oct-2021 — the exact provision needed to settle the reclassification dispute and compute total liability.

questions.q: Zephyr Agrofoods LLP (GSTIN 29AABFZ9012K1ZT, Karnataka) operates as both a manufacturer and an exporter. In November 2021, it ships 10,000 cases of 'CitroBurst' (carbonated water with 5% lemon juice concentrate, HS 2202) to a buyer in Dubai under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT), claiming zero-rated export with no Cess paid. Simultaneously, it supplies the same SKU domestically to a Bengaluru modern-trade chain under the bill-to (Bengaluru chain) / ship-to (chain's warehouse in Tamil Nadu) arrangement. The Karnataka GST officer during scrutiny contends: (a) Compensation Cess is payable on the domestic leg because a specific 12% Cess entry for carbonated fruit beverages was inserted in October 2021; and (b) the export leg under LUT does not exempt Cess — only IGST is covered by the LUT benefit. Advise Zephyr on: (i) whether the 12% Cess applies to the domestic supply; (ii) the correct place of supply for the bill-to/ship-to transaction; and (iii) whether Compensation Cess is indeed leviable on zero-rated exports cleared under LUT.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's insertion of 12% Cess on HS 2202 carbonated fruit beverages from 1-Oct-2021 is the foundational rate provision the officer is invoking; without this specific notification entry, neither the domestic Cess demand nor the export Cess argument can be evaluated.

title: Imposition of Service Tax on Mandap keeper and by an Air travel Agents-regarding
text:
NOTIFICATION

OFFICE OF THE COMMISSIONER OF CENTRAL EXCISE

MUMBAI-I

115, M.K. ROAD, CHURCHGATE, MUMBAI-400020

(SERVICE TAX CELL)

TRADE NOTICE NO. 6/97-SERVICE TAX DT. 1.7.97

SUBJECT: Imposition of service tax on services rendered by Mandap keeper and by an Air Travel Agents reg.

1.     Attention of the Trade/Public and all concerned, is invited to Section 88 of the Finance Act, 1997 which provides interalia
for levy of service tax on services rendered by Mandap these services will come into force from 1.7.97 (Notification No. 19/97-
Service Tax dt. 26.6.97 referred) Notification 20/97-Service Tax dated 26.6.97 has been issued to define the person
responsible for collecting the Service Tax in relation to Air Travel Agents and Mandap keepers.

2. MANDAP KEEPERS

In the context of these two services, certain points have been raised for clarification which are discussed below:

2.1     As per clause (19) of Section 65 of the Finance Act, 1994, "Mandap" means any immovable property as defined in
Section 3 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882 and includes any furniture, fixtures, light fittings and floor coverings therein, let
out for consideration for organising any official, social or business function. "Mandap Keepers" has been defined as " a person
who allows temporary occupation of a mandap for consideration for organising any official, social or business function". It may
kindly be noted that the definition of mandap is very wide in its coverage and covers all immovable properties let out for
organising social, official or business function. It includes within its scope places like kalyana mandaps or marriage halls,
banquet halls, conference halls, etc. Hotels and restaurants providing any such facility within their premises for organising any
social, official or business function shall also be obviously included in the coverage of service tax. Therefore, the levy of the
service tax will apply in all these cases.

2.2     The taxable service has been defined as services rendered to a client by mandap keeper in relation to the use of
mandap in any manner including the facilities provided to the client in relation to such use and also services, if any rendered as
a caterer and the value of the taxable service means the gross amount charged by the mandap including the facilities provided
to the client in relation to such use and also the charges for catering, if any.

2.3     A number of points have been raised by the associations representing mandap keepers with respect to the levy of
service tax on their activities. These are discussed in the ensuing paragraphs.

2.4     The person responsible for collecting the service tax will be "mandap keeper". The service tax would fall not only on the
hire charges for the mandap but also charges for electricity, whether on actual basis or otherwise, charged to the customer.
therefore, it may be ensured that the tax is collected on the whole amount even if separate bills are issued one for the rental and
the other for electricity charges. The mandap keeper may also bill the client for other services rendered by him such as charges
for providing furniture, fixtures, lighting fittings, vessels, crockery, cutlery, etc. all these charges are includible in the value of
taxable service for the levy of service tax.

2.5     Where a mandap keeper also provides catering services, that in supply of food, in addition to the letting out of the
mandap and charges the customer for supply of food, and abatement of 40% of the total amount charged has been provided
while computing the value of the taxable service. This will be deemed to be the value of taxable service in such cases. In other
words, service tax will be leviable on 60% of the total amount billed in such cases (vide notification No. 21/97-Service Tax,
dated 26th June, 97). This mainly happens in the case of hotels, clubs etc.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the 1997 service tax levy on mandap keepers under Finance Act 1994 s.65(19), specifically defining the taxable value as inclusive of electricity, furniture, catering, and ancillary charges — even when billed separately. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because practitioners must link the definition of 'mandap', the composite valuation rule, and the caterer overlap to determine aggregate tax liability in real multi-service scenarios.

questions.q: Celebration Venues Pvt Ltd, a banquet hall operator in Mumbai (registered under service tax as a mandap keeper), hosted a corporate awards ceremony for Zenith Pharma Ltd on 15-March-2005. Celebration Venues raised three separate invoices: Rs 3,50,000 for hall rental, Rs 1,20,000 for electricity and generator charges billed at actuals, and Rs 4,80,000 for food and beverages supplied through its in-house catering wing. Zenith Pharma's CFO is contesting that service tax should apply only to the hall rental invoice since the electricity is a pass-through reimbursement and the catering is a separate activity. Advise Celebration Venues on the correct taxable value and whether splitting invoices insulates any portion from service tax, citing the relevant valuation rule and the definition of taxable service applicable to mandap keepers.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that electricity charges (whether actual or otherwise) and catering charges are includible in the gross taxable value even when billed under separate invoices, and that catering by the mandap keeper is part of the taxable service — directly defeating the invoice-splitting argument.

questions.q: The Taj Lands End hotel in Mumbai let out its 'Sea View Ballroom' to Meridian Events LLP for a three-day international trade conference in October 2004. The hotel raised a consolidated bill covering: (a) ballroom hire for 3 days at Rs 6,00,000; (b) audio-visual and stage setup at Rs 2,10,000; (c) accommodation for 40 overseas delegates at Rs 18,00,000; and (d) food and beverage service during the conference at Rs 9,50,000. The hotel's service tax consultant is arguing that the hotel is not a 'mandap keeper' because it is primarily a hotel, and alternatively that even if it qualifies, the accommodation and F&B components are outside the service tax net for mandap keeper services. Meridian Events LLP, as the recipient, wants to know its exposure if the hotel has not collected and remitted the correct service tax. Analyse whether the hotel falls within the definition of a mandap keeper for this engagement, which invoice components form part of the taxable value, and whether Meridian Events bears any secondary liability.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk expressly includes hotels and restaurants providing banquet/conference facilities within the scope of 'mandap keeper', and specifies that furniture, fixtures, lighting, and catering are all includible in taxable value — the hotel's primary-hotel argument and the component-exclusion argument are directly answered by this chunk's clarifications.

title: Extended Producer Responsibility on New and Waste Tyres
text:
4  
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
   [PART II—SEC. 3(i)]

4. 
जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व व्यवस्ट्था की पद्जतर्ां – (1) सभी उत्पािकं पर जनम्नानुसार जवस्ट् ताररत

उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व बाध् र्ताएं हंगी, अथाात्:

(क) 
नए टार्रं के जवजनमााताओं र्ा आर्ातकं के जलए:

क्र.सं. 
वषा 
विन में अपजिि टार्र पुन:चक्रीकरण लक्ष्र्

(दकलोग्राम र्ा टन)

(1) 
(2) 
(3)

(i) 
वषा 2022-23 की ईपीआर बाध् र्ता (वषा जिसमें 
र्ह अनुसूची प्रवृत् त है)

वषा 2020-21 में जवजनर्मात र्ा आर्ाजतत नए टार्रं की 
मात्रा का 35%

(ii)   
वषा 2023-24 की ईपीआर बाध् र्ता 
वषा 2021-22 में जवजनर्मात र्ा आर्ाजतत नर्े टार्रं की 
मात्रा का 70%

(iii) 
वषा 2024-25 की ईपीआर बाध् र्ता 
वषा 2022-23 में जवजनर्मात र्ा आर्ाजतत नर्े टार्रं की 
मात्रा का 100%

(iv) 
वषा 2024-25 (वषा वाई) के पश् चात, वषा (वाई-2) में जवजनर्मात र्ा आर्ाजतत नए टार्रं की मात्रा का 
100% जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व संबंधी बाध् र्ता होगी।

(v) 
तारीख 1 अप्रैल, 2022 के पश् चात् स्ट् थाजपत इकाइर्ं के जलए,जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व संबंधी 
बाध् र्ता िो वषा (वाई) के पश् चात िुरू हंगे और वषा (वाई-2) में जवजनर्मात र्ा आर्ाजतत नए टार्रं की 
मात्रा के 100% होगी।

(ख) 
अपजिष् ट टार्र आर्ातक के जलए:

(i) 
वषा (वाई) में अपजिष् ट टार्र के आर्ातक के जलए जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व संबंधी बाध् र्ता वषा 
(वाई-1) में आर्ाजतत टार्र का 100% होगी।

(ii)  पाइरोजलजसस तेल/चार के उत्पािन के प्रर्ोिन से अपजिष् ट टार्र का आर्ात प्रजतजषद् है।

(2) 
रररेसडंग:

(i) अपजिष् ट टार्र पर रररेसडंग को अनुमजत प्रिान की िाएगी और रररेडर को रररेसडंग प्रमाणपत्र के प्रचालन

के जलए पोटाल पर रजिस्ट् रीकृत होना होगा।

(ii) रररेसडंग प्रमाण पत्र प्रस्ट् तुत करने पर, जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व संबंधी बाध् र्ता को अपजिष् ट टार्र

की तत् स्ट् थानी मात्रा के जलए एक वषा के जलए आस्ट् थजगत दकर्ा िाएगा;

परंतु बाध् र्ता रजिस्ट् रीकृत पुनचाक्रणकताा के माध् र्म से केवल इनके जनपटान के पश् चात ही जनवााजपत हो 
िाएगी।

(3) 
उत् पािक के जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व संबंधी लक्ष्र् टार्र के टूट-िूट के कारण केंरीर् प्रिूषण जनर्ंत्रण 
बोडा द्वारा अजधदक थत कारक के आधार पर कम दकर्ा िाएगा।

(4)  
(i)  
उत् पािक अपने जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व संबंधी बाध् र्ता को केवल रजिस्ट् रीकृत पुनचाक्रकं से 
ईपीआर प्रमाण-पत्र की ऑनलाइन खरीि के माध् र्म से पूरा करेगा और जतमाही जववरणी िाइल करके 
पोटाल पर इसे ऑनलाइन प्रस्ट् तुत करेगा।

(ii)  जतमाही जववरणी उस जतमाही के समाप् त होने के बाि उत् तरवतती माह के अंत तक िाइल दकर्ा िाएगा।

(iii) उत् पािकं और रजिस्ट् रीकृत पुनचाक्रकं द्वारा प्रित् त ब् र्ौरे की ऑनलाइन पोटाल पर पुन: िांच की िाएगी।

(iv) अंतर होने के मामले में उत् पािक के जवस्ट् ताररत उत् पािक उत् तरिाजर्त् व संबंधी बाध् र्ता की पूर्ता के जलए

जनम् नतर अंक पर जवचार दकर्ा िाएगा।
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively prescribes the phased EPR recycling targets for new-tyre manufacturers/importers (35%→70%→100% of Y-2 production), the special 2-year grace period for units established after 1-Apr-2022, the 100% same-year-minus-1 obligation for waste-tyre importers, the absolute prohibition on importing waste tyres for pyrolysis/char, and the retreading-certificate deferral mechanism — all of which are indispensable to resolving complex multi-entity compliance scenarios.

questions.q: Zenith Rubbertech Pvt Ltd was incorporated in Maharashtra on 12 June 2023 and began commercial production/import of new tyres in FY 2023-24. In FY 2024-25 they imported 8,000 tonnes of new tyres from South Korea. A sister concern, Zenith Retreads LLP (registered recycler), holds retreading certificates for 2,200 tonnes of waste tyres processed in FY 2024-25. Zenith Rubbertech wants to: (a) determine whether any EPR recycling obligation attaches to them for FY 2024-25 and FY 2025-26 given their post-1-April-2022 incorporation date, (b) ascertain the exact baseline volume and percentage that triggers their first EPR target year, and (c) evaluate whether the retreading certificates issued to the sister LLP can be used to defer Zenith Rubbertech's own EPR obligation and, if so, for how long — keeping in mind that the sister entity is a registered retreader but not the same legal entity as the producer. Please advise on all three limbs.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause 4(1)(a)(v) specifically governs units established after 1-Apr-2022 — their EPR obligation starts only after two years (Year Y+2) and is pegged at 100% of Y-2 imports; without this provision the first-obligation year cannot be determined. Clause 4(2)(ii) on retreading-certificate deferral (and the proviso that obligation is discharged only after disposal through a registered recycler) is directly needed to assess the sister-LLP certificate question.

questions.q: Crescent Polymer Solutions Pvt Ltd, based in Tamil Nadu (GSTIN 33AADCC1234F1ZX), is engaged in importing waste tyres and operates a dual-stream facility: one line converts waste tyres into retreaded tyres (covered by retreading certificates), and a second line extracts pyrolysis oil and carbon black char. In FY 2025-26 they imported 5,000 MT of waste tyres, of which 3,000 MT went to the retreading line and 2,000 MT to the pyrolysis line. Customs authorities at Chennai Port have issued a show-cause notice alleging that the entire 5,000 MT import is tainted by the 2,000 MT diverted to pyrolysis, and have proposed confiscation of the entire consignment. Separately, the EPR portal shows a discrepancy: Crescent has claimed deferral of obligation for all 5,000 MT citing retreading certificates, but CPCB's cross-verification reveals only 3,000 MT is backed by valid certificates. Advise: (i) whether the Customs confiscation proposal for the full 5,000 MT is legally sustainable given the partial end-use split, (ii) what EPR obligation (in MT) attaches to Crescent for FY 2025-26 after applying the retreading deferral only to the eligible 3,000 MT, and (iii) what compliance consequence flows from the portal discrepancy under the quarterly-return verification mechanism.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause 4(1)(b)(ii) provides the absolute prohibition on importing waste tyres for pyrolysis oil/char production — this is the foundation of the Customs SCN and cannot be sourced from any generic GST or Customs tariff chunk. Clause 4(2) governs retreading-certificate deferral scope and its proviso (discharge only via registered recycler disposal), and clause 4(4)(iii)-(iv) specifies that portal cross-verification of producer vs recycler data resolves discrepancies at the lower figure — all three sub-questions hinge on these specific provisions.

title: Notice to a third person under section 79(1) (c)
text:
3. The form may be filled up if amount from one major / minor head is intended to be transferred to another major/minor head. Minor head for

transfer of amount may be same or different.

4. The amount from one minor head can also be transferred to another minor head under the same major head.  
5. Amount can be transferred from the head only if balance under that head is available at the time of transfer.]

252
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the procedural rules for transferring amounts between major/minor heads in the GST payment ledger (e.g., CGST to IGST, or between minor heads under the same major head), with the critical constraint that a transfer is only permissible if a balance exists under the source head at the time of transfer. This supports HIGH-complexity queries involving wrongly-paid tax head scenarios with multi-jurisdiction and multi-provision reasoning.

questions.q: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM1234Q1ZK, Maharashtra) paid Rs 18,40,000 in April 2025 as CGST while filing GSTR-3B for a supply that their legal team later determined was an inter-state B2B transaction to a buyer in Telangana (GSTIN 36AACFC5678R1ZP), making it IGST-liable instead. The CGST head now shows a surplus of Rs 18,40,000 and the IGST head shows a shortfall of the same amount. The accounts team wants to transfer the entire amount from the CGST major head to the IGST major head without making a fresh cash payment. (a) Is such a cross-major-head transfer permissible under the GST payment framework, and what is the governing condition that must be satisfied before the transfer is executed? (b) If only Rs 11,00,000 of the Rs 18,40,000 remains in the CGST head after set-off of other liabilities processed on the same day, what is the maximum transferable amount and what happens to the residual IGST shortfall? (c) Does the place-of-supply determination under the IGST Act interact with the availability-of-balance condition for this transfer, or are these assessed independently?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rules 3, 4, and 5 of the chunk are directly indispensable: Rule 3 permits cross-major-head transfer (CGST→IGST), Rule 5 imposes the balance-availability condition at the time of transfer (limiting the transfer to Rs 11,00,000 in sub-question b), and Rule 4 clarifies same-major-head minor-head transfers — together these govern every sub-question.

questions.q: Pinnacle Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCP9876N1Z3, Karnataka) filed its GSTR-3B for March 2025 and inadvertently deposited Rs 6,20,000 under the CGST minor head '006 - Tax on outward supplies' instead of minor head '009 - Interest'. The tax officer has issued a recovery notice for interest arrears. The CFO wants to know: (a) Can the company transfer the Rs 6,20,000 sitting under minor head 006 to minor head 009 within the same CGST major head to extinguish the interest demand, and is the minor head required to be different for such a transfer to be valid? (b) If, simultaneously, there is zero balance under minor head 006 due to a refund adjustment processed earlier that morning, can the company still execute the transfer — and does the sequence of the refund adjustment vs. the transfer request matter legally? (c) Would the answer differ if the company instead wanted to move the amount from the CGST major head minor head 006 to the SGST major head minor head 009, given that Karnataka SGST and CGST are administered under a single integrated return?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 4 (same-major-head, different-minor-head transfer is permissible) and Rule 5 (balance must exist at time of transfer, making the sequence of the morning refund adjustment determinative in sub-question b) are the indispensable operative rules; Rule 3 governs sub-question c's cross-major-head scenario.

title: Advance Licence- No delay in clearance of AR4 Forms 98
text:
Circular No. 98/9/95-CX

dated 20/2/95

F.No. 209/5/95-CX.6

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue, New Delhi

Subject: Exports - Non-clearance of AR4s. (under Value Based Advance Licence/ quantity Based Advance Licence)
immediately affecting the exports - Regarding.

I am directed to say that a number of complaints have been received from the trade circle to the effect that delay in clearance of
AR4s. (under VBAL/QBAL) by the Central Excise officers, is adversely affecting the exports.

2.    The issue have been examined by the Board. All Collectors are advised to ensure that AR4s are cleared by Central Excise
officers expeditiously and there is no delay in clearance of the the same. It should be impressed upon the concerned officers
that delay in export processing is not pardonable. In case of serious persistent complaints of delay effective administrative
action must be taken.

3.    The Collectors are also requested to pay personal attention to exporter"s problems and to devise an effective system of
accountability.

Sd/-

(I.P.Lal)

Deputy Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk is a 1995 Central Excise circular directing Collectors to ensure expeditious clearance of AR4 forms under Value-Based and Quantity-Based Advance Licences, directly relevant to export obligation compliance and excise procedure. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving the intersection of advance licence conditions, AR4 clearance timelines, and consequences for exporters caught between excise officers' delays and export obligation deadlines.

questions.q: Prestige Textiles Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer-exporter in Surat (Gujarat), holds a Quantity-Based Advance Licence with an export obligation of ₹2.5 crore to be fulfilled by 31-March-1995. They manufactured and packed the export consignment by 10-February-1995 and submitted AR4 forms to the Central Excise Range office on 12-February-1995. The Range Superintendent did not clear the AR4s until 28-March-1995, causing the shipping bill to be filed only on 1-April-1995 — one day after the obligation period ended. DGFT is now proposing to treat the export obligation as unfulfilled and initiate recovery of customs duty foregone on the imported inputs. Can Prestige Textiles argue that the delay in export obligation fulfilment was caused by the Central Excise department's own failure, and is there any Central Excise Board-level direction that acknowledges such delays as an administrative failing rather than the exporter's default?
questions.why_this_chunk: The circular explicitly acknowledges that delays in AR4 clearance by Central Excise officers 'adversely affect exports' and directs that 'delay in export processing is not pardonable' — this is the specific Board-level admission that administrative delay exists and is impermissible, which is indispensable to the exporter's defence against DGFT recovery.

questions.q: Kamala Pharma LLP, a manufacturer in Hyderabad operating under a Value-Based Advance Licence, exported active pharmaceutical ingredients to a buyer in Germany in January 1995. The goods were ready for export on 5-January-1995 but the Central Excise Divisional office withheld clearance of the AR4 forms citing a pending internal audit query on the previous quarter's ER-1 return. The AR4s were finally cleared on 20-February-1995. The foreign buyer, bound by a shipment-by-31-January contract, cancelled the order. Kamala Pharma now faces (a) a claim from the buyer for damages, (b) potential invalidation of the advance licence by DGFT for non-utilisation within the validity period, and (c) the Central Excise department asking Kamala to reverse CENVAT credit on the inputs since the goods were not exported. Is there a binding Central Excise Board direction that would support Kamala's position that withholding AR4 clearance for reasons unrelated to the export transaction itself is impermissible, and what administrative remedy does that direction contemplate?
questions.why_this_chunk: The circular's direction to Collectors to 'ensure AR4s are cleared expeditiously', to 'devise an effective system of accountability', and to take 'effective administrative action' on persistent delays is the specific Board-level remedy Kamala Pharma must invoke to challenge the Divisional office's conduct and resist the downstream CENVAT reversal demand.

title: The Foreign Trade Policy (FTP), 2015-2020
