97 
 
6.07 
 Applications & Approvals

(a)   
Applications for setting up of units under EOU scheme shall be approved or rejected by the Units Approval 
Committee within 15 days as per criteria indicated in Handbook of Procedures (HBP).

(b)   
In other cases, approval may be granted by BOA set up for this purpose as indicated in HBP.

(c)   
Proposals for setting up EOU requiring industrial licence may be granted approval by DC after clearance of 
proposal by BOA and DIPP within 45 days.

(d)   
Applications for conversion into an EOU / EHTP / STP / BTP unit from existing DTA units, having an investment 
of Rs. 50 crores and above in plant and machinery or exporting Rs. 50 crores and above annually, shall be placed 
before BOA for a decision.

6.08 
DTA Sale of Finished Products / Rejects / Waste/  Scrap / Remnants and By-products

Entire production of EOU / EHTP / STP / BTP units shall be exported subject to following:

(a)  
Units, other than gems and jewellery units, may sell goods upto 50% of FOB value of exports, subject to 
fulfilment of positive NFE, on payment of concessional duties. Within entitlement of DTA sale, unit may sell 
in DTA, its products similar to goods which are exported or expected to be exported from units. However, 
units which are manufacturing and exporting more than one product can sell any of these products into DTA, 
upto 90% of FOB value of export of the specific products, subject to the condition that total DTA sale does 
not exceed the overall entitlement of 50% of FOB value of exports for the unit, as stipulated above. No DTA 
sale at concessional duty shall be permissible in respect of motor cars,  alcoholic  liquors,  books,  tea  
(except instant tea), pepper & pepper products, marble and such other items as may be notified from time to 
time.

Such DTA sale shall also not be permissible to units engaged in activities of packaging/labelling/segregation / 
refrigeration/compacting/micronisation/pulverization/granulation/conversion of monohydrate form of chemical 
to anhydrous form or vice-versa. Sales made to a unit in SEZ shall also be taken  into  account  for  purpose  of  
arriving  at  FOB value of export by EOU provided payment for such sales are made from Foreign Currency 
Account of SEZ unit. Sale to DTA would also be subject to mandatory requirement of registration of 
pharmaceutical products (including bulk drugs). An amount equal to Anti Dumping duty under section 9A of 
the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 leviable at the time of import, shall be payable on the goods used for the purpose 
of manufacture or processing of the goods cleared into DTA from the unit.

(b)    
For services, including software units, sale in DTA in any mode, including on line data communication, shall 
also be permissible up to 50% of FOB value of exports and /or 50% of foreign exchange earned, where 
payment of such services is received in foreign exchange.

(c)    
Gems and jewellery units may sell upto 10% of FOB value of exports of the preceding year in DTA, subject to 
fulfilment of positive NFE. In respect of sale of plain jewellery, recipient shall pay concessional rate of duty as 
applicable to sale from nominated agencies. In respect of studded jewellery, duty shall be payable as applicable.

(d)   
Unless specifically prohibited in LoP, rejects within an overall limit of 50% may be sold in DTA on payment of 
duties as applicable to sale under Sub - para 6.08 (a) on prior intimation to Customs authorities.  Such sales 
shall be counted against DTA sale entitlement. Sale of rejects upto 5% of FOB value of exports shall not be 
subject to achievement of NFE.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers EOU DTA sale entitlements under FTP 2015-2020, including the 50% FOB cap, product-specific sub-limits (90% per product), prohibited goods/activities, SEZ-to-EOU payment rules, anti-dumping duty liability on DTA clearances, and gems/jewellery carve-outs — all of which are critical multi-provision, cross-scheme practitioner issues.

questions.q: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd, an EOU unit in Pune (GSTIN 27AABCP1234Q1Z5) manufactures three products: HDPE pipes (exported FOB Rs. 8 crore in FY 2024-25), PVC granules (exported FOB Rs. 5 crore), and polypropylene sheets (exported FOB Rs. 3 crore). The unit wants to maximize DTA sales in FY 2025-26. Their CFO claims they can sell PVC granules into DTA up to Rs. 4.5 crore (90% of Rs. 5 crore FOB) without breaching the overall cap. The tax counsel disputes this, arguing the 90% sub-limit is subject to the overarching 50% total FOB ceiling of Rs. 8 crore. Additionally, the DTA buyer is asking whether anti-dumping duty under Customs Tariff Act s.9A will be added on top of the concessional duty. Who is correct, and what is the maximum permissible DTA sale for each product and in aggregate?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's para 6.08(a) explicitly states the 90% sub-limit per product is 'subject to the condition that total DTA sale does not exceed the overall entitlement of 50% of FOB value of exports for the unit' and separately mandates anti-dumping duty equivalent under s.9A of Customs Tariff Act on goods used in manufacture of DTA-cleared goods — both facts are indispensable to resolving the dispute.

questions.q: Celestial Gems LLP, a gems and jewellery EOU in Surat, achieved FOB exports of Rs. 40 crore in FY 2024-25 (Rs. 25 crore plain gold jewellery, Rs. 15 crore diamond-studded pieces). In FY 2025-26, they plan to: (i) sell Rs. 4 crore of plain gold jewellery in DTA to a registered dealer in Gujarat; (ii) sell Rs. 1.5 crore of studded jewellery in DTA; and (iii) treat a Rs. 2 crore supply to an SEZ unit in SEEPZ, Mumbai (payment routed through the SEZ unit's Foreign Currency Account) as counting toward their NFE and DTA entitlement. Their CA wants to know: (a) what is the maximum permissible DTA sale cap, (b) what duty rates apply to plain vs studded jewellery, (c) whether the SEEPZ supply counts as 'export' for FOB calculation purposes, and (d) whether the proposed DTA sales in aggregate are within the FTP ceiling.
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 6.08(c) sets the 10% FOB cap specific to gems/jewellery EOUs and distinguishes duty treatment for plain jewellery (nominated agency rates) vs studded jewellery; para 6.08(a) provides the SEZ-sale-as-export rule conditioned on payment from Foreign Currency Account — both provisions are uniquely applicable here and not answerable from generic GST/Customs chunks.

title: Notice to a third person under section 79(1) (c)
text:
8. Verification

I __________________________________________ hereby solemnly affirm and declare that the information given hereinabove is true and correct to the best of my knowledge and 
belief and nothing has been concealed there from.

Signature of authorised signatory _______________________________________

Name _______________________________________

Designation/Status ____________________________

Date---dd/mm/yyyy

Instructions:

1. Transferor refers to the registered person who has an existing registration in a State or Union territory.

2. Transferee refers to the place of business for which a separate registration has been obtained under rule 11.]25

25Inserted vide Notf no. 03/2019-CT dt. 29.01.2019wef 01.02.2019

91
claude_reasoning: This chunk is the verification/signature block and instructions for a GST registration transfer form (rule 11, transferor/transferee definitions), inserted via Notf 03/2019-CT. It authoritatively covers the procedural and definitional requirements for transferring registration between a registered person and a newly registered place of business under rule 11.

questions.q: Meridian Exports LLP, registered in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABFM1234Q1ZX), is undergoing a business restructuring where its Pune warehouse operations are being carved out as a separately registered place of business under the same PAN. The Pune unit has already obtained a fresh GSTIN. During the transfer process, the authorised signatory of Meridian Exports LLP signed the declaration form but the GST officer is insisting that the signatory of the *transferee* entity (the new Pune registration) must also separately affirm the verification. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) who qualifies as the 'transferor' and 'transferee' in this context under the applicable rule, (b) whether a single verification by the transferor's authorised signatory suffices or whether the transferee must independently affirm, and (c) what are the legal consequences if the form is filed with only one verification before the effective date of 01-Feb-2019 versus after?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Instructions section uniquely defines 'Transferor' as the registered person with existing registration and 'Transferee' as the place of business that obtained separate registration under rule 11, and carries the verification/affirmation block — these definitions and the affirmation structure are indispensable to resolving who must sign and what the verification obligation covers.

questions.q: Nexora Digital Services Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AADCN5678R1ZP, Karnataka) acquired the business of a sole proprietorship — 'Sharma Trading Co.' — operating from Bengaluru, which had an active GST registration. As part of the transfer, Nexora's tax counsel is preparing the statutory form for notifying the department. The authorised signatory of Nexora has executed the verification clause, but Sharma Trading Co. ceased operations on 31-Mar-2025 and the proprietor is now untraceable. The jurisdictional officer is raising an objection that the transferor's verification is missing and the form is incomplete. Nexora's counsel argues the transferee's verification should suffice since the transferor entity no longer legally exists. Analyse whether the officer's objection is valid, what the specific verification requirements are under the applicable notification, and whether the insertion effective 01-Feb-2019 changes the compliance obligation when the transferor is a natural person (proprietor) who has dissolved the business.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's verification block (which requires the authorised signatory to affirm on behalf of a specific party) and the Instructions defining transferor as 'the registered person who has an existing registration' are critical — the question turns on whether a defunct proprietor-transferor can satisfy this verification requirement, directly engaging the chunk's affirmation language and transferor definition.

title: Regarding issue of Custom House Agent License
text:
obligations on the CHAs to verify the antecedent, correctness of Import Export
Code (IEC) Number, identity of his client and the functioning of his client in the declared address by using reliable,
independent, authentic documents, data or information. In this regard, a detailed guideline on the list of documents to be
verified and obtained from the client/ customer is enclosed in the Annexure.  It would also be obligatory for the client/ customer
to furnish to the CHA, a photograph of himself/herself in the case of an individual and those of the authorised signatory in
respect of other forms of organizations such as company/ trusts etc., and any two of the listed documents in the annexure.

(v) Time limit for completion of suspension proceedings against CHA licensee under regulation 22:

7.1.       The present procedure prescribed for completion of regular suspension proceedings takes a long time since it involves
inquiry proceedings, and there is no time limit prescribed for completion of such proceedings. Hence, it has been decided by
the Board to prescribe an overall time limit of nine months from the date of receipt of offence report, by prescribing time limits
at various stages of issue of Show Cause Notice, submission of inquiry report by the Deputy Commissioner of Customs or
Assistant Commissioner of Customs recording his findings on the issue of suspension of CHA license, and for passing of an
order by the Commissioner of Customs. Suitable changes have been made in the present time limit of forty five days for reply
by CHA to the notice of suspension, sixty days time for representation against the report of AC/DC on the grounds not
accepted by CHA, by reducing the time to thirty days in both the cases under the Regulations.

7.2.       In cases where immediate suspension action against a CHA is required to be taken by a Commissioner of Customs
under regulation 20(2), there is no need for following the procedure prescribed under Regulation 22 since such an action is
taken immediately and only in justified cases depending upon the seriousness or gravity of offence.  However, it has been
decided by the Board that a 'post-decisional hearing' should be given in all such cases so that errors apparent, if any, can be
corrected and an opportunity for personal hearing is given to the aggrieved party.  Further, Board has also prescribed certain
time limits in cases warranting immediate suspension under Regulation 20(2).  Accordingly, the investigating authority shall
furnish its report to the Commissioner of Customs who had issued the CHA license (Licensing authority), within thirty days of
the detection of an offence.  The Licensing authority shall take necessary immediate suspension action within fifteen days of
the receipt of the report of the investigating authority.  A post-decisional hearing shall be granted to the party within fifteen days
from the date of his suspension.  The Commissioner of Customs concerned shall issue an Adjudication Order, where it is
possible to do so, within fifteen days from the date of personal hearing so granted by him.

(vi) CHA licenses in respect of individuals who had passed the examination under CHALR, 1984:

8.1.       The issue of granting CHA license in respect of persons who had already passed the written and oral examinations
held under Regulation 9 examination of Customs House Agents Licensing Regulations (CHALR), 1984 and are yet to be
considered for issue of CHA license, was examined by the Board. On this issue, the Board in its earlier meeting had held that
with the introduction of CHALR, 2004, there was no generalized case for grant of CHA licence to such applicants having
passed Regulation 9 examination under CHALR, 1984 as the requirements of educational qualification and also examination
curriculum were different in the two regulations.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers two distinct procedural frameworks: (1) CHA KYC/due-diligence obligations (IEC verification, identity verification, document requirements from clients), and (2) time-bound suspension/revocation proceedings under Regulation 20(2) and Regulation 22 — including the 'post-decisional hearing' requirement and specific day-counts at each stage. These are specific enough to anchor hard practitioner scenarios.

questions.q: Meridian Freight Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN: 27AABCM1234F1Z5), a licensed Custom House Agent operating out of JNPT, Mumbai, was approached in March 2025 by a new exporter — Pinnacle Organics LLP, claiming to be based in Surat, Gujarat — for clearance of a consignment of 5,000 kg of castor oil valued at ₹42 lakhs. The CHA completed the shipping bill, but the IEC submitted turned out to belong to a different firm entirely, and the Surat address was found to be non-functional. The Commissioner of Customs (NS-IV) initiated suspension proceedings. The CHA contends that it collected a copy of the IEC certificate and a cancelled cheque as verification documents, and that no photograph of the authorised signatory was obtained because the client described itself as an LLP. Can the Commissioner sustain the suspension on grounds of deficient KYC, and specifically, does the failure to collect the authorised signatory's photograph and verify the functional address constitute a breach of CHA obligations, given that only two documents from the prescribed list were collected?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly mandates that for organisations such as companies/trusts (and by extension LLPs), the CHA must obtain a photograph of the authorised signatory AND any two listed documents — both elements are directly in dispute in this scenario. The obligation to verify IEC correctness and functioning at declared address is also verbatim from this chunk.

questions.q: On 10-January-2025, the Additional Commissioner of Customs, Chennai Air Cargo, detected that Translink Cargo Services — a CHA licensee holding a license issued by the Commissioner of Customs, Chennai-I — had filed 37 bills of entry using forged IEC numbers belonging to unrelated importers, facilitating mis-declaration of electronic components worth ₹2.3 crores. The investigating authority (Additional Commissioner, Air Cargo) submitted its offence report to the Commissioner of Customs, Chennai-I (the licensing authority) on 25-February-2025. The licensing authority issued the immediate suspension order on 20-March-2025. The CHA is now challenging the suspension on two grounds: (a) the licensing authority took 23 days after receipt of the report to pass the suspension order, which the CHA argues breaches the prescribed timeline; and (b) no post-decisional hearing has been scheduled despite 30 days having elapsed since suspension. Separately, the CHA argues that since an offence report exists, Regulation 22 inquiry proceedings must be followed instead of the immediate suspension route under Regulation 20(2). Evaluate the validity of each ground of challenge, identifying which timelines were met or breached and whether the Board's direction on post-decisional hearing creates an enforceable right in the CHA's favour.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk prescribes exact day-counts for Regulation 20(2) immediate suspension: investigating authority must report within 30 days of detection, licensing authority must act within 15 days of receipt, post-decisional hearing within 15 days of suspension. It also clarifies that Regulation 22 proceedings are NOT required when Regulation 20(2) immediate suspension is triggered. Every limb of the CHA's challenge maps directly to text in this chunk.

title: Rebate of excise duty paid on ingots and billets of non-alloy steel and hot re-rolled products of non-alloy steel notified under section 3A of the Central Excise Act, 1944 for the period from 1.8.97 to 23.8.98
text:
sive to 23.8.98 and the formula for computation of rebate and other procedures prescribed in the
said notifications should be applied while granting such rebates.

4. The Board further desires that the Trade/exporters and the field formations may be informed quickly about this decision. The
exporters will be allowed to submit additional calculation sheet based on the formula prescribed in the said notifications even in
cases where they filed rebate claims earlier and are lying pending with the Department. All rebate claims pertaining to the
period in question should be taken on priority basis.

5. Any difficulty experienced in the regard may be brought to the notice of the Board immediately.

Sd/-

(P K Sinha)

Under Secretary (CX.6)
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers a procedural circular about rebate of Central Excise duty on ingots/billets of non-alloy steel and hot re-rolled products under Section 3A for the period 1.8.97 to 23.8.98, specifically authorizing submission of additional calculation sheets for pending rebate claims using the formula in specified notifications. It can anchor HIGH-complexity questions involving legacy excise rebate claims, cross-period computation disputes, and interaction between Section 3A notifications and rebate claim procedures.

questions.q: Tata Sponge Iron Ltd (now restructured as a successor entity) exported hot re-rolled non-alloy steel bars from its Jharkhand plant between October 1997 and July 1998 under ARE-1 forms. The company originally filed rebate claims under Rule 12 of the Central Excise Rules 1944 using its own cost-based workings, which the jurisdictional Commissioner (Ranchi) kept pending citing incorrect formula application. After the trade notice issued pursuant to the Board's clarification, the company now wants to file revised calculation sheets using the formula prescribed in the Section 3A notifications. The department is objecting on the ground that the original claims were filed before the clarification and cannot be revised at this stage. Can the company legally insist on submitting additional/revised calculation sheets for the already-pending claims, and does the Board's clarification override the Commissioner's objection about timing of submission?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that exporters will be allowed to submit additional calculation sheets based on the formula in the Section 3A notifications 'even in cases where they filed rebate claims earlier and are lying pending with the Department' — this directly resolves the dispute about whether pre-clarification pending claims can be revised.

questions.q: Bhushan Steel & Strips Pvt Ltd exported ingots of non-alloy steel produced at its Sahibabad (UP) plant during the period August 1997 to March 1998. These goods were notified under Section 3A of the Central Excise Act and duty was determined on a tonnage/capacity basis rather than on actual production value. The company filed rebate claims for excise duty embedded in the export price. The jurisdictional AC (Ghaziabad-II) is now asking whether: (a) the rebate quantum should be computed on the basis of the Section 3A notification formula or on the actual duty paid per invoice; and (b) whether claims for the entire period up to 23.8.98 can be clubbed and processed together or must be adjudicated month-by-month. The company also has one claim from September 1997 that was informally rejected verbally but never through a written order — can that claim be revived? Advise on all three questions.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies both the period (1.8.97 to 23.8.98) and the methodology (formula in the Section 3A notifications must be applied) and mandates that all rebate claims pertaining to the period be taken on priority basis — these three specific facts are indispensable to answering (a), (b), and the revival question.

title: Miscellaneous issues relating to the services on which service tax has been imposed with effect from 1-7-2003.
text:
were also
brought under the tax net w.e.f 1-7-2003. As per the definition in law foreign exchange brokers include authorized dealers of
foreign exchange. Authorised dealer of foreign exchange has been assigned the meaning of "authorized person" under the
FEMA, 1999. Accordingly authorized dealers/money changer etc. which are authorized to deal in foreign exchange are
covered in the definition of "foreign exchange brokers" under service tax provisions. However, as explained above only the
service of "foreign exchange broking" when provided by foreign exchange brokers (other than banking company/financial
institution/body corporate which are already covered) has been brought under the tax net.

5. Service rendered free of charge:

5.1 In the context of certain services, a doubt has been raised as to whether service tax will be payable if the service is
provided free of charge.

5.2 As per charging section viz. section 66 of the Act, service tax is chargeable at the rate of 8% of the value of taxable service.
Thus if the value is zero the tax will also be zero even though the service is taxable.

6.         Receipt of this letter may please be acknowledged.

Gautam Ray

Joint Secretary (TRU)

Telephone: 011-23092687

Fax. 011-23092031
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers two distinct service tax principles under pre-GST law: (1) the scope of 'foreign exchange broking' services and which entities (authorized dealers, money changers under FEMA) fall within that definition, and (2) the principle that services rendered free of charge attract zero service tax since the charging section applies to 'value of taxable service'. Both are source-of-truth rules for practitioner disputes involving service tax liability.

questions.q: Priya Forex Services LLP, a Pune-based FEMA-authorized money changer (AD-II category), provided foreign exchange conversion services to retail walk-in customers during the period August 2003 to March 2004. The LLP is not a banking company, financial institution, or body corporate. The department has issued a show-cause notice demanding service tax on these transactions on the ground that the LLP is a 'foreign exchange broker' under the service tax provisions effective 1-July-2003. The LLP's counsel argues that the term 'foreign exchange broker' does not cover AD-II money changers, only full-service authorized dealers. Additionally, the LLP contends that on several festive days it offered zero-margin currency conversion to preferred clients, charging no fee or commission, and those transactions should be entirely excluded from the tax base. How should the LLP respond to the SCN, and what is the correct legal position on both the entity-classification issue and the zero-charge transactions?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk expressly states that 'authorized dealers/money changers etc. which are authorized to deal in foreign exchange are covered in the definition of foreign exchange brokers' and separately establishes that when value is zero, tax liability is also zero under the charging section — both limbs are directly indispensable to resolving the SCN.

questions.q: Global Remit Solutions Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-incorporated company holding an Authorized Dealer Category-I license under FEMA 1999, also operates a money-changing subsidiary, QuickFX Traders (proprietorship, same director), which holds only a Restricted Money Changer license. During Q3 of FY 2003-04, Global Remit executed interbank foreign exchange transactions on behalf of QuickFX, charging a broking commission of Rs 18 lakhs. Separately, QuickFX provided retail currency exchange to individual customers and on approximately 40 transactions provided the service free of cost as a promotional offer, with nil commission charged and documented via internal memos. The assessing officer proposes to (a) aggregate the turnovers of both entities for service tax purposes treating them as a single taxable person, and (b) include the nil-commission transactions in the taxable value on the basis that a service was nonetheless 'rendered'. Advise on the tenability of both propositions under the service tax law applicable from 1-July-2003, distinguishing the liability of Global Remit (a body corporate) versus QuickFX (a non-corporate authorized person) and addressing the zero-value transactions.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's carve-out that 'only the service of foreign exchange broking when provided by foreign exchange brokers other than banking company/financial institution/body corporate which are already covered' creates a critical entity-type distinction between Global Remit (body corporate, already covered on a different footing) and QuickFX (non-corporate authorized person, covered as 'foreign exchange broker'); and the zero-value principle directly governs the nil-commission transactions.

title: Regarding detailed manual scrutiny of Service Tax Returns
text:
Circular 185/4/2015-Service Tax

F.No.137/314/2012-Service Tax 
Government of India 
Ministry of Finance 
Department of Revenue 
Central Board of Excise and Customs 
Service Tax Wing 
WH 
New Delhi, dated 30" June, 2015

To

All Principal Chief Commissioners of Central Excise

All Chief Commissioners of Central Excise/ Service Tax 
Principal Directors General of Service Tax/Central Excise 
Intelligence/Systems 
Director General of Audit

All Principal Commissioners of Central Excise/Service Tax

All Commissioners of Central Excise/Service Tax

All Principal Commissioners/Commissioners LTU

Subject: Detailed Manual Scrutiny of Service Tax Returns

Madam/Sir

1.1 
In the era of self-assessment, the need for a strong compliance verification mechanism 
cannot be over emphasized. Such a mechanism has three important prongs — audit, anti- 
evasion and return scrutiny. 
In order to put in place a strong ‘return scrutiny’ system, a two-

part system of return scrutiny was envisaged— a preliminary scrutiny which would be online 
covering all the returns; and a detailed manual scrutiny of select returns, identified on the

basis of risk parameters, to be done by the Division/ Range offices.

1.2 
The Board vide Circular No. 113/07/2009-ST dated 23-4-2009 had laid down the 
procedure for carrying out detailed scrutiny of returns and had circulated a Return Scrutiny 
Manual for Service Tax. However, with the introduction of the Point of Taxation Rules, 
2011, which shifted the liability of payment of service tax from receipt basis to accrual basis, 
and the advent of negative list-based comprehensive taxation of services in 2012, it was felt 
that the guidelines for detailed scrutiny of returns needed a revision. In this background, it

has been decided that detailed scrutiny of ST-3 returns, with effect from 01.08.2015, should

be carried out in the manner outlined below:-

2.00 
PRELIMINARY ONLINE SCRUTINY

2.1 
The purpose of preliminary scrutiny of returns includes ensuring the completeness of

the information furnished in the return, arithmetic correctness of the amount computed as tax 
and its timely payment, timely submission of the return and identification of non-filers and

stop-filers. On the basis of the validation checks incorporated in ACES by the Directorate 
General of Systems & Data Management (DGS&DM), preliminary scrutiny of all returns is 
done online 
in ACES and the returns having certain errors are marked for Review and 
Correction (RnC). These have to be processed accordingly by the Range Officers.

Circular 185/4/2015-Service Tax 
Page 1 of 24
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the CBIC's 2015 circular on detailed manual scrutiny of Service Tax ST-3 returns, including the shift from receipt-basis to accrual-basis taxation under Point of Taxation Rules 2011 and the two-tier scrutiny framework (preliminary online + detailed manual). It supports HIGH-complexity queries about scrutiny proceedings, POT-triggered liability disputes, and compliance verification for legacy service tax periods.

questions.q: Meridian Facilities Management Pvt Ltd, a Pune-based integrated facility services provider (GSTIN legacy ST registration GCS-PUNE-2014), filed its ST-3 returns for April 2014 to March 2015 on receipt basis, reflecting service tax liability of ₹1.82 crore. The company argues that since invoices were raised but payment was not received before 31 March 2015, no tax was payable for that period. A scrutiny notice has now been issued by the Range Officer under the detailed manual scrutiny programme. The company's CA contends that the Point of Taxation Rules 2011 had already shifted liability to accrual/invoice basis by the time these returns were filed, but the internal accounts team continued booking on receipt basis. Does the receipt-basis reporting in the ST-3 returns constitute an understatement of tax liability that can be quantified and confirmed during detailed manual scrutiny, and what is the legal exposure if the Range Officer concludes the POT Rules 2011 were not correctly applied?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that Circular 185/4/2015 was necessitated precisely because the Point of Taxation Rules 2011 shifted liability from receipt basis to accrual basis, and the revised scrutiny procedure addresses ST-3 returns from 01.08.2015 onwards — making this the authoritative source on why POT-driven misreporting is a key scrutiny risk and how Range Officers are empowered to act on it.

questions.q: Crestline Consulting LLP, a Delhi-based management consulting firm with pan-India operations, received a detailed manual scrutiny notice for its consolidated ST-3 return for October 2012 to March 2013 — the period immediately following introduction of negative-list-based comprehensive taxation in July 2012. The LLP had claimed exemption on a significant portion of its billings (₹3.4 crore) to a Karnataka SEZ unit, treating those as zero-rated supplies under the then-prevailing SEZ exemption notifications, and did not reflect them as taxable in the return. The scrutiny memo from the Range Officer (Connaught Place Division) flags these as 'non-disclosure of taxable value' and proposes to quantify demand. The LLP's advocate argues that the scrutiny manual in force at the time (Circular 113/07/2009) did not contemplate post-negative-list scenarios and therefore the current notice under the revised 2015 framework is procedurally improper for pre-August-2015 returns. Assess the procedural validity of applying the revised scrutiny guidelines to this return period, and advise on the merits of the SEZ exemption claim under the negative-list regime.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses both the supersession of Circular 113/07/2009-ST by the 2015 circular and the rationale that the negative-list comprehensive taxation of 2012 necessitated revised scrutiny guidelines — making it the precise authoritative source on whether the old or new scrutiny framework applies to returns spanning the negative-list transition, and on the procedural basis for the Range Officer's action.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the specific list of pre-GST State Acts subsumed into GST under the GST Compensation to States framework, particularly Himachal Pradesh entertainment and luxury taxes. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving transition-era compensation claims and the net-of-exclusion calculation basis.

questions.q: Shimla Cineplex Pvt Ltd operated a multiplex in Himachal Pradesh and paid Entertainment Tax under the Himachal Pradesh Entertainments Tax (Cinematograph Shows) Act, 1968 up to June 2017. Post-GST, the State of Himachal Pradesh is claiming that the compensation cess base should include the full entertainment tax collections without netting out certain exemptions granted under a State Government notification issued in 2016. The company's CA is contesting this, arguing that only taxes 'net of exclusions under sub-section (1) of section 5 of the GST (Compensation to States) Act, 2017' should form the base. Which specific exclusions apply, and does the 2016 State exemption notification fall within those statutory exclusions while computing the protected revenue base for HP's compensation entitlement?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly identifies the HP Entertainments Tax (Cinematograph Shows) Act 1968 as a subsumed Act under entry 62 of List-II, and critically specifies the compensation base is 'net of exclusions under sub-section (1) of section 5 of the GST (Compensation to States) Act, 2017' — this precise netting formula is indispensable to the dispute.

questions.q: Regal Retreats LLP runs a chain of boutique hotels in Shimla and Manali and paid Luxury Tax under the Himachal Pradesh Tax on Luxuries (in Hotels and Lodging Houses) Act pre-GST. In a compensation-related audit for FY 2017-18, the GST Council's designated officer is questioning whether rooms let out to foreign tourists — which were exempt from luxury tax under an HP Government circular — should be added back to the base revenue for computing HP's protected revenue under the compensation mechanism. The LLP's advocate argues that the 'net of exclusions' language in the subsuming notification means these foreign-tourist rooms must remain excluded. Separately, the Central Government is arguing that since these rooms attract IGST (as a supply to a foreign tourist), they cannot simultaneously be excluded from the compensation base. Analyze whether IGST liability on foreign-tourist room bookings affects the HP Luxury Tax exclusion for the purposes of the protected revenue base calculation.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk lists the HP Tax on Luxuries (in Hotels and Lodging Houses) Act as a subsumed Act under entry 62 with the net-of-exclusions caveat, and the cross-act tension between IGST applicability and the State compensation base exclusion directly requires interpreting both the subsuming notification and the compensation statute simultaneously.

claude_reasoning: This chunk is the amendment history/notification preamble for CGST Rules 2017 Part B (Forms), specifically anchoring the provisional attachment of property form under section 83. While the chunk itself is largely a list of amending notifications, it authoritatively establishes the legal lineage and form-basis for provisional attachment orders — a high-stakes enforcement action. Questions can probe the intersection of the provisional attachment power, the form requirements under these notified rules, and the procedural/substantive rights of taxpayers facing such orders.

questions.q: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234Q1ZX, Maharashtra) is a manufacturer of engineering plastics with annual turnover of ₹42 crore. On 10-March-2025, the GST department issued a provisional attachment order freezing three bank accounts and a factory plot in Pune, citing apprehension of tax evasion of approximately ₹8.5 crore under an ongoing scrutiny proceeding. The company's CFO notes that the scrutiny notice was issued only 18 days ago and no show-cause notice has yet been served. The company's advocate wants to know: (a) under what precise statutory conditions can provisional attachment be invoked before a show-cause notice is issued, (b) which prescribed form must the attachment order take under the CGST Rules as notified and subsequently amended, and (c) whether simultaneous attachment of both movable bank assets and immovable factory land in a single order is procedurally valid — and if so, what is the outer time limit for which such attachment can survive without adjudication?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk identifies the specific notification (3/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017, as amended through subsequent notifications) that notified the CGST Rules 2017 Part B Forms — the prescribed form for provisional attachment under section 83 derives its legal authority from exactly this notification chain, making the form's validity and the procedural requirements indispensable to answering parts (b) and (c).

questions.q: Axiom Exports LLP (GSTIN 33AABFA5678R1ZP, Tamil Nadu) is an exporter of auto components. During an anti-evasion investigation in January 2025, the department provisionally attached Axiom's current account at Indian Bank, Chennai, as well as receivables owed to Axiom by a third-party debtor, Meridian Auto Parts Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 07AABCM9012S1ZK, Delhi). Meridian has now approached its own CA asking whether it is legally obligated to honour the attachment notice directed at amounts it owes Axiom, whether non-compliance exposes Meridian to independent GST liability, and whether the fact that the underlying supply between Axiom and Meridian is an inter-state B2B transaction (attracting IGST) has any bearing on the jurisdictional authority of the Tamil Nadu GST officer to issue an attachment that effectively operates in Delhi — specifically given the multi-state character of the receivable and the notification-based form requirements governing such orders.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's authority over the notified form and amendment history for provisional attachment under CGST Rules Part B is central to determining whether the Tamil Nadu officer's order in the prescribed form has pan-India operative force over a Delhi-based debtor — the legal validity of that cross-state reach turns on whether the CGST Rules form (notified via this specific notification chain) confers authority beyond the issuing officer's state jurisdiction.

title: CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the definition of 'output service' under CENVAT Credit Rules 2004 (including the Explanation deeming a reverse-charge payer's service as output service) and the enumerated heads of CENVAT credit available under Rule 3(1) — specifically excise duties and NCCD. These provisions are indispensable for cross-act CENVAT eligibility disputes involving reverse-charge service recipients and manufacturers claiming credit on multiple duty heads.

questions.q: Prakash Woollen Mills Pvt Ltd, a textile manufacturer in Ludhiana, Punjab (Central Excise registration RP-CHD-2004-001), procures job-work services from multiple small processing units. The job-work charges are paid under reverse charge, and Prakash Woollen Mills itself does not provide any outward taxable service. In FY 2006-07, it paid service tax of ₹18.4 lakhs as the 'person liable' under reverse charge on GTA and job-work services. The Deputy Commissioner has disallowed CENVAT credit of this ₹18.4 lakhs on the ground that Prakash Woollen Mills is not a 'provider of taxable service' and hence cannot treat these services as 'output services' for credit purposes. Simultaneously, the company is also claiming CENVAT credit on National Calamity Contingent Duty (NCCD) paid on inputs sourced from a supplier in Haridwar. The adjudicating authority contends that NCCD is not a creditable duty head under the CENVAT chain. Advise on (a) whether the ₹18.4 lakhs service tax paid under reverse charge qualifies as credit-eligible output service, and (b) whether NCCD paid on inputs is a permissible CENVAT credit head, citing the specific statutory basis for each position.
questions.why_this_chunk: The Explanation to clause (p) — 'output service' definition — directly resolves point (a) by deeming that a person liable to pay service tax who does not himself provide taxable service shall still have that service treated as output service. Rule 3(1)(v) explicitly lists NCCD leviable under section 136 of Finance Act 2001 as a creditable duty head, resolving point (b).

questions.q: Meridian Logistics LLP, a goods transport agency in Maharashtra with Central Excise/Service Tax registration, had its CENVAT credit account audited for the period April 2005 to March 2007. The audit team raised a demand on the following grounds: (i) Meridian availed CENVAT credit of additional excise duty paid under the Additional Duties of Excise (Goods of Special Importance) Act, 1957 on HSD/diesel used in its trucks, contending this duty head is outside the permissible credit chain; (ii) Meridian, being a GTA service provider, also paid service tax under reverse charge on certain brokerage services it received from commission agents in Delhi, and claimed those reverse-charge payments as output service tax eligible for credit flow; and (iii) Meridian further claimed credit on additional excise duty paid under the Textile and Textile Articles Act, 1978 on packing material used in its warehouse operations. The CGST Audit party (reviewing legacy records post-GST) insists all three credits were irregular. Evaluate each of the three grounds, identifying which specific duty heads and definitional provisions in the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004 govern eligibility, and advise whether Meridian can sustain its credit positions before the appellate authority.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 3(1)(iii) and 3(1)(iv) of this chunk enumerate AED (Textiles) and AED (Goods of Special Importance) as distinct, explicitly creditable duty heads — directly deciding grounds (i) and (iii). The Explanation to the 'output service' definition in clause (p), together with clause (r) including a 'person liable for paying service tax' within 'provider of taxable service', directly governs ground (ii).

title: Amends forms for filing appeal in the CESTAT (S.T.-5, S.T.-6, S.T.-7)
text:
7.  Whether the decision or order appealed against involves any question having a 
relation to the rate of service tax or to the value of service for the purpose of      
assessment.  
 
8. Description of service and whether under ‘negative list’. 
 
9.  Period of dispute. 
 
10. (A) In case of cross-objections filed by a person other than the Commissioner of 
Central Excise/ Service Tax/ Large Taxpayer Unit;

i) Amount of service tax, if any, demanded for the period of dispute

ii) Amount of interest involved upto the date of the order appealed against.

iii) Amount of refund, if any, rejected or disallowed for the period of dispute

iv) Amount of penalty imposed.

(B)  (i)  Amount of tax or penalty or interest deposited. If so, mention the amount 
deposited under each head in the box below. (A copy of the challan under which the 
deposit is made should be furnished)

Service Tax  
Penalty

(ii) If not, whether any application for dispensing with such deposit has been

11. (A) In case of cross-objections filed by the Commissioner of Central Excise/

(ii) Amount of interest demand dropped or reduced for the period of dispute

(iii) Amount of refund sanctioned or allowed for the period of dispute

(B) Whether an application for staying the operation of the order appealed against

12. Subject matter of dispute in order of priority. (please choose two items from the 
list below)    
[i) Taxability - Sl. No. of Negative List, ii) Classification of Services, iii) 
Applicability of Exemption Notification-Notification No., iv) Export of services., 
v) Import of services., vi) Point of Taxation., vii) CENVAT., viii) Refund., ix) 
Valuation., x) Others.]

Priority 1 
Priority 2

13. Central Excise Assessee Code, if registered with Central Excise.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the mandatory disclosures required in a CESTAT cross-objection form under Service Tax — specifically the financial quantification fields (tax demanded, interest, refund, penalty, pre-deposit) and the subject-matter classification list. It anchors HIGH-complexity questions because practitioners filing cross-objections in multi-issue disputes must navigate pre-deposit requirements, correct classification of dispute subject matter, and the asymmetric obligations between assessee-filers and Commissioner-filers.

questions.q: Meridian Hospitality Services Pvt Ltd, registered in Maharashtra (Mumbai), received an SCN demanding Rs 1.8 crore in service tax on 'renting of immovable property' services for the period April 2013 to March 2016, plus interest of Rs 62 lakh computed to the date of the original OIO, and a penalty of Rs 1.8 crore under the equivalent of Section 78. The Commissioner (Appeals) partially dropped the demand — confirmed only Rs 90 lakh tax, Rs 31 lakh interest, and imposed a 25% penalty — holding that certain floors were used for exempt purposes. Both Meridian and the jurisdictional Commissioner (Service Tax, Mumbai-II) are now cross-filing before CESTAT. Meridian's CFO asks: when filling the CESTAT cross-objection form, which financial figures must Meridian disclose under the 'amount deposited' sub-head versus the 'demand confirmed' sub-head, and does Meridian need to separately furnish a challan copy for the pre-deposit already paid at the Commissioner (Appeals) stage? Separately, for the subject-matter classification, should Meridian prioritise 'Taxability – Negative List' or 'Applicability of Exemption Notification' as Priority 1, given that renting of immovable property was brought under the negative list only from July 2012 but was exempt via a notification before that?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's fields 10(B)(i) (amount deposited under each head with challan copy requirement) and 10(A)(i)–(iv) (tax demanded, interest, refund, penalty for cross-objections by persons other than Commissioner) are directly indispensable; additionally, field 12's subject-matter list — specifically items i) Taxability/Negative List and iii) Applicability of Exemption Notification — is the only authoritative source for how to classify the dispute priority.

questions.q: Aravind Freight Forwarders LLP, a customs house agent based in Chennai (Tamil Nadu), was served an order-in-original confirming a service tax demand of Rs 45 lakh on 'clearing and forwarding agent' services for FY 2014-16, with interest of Rs 18 lakh to the OIO date and a penalty of Rs 45 lakh. Aravind had paid Rs 10 lakh as pre-deposit under protest at the Commissioner (Appeals) stage but the appeal was rejected in full. Aravind is now filing a cross-objection before CESTAT challenging valuation (alleging that reimbursements collected from clients were wrongly included in taxable value) while simultaneously the jurisdictional LTU Commissioner is filing a cross-objection seeking enhancement of penalty to 100% of tax. For Aravind's cross-objection, counsel needs to know: (a) must Aravind disclose the Rs 10 lakh pre-deposit paid at Commissioner (Appeals) stage in field 10(B)(i), and if so, must a challan copy be attached even if the original challan was submitted at the lower appellate stage; (b) under field 12, if Aravind wishes to prioritise the valuation ground but also wants to preserve the CENVAT credit ground as a fallback, which two priority slots should be used and does the form allow any written elaboration beyond the two-item selection; and (c) for the LTU Commissioner's parallel cross-objection, which fields under clause (B) of item 11 are applicable and does the Commissioner need to file a stay application given that the original order has been upheld?
questions.why_this_chunk: Field 10(B)(i)'s requirement to disclose amounts deposited under each head (Service Tax, Penalty, Interest) with challan copies is directly triggered; field 12's two-priority subject-matter list (items ix) Valuation and vii) CENVAT) governs ground selection; and field 11(B)'s stay-application question specifically applies to the Commissioner's cross-objection — all three sub-questions are answerable only by reference to this chunk's specific form fields.

title: The Finance Act, 2023
text:
16
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINARY
[PART II—

Provided also that if the premium is payable by a person for more
than one life insurance policy, other than unit linked insurance policy,
issued on or after the 1st day of April, 2023, the provisions of this clause
shall apply only with respect to those life insurance policies, other than
unit linked insurance policies, where the aggregate amount of premium

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers two Finance Act 2023 amendments to income-tax exemptions: (a) the provisos governing aggregate-premium caps for multiple non-ULIP life insurance policies issued on/after 1-Apr-2023, and (b) the new clause 12C exempting Agniveer Corpus Fund payments to enrollees or their nominees under the Agnipath Scheme (defined by cross-reference to section 80CCH). Both provisions have enough interpretive nuance and multi-provision linkage to anchor HIGH-complexity practitioner questions.

questions.q: Rajesh Menon, a resident individual in Kerala, holds three life insurance policies (all non-ULIP, all issued on 15-June-2023): Policy A with annual premium ₹2.8 lakh, Policy B with annual premium ₹1.5 lakh, and Policy C with annual premium ₹90,000. His wife Priya is the nominee on Policy C. For PY 2025-26 he receives a maturity payout of ₹18 lakh under Policy B (he is alive). His CA is arguing that Policy B's maturity proceeds are fully exempt because the premium on Policy B alone does not breach the ₹2.5 lakh threshold, while the income-tax officer contends that the aggregate of all three policies must be tested together. Which interpretation is correct, and does the answer change if Policy A lapses mid-term before the maturity of Policy B? Cite the exact proviso structure that governs this aggregation test.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the sixth and seventh provisos to the life-insurance exemption clause, explicitly setting out how the aggregate-premium cap applies 'with respect to those life insurance policies … where the aggregate amount of premium does not exceed the amount referred to in the sixth proviso in any of the previous years during the term of any of those policies' — this specific aggregation rule across multiple non-ULIP policies is the crux of the dispute and is only resolvable using this text.

questions.q: Corporal Arvind Kumar (GSTIN not applicable — individual) was enrolled under the Agnipath Scheme in July 2023. He was medically discharged in February 2026. His mother Kamla Devi, who is his sole nominee, receives the full corpus payout from the Agniveer Corpus Fund in March 2026. Simultaneously, Arvind himself receives a partial 'exit benefit' credited directly to his account. The assessing officer for AY 2026-27 proposes to tax (i) the payout to Kamla Devi as income from other sources in her hands, and (ii) the exit benefit in Arvind's hands on the ground that the discharge was not a 'death' and no specific exemption applies to a living dischargee. Advise on whether either or both amounts are exempt, specifically addressing whether the exemption clause extends to nominees of living (non-deceased) enrollees and how the term 'Agniveer Corpus Fund' is defined for this purpose.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 12C inserted by this chunk grants exemption to 'any payment from the Agniveer Corpus Fund to a person enrolled under the Agnipath Scheme, or to his nominee' — the phrase 'or to his nominee' without any restriction to death scenarios, and the Explanation cross-referencing section 80CCH for the definition of 'Agniveer Corpus Fund', are both indispensable to resolving whether Kamla Devi's receipt (nominee of a living person) and Arvind's own exit receipt qualify.

title: Rate of Duty to be the same even if classification is changed, except few - Dairy Spread - Classified under S.H. No. 2106.90 & rate of duty is 50% - Frozen roots and tubers and oaknut and chestnut extract will have lower rate of duty since
text:
ed by the three notification mentioned in para 2 above in order to maintain the existing
level of effective rate of Basic customs duties. In any case, there is no increase in rate of duty on any of the affected items. In
the event of any item being found to have undergone a change in the effective duty structure consequent to the switch over to
the 1.1.96 version of the Tariff and which is not covered by the notifications mentioned in para 2 above (excluding the three
items mentioned in para 3 above), the same may be immediately brought to the notice of the Board, in the from of Telex/ Fax
and followed by a detailed D.O. letter addressed to the Member (Customs) for taking appropriate action.

Sd/-

(S.C. Choudhury)

Director (Customs)
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the transition to the 1.1.96 version of the Customs Tariff, specifically the principle that effective Basic Customs Duty rates must be maintained across the reclassification, with three named exceptions (Dairy Spread, Frozen roots/tubers, oaknut/chestnut extract). It authoritatively governs what happens when an importer finds a duty-rate discrepancy post-reclassification and the escalation procedure required.

questions.q: Bharat Agro Exports Pvt Ltd (IEC: AACB1234X), based in Pune, Maharashtra, imported a consignment of frozen cassava roots (HS 0714) in February 1996, shortly after the Customs Tariff was revised to its 1996 version. The assessing officer at JNPT has applied a Basic Customs Duty rate of 35%, whereas the company's CHA claims the effective rate prior to reclassification was 25% and that no duty increase was intended by the Board. The importer has received no specific exemption notification covering this item. What is the legal position — can the importer contend that the pre-1.1.96 effective rate must be preserved, and if the CHA believes the duty structure has inadvertently changed due to the tariff switch, what is the prescribed escalation mechanism to seek correction from the Board?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk expressly states that the notifications were issued to maintain the existing level of effective Basic Customs Duty rates and that there should be no increase on any affected item; it also prescribes the exact escalation path (Telex/Fax to the Board followed by a D.O. letter to Member (Customs)) for items whose effective duty structure changed post-switchover and are not covered by the three listed notifications.

questions.q: Sunrise Dairy Products LLP, a Delhi-based importer, cleared a shipment of 'dairy spread' (a fat-blend product) from New Zealand in January 1996 under what it believed was a general food-preparations heading, paying Basic Customs Duty at 40%. Post-clearance, a Show Cause Notice has been issued reclassifying the goods under the dairy-spread heading with a duty rate of 50%, which the department says is one of the expressly carved-out exceptions where the rate was deliberately changed when switching to the 1.1.96 tariff. The importer argues that the circular's overarching principle of rate-neutrality should protect it from any differential demand. As the importer's advocate, analyse: (a) whether the general rate-preservation principle in the circular can override the specific exception carved out for dairy spread, and (b) the quantum of differential duty and interest exposure if the reclassification stands.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the authoritative source for the carve-out: it identifies dairy spread as one of the three items specifically excluded from the rate-neutrality principle when transitioning to the 1.1.96 tariff version, making it indispensable to determining whether the importer can invoke the general non-increase assurance or must accept the 50% rate.

title: Seeks to amend notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) to prescribe change in CGST rate of goods.
text:
[To be published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 01/2021 – Central Tax (Rate)

New Delhi, the 2nd June, 2021

G.S.R.....(E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 9 and sub-section (5) of section 15 of

the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017),the Central Government, on the recommendations of the

Council, hereby makes the following further amendments in the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry

of Finance(Department of Revenue), No.1/2017-Central Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017,published in the Gazette

of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 673(E), dated the 28th June, 2017,

namely:-

In the said notification, -

(a) in Schedule I - 2.5%,against S. No. 259A, for the entry in column (2), the entry “9503” shall be substituted;

(b) after Schedule I, in the List 1,after serial number 230 and the entries relating thereto, the following shall be

inserted, namely-

“(231). Diethylcarbamazine ”.

2. This notification shall come into force on the 2nd day of June, 2021.

[F. No. 354/53/2021]

(Rajeev Ranjan)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note: - The principal notification No.1/2017-Central Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017 was published in the Gazette

of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 673(E), dated the 28th June, 2017 and was

last amended by notification No.03/2020- Central Tax(Rate), dated the 25th March, 2020, published in the Gazette of

India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number GSR 216(E), dated the 25th March, 2020.
claude_reasoning: This notification authoritatively covers two specific rate changes effective 2-Jun-2021: (1) HS code 9503 moved to Schedule I (2.5% CGST) at S.No. 259A, and (2) Diethylcarbamazine added to List 1 (exemption/concessional list) after serial 230. Both are narrow, date-specific amendments to the master rate notification, making them ideal anchors for HIGH-complexity questions involving transitional periods, classification disputes, and supply chain scenarios.

questions.q: Sparkle Toys & Games Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234A1Z5, Maharashtra) manufactures plastic toys classified under HS 9503 and was charging 6% CGST (Schedule II rate) on all domestic supplies made between April 2021 and July 2021. A consignment of Rs 45 lakhs was invoiced to a retailer in Gujarat on 28-May-2021 and another consignment of Rs 38 lakhs was invoiced on 5-June-2021. The Gujarat buyer is now claiming that Sparkle overcharged CGST on the June invoice and is demanding a credit note. Sparkle's CFO argues the rate was the same throughout. Who is correct, what is the correct CGST rate applicable to each invoice, and what documentary steps must Sparkle follow under the CGST rules to issue or refuse the credit note?
questions.why_this_chunk: The notification explicitly moves HS 9503 to Schedule I (2.5% CGST) effective 2-June-2021, meaning the pre- and post-2-June rate differ; the May invoice falls under the old rate and the June invoice under the amended rate — this transitional distinction is only resolvable by reference to this specific notification and its effective date.

questions.q: Pharma Distributors LLP (GSTIN 29AABFP9876B1ZK, Karnataka) purchased 500 kg of Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) API from a manufacturer in Hyderabad under an invoice dated 15-May-2021 and paid CGST + SGST at the rate applicable at that time. They then resold the same stock to a government hospital in Kerala under a supply contract on 10-June-2021, charging the same rate. The Kerala hospital's purchase committee is contesting the CGST charged on the June supply, arguing DEC now attracts a lower/nil rate under a recently issued notification. Determine (a) what CGST rate applied to each leg of the transaction, (b) whether the LLP can claim a transitional ITC adjustment on the stock purchased in May but supplied in June, and (c) what impact, if any, arises under the place-of-supply rules given the inter-state movement from Karnataka to Kerala.
questions.why_this_chunk: The notification specifically inserts 'Diethylcarbamazine' at serial 231 of List 1 to the master rate notification effective 2-June-2021, which is the operative fact that changes the rate between the May purchase and June supply legs; without this chunk the question cannot be answered as to which rate applies post-2-June-2021 for DEC.

title: Anti dumping duty on import of sodium tripolyphosphate
text:
bove, exporting directly or through any exporter other than M/S Chung Chemicals SON
BHD, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, it shall be deemed to have been specified against S.No.3 of the said Table for the purposes of
this notification.

2.            The anti-dumping duty imposed under this notification shall be effective upto and inclusive of the 16 th day of
December, 2002, and shall be payable in Indian currency.

Explanation. - For the purposes of this notification, -

(a)     "landed value" means the assessable value as determined under the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962) and includes all
duties of customs except duties levied under sections 3, 3A, 8B, 9 and 9A of the said Customs Tariff Act; and

(b)     rate of exchange applicable for the purposes of calculation of such anti-dumping duty shall be the rate which is specified
in the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), issued from time to time, in
exercise of the powers conferred by sub-clause (i) of clause (a) of sub-section (3) of section 14 of the said Customs Act, and
the relevant date for the determination of the rate of exchange shall be the date of presentation of the bill of entry under section
46 of the said Customs Act.

G.D.Lohani

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the anti-dumping duty notification 62/2002-Custom on sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP) imports, specifically: (1) the exporter-specific duty structure where imports from any exporter other than M/s Chung Chemicals SDN BHD, Kuala Lumpur get classified under S.No.3 (higher duty), (2) the sunset date of 16-Dec-2002, (3) the precise 'landed value' definition excluding duties under sections 3, 3A, 8B, 9, 9A of the Customs Tariff Act, and (4) the exchange rate determination mechanism tied to bill of entry date under section 46. These are highly specific procedural/computation anchors that support complex practitioner queries.

questions.q: Our client, Parekh Chemicals Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, IEC: AABCP1234Q), imported 500 MT of sodium tripolyphosphate from Petronas Trading Sdn Bhd, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia in November 2002. The CIF invoice value was USD 185,000. At the time of filing the bill of entry on 18-Nov-2002, the RBI reference rate was Rs 48.20/USD, but the CBEC exchange rate notification in force that day showed Rs 47.85/USD. The assessable value was determined at Rs 88,72,500 by the appraising officer. Now, customs audit is questioning whether the anti-dumping duty base should have included the CVD (section 3 duty) in the 'landed value' computation, and whether the correct exchange rate was applied. Separately, since Petronas Trading is not the manufacturer but merely a trader sourcing from Chung Chemicals' plant, the importer argues they qualify for the Chung Chemicals-specific lower duty rate. Can you advise on (a) the correct 'landed value' computation for anti-dumping duty base, (b) which exchange rate governs, and (c) whether a trader sourcing from Chung Chemicals qualifies for the manufacturer-specific duty rate?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's Explanation clause (a) explicitly excludes section 3 duties (CVD) from 'landed value', directly resolving audit question (a). Explanation clause (b) mandates the CBEC exchange rate notification under section 14(3)(a)(i) as of bill of entry date under section 46, resolving question (b). The deeming provision that any exporter 'other than M/s Chung Chemicals SDN BHD' falls under S.No.3 directly resolves question (c) — a trader is not Chung Chemicals even if sourcing from them.

questions.q: Rajasthan Detergents LLP (Jaipur, IEC: AABFR5678M) entered into a contract in October 2002 to import 1,200 MT of sodium tripolyphosphate from M/s Chung Chemicals SDN BHD, Kuala Lumpur, directly, with shipment in two tranches: 600 MT shipped on 10-Dec-2002 (bill of entry filed 14-Dec-2002) and 600 MT shipped on 20-Dec-2002 (bill of entry filed 24-Dec-2002). The goods in the second tranche were held at Nhava Sheva port pending clearance. The client's clearing agent is now arguing that since the contract was signed with Chung Chemicals and the goods were shipped before 20-Dec, the anti-dumping duty should not apply to the second tranche. Additionally, for the first tranche, the importer is claiming the Chung Chemicals-specific lower duty rate. Advise on: (a) whether the anti-dumping duty applies to the second tranche given the notification's stated expiry date, (b) which date governs — shipment date or bill of entry date — for determining duty liability, and (c) whether the first tranche qualifies for the Chung Chemicals-specific rate and what landed value computation would apply.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states the notification is 'effective upto and inclusive of the 16th day of December, 2002' — the bill of entry date (24-Dec) falls after expiry, making the date-of-presentation under section 46 (per Explanation (b)) the operative date, resolving questions (a) and (b). For question (c), the chunk's deeming provision and Explanation (a)'s landed value definition (excluding sections 3, 3A, 8B, 9, 9A duties) are indispensable.

title: Transhipment of Cargo to Nepal under Electronic Cargo Tracking System (Amendment) Regulations, 2020.

firm or the directors of the company as the case may be)

of filing Declaration of Transhipment).

(Ananth Rathakrishnan)
