Note: The principal Notification No. 68/2019-Customs (N.T.) was published in the Gazette of 
India, Extraordinary, vide G.S. R. 704(E), dated the 30th September, 2019.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the application form requirements for transhipment agents filing Declarations of Transhipment for cargo to Nepal under the ECTS framework — specifically the mandatory disclosures (authorised persons, educational qualifications, customs cases history, and the foreign agent contract/MOU enclosure). It supports HIGH-complexity queries about who can legitimately file transhipment declarations and what compliance failures in the authorisation chain expose a consignee/shipper to.

questions.q: Himalayan Logistics Pvt Ltd, incorporated in Delhi with its CHA licence, has been engaged by Kathmandu-based Everest Trading Co to move a consignment of 500 MT of rice from Kolkata port to Nepal via the Electronic Cargo Tracking System. The company's board has authorised only one director — Mr. Suresh Nair — to sign customs documents, but the actual Declaration of Transhipment at Kolkata customs is being filed by a junior operations executive, Ms. Priya Das, who holds a graduate degree in commerce and has no separate board authorisation. Himalayan Logistics also has one compounding case pending against it under the Customs Act from 2021. At the time of filing, they submit only an email chain as evidence of their arrangement with the Nepali foreign authorising agent rather than a formal MOU or contract. Can Customs at Kolkata reject the transhipment declaration on procedural grounds, and if so, on which specific deficiencies in the application — and does the pending customs case automatically disqualify the company or merely require disclosure?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's form fields 5 (authorised persons must be named partners/directors/duly authorised employees), 6 (educational qualification of persons actually filing), 7 (mandatory disclosure of cases booked under Customs Act), and 8 (enclosures must be a formal contract, MOU, or agreement — not an informal email) are each directly implicated in determining which deficiencies are procedurally fatal vs merely disclosable.

questions.q: Shree Vyankatesh Exports LLP, a Pune-based merchant exporter (GSTIN 27AADFS1234B1ZV), has entered into a tripartite arrangement where goods (auto components valued at ₹2.4 crore) manufactured by a third-party job worker in Ludhiana are to be shipped from Amritsar ICD to Nepal, with the Nepal importer being Pashupati Auto Parts Pvt Ltd of Kathmandu. The LLP proposes to file the Declaration of Transhipment itself rather than appointing a separate customs broker, and nominates two of its three partners — one being a Class 10 pass — as the authorised persons for ECTS filing. The foreign authorising agent relationship is evidenced by a memorandum of understanding. A search of the LLP's customs history reveals a show-cause notice issued but not yet adjudicated. Does the LLP's self-filing qualify under the ECTS transhipment regulations, is the Class 10 educational qualification of the nominated partner sufficient or does it create a grounds for rejection, and how does the unadjudicated show-cause notice interact with the disclosure obligation under the application form?
questions.why_this_chunk: Field 5 (partners of a firm can be authorised persons, but they must be named — testing whether self-filing by an LLP partner is valid), field 6 (educational qualification of each person actually filing must be disclosed, raising the question of whether a minimum qualification is prescribed or merely disclosed), field 7 (cases 'booked' — whether an unadjudicated SCN counts as a 'case booked'), and field 8(b) (MOU as a valid enclosure) are all directly required to answer this question.

title: Certificate action under clause (e) of sub-section (1) section 79
text:
Instructions:-

1. Terms used:

a. GSTIN:  Goods and Services Tax Identification Number 
b. UIN:     Unique Identity Number 
c. UQC:     Unit Quantity Code 
d. HSN:    Harmonized System of Nomenclature 
e. POS:     Place of Supply (Respective State) 
f. B to B:   From one registered person to another registered person 
g. B to C:   From registered person to unregistered person

2. GSTR-5 is applicable to non-resident taxable person and it is a monthly return. 
3. The details in GSTR-5 should be furnished by 20thof the month succeeding the

relevant tax period or within 7 days from the last date of the registration whichever is 
earlier.

4. Table 3 consists of details of import of goods, bill of entry wise and taxpayer has to

specify the amount of ITC eligible on such import of goods.

5. Recipient to provide for Bill of Entry information including six digits port

code and seven digits bill of entry number.

6. Table 4 consists of amendment of import of goods which are declared in the returns

of earlier tax period.

7. [Invoice-level information, rate-wise, pertaining to the tax period should be reported

as under: 
(i.) 
for all B to B supplies (whether inter-State or intra-State), invoice level

details should be uploaded in Table 5; 
(ii.) 
for all inter-state B to C supplies, where invoice value is more than Rs.

2,50,000/- (B to C Large) invoice level detail to be provided in Table 6; and 
(iii.) 
for all B to C supplies, other than those reported in table 6, shall be reported

in Table 7 providing State-wise summary of such supplies.]43

8. 
Table 8 consists of amendments in respect of -

i. 
B2B outward supplies declared in the previous tax period;

ii. 
―B2C inter-State invoices where invoice value is more than [rupees]442.5 
lakhs‖ reported in the previous tax period; and

iii. 
Original Debit and credit note details and its amendments.

9. 
Table 9 covers the Amendments in respect of B2C outward supplies other than

inter-State supplies where invoice value is more than Rs 250000/-.

43Substitued vide Notf No. 79/2020-CT dated 15.10.2020 for ―7.Invoice-level information, rate-wise, 
pertaining to the tax period separately for goods and services should be reported as under:  
i.For all B to B supplies (whether inter-State or intra-State), invoice level details should be uploaded in Table 
5; 
ii.For all inter-state B to C supplies, where invoice value is more than Rs. 2,50,000/- (B to C Large) invoice 
level detail to be provided in Table 6; and 
iii.For all B to C supplies (whether inter-State or intra-State) where invoice value is up to Rs. 2,50,000/- State-
wise summary of supplies shall be filed in Table 7.‖. 
44 Inserted vide Notf No. 79/2020-CT dated 15.10.2020.

158
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers GSTR-5 filing instructions for non-resident taxable persons, including the 20th-of-month / 7-days-from-registration-end deadline, B2B vs B2C invoice thresholds (Rs 2.5 lakh for inter-state B2C large), import of goods via bill of entry, and amendment tables — all specific to NRTPs, making it a strong anchor for practitioner scenarios involving foreign entities doing temporary business in India.

questions.q: GlobalEdge Solutions GmbH, a German software firm with no permanent establishment in India, obtains a temporary non-resident taxable person registration (GSTIN 27TEMP1234A1Z5) in Maharashtra to provide on-site technical services at a client's Mumbai facility during March and April 2026. Their registration period ends on 30-April-2026. They supply services worth Rs 3,20,000 to Reliance Industries Ltd (a registered person in Maharashtra) on 15-April-2026, and also supply services worth Rs 2,80,000 to an unregistered individual in Delhi on 20-April-2026. By what date must GlobalEdge Solutions GmbH file their GSTR-5 for April 2026, and in which tables must each of the two supplies be reported — specifically, does the Delhi supply go into Table 6 or Table 7 given the invoice value and the inter-state nature of the transaction?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies the dual deadline rule (20th of succeeding month OR 7 days from last date of registration, whichever is earlier) and the exact threshold rule: inter-state B2C invoices above Rs 2,50,000 go to Table 6 while others go to Table 7 — both facts are indispensable to answering which table the Rs 2.8 lakh Delhi supply lands in and whether the April 30 registration end triggers the 7-day rule.

questions.q: TechBridge Pte Ltd, a Singapore-incorporated company, registers as a non-resident taxable person in India (GSTIN 07NRTP9876B1Z3) for a Delhi trade exhibition running 1–25 March 2026. During the period they import exhibition equipment (HDDs, display units) via Air Cargo Complex Delhi, receiving two bills of entry — BE No. 4521678 on 5-March and BE No. 4523901 on 12-March — and claim ITC on both. They also make B2B supplies to three registered Delhi buyers totalling Rs 8,40,000, and one inter-state B2C supply of Rs 3,10,000 to an unregistered buyer in Karnataka. Their registration lapses on 25-March-2026. What is the last permissible date for filing their GSTR-5, how must the two bills of entry be reported (including the port code requirement), and if they incorrectly reported BE No. 4521678 in an earlier return, which table must the amendment appear in?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the sole authority for: (a) the 7-days-from-registration-end deadline that overrides the 20th-of-month rule when registration ends mid-month; (b) Table 3 for bill-of-entry-wise import ITC with 6-digit port code + 7-digit BE number; (c) Table 4 for amendments to previously declared import entries — all three facts are necessary and specific to GSTR-5 NRTPs.

title: Customs Tariff (Determination of Origin of Goods under the Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement between the Republic of India and Japan) Rules, 2011
text:
agraph 7 within the period referred to in sub-paragraph (4) of paragraph 7; or

(c) where the information provided to the customs authority of the importing Party pursuant to paragraph 6 or paragraph 7, is
not sufficient to prove that the good qualifies as an originating good of the exporting Party.

(3) After carrying out the procedures outlined in paragraph 6 or paragraph 7 as the case may be, the customs authority of the
importing Party shall provide the competent governmental authority of the exporting Party with a written determination of
whether or not the good qualifies as an originating good of the exporting Party, including findings of fact and the legal basis for
the determination:

Provided that the competent governmental authority of the exporting Party shall inform such determination by the customs
authority of the importing Party to the exporter, or the producer of the good in the exporting Party, whose premises were
subject of the visit referred to in paragraph 7.

10. Penalties and measures against fraudulent acts. - Each Party shall establish or maintain, in accordance with its laws
and regulations, appropriate penalties or other measures against its exporters to whom a certificate of origin has been issued
and the producers of a good in the exporting Party referred to in clause (b) of sub-paragraph (6) of paragraph 3 who have
committed fraudulent acts in connection with a certificate of origin, including submission of false declarations or documents to
its competent governmental authority of the exporting Party or its designees.

11. Implementing procedures.- The Implementing procedures for implementation of this Annexure is enclosed as
Appendix-A .
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the India-Japan CEPA origin verification procedures — specifically the written determination requirement after verification visits, penalties for fraudulent certificates of origin, and the legal obligations on exporters/producers. These provisions are indispensable for practitioners dealing with origin disputes, customs audits, and fraud liability under CEPA.

questions.q: Nakamura Electronics India Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) exported precision sensors to Osaka Denki KK in Japan under the India-Japan CEPA, claiming preferential duty at 0% by furnishing a certificate of origin. The Japanese customs authority (NACCS) conducted a verification visit to the Mumbai plant in January 2026 and found that certain sub-assemblies sourced from a Chinese supplier did not satisfy the value-added threshold. Japanese customs has now issued a written determination denying originating status. Nakamura's management wants to know: (a) what legal standard must the Japanese customs authority's written determination satisfy under the CEPA rules, (b) through which channel must the Indian competent authority communicate this adverse determination back to Nakamura as the producer, and (c) what exposure does Nakamura face under Indian law if it is established that the original certificate of origin was supported by a false bill of materials submitted to DGFT?
questions.why_this_chunk: Paragraph 10 (3) proviso mandates the specific communication chain — importing Party customs → exporting Party competent authority → exporter/producer — and paragraph 10 sets out the penalty framework for fraudulent CoO submissions, both of which are essential to answering all three sub-questions.

questions.q: Tata Precision Components LLP, a manufacturer in Pune (Maharashtra), supplies automobile gear shafts to a Toyota group entity in Japan under CEPA preferential tariff. During a desk-based verification initiated by Japanese customs in October 2025, the LLP failed to furnish supplementary cost-build-up data within the prescribed response period. Japanese customs accordingly suspended the preferential tariff benefit and is now assessing back-duty on all past shipments. The LLP's CFO asks: (a) what is the legal consequence under the CEPA rules of failing to respond within the verification period, (b) does the Japanese customs authority still owe Tata a written determination with findings of fact even after the non-response triggers denial, and (c) if the LLP can demonstrate the non-response was due to a bona fide document-retention lapse rather than fraud, does that affect the penalty exposure of the LLP as producer under the exporting Party's obligations?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-paragraph (c) of the chunk's denial triggers (non-sufficient information) and the written determination requirement under paragraph 10(3) — including the proviso on communicating to the producer — are the precise provisions that determine whether a written determination is still required post-non-response, and paragraph 10 on penalties distinguishes fraudulent acts from procedural lapses.

title: Seeks to amend CGST Rules notification no 3/2017-Central Tax dt 19.06.2017
text:
(5) The fees for filing of appeal or restoration of appeal shall be one thousand rupees for 
every one lakh rupees of tax or input tax credit involved or the difference in tax or input tax 
credit involved or the amount of fine, fee or penalty determined in the order appealed 
against, subject to a maximum of twenty five thousand rupees.

(6) There shall be no fee for application made before the Appellate Tribunal for rectification 
of errors referred to in sub-section (10) of section 112.

111. Application to the Appellate Tribunal.- (1) An application to the Appellate Tribunal 
under sub-section (3) of section 112 shall be made electronically or otherwise, in FORM 
GST APL-07, along with the relevant documents on the common portal.

(2)  A certified copy of the decision or order appealed against shall be submitted within 
seven days of filing the application under sub-rule (1) and an appeal number shall be 
generated by the Registrar.

112. Production of additional evidence before the Appellate Authority or the Appellate 
Tribunal.-  (1) The appellant shall not be allowed to produce before the Appellate Authority 
or the Appellate Tribunal any evidence, whether oral or documentary, other than the 
evidence produced by him during the course of the proceedings before the adjudicating 
authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate Authority except in the following 
circumstances, namely:-

(a) where the adjudicating authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate

Authority has refused to admit evidence which ought to have been admitted; 
or

(b) where the appellant was prevented by sufficient cause from producing the

evidence which he was called upon to produce by the adjudicating authority 
or, as the case may be, the Appellate Authority; or

(c) where the appellant was prevented by sufficient cause from producing before

the adjudicating authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate Authority any 
evidence which is relevant to any ground of appeal; or

(d) where the adjudicating authority or, as the case may be, the Appellate

Authority has made the order appealed against without giving sufficient 
opportunity to the appellant to adduce evidence relevant to any ground of 
appeal.

(2) No evidence shall be admitted under sub-rule (1) unless the Appellate Authority or

the Appellate Tribunal records in writing the reasons for its admission.

(3) The Appellate Authority or the Appellate Tribunal shall not take any evidence

produced under sub-rule (1) unless the adjudicating authority or an officer 
authorised in this behalf by the said authority has been allowed a reasonable 
opportunity -

(a) to examine the evidence or document or to cross-examine any witness

produced by the appellant; or

(b) to produce any evidence or any witness in rebuttal of the evidence produced

by the appellant under sub-rule (1).

(4) Nothing contained in this rule shall affect the power of the Appellate Authority or

the Appellate Tribunal to direct the production of any document, or the examination 
of any witness, to enable it to dispose of the appeal.

53
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GST appellate procedure rules: fees for filing appeals to the Appellate Tribunal (Rule 110(5)-(6)), application procedure under Rule 111, and the strict evidentiary rules under Rule 112 governing production of additional evidence before the Appellate Authority or Tribunal. These provisions are indispensable for high-complexity practitioner queries involving appeal strategy, fee computation, and evidence admissibility disputes.

questions.q: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM1234C1ZX, Maharashtra) received an adverse order from the adjudicating authority dated 12-Jan-2025 confirming a demand of ₹18.5 lakh IGST on alleged mis-classification of goods exported to a Dubai buyer, plus a penalty of ₹3.7 lakh. The LLP had submitted certain e-way bills and shipping bills during the original proceedings, but the adjudicating authority rejected them without recording reasons. The LLP now wants to (a) compute the exact filing fee for appeal before the GST Appellate Tribunal, and (b) introduce three additional commercial invoices and a customs examination report that were available at the time of original proceedings but not submitted due to the authorized representative's illness. Can the LLP introduce this evidence before the Tribunal, and if so, under what precise conditions — and what is the total fee payable if the LLP is also challenging the ₹3.7 lakh penalty in the same appeal?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 110(5) governs the fee formula (₹1,000 per lakh, max ₹25,000) applied separately to the tax demand and the penalty amount; Rule 112(1)(a) and (c) govern whether evidence wrongly refused below or not produced due to sufficient cause can be admitted, and Rule 112(2)-(3) impose the written-reasons and cross-examination preconditions indispensable to answering whether the new invoices can be admitted.

questions.q: Precision Pharma Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AAECP5678K1ZM, Karnataka) lost a first appellate order dated 05-Mar-2025 confirming denial of ITC of ₹42 lakh on capital goods, on the ground that the supplier (a Gujarat-registered vendor, GSTIN 24AABCS9101L2ZQ) had allegedly not filed its GSTR-1 for the relevant period. Precision's advocate argues that during the first appeal, Precision tried to submit a CA-certified ledger extract and the supplier's rectified GSTR-3B as additional evidence, but the Appellate Authority refused admission without recording any written reasons. Precision now wants to move the GST Appellate Tribunal. Advise on: (i) the fee for filing the Tribunal application; (ii) whether the earlier wrongful refusal of evidence by the Appellate Authority creates a valid ground to produce that same evidence before the Tribunal; and (iii) whether Precision can simultaneously seek rectification of a clerical error in the Appellate Authority's order (wrong GSTIN of supplier recorded) at the Tribunal without paying any additional fee.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 110(5) provides the fee computation on the ITC amount (₹42 lakh → capped at ₹25,000); Rule 112(1)(a) directly covers the scenario where the Appellate Authority refused to admit evidence that ought to have been admitted, creating a specific ground for the Tribunal to allow it; Rule 110(6) explicitly provides zero fee for rectification applications under section 112(10), making all three sub-questions answerable only from this chunk.

133
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers GSTR-2B ITC reconciliation rules, reverse charge liability, credit note reversals, and duplicate credit prohibition — authoritative for ITC eligibility disputes and reconciliation failures in complex supply chains.

questions.q: Meridian Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) receives a credit note dated 18-Mar-2025 from its API supplier in Hyderabad for ₹14.2 lakh relating to a February 2025 supply. The credit note appears in GSTR-2B for March 2025, but Meridian's accounts team has already reversed the ITC in their February 2025 GSTR-3B based on the supplier's intimation email. Now in April 2025, GSTR-2B again reflects the same credit note under a corrected document number issued by the supplier. The tax manager wants to know: (a) is Meridian obligated to reverse ITC again in April 2025 on the basis of the re-appearing credit note in GSTR-2B, or does the February reversal suffice; and (b) if the supplier had also raised a debit note in March correcting the original invoice amount upward by ₹3.1 lakh, how should Meridian treat the ITC on that debit note given it was not in February's GSTR-2B but appears only in March's — specifically, can Meridian claim the incremental credit in GSTR-3B for March without risking a dual-credit allegation?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's explicit rule that 'no credit shall be taken twice for any document under any circumstances' and the continuing obligation to 'reverse credit wherever necessary' on receipt of credit notes are indispensable to resolving both sub-questions about the re-appearing credit note and the debit note ITC claim.

questions.q: Arjun Castings LLP (GSTIN 29AABFA5678K1ZQ, Karnataka) is both a recipient of GTA services (liable on reverse charge) and an ISD distributing ITC to its three manufacturing units across Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh. During Q4 FY 2024-25, one of its Tamil Nadu units (GSTIN 33AABFA5678K2ZR) discovers through GSTR-2B reconciliation that ₹8.6 lakh of ITC distributed by the ISD relates to invoices that the head office vendor has since cancelled and re-raised under fresh document numbers — the cancelled invoices still appear in the unit's GSTR-2B download for January 2025 while the fresh invoices appear in February 2025. Simultaneously, Arjun Castings LLP has an unresolved reverse charge liability of ₹2.4 lakh for inward GTA services for December 2024 that was omitted from GSTR-3B. The CFO asks: must the Tamil Nadu unit reverse the ₹8.6 lakh ITC in January itself (even though the fresh invoices appear in February GSTR-2B and the ITC will effectively be re-claimable), and can the unpaid reverse charge liability for December be set off against the ITC available in February's GSTR-2B, or must it be paid in cash first before any ITC claim on the fresh invoices?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's rules that reverse charge tax liability 'continues' independently of ITC credits, that credit must be 'reversed wherever necessary' on receipt of credit notes/cancellations, and the prohibition on double-credit directly govern both the ISD reversal timing question and the interaction between outstanding RCM liability and fresh ITC availability.

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Finance Act 1995 amendments to Income-tax sections 80HHE (deletion of the proviso restricting software-export deduction, effective 1-Apr-1996) and 80-IA (expansion to infrastructure facilities under a BOT/transfer agreement), both of which generate cross-act disputes when the same transaction is scrutinised under GST/service-tax rules simultaneously. The 80-IA sub-section (4A) conditions (Indian-registered company/consortium, government agreement, mandatory transfer) are fact-specific enough to anchor hard scenario questions.

questions.q: Meridian Infracon Pvt Ltd, a Karnataka-incorporated company (GSTIN 29AABCM1234F1Z5), entered a 25-year BOT concession agreement with NHAI in FY 2024-25 to develop and operate a national highway stretch in Rajasthan. The company is also a JV partner in a consortium that separately holds the EPC sub-contract for the same highway. Meridian's CFO wants to know: (a) whether Meridian satisfies the ownership and transfer conditions under the 1995 Finance Act amendments for infrastructure deduction eligibility, given that the consortium — not Meridian alone — is registered as the concessionaire; (b) how the GST liability on toll collections and on EPC sub-contract receipts should be bifurcated between Meridian (service provider) and the consortium entity, especially where the NHAI agreement designates the consortium as the taxable person; and (c) whether the mandatory transfer clause in the concession agreement triggers a 'supply' event under CGST for the purpose of determining time-of-supply and valuation when the highway is handed back to NHAI at the end of the concession period.
questions.why_this_chunk: Sub-section (4A) inserted by this chunk sets the exact three conditions — Indian-registered company or consortium ownership, government agreement with mandatory transfer, and commencement of operations — that determine Income Tax eligibility; answering (a) requires applying those conditions to the split consortium/JV structure, and answering (c) requires cross-reading the mandatory-transfer condition with CGST Schedule II and valuation rules.

questions.q: Softura Solutions LLP, a Chennai-based STPI unit (GSTIN 33AABFS5678K1ZP) that exports customised ERP software to a US client, filed its Income Tax return for AY 1996-97 claiming deduction under section 80HHE without applying the erstwhile proviso that had restricted the deduction only to cases where the export turnover exceeded a specified threshold — relying on the Finance Act 1995 amendment that omitted that proviso effective 1-Apr-1996. The Assessing Officer proposes to disallow part of the deduction, arguing that income attributable to post-delivery support services billed in INR to the Indian liaison office of the same US client should be excluded from 'export turnover'. Simultaneously, the GST department is scrutinising whether those INR-denominated support service invoices raised to the Indian liaison office constitute a zero-rated supply (export of services under LUT) or a taxable domestic supply, given that the place of supply under IGST rules defaults to the location of the service recipient (the Indian liaison office). How should Softura's counsel argue both the Income Tax deduction quantum and the GST zero-rating treatment in a manner that is internally consistent — specifically, does characterising the support income as 'export receipts' for 80HHE purposes help or hurt the GST zero-rating argument, and vice versa?
questions.why_this_chunk: The omission of the proviso in section 80HHE (as enacted by this Finance Act 1995 chunk, effective 1-Apr-1996) is the operative Income Tax rule that determines deduction eligibility without a turnover floor; the question requires applying that specific amendment alongside IGST place-of-supply rules for intermediary/liaison-office scenarios, making this chunk an indispensable source for the IT limb of the analysis.

title: Seeks to make amendments (Amendment, 2024) to the CGST Rules, 2017.
text:
125 
or section 127 or section 129 or section 130, has been paid by the said person  through an intimation in 
FORM GST DRC-03 under sub-rule (2), instead of crediting the said amount in the electronic liability 
register in FORM GST PMT –01 against the debit entry created for the said demand,  the said person 
may file an application in FORM GST DRC-03A electronically on the common portal,  and the amount 
so paid and intimated through FORM GST DRC-03 shall be credited in Electronic Liability Register in 
FORM GST PMT –01 against the debit entry created for the said demand,  as if the said payment was 
made towards the said demand on the date of such intimation made through FORM GST DRC-03:

Provided that where an order in FORM GST DRC-05 has been issued in terms of sub-rule (3)

concluding the proceedings, in respect of the payment of an amount in FORM GST DRC-03, an 
application in FORM GST DRC-03A cannot be filed by the said person in respect of the said 
payment.‖.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the corrective mechanism (DRC-03A) for mis-routed demand payments under sections 73/74/76/79/125/127/129/130 — where a taxpayer paid via DRC-03 voluntary intimation instead of crediting the electronic liability register against a specific demand debit entry, and the proviso that forecloses DRC-03A once DRC-05 (conclusion order) has been issued.

questions.q: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZK, Maharashtra) received a demand order under section 74 for ₹38 lakhs (tax + interest + penalty) for FY 2021-22 on account of alleged ITC reversal. The CFO, mistakenly believing the company was making a voluntary disclosure, paid ₹38 lakhs through a DRC-03 intimation in March 2025 citing 'others' as the reason — rather than paying it against the specific debit entry raised in the electronic liability register. The demand adjudicating authority has not yet issued a DRC-05 concluding the proceedings. Now the company's GST consultant wants to get this payment properly mapped to the demand so the adjudication proceedings are closed. What is the correct procedural remedy available, what form must be filed on the common portal, and what is the legal effect on the date of payment for limitation/interest purposes once the credit is mapped?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly provides the DRC-03A remedy: the taxpayer may file DRC-03A electronically, the payment gets credited in PMT-01 against the debit entry 'as if' paid on the date of original DRC-03 intimation — this backdating rule is indispensable to the interest/limitation analysis. The proviso (DRC-05 not yet issued) is the key eligibility gate.

questions.q: Aravalli Infracon LLP (GSTIN 08AACFA5678B1ZP, Rajasthan) had proceedings initiated against it under both section 129 (detention/seizure of goods in transit) and section 130 (confiscation) for a consignment of steel coils intercepted in Jodhpur in December 2023. The LLP paid the amounts demanded under both proceedings via separate DRC-03 filings in January 2024, intending to close the matter, but the payments were not linked to the specific debit entries created in the electronic liability register. In April 2025, the adjudicating officer issued a FORM GST DRC-05 concluding the section 129 proceedings (acknowledging that payment) but proceedings under section 130 are still open. The LLP's advocate now wants to: (a) file DRC-03A to map the section 130 payment to the correct debit entry, and (b) also file DRC-03A for the section 129 payment. Advise on admissibility of each application separately, citing the specific bar that applies, and what recourse (if any) exists for the section 129 amount that cannot be re-mapped.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's proviso is the exact operative rule: once DRC-05 has been issued concluding proceedings in respect of a DRC-03 payment, DRC-03A cannot be filed for that payment — making section 129 DRC-03A inadmissible while section 130 DRC-03A remains open. This split-outcome analysis is only possible using this chunk's proviso language.

title: 100% EOUs etc. - CE Commissioners to handle cases where bothCus. & Excise Commissioners exit
text:
Circular No. 27/96-Cus.

dated 10/5/96

F.No. 305/4/95-FTT

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue, New Delhi

Subject :- Administrative control over the 100% Export Oriented Units (EOU) - Instructions regarding

I am directed to refer to the Ministry Circular No. 126/95 of even No. dated 12.12.95, on the above subject and to say that on
re-examination of the said instructions it has been decided by the Board that sub-para (b) of para 2 of the said circular will be
substituted as below :-

"(b)    The administrative work of 100% EOU/ EPZ/ EHTP/ STP etc. units in the port cities where there is overlapping
jurisdiction of Commissioners of Customs and Commissioners of Central Excise, will be handled by the Commissioners of
Central Excise".

Sd/-

(A.K. Raghunathan)

Senior Technical Officer (FTT)
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the administrative jurisdiction rule for 100% EOUs/EPZs/EHTPs/STPs in port cities where both Customs and Central Excise Commissioners have overlapping jurisdiction — specifically that Central Excise Commissioners handle such units. This supports HIGH-complexity queries involving EOU administrative control, jurisdictional disputes, and cross-act (Customs + Excise/GST) compliance obligations.

questions.q: Precision Circuits Pvt Ltd is a 100% EOU located in Chennai (a port city) with operations spanning both dutiable inputs imported under Customs and domestically procured capital goods. During a compliance audit in March 2025, the unit received simultaneous show-cause notices from the office of the Commissioner of Customs (Chennai Sea) and the Commissioner of GST & Central Excise (Chennai North) — both claiming jurisdiction over the unit's alleged duty-free import irregularities and Central Excise classification disputes. The MD is asking: which Commissioner's office has the primary administrative jurisdiction over Precision Circuits Pvt Ltd as a 100% EOU in Chennai, and how does the 1996 CBIC circular on overlapping jurisdictions settle this dispute? Does this jurisdictional allocation affect which authority can adjudicate the show-cause notice on customs duty demands as well?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly mandates that in port cities with overlapping Customs and Central Excise Commissioner jurisdictions, the Commissioner of Central Excise handles 100% EOU administrative work — directly resolving which SCN issuing authority has primacy.

questions.q: Nova Biopharma LLP operates as a 100% EOU in Nhava Sheva (Navi Mumbai), a port city, manufacturing pharmaceutical APIs for export. In April 2025, the LLP applied for a post-export IGST refund of ₹1.8 crore under GST law and simultaneously sought clearance of a fresh import consignment of raw materials under an Advance Authorization. Two separate offices — the Customs Commissioner (NS-II) and the GST & Central Excise Commissioner (Belapur) — are both claiming that refund processing and import clearance approval fall under their respective jurisdictions, creating a deadlock that has frozen the LLP's working capital. Which authority, under the applicable CBIC administrative instructions, should Nova Biopharma LLP approach as the single administrative window for both the IGST refund and the import clearance, and what is the legal basis for that determination in a port city with overlapping Commissioner jurisdictions?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's substituted sub-para (b) directly establishes that in port cities with overlapping Customs and Central Excise jurisdiction, the Central Excise Commissioner is the designated administrative authority for 100% EOUs — the indispensable rule needed to break the jurisdictional deadlock described.

title: Petroleum Shall Not Be Imported Into India By Sea/Land Except Through The Ports/Places Which Are Duly Approved For This Purpose By The Ministry Of Shipping, Government Of India, In Consultation With The Chief Controller And Declared As Cust
text:
receptacles or pipe has been rendered free from petroleum and any inflammable 
vapour: Provided that this condition shall not be deemed to prohibit the usual soldering operations 
connected with the filling and dispatching of receptacles containing petroleum Class B or 
petroleum Class C. 
17) All empty receptacles which have contained petroleum Class A shall except when they are 
opened for the purpose of cleaning them and rendering them free from petroleum vapour, be kept 
securely closed unless they have been thoroughly cleaned and freed from petroleum and 
inflammable vapours. 
18) a) Adequate precautions shall be taken at all times for the prevention of accident by fire or 
explosion. 
b) Wherever so specified by the Chief Controller, storage tanks shall be fitted with approved fire 
foam and/or water sprinkler attachments which shall be maintained in proper order at all times.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers safety and handling rules for petroleum storage/receptacles under the Petroleum Rules, specifically conditions for soldering, empty receptacle closure, and fire/explosion prevention requirements including foam/sprinkler fittings as specified by the Chief Controller. These operational safety obligations are specific enough to anchor HIGH-complexity compliance questions involving multi-party import/storage scenarios.

questions.q: Horizon Fuels Pvt Ltd, a licensed petroleum importer based in Kandla, Gujarat (IEC: 0819012345), has imported a consignment of Petroleum Class A in 200-litre steel drums via sea. After offloading, their depot contractor, Shree Logistics LLP, proposes to reuse 150 empty drums at the same bonded warehouse — some drums are being opened for internal cleaning but have not yet been certified vapour-free, while others remain sealed awaiting dispatch. The Chief Controller has also issued a site-specific order requiring foam fire suppression on all storage tanks at this facility. During a customs clearance inspection, the officer flags that (a) certain unsealed empty Class A drums have not been documented as undergoing the cleaning process, and (b) the foam attachment on Tank #3 is non-operational. Horizon Fuels' counsel argues these are 'operational' lapses not attracting customs detention of the remaining import consignment. Does the customs officer have grounds to link the safety non-compliance at the bonded warehouse to the clearance of the pending Bill of Entry, and what specific obligations under the petroleum handling rules are being violated by each of the two lapses?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 17 of the chunk directly governs the obligation to keep empty Class A petroleum receptacles securely closed unless opened specifically for cleaning/vapour-freeing; Clause 18(b) mandates that where the Chief Controller specifies, foam/sprinkler attachments must be maintained in proper order — both are the precise rules being violated in this scenario.

questions.q: Bharat Petrochem Traders, a proprietorship firm holding a petroleum storage licence in Vizag (Andhra Pradesh), imports Petroleum Class B in receptacles via the Visakhapatnam port. During port storage, their authorised handling agent carries out routine soldering to seal filled receptacles before dispatch to an upcountry buyer in Telangana. A port authority inspector raises an objection that soldering on Class B receptacles is impermissible because the area has not been certified as free from petroleum and inflammable vapour by a competent person. Bharat Petrochem's advocate contends that the proviso to the handling rules expressly permits 'usual soldering operations connected with the filling and dispatching of receptacles containing petroleum Class B,' and that no vapour-free certification is a precondition for this specific operation. Is the advocate's reading of the proviso correct, and does the general vapour-free pre-condition still apply to soldering on Class B filled receptacles — or is the proviso a full carve-out from that requirement?
questions.why_this_chunk: The specific proviso in this chunk states that the vapour-free condition 'shall not be deemed to prohibit the usual soldering operations connected with the filling and dispatching of receptacles containing petroleum Class B or Class C' — interpreting the scope and limits of this proviso is the core legal question, making this chunk indispensable.

title: Export of Rough Diamonds purchased from the Market Permitted
text:
Circular No. 64/ 97

dated 25/ 11/ 97

[F.No. 305/ 90/ 97- FTT]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue, New Delhi

Subject :- Export of rough Diamonds under Exim Policy, 1997-2002- Regarding.

A doubt had arisen as to whether rough diamonds purchased from the open market can be exported without any restriction. The issue was
examined in consultation with Ministry of Commerce and it was clarified by the Board that there should be no bar for export of rough diamonds
even if procured from domestic market provided the payment is received is freely convertible  currency.

2.    The above question had also been examined by the Directorate General of. Foreign Trade in consultation with the Ministry of Commerce.
The DGFT has now clarified the issue under their F.No. 1/ 9/ 44/ 97-98/ PC-II / 527 dated 18.6.97 (copy enclosed for ready reference).

3.    I am directed to inform you to follow the above said clarification for the export of rough diamonds.

Sd /-

(O.P. Khanduja)

Senior Technical Officer (FTT)

______________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Government of India, Ministry of Commerce

Directorate General of Foreign Trade

Udyog Bhawan, New Delhi- 110011

No. 1/9/44/ 97-98/ PC -II/ 527

Dated 18.6.1997

Subject : Clarification on export or rough diamonds

Dear Sh. V.S.A. Suri, Secretary, C & JEPC,

Kindly refer to your various letters on the subject. We have also received some other representations from individual exporters on the above
subject. In consultation with the Ministry of Commerce, it has been decided to clarify as follows :-

(1)    Third party exports of rough diamonds are allowed in terms of paragraph 8.2 of Exim Policy, 1997-2002. However, the export of rough
diamonds will be further governed by the provisions of para 8.9 of Handbook of Procedures (Vol.1).

(2)    Third party imports of rough diamonds are not allowed. Even where the licence holder wants to take the help of agent, then even this
agent has to work only as a facilitator and not as an importer as per para 5.7 of Handbook of Procedures (Vol.1).

(3)    As per para 8.9 of Handbook of Procedures (Vol.1) the licensing authority while issuing the REP licence shall make suitable
endorsement on the original bill of entry to avoid further use of the same bill of entry. The customs are not required to verify bill of entry
because export of rough diamonds will be either under para 8.9 of Handbook of Procedures in which case they will have to come to Joint
DGFT Office or under para 11.8 of Exim Policy in which case the operation will be from custom bonded warehouses.

(4)    The value of rough diamonds are allowed as per para 8.9 of Handbook of Procedures (Vol.1) and in case export in excess of limit
prescribed under para 8.9 of Handbook of Procedures (Vol. 1) is to be done then it will have to be done under para 11.8 of Exim Policy,
1997-2002. This is because the rough diamonds are appearing in the Negative List of Imports and since these rough diamonds are
imported and these have to be exported within the parameters of paragraph 8.9 of Handbook of Procedures (Vol. 1) read with paragraph
11.8 of Exim Policy 1997-2002.

(5)    Export can be on the basis of several bill of entries clubbed together however, the total value of export of rough diamonds should not
exceed 10% of the value of imports made against relevant bill of entry.

(6)    As regards export of rough diamonds the exporter shall furnish bill of entry in his own name for imports of rough diamonds effected on
or after 1.4.97
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the 1997 CBIC/DGFT clarification on export of rough diamonds purchased from the open market, including third-party export permissions, the 10% value cap on re-export relative to import bill of entry, the requirement for freely convertible currency payment, and the interplay between Exim Policy para 11.8 and Handbook of Procedures para 8.9. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving multi-entity diamond trade chains and cross-regulatory interpretation.

questions.q: Kiran Gems Pvt Ltd, a Surat-based diamond merchant with IEC 0816012345, purchased rough diamonds worth USD 2,00,000 from the domestic open market (not imported under a REP licence) and now wishes to export them to a buyer in Antwerp, Belgium, receiving payment in Euros. Their customs broker is advising that since the goods were not originally imported under any specific licence, the export requires a separate DGFT permission and cannot proceed directly. A second transaction involves their associate firm, Kiran International LLP, acting as the named exporter on the shipping bill while Kiran Gems is the actual seller — the broker is flagging this as a 'third party export'. (a) Is there any restriction on exporting rough diamonds sourced entirely from the Indian domestic market, and does receipt of payment in Euros (freely convertible currency) satisfy the condition? (b) Is the third-party export arrangement between Kiran Gems and Kiran International LLP permissible under the applicable policy, and what procedural paragraph governs it?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly clarifies that export of rough diamonds procured from the domestic market is permitted provided payment is in freely convertible currency (para 1 of the circular), and that third-party exports of rough diamonds are allowed under para 8.2 of Exim Policy 1997-2002, with para 8.9 of HBP Vol.1 as the governing procedural provision.

questions.q: Ratnadeep Exports Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, IEC 0310098765) holds a REP licence and has imported rough diamonds under three separate bills of entry: BoE-1 for USD 5,00,000, BoE-2 for USD 3,00,000, and BoE-3 for USD 8,00,000. They want to re-export rough diamonds worth USD 1,60,000 by clubbing all three bills of entry together on a single shipping bill. Their licencing officer is insisting that the export value against the clubbed BoEs cannot exceed USD 1,60,000 in aggregate and that anything above the 10% threshold per BoE must be routed through a customs bonded warehouse under a different policy provision. Separately, their agent Shree Diamond Brokers is listed as the facilitator but wants to be shown as the importer on record for BoE-3 to claim drawback. (a) What is the correct maximum permissible export value when clubbing these three BoEs, and which provision governs exports that exceed the per-BoE limit? (b) Can Shree Diamond Brokers be shown as the importer on BoE-3, and what is the precise restriction on the role an agent may play in such transactions?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause (5) establishes the 10% cap on re-export value relative to each relevant BoE and permits clubbing of multiple BoEs; clause (4) specifies that exports exceeding para 8.9 HBP limits must be routed under para 11.8 of Exim Policy via customs bonded warehouses; clause (2) explicitly prohibits third-party imports and restricts agents to a facilitator role only.

title: Regarding procedure for electronic filing of Central Excise and Service Tax returns and for electronic payment of excise duty and service tax.
text:
nks (currently there are 28

banks, list of which is available on the EASIEST (Electronic Accounting System in Excise and service Tax) website of
CBEC, maintained by NSDL ( https://cbec.nsdl.com/EST/ ). Payment through ICICI Bank, HDFC Bank and Axis Bank
can be done by assesses for select Commissionerates only, list of which is published in the aforesaid EASIEST website.
Payment through all other authorized banks can be made for all Commissionerates.

1. For effecting payment, assessees can access the ACES website ( http://www.aces.gov.in/ ) and click on the e-payment

link that will take them to the EASIEST portal ( https://cbec.nsdl.com/EST/ ) or they can directly visit the EASIEST portal.

1. Procedure for e-Payment:

1. To pay Excise Duty and Service Tax online, the assessee has to enter the 15 digit Assessee Code allotted by the

department under erstwhile SACER/SAPS or the current application ACES.

2. There will be an online check on the validity of the Assessee Code entered.
3. If the Assessee code is valid, then corresponding assessee details like name, address, Commissionerate Code

etc. as present in the Assessee Code Master will be displayed.

4. Based on the Assessee Code, the duty / tax i.e. Central Excise duty or Service Tax to be paid will be automatically

selected.

5. The assessee is required to select the type of duty / tax to be paid by clicking on Select Accounting Codes for

Excise or Select Accounting Codes for Service Tax, depending on the type of duty / tax to be paid.

6. At a time the assessee can select up to six Accounting Codes.
7. The assessee should also select the bank through which payment is to be made.
8. On submission of data entered, a confirmation screen will be displayed. If the taxpayer confirms the data entered in

the screen, it will be directed to the net-banking site of the bank selected.

9. The taxpayer will login to the net-banking site with the user id/ password, provided by the bank for net-banking

purpose, and will enter payment details at the bank site.

10. On successful payment, a challan counterfoil will be displayed containing CIN , payment details and bank name

through which e-payment has been made. This counterfoil is proof of payment made.

h. Responsibility of the Assessee

1. It is the legal responsibility of the assessees, who are required to file returns, to file it within the due date as prescribed

under law.  The electronic filing of returns is mandatory for select class of Central Excise and Service Tax assessees, as
mentioned in Notification No 04/2010-Central Excise (N.T.) dated the 19 th February, 2010, and Notification No. 01/2010
- Service Tax dated the 19 th February, 2010 respectively. Other assessees can also use ACES and file their returns
electronically. It may, however, be noted that merely uploading the returns will not be considered as returns having been
filed with the department. A return will be considered as filed, when the same is successfully accepted by the application
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the EASIEST e-payment procedure for Central Excise and Service Tax — specifically the 15-digit Assessee Code validation, the 6-accounting-code limit per transaction, bank eligibility restrictions by Commissionerate, and the critical legal distinction that uploading a return ≠ filed return. These procedural specifics can anchor HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-obligation payment scenarios and legal liability disputes.

questions.q: Nakshatra Polymers Pvt Ltd, a Central Excise assessee registered under the Thane-II Commissionerate (Maharashtra), attempted to discharge its March 2010 duty liability of ₹18,42,000 across seven accounting heads — covering Basic Excise Duty, Education Cess, SHE Cess, NCCD, AED (GSI), AED (TTA), and a compounded levy — in a single e-payment session through the EASIEST portal using their ICICI Bank net-banking account. The payment portal accepted the Assessee Code, populated their details correctly, but the session terminated after six accounting codes were selected. The company's accountant, believing the session was a technical glitch, manually paid the seventh head via a paper challan at the bank counter the next day — one day after the due date. The department has now raised a show-cause notice demanding interest under Rule 8(3) of the Central Excise Rules, 2002 for late payment of the seventh head, and separately questioning whether ICICI Bank was an authorized channel for the Thane-II Commissionerate. As their advocate, advise on: (a) whether the six-code restriction is a systemic constraint or an assessee error, (b) the validity of ICICI Bank for this Commissionerate, and (c) whether a defense exists against the interest demand given the procedural limitation of the EASIEST portal.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states the 6-accounting-code per session limit, the Commissionerate-specific restriction on ICICI Bank (only for 'select Commissionerates' listed on EASIEST), and the e-payment workflow — all three are indispensable to advising on liability and the department's SCN.

questions.q: Cauvery Fragrance Works, a proprietorship manufacturing perfumery compounds (Chapter 33, CETA) in Mysore under the Central Excise Commissionerate Mysore, uploaded its ER-1 return for Q4 FY 2009-10 on the ACES portal on 10-Apr-2010 — within the due date — but due to a server timeout the return remained in 'submitted' status and was never accepted by the application. The proprietor printed the acknowledgment screen as proof. Three months later, a scrutiny notice treats the return as non-filed and proposes a penalty under Rule 27 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002. The proprietor argues: (i) the electronic upload constitutes filing, and (ii) Notification No. 04/2010-CE (N.T.) dated 19-Feb-2010 mandates electronic filing but does not specify 'successful acceptance' as a condition precedent. Separately, Cauvery had made the corresponding duty payment via EASIEST through Axis Bank; assess whether Axis Bank was an authorized channel for the Mysore Commissionerate and what the payment record (CIN on the counterfoil) establishes as proof of payment independent of the return-filing dispute.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's explicit statement that 'merely uploading the returns will not be considered as returns having been filed' and that a return is 'filed' only when 'successfully accepted by the application' directly resolves the core legal dispute; the Axis Bank Commissionerate restriction and the CIN-as-proof-of-payment rule are also chunk-specific facts.

title: Order for rejection / allowance of compounding of offence
text:

Proof of Place of Business of representative in India, if any:   
(a) For own premises –  
Any document in support of the ownership of the premises like Latest Property Tax 
Receipt or Municipal Khata copy or copy of Electricity Bill.    
(b) For Rented or Leased premises –  
A copy of the valid Rent / Lease Agreement with any document in support of the 
ownership of the premises of the Lessor like Latest Property Tax Receipt or Municipal

solemnly affirm and declare that <<name of the authorised signatory>> to act as an 
authorised signatory for the business << Name of the Business>> for which application

All his actions in relation to this business will be binding on me/ us.

(Name)
