claude_reasoning: This chunk is a narrow technical amendment to a customs exemption notification, substituting a materials description ('UV resin-142/Ribbon matrix' → 'UV resin 1 & 2/Ribbon matrix') in List 5, item 10 of Notification 21/2002-Customs. It can anchor HIGH-complexity questions involving classification disputes and exemption eligibility for UV resin variants used in manufacturing.

questions.q: Prism Optics Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer in Pune (Maharashtra), imports 'UV resin Type 2' from a South Korean supplier under a bill of entry filed on 20-April-2025. Their clearing agent claims exemption under the concessional duty scheme applicable to List 5 inputs under Notification 21/2002-Customs. The customs officer at JNPT raises an objection that the description on the invoice ('UV resin Type 2') does not match the entry as it stood prior to June 2006, and questions whether the 2006 amendment is applicable retrospectively to pending re-assessments for imports made in March 2006. Meanwhile, the importer's CFO wants to know: (a) what exactly changed in the item description under List 5 item (10), (b) whether 'UV resin Type 2' falls within the amended or unamended entry, and (c) if the amendment applies only prospectively from 15-June-2006, how should they handle a prior import where 'UV resin-142' was declared but 'UV resin 2' was actually supplied? Advise.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the sole source specifying the precise substitution from 'UV resin-142/Ribbon matrix' to 'UV resin 1 & 2/Ribbon matrix' in List 5 item (10), and its effective date of 15-June-2006 — both are indispensable to resolving the classification and retrospectivity dispute.

questions.q: Luminos Displays LLP, an EOU unit in Noida (UP) with GSTIN 09AABCL1234F1ZK, imports UV resin and ribbon matrix materials for manufacturing display modules that are partly exported and partly cleared into the DTA. For the DTA-cleared portion on 1-July-2025, customs duty is being assessed as if the full exemption under Notification 21/2002-Customs (List 5, item 10) applies. A show-cause notice is issued alleging that the specific grade imported — described in the import contract as 'UV resin Grade-142' — does not match the current amended description in List 5 item (10) post the June 2006 amendment, and therefore full Basic Customs Duty + IGST applies. The company argues the 'Grade-142' is a subset of the broader 'UV resin 1 & 2' category post-amendment. Simultaneously, for the exported portion, they claim the IGST exemption on imports by EOU is linked to the same List 5 eligibility. Advise Luminos on: (i) the correct reading of List 5 item (10) post-amendment, (ii) whether 'UV resin Grade-142' satisfies the amended description, and (iii) the cascading impact on their IGST liability for DTA clearances if the exemption is denied.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk provides the verbatim pre- and post-amendment text of List 5 item (10), which is the crux of whether 'UV resin Grade-142' qualifies — without this chunk, the exact description change cannot be determined and neither the customs exemption nor the cascading IGST analysis can be resolved.

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the DFRC (Duty Free Replenishment Certificate) scheme under Exim Policy 2002-07, specifically: (1) the removal of quality/specification ascertainment for most items except those listed in para 4.31 of HBP Vol.1, (2) the continued SION-based quantity permitting, and (3) the free transferability of DFRC licenses. These procedural specifics are indispensable to answering practitioner queries about DFRC license utilization and transfer disputes.

questions.q: Sunrise Exports Pvt Ltd, a merchant exporter in Surat (GSTIN 24AABCS1234F1ZP), exported blended yarn to a buyer in Germany on 15-March-2003 under a shipping bill declaring specific technical specifications. They subsequently obtained a DFRC license and transferred it to Bharat Textiles LLP in Ahmedabad. Bharat Textiles is now importing raw cotton under that license, but the Customs officer at Mundra port is insisting on re-verifying the quality and technical specifications of the imported inputs against the original export product's declared specs on the shipping bill. Sunrise argues this verification requirement was abolished under the 2002-07 Exim Policy changes. Who is correct, and does the answer change if the imported input falls under any specifically notified category?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses this dispute: quality/specification ascertainment was removed for most items BUT continues to apply for items listed in para 4.31 of HBP Vol.1 as amended by DGFT Notice No. 4/2002-07 dated 1.4.2002. The officer's insistence is valid only if the input falls under that notified list — this specific carve-out is exclusively in this chunk.

questions.q: Metro Handicrafts Pvt Ltd in Jaipur obtained a DFRC license post-export of decorative brass items to a UAE buyer in FY 2002-03, with the license covering import of brass scrap as input per SION norms. They later sold this DFRC license to Rajputana Metals Pvt Ltd in Jodhpur at a premium. Rajputana is now attempting to import a quantity of brass scrap 15% in excess of what SION norms permit for the relevant export product, claiming the license is freely transferable and the quantity restriction no longer applies. A Customs officer at ICD Jodhpur is disputing this, arguing that SION-based quantity limits remain binding regardless of transfer. Advise on the validity of both the transfer and the quantity restriction, citing the applicable policy framework post the 2002-07 Exim Policy changes.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states two facts critical to this answer: (1) DFRC licenses 'are freely transferable as earlier' — validating the transfer to Rajputana; and (2) 'Quantity of each input shall be permitted in the DFRC license in terms of SION for the relevant export product as earlier' — confirming the officer is correct that SION-based quantity caps remain binding even post-transfer.

15A fails to do so within the period specified in the notice issued under sub-section
(3) thereof, the prescribed authority may direct that such person shall pay, by way of
penalty, a sum of one hundred rupees for each day of the period during which the
failure to furnish such return continues.”.

Amendment of
section 31.

98.   In the Central Excise Act, in section 31, in clause (g), for the words “Customs and
Central Excise Settlement Commission”, the words “Customs, Central Excise and Service
Tax Settlement Commission” shall be substituted.

Amendment of
section 32.

99.  In the Central Excise Act, in section 32, in sub-section (1), for the words “the
Customs and Central Excise Settlement Commission”, the words “the Customs, Central
Excise and Service Tax Settlement Commission” shall be substituted.

100.    In the Central Excise Act, in section 32E,—

Amendment of
section 32E.

(i) in sub-section (1),—

(a)  in the first proviso, in clause (d), for the word, figures and letters
“section 11AB”, the word, figures and letters “section 11AA” shall be
substituted;

(b) in the second proviso, for the words “Provided further that”, the
following shall be substituted, namely:—

“Provided further that the Settlement Commission, if it is satisfied
that the circumstances exist for not filing the returns referred to in clause
(a) of the first proviso to sub-section (1), may after recording the reasons
therefor, allow the applicant to make such application:

Provided also that”;

(ii) sub-section (2) shall be omitted.

Amendment of
section 32-O.

101. In the Central Excise Act, in section 32-O, in sub-section (1), in clause (i), the
following Explanation shall be inserted, namely:—

“Explanation.— In this clause, the concealment of particulars of duty liability
relates to any such concealment made from the Central Excise Officer.”.

102.  In the Central Excise Act, in section 35B,—

Amendment
of section
35B.

(a)  in sub-section (1), in the second proviso, for the words “fifty thousand
rupees”, the words “two lakh rupees” shall be substituted;

(b) in sub-section (1B), in clause (i), for the words “by notification in the
Official Gazette”, the words “by order” shall be substituted;

(c) in sub-section (7), in clause (a), the words “for grant of stay or” shall be
omitted.

103. In the Central Excise Act, in section 35C, in sub-section (2A), the first, second
and third proviso shall be omitted.

Amendment
of section
35C.

104. In the Central Excise Act, in section 35E, in sub-section (3), the following proviso
shall be inserted, namely:—

Amendment
of section
35E.

“Provided that the Board may, on sufficient cause being shown, extend the said
period by another thirty days.”.

105.  In the Central Excise Act, for section 35F, the following section shall be
substituted, namely:—

Substitution of
new section for
section 35F.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers two substantive Finance Act 2014 amendments to the Central Excise Act: (a) the renaming of the Settlement Commission to include 'Service Tax' (sections 98–99), expanding its jurisdiction, and (b) the substitution of section 35B raising the monetary limit for departmental appeals from Rs. 50,000 to Rs. 2 lakh. Both changes have direct practitioner impact on pending disputes and settlement eligibility, supporting HIGH-complexity scenario queries.

questions.q: Radiant Coatings Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer of industrial paints in Pune (Maharashtra), received a show-cause notice in March 2014 for alleged short-payment of service tax of Rs. 38 lakhs on job-work services rendered to automotive OEMs, along with a Central Excise duty demand of Rs. 12 lakhs on certain captively-consumed inputs — total demand Rs. 50 lakhs. Their advocate advised in early 2014 that a settlement application before the then 'Customs and Central Excise Settlement Commission' was not available because service tax proceedings could not be clubbed with excise matters before that body. After the Finance Act 2014 received assent, can Radiant Coatings now file a consolidated settlement application covering both the service tax and excise components before the reconstituted commission, and does the amendment operate retrospectively so as to cover a show-cause notice already issued before the Act's commencement?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sections 98 and 99 of this chunk substitute 'Customs and Central Excise Settlement Commission' with 'Customs, Central Excise and Service Tax Settlement Commission' in sections 31 and 32 of the Central Excise Act — the precise textual change that determines whether service tax matters could be brought before the commission post-amendment, and whether a pre-existing SCN qualifies.

questions.q: Sunrise Ferro Alloys LLP, a Central Excise assessee in Raipur (Chhattisgarh), had its classification dispute adjudicated against it in January 2014 with a confirmed duty demand of Rs. 1.4 lakhs. The department's jurisdictional Commissioner reviewed the order and decided to file an appeal before CESTAT; under the law then in force the monetary threshold for such departmental appeals was Rs. 50,000, so the matter was clearly appealable. By the time the appeal paperwork was being filed in June 2014 — after the Finance Act 2014 had come into effect raising the limit to Rs. 2 lakhs — the jurisdictional officer contends the appeal is now barred since Rs. 1.4 lakhs falls below the new threshold. Sunrise's counsel counters that the right to appeal accrued to the department under the old law and the amendment cannot extinguish it. Who is correct, and how does the amended proviso in section 35B interact with any pending or saved proceedings under the transitional savings clause?
questions.why_this_chunk: Section 102(a) of this chunk substitutes 'fifty thousand rupees' with 'two lakh rupees' in the second proviso to section 35B(1) of the Central Excise Act — the exact provision governing the monetary ceiling for departmental appeals to CESTAT, which is the fulcrum of the entire dispute about whether the department's appeal is maintainable.

title: Disputes between CBEC and PSUs - Clearance by the Committee on Disputes- Implementation of decision
text:
Circular No. 156/67/94-CX.

dated 17/11/95

F.No. 390/P/258/95-JC

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue, New Delhi

Subject: Disputes between CBEC and PSUs-Clearance by the Committee on Disputes- Implementation of decisions -
Regarding.

As you are aware, in pursuance of the Supreme Courts" directive for reducing litigation between the Deptt. of Revenue and
PSUs, ect, litigation"s before CEGAT and Courts can be pursued only with the prior clearance of the High Powered Committee
on Disputes constituted in the Cabinet Sectt. Those cases which are not considered fit for further litigation by the Committee
cannot be pursued by the concerned appellants viz. PSUs or the Deptt. or Revenue, as the case may be, before CEGAT or the
Courts.

2.    The Committee on Disputes has been holding regular meetings, and issuing minutes in regard to the decisions in the
cases taken up the Committee. The gist of these Minutes is regularly being communicated to the concerned Commissioners
for necessary action. However, there has been no feed back from the Commissioners in regard to the action taken by them.

3.    It is presumed that the concerned Commissioners have taken necessary action i pursuance of the Minutes of the COD
meetings communicated to them. In those cases of Department"s appeals where permission was refused by the COD,
necessary application has to be made before the CEGAT/Court for treating such cases as withdrawn. In other cases, we have
to communicate the Minutes of the COD Meetings and request CEGAT/ Courts to expedite proceedings.

4.    Further, in those cases of PSU-assessees" appeals wherein permission for further litigation has been refused by the COD,
it has to be seen that CEGAT or the concerned Court is apprised of the position so that their appeals are withdrawn leading to
the recovery of pending dues, if any.

5.    It is desired by the Board that compliance report on the action taken in pursuance of the Minutes of the COD meetings,
communicated so far for the past cases, may please be sent in the enclosed*  proforma. This should be attended to within 30
days of the receipt of this Circular.

6.    For future cases, on receipt of the Minutes of the COD Meetings, Compliance should be reported in the same proforma
within a reasonable period of 30 day or so.

Sd/-

(Meenu S. Kumar)

Under Secretary to the Govt. of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the procedural mechanism for implementing Committee on Disputes (COD) decisions in Central Excise litigation between CBEC and PSUs — specifically the withdrawal of appeals when COD refuses permission and the compliance reporting obligation. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving PSU litigation strategy, departmental appeal withdrawal, and recovery of dues in a multi-party (PSU + Department + CEGAT/Court) procedural context.

questions.q: Bharat Heavy Electricals Ltd (BHEL), Bhopal unit, is an appellant before CESTAT in a Central Excise dispute involving classification of capital goods worth ₹4.2 crore for assessment years 1991–93. The Committee on Disputes has now issued minutes refusing permission for BHEL to pursue further litigation. BHEL's counsel argues that since CESTAT proceedings are already at the final hearing stage and BHEL has filed its synopsis, the COD refusal cannot be given effect retroactively and the appeal should be allowed to proceed to judgment. The departmental officer, on the other hand, insists that CESTAT must be informed immediately and the appeal treated as withdrawn, with recovery proceedings initiated for pending dues. Which position is correct under the applicable circular framework, and what specific procedural steps must the Commissioner take within what timeframe to implement the COD decision?
questions.why_this_chunk: Paragraph 4 of the circular expressly states that where a PSU-assessee's appeal has been refused permission by the COD, CESTAT or the concerned Court must be apprised so that the appeal is withdrawn leading to recovery of pending dues — directly resolving the dispute between BHEL's counsel and the departmental officer, and the 30-day compliance window in paragraph 6 fixes the timeframe.

questions.q: The Commissioner of Central Excise, Kolkata-II, received COD meeting minutes six weeks ago covering three separate matters: (a) a Department appeal against Steel Authority of India Ltd (SAIL) pending before CESTAT, Delhi, where COD refused permission to the Department to pursue the appeal; (b) a SAIL appeal against an order-in-original where COD refused SAIL permission to litigate further; and (c) a GAIL (India) Ltd matter where COD directed expedited hearing. No compliance report has yet been sent to the Board. SAIL's advocate has now filed an application before CESTAT arguing that since the Department has not formally withdrawn its appeal in matter (a) within a reasonable time, CESTAT should proceed ex-parte against the Department. Simultaneously, the recovery section is asking whether they can initiate recovery of ₹1.8 crore in matter (b) before CESTAT formally records SAIL's withdrawal. Advise the Commissioner on (i) the precise procedural obligation and deadline for each of the three matters, (ii) whether SAIL's ex-parte argument has merit, and (iii) whether recovery can commence in matter (b) prior to formal CESTAT withdrawal.
questions.why_this_chunk: Paragraphs 3, 4, and 5 of the circular are each independently indispensable: para 3 governs the Department's obligation to apply for withdrawal in matter (a); para 4 governs apprising CESTAT in matter (b) to enable withdrawal and trigger recovery; para 5 mandates a compliance report within 30 days establishing the timeline breach — all three sub-questions map directly to distinct provisions of this circular.

title: Appoints Comminsioner Of Customs, Kandla
text:
[To be published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (ii)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Notification

No.70 / 2010-Customs (N.T.)

New Delhi, dated the  12 th August, 2010.

S.O.          (E). - In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 4 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the
Central Board of Excise and Customs hereby appoints the Commissioner of Customs, Kandla to exercise the powers and
discharge the duties conferred or imposed on-

(i)         the Commissioner of Customs (Import), New Customs House,Mumbai; and

(ii)        the Commissioner of Customs (Export), New Customs House,Mumbai,

for the purposes of adjudicating the matters relating to Show Cause Notice issued to M/s. Frost International Limited, 402,
Kalpana Plaza, 24/147B, Birhana Road, Kanpur and others, vide F.No. DRI/GRU/INV-01/09-10.Pt.II dated 25 th May, 2010 by
the Additional Director General, Directorate of Revenue Intelligence, Ahmedabad Zonal Unit, Ahmedabad.

[F.No. 437/35/2010-Cus.IV]

(Navraj Goyal)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the Central Board's power under Section 4(1) of the Customs Act to vest adjudicating jurisdiction in a Commissioner outside the originating customs zone, and the specific vesting of Mumbai Import/Export Commissioner powers in Kandla Commissioner for a DRI-initiated SCN — directly relevant to jurisdictional validity challenges and cross-zone adjudication disputes.

questions.q: M/s. Frost International Limited, a Kanpur-based trading company, received a Show Cause Notice in May 2010 from the DRI's Ahmedabad Zonal Unit alleging mis-declaration of imported goods worth approximately ₹18 crore. The adjudication order was subsequently passed by the Commissioner of Customs, Kandla — not by the Commissioner of Customs (Import), Mumbai, before whom the goods were originally assessed. Frost's advocate is now challenging the order in CESTAT Mumbai, arguing that Kandla had no territorial or subject-matter jurisdiction over Mumbai port import transactions, and that the adjudication by Kandla Commissioner is void ab initio. The department counters that the CBEC formally vested Mumbai Import and Export powers in the Kandla Commissioner specifically for this SCN. (a) What is the precise legal mechanism under the Customs Act that enables CBEC to confer such cross-zone adjudicating powers on a Commissioner, and does such conferral require Frost's consent or prior notice? (b) If the CESTAT finds that the notification vesting powers was issued after the SCN date but before the adjudication order, does that affect the validity of the adjudication? (c) Can 'others' named in the same SCN — say, a Mumbai-based CHA and a Gujarat-based freight forwarder — independently challenge jurisdiction on the ground that Kandla Commissioner's vested powers were granted only for the Frost SCN reference number?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the direct source of the jurisdictional vesting — it identifies the exact SCN reference (F.No. DRI/GRU/INV-01/09-10.Pt.II dated 25th May 2010), names M/s. Frost International Limited specifically, and grants Kandla Commissioner the powers of both Mumbai Import and Export Commissioners under Section 4(1). No other chunk can answer whether the vesting was SCN-specific or whether it covers 'others' in the same notice.

questions.q: Sunrise Exports LLP, a Gujarat-based SEZ unit, was co-noticee along with M/s. Frost International Limited in a DRI investigation initiated out of Ahmedabad. The consolidated Show Cause Notice covered alleged over-valuation of imported capital goods at Mumbai port by Frost and alleged re-export violations by Sunrise from Kandla SEZ. The adjudication was assigned to the Commissioner of Customs, Kandla by CBEC. Sunrise's counsel argues that: (i) the Commissioner of Customs, Kandla already exercises independent jurisdiction over Kandla SEZ transactions under the SEZ Act read with Customs Act, and therefore the CBEC notification vesting additional Mumbai Import/Export powers creates a conflict — specifically, can the same officer adjudicate Sunrise's SEZ violation (under his original Kandla jurisdiction) and simultaneously adjudicate Frost's Mumbai import mis-declaration (under the vested Mumbai powers) in a single order without bifurcating the proceedings? (ii) If the adjudication order mixes findings under the vested Mumbai powers for Frost with findings under the original Kandla jurisdiction for Sunrise, which appellate forum — CESTAT Mumbai or CESTAT Ahmedabad — has territorial jurisdiction to hear Sunrise's appeal? Advise on the jurisdictional and forum-selection issues, citing the operative legal authority that enabled the Kandla Commissioner to exercise Mumbai's powers in the first place.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is indispensable because it identifies Kandla as the appointed adjudicating authority vested with Mumbai Import and Export Commissioner powers for a specific SCN, enabling analysis of whether a single officer can exercise dual-source jurisdiction (original Kandla + vested Mumbai) over different co-noticees in one proceeding — a question that cannot be answered without knowing the precise scope and source of the vested authority established in this notification.

title: Order for recovery through specified officer under section 79
text:
Instructions:-

1. Terms used:

a. GSTIN:  Goods and Services Tax Identification Number 
b. UIN:     Unique Identity Number 
c. UQC:     Unit Quantity Code 
d. HSN:    Harmonized System of Nomenclature 
e. POS:     Place of Supply (Respective State) 
f. B to B:   From one registered person to another registered person 
g. B to C:   From registered person to unregistered person

2. GSTR-5 is applicable to non-resident taxable person and it is a monthly return. 
3. The details in GSTR-5 should be furnished by 20thof the month succeeding the

relevant tax period or within 7 days from the last date of the registration whichever is 
earlier.

4. Table 3 consists of details of import of goods, bill of entry wise and taxpayer has to

specify the amount of ITC eligible on such import of goods.

5. Recipient to provide for Bill of Entry information including six digits port

code and seven digits bill of entry number.

6. Table 4 consists of amendment of import of goods which are declared in the returns

of earlier tax period.

7. [Invoice-level information, rate-wise, pertaining to the tax period should be reported

as under: 
(i.) 
for all B to B supplies (whether inter-State or intra-State), invoice level

details should be uploaded in Table 5; 
(ii.) 
for all inter-state B to C supplies, where invoice value is more than Rs.

2,50,000/- (B to C Large) invoice level detail to be provided in Table 6; and 
(iii.) 
for all B to C supplies, other than those reported in table 6, shall be reported

in Table 7 providing State-wise summary of such supplies.]43

8. 
Table 8 consists of amendments in respect of -

i. 
B2B outward supplies declared in the previous tax period;

ii. 
―B2C inter-State invoices where invoice value is more than [rupees]442.5 
lakhs‖ reported in the previous tax period; and

iii. 
Original Debit and credit note details and its amendments.

9. 
Table 9 covers the Amendments in respect of B2C outward supplies other than

inter-State supplies where invoice value is more than Rs 250000/-.

43Substitued vide Notf No. 79/2020-CT dated 15.10.2020 for ―7.Invoice-level information, rate-wise, 
pertaining to the tax period separately for goods and services should be reported as under:  
i.For all B to B supplies (whether inter-State or intra-State), invoice level details should be uploaded in Table 
5; 
ii.For all inter-state B to C supplies, where invoice value is more than Rs. 2,50,000/- (B to C Large) invoice 
level detail to be provided in Table 6; and 
iii.For all B to C supplies (whether inter-State or intra-State) where invoice value is up to Rs. 2,50,000/- State-
wise summary of supplies shall be filed in Table 7.‖. 
44 Inserted vide Notf No. 79/2020-CT dated 15.10.2020.

158
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers GSTR-5 filing rules for non-resident taxable persons (NRTPs), including filing deadlines, import-of-goods ITC reporting (Table 3/4), and the B2B/B2C invoice classification thresholds (Rs. 2.5 lakh cutoff, inter-state vs intra-state, Table 5/6/7 allocation) as amended by Notf 79/2020-CT. These rules create high-complexity scenarios when an NRTP makes mixed supplies, imports goods mid-period, or straddles the B2C Large threshold across multiple states.

questions.q: Nexus Exhibitions GmbH, a German company with a temporary GSTIN (non-resident registration) obtained for an electronics trade show held in Mumbai from 10-Mar-2025 to 18-Mar-2025, made the following supplies during the period: (a) sold display equipment worth Rs. 3,80,000 to Infosys Ltd (registered in Karnataka, GSTIN 29AAACI1681G1ZR) under a B2B invoice; (b) sold merchandise worth Rs. 2,70,000 to an unregistered walk-in visitor from Rajasthan; and (c) sold brochure kits worth Rs. 1,10,000 to another unregistered buyer from Maharashtra. Additionally, Nexus imported spare parts via a Bill of Entry at JNPT (port code 0500, BE No. 4521873) on 12-Mar-2025 and wants to claim ITC on those imports. The registration was valid only until 20-Mar-2025. By what date must Nexus file GSTR-5, in which tables must each of the three supply transactions be reported, and how should the Bill of Entry details be disclosed to correctly claim import ITC — and what happens to filing obligations if the return is not filed before registration lapses?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies (i) the GSTR-5 deadline as 20th of succeeding month OR 7 days from last date of registration, whichever is earlier — directly resolving the lapse scenario; (ii) Table 5 for B2B supply; (iii) Table 6 for inter-state B2C > Rs. 2.5 lakh (Rajasthan buyer); (iv) Table 7 for B2C ≤ Rs. 2.5 lakh (Maharashtra buyer); and (v) Table 3 with six-digit port code and seven-digit BE number for import ITC claim.

questions.q: Meridian Events Co. LLC, a US-based entity registered as a non-resident taxable person in India with a temporary GSTIN for a consulting workshop conducted across Delhi and Bengaluru in February 2025, has the following reporting dilemma: it issued four invoices — (1) Rs. 5,00,000 to Tata Consultancy Services Ltd (registered, Delhi, B2B), (2) Rs. 2,60,000 to an unregistered participant from Haryana (inter-state B2C), (3) Rs. 2,40,000 to an unregistered participant from Tamil Nadu (inter-state B2C), and (4) Rs. 1,80,000 to an unregistered participant who physically attended in Delhi (intra-state B2C). In the original GSTR-5 for February 2025 (filed in March 2025), invoice (2) was accidentally reported in Table 7 instead of Table 6. The company now wants to correct this in the March 2025 return period. Which tables must each invoice appear in for the February period, which table and amendment mechanism corrects the mis-reported invoice (2) in the subsequent return, and does the Rs. 2,50,000 threshold apply to the invoice value inclusive or exclusive of GST for determining B2C Large classification?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk defines the precise Rs. 2,50,000 threshold separating Table 6 (B2C Large inter-state) from Table 7 (other B2C), and Table 8 as the amendment table for 'B2C inter-State invoices where invoice value is more than Rs. 2.5 lakhs reported in the previous tax period' — making Table 8 the indispensable correction mechanism for invoice (2), while also creating a legal interpretation question on the threshold's tax-inclusive/exclusive application.

title: Central Excise (Appeals) Rules, 2001
text:
Central Excise (Appeals) Rules, 2001

[ Notification No. 32/2001-C.E. (N.T.) , dated 21.06.2001]

In exercise of the powers conferred by section 37 of the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944), the Central Government hereby
makes the following Rules, namely:-

Rule 1. Short title, extent and commencement.-

(1) These Rules may be called the Central Excise (Appeals) Rules, 2001.

(2) They extent to the whole of India.

(3) They shall come into force on and from the 1st day of July, 2001.

Rule 2. Definitions.-

In these Rules, unless the context otherwise requires, -

(a) "Act" means the Central Excise Act, 1944 (1 of 1944);

(b) "Form" means a form appended to these Rules;

(c) words and expressions used in these Rules and not defined but defined in the Act shall have the meanings respectively
assigned to them in the Act.

Rule 3. Form of appeal to Commissioner (Appeals ).-

(1) An appeal under sub-section (1) of section 35 of the Act to the Commissioner (Appeals) shall be made in Form No.E.A.-1 .

(2) The grounds of appeal and the form of verification as contained in Form No. E.A.-1 . shall be signed, -

(a) in the case of an individual, by the individual himself or where the individual is absent from India, by the individual concerned
or by any person duly authorized by him in this behalf; and where the individual is a minor or is mentally incapacitated from
attending to his affairs, by his guardian or by any other person competent to act on his behalf;

(b) in the case of a Hindu undivided family, by the Karta and, where the Karta is absent from India or is mentally incapacitated
from attending to his affairs, by any other adult member of such family;

(c) in the case of a company or local authority, by the principal officer thereof;

(d) in the case of a firm, by any partner thereof, not being a minor;

(e) in the case of any other association, by any member of the association or the principal officer thereof; and

(f) in the case of any other person, by that person or any person competent to act on his behalf.

(3) The form of appeal in Form No.E.A.-1 shall be filed in duplicate and shall be accompanied by a copy of the decision or order
appealed against.

Rule4. Form of application to the Commissioner (Appeals ).-

(1) An application under sub-section (4) of section 35E of the Act to the Commissioner (Appeals) shall be made in Form No.
E.A.-2 .

(2) The form of application in Form No. E.A.-2 shall be filed induplicate and shall be accompanied by a certified copy of the
decision or order passed by the adjudicating authority and a copy of the order passed by the Commissioner of Central Excise
directing such authority to apply to the Commissioner (Appeals).

Rule 5. Production of additional evidence before Commissioner (Appeals). -

(1) The appellant shall not be entitled to produce before the Commissioner (Appeals) any evidence, whetheroral or documentary,
other than the evidence produced by him during the course of the proceedings before the adjudicating authority except in the
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the Central Excise (Appeals) Rules, 2001 — specifically procedural rules for filing appeals to Commissioner (Appeals), including who may sign the appeal form for different entity types (Rule 3) and the bar on producing additional evidence (Rule 5). These rules support HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-entity signing authority disputes and evidentiary bar scenarios in legacy central excise matters that transition into GST-era litigation.

questions.q: Prism Coatings Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer of industrial paints (HSN 3208) based in Pune, Maharashtra, received an adverse adjudication order dated 12-March-2019 from the Additional Commissioner of Central Excise raising a demand of ₹1.84 crore on account of alleged clandestine clearances for the period 2015–2017. The company's Managing Director, Mr. Arun Desai, is currently resident in Singapore and has not executed a formal Power of Attorney in favour of any India-based officer. The company's CFO, Mr. Rajeev Kulkarni, wishes to file an appeal before the Commissioner (Appeals) and sign the appeal form on behalf of the company. The in-house legal team is unsure (a) whether Mr. Kulkarni qualifies as the 'principal officer' for the purpose of signing the appeal form, (b) whether an additional document — a board resolution authorizing him — is legally required under the appeals rules, and (c) whether the appeal will be treated as defective and time-barred if submitted without such authorization. The limitation period expires on 20-May-2026. Please advise on the validity of the proposed filing and any curative steps required.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 3(2)(c) specifically provides that for a company the grounds of appeal shall be signed by 'the principal officer thereof' — the entire question turns on whether a CFO qualifies as principal officer, making this rule the indispensable authoritative source; no generic GST chunk answers this.

questions.q: Bharat Forge Components LLP, a registered job-worker in Faridabad, Haryana, is contesting a central excise demand for the period April 2014 to June 2017 relating to alleged non-payment of duty on goods cleared from its job-work premises. During the original adjudication proceedings the LLP did not submit certain cost-audit reports and machine-utilisation records that are critical to its defence. The adjudicating authority passed an order-in-original on 30-November-2018. The LLP now wants to file an appeal before the Commissioner (Appeals) and simultaneously introduce these cost-audit records as fresh evidence. Two partners are available to sign the appeal: Mr. Harpreet Singh (a designated partner, age 34) and Ms. Neha Rawat (a sleeping partner, age 19, who joined as a minor and has since turned 19). The LLP's advocate asks: (i) which partner(s) are legally competent to sign the appeal form, (ii) under what specific conditions, if any, the Commissioner (Appeals) can permit the fresh documentary evidence to be admitted that was not placed before the adjudicating authority, and (iii) whether the evidentiary bar operates differently where the evidence existed at the time of adjudication but was simply not submitted.
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 3(2)(d) bars a minor partner from signing — directly relevant to Ms. Rawat's eligibility — while Rule 5(1) imposes the evidentiary bar and its exceptions, both of which are the specific operative provisions needed to answer parts (i) and (ii)/(iii) respectively.

title: Seeks to amend notification No. 28/2021-Customs to exempt customs duty on import of Amphotericin B, and also to extend the exemptions under the said notification up to 31st August, 2021
text:

published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary vide number G.S.R. 286(E), dated the 24th 
April, 2021.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the customs duty exemption on import of Amphotericin B (antifungal drug) under notification 31/2021-Customs, which amended 28/2021-Customs to add Amphotericin B to the exemption list and extended the exemption period from 31st July to 31st August 2021. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving importers claiming exemption during the extended window, classification disputes under Chapter 29/30, and interplay between customs exemption and GST IGST on imports.

questions.q: Mehta Pharmaceuticals Pvt Ltd, GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZX, Mumbai, imported a consignment of Amphotericin B (bulk drug, Chapter 29) from a US supplier on 15th August 2021, Bill of Entry filed on 18th August 2021. Customs has raised a demand saying the exemption notification they are relying upon had lapsed on 31st July 2021 and the amendment extending it to 31st August 2021 was not applicable because their B/E was filed after the goods had already arrived (IGM filed 10th August). The importer argues the exemption is available since the B/E was filed within the extended period. Additionally, the supplier invoiced it under a description 'antifungal polyene antibiotic' without the INN name 'Amphotericin B'. Does Mehta Pharmaceuticals have a valid claim to the nil customs duty exemption, and does the description on the commercial invoice affect the classification under Chapter 29 vs Chapter 30 for this exemption?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the sole authority establishing that (a) Amphotericin B was inserted into the exemption table under S.No. 19 covering Chapter headings 29 or 30, and (b) the exemption was extended to 31st August 2021 — both facts are indispensable to resolving whether the B/E filed on 18th August 2021 qualifies, and which chapter heading applies.

questions.q: Arogya Life Sciences LLP, an EOU registered in Hyderabad (AP GSTIN 37AACJA5678B1ZQ), imported Amphotericin B in two tranches: first tranche (500 kg) arrived and B/E filed on 25th July 2021; second tranche (800 kg) arrived 2nd August 2021 with B/E filed on 5th August 2021. The EOU had procured it for re-export as finished formulations. Customs cleared the first tranche at nil BCD citing the COVID exemption notification, but is now disputing the second tranche on grounds that (i) EOU imports are governed by a separate FTP/Customs notification regime and the COVID exemption notification may not apply cumulatively, and (ii) IGST on import (which is not covered by the COVID exemption notification) was not paid for either tranche. The LLP's consultant argues both tranches are exempt on BCD and IGST should also be covered via a separate IGST exemption. Analyse the customs duty and IGST treatment for both tranches, specifically whether the extension of the exemption to 31st August 2021 covers the second tranche and whether an EOU importer can simultaneously claim this COVID exemption and EOU-specific customs benefits.
questions.why_this_chunk: This chunk is the direct authority for the 31st August 2021 extended deadline (without which the second tranche filed on 5th August would be disputed) and for the dual chapter-heading coverage (29 or 30) of Amphotericin B, which is central to determining whether the EOU's import classification aligns with the exemption entry.

title: Amends Central Excise notification numbers 22/2003-Central Excise, dated the 31stMarch, 2003 and 23/2003-Central Excise, dated the 31stMarch, 2003
text:
e, certifying that the said organization is recognized by the Central Government or
the State Government or the Government of the Union territory and is run on a non commercial basis.";

(e) after paragraph 10, the following paragraphs shall be  inserted, namely:-

"10A. Notwithstanding anything contained in this notification, the exemption contained herein shall apply to
spares and consumables up to 1.5 (one point five) per cent. of Free on Board (FOB) value  of manufactured
articles exported  out of  India  by the  unit which manufactured such articles during the preceding  year for the
purpose of supply of such spares and consumables with such exported articles  for after- sale- service .

10B. Notwithstanding anything contained in this notification, if the said officer is satisfied that the unit,
engaged in manufacture of textiles and textile articles, has left over textile fabric or textile material, he shall
allow such unit to clear such left over textile fabric or textile material into Domestic Tariff Area on payment of
duty on assessable value, as if the goods have been manufactured in the unit:

Provided that such clearance shall be allowed only where the said officer certifies that the textile fabric or
textile material proposed to be cleared are left over, and such clearance do not exceed two per cent. of
assessable value of the consignment or two percent. of the quantity of the consignment to which this left over
textile fabric or textile material relates, whichever is lower.";

(f)  in Explanation occurring after paragraph 13,-

(a)  for clause (iii),  the following clause shall be substituted, namely:-
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers two specific EOU/export-unit rules: (10A) spares/consumables up to 1.5% of FOB value allowed for after-sale-service with exported articles, and (10B) clearance of leftover textile fabric/material into DTA on payment of duty, capped at 2% of assessable value or quantity (whichever lower), with officer certification. These are narrow, proviso-laden rules that support HIGH-complexity practitioner queries.

questions.q: Rajlakshmi Textiles Pvt Ltd, an EOU in Surat (Gujarat) manufacturing and exporting cotton shirting fabric, exported consignments worth ₹8 crore FOB in the preceding year. In the current year, after completing a consignment for a buyer in Germany, they have 800 metres of leftover shirting fabric (out of a total consignment of 40,000 metres) assessed at ₹12 lakh, against a total consignment assessable value of ₹6.5 crore. The unit wants to clear this leftover fabric into the Domestic Tariff Area. Additionally, the unit proposes to send sewing machine spare parts worth ₹14 lakh to the German buyer for after-sale-service of the exported shirts. The Assistant Commissioner of Customs (EOU) is reviewing both requests. Advise Rajlakshmi Textiles on (a) whether the leftover fabric clearance is permissible and up to what limit, (b) whether the spare parts dispatch qualifies for exemption and up to what value, and (c) what documentary/certification requirements must be met for each — citing the specific provisos and caps that govern both situations.
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 10B governs DTA clearance of leftover textile fabric (2% cap on assessable value or quantity, whichever lower, with officer certification); Para 10A governs the 1.5% FOB cap for spares/consumables for after-sale-service — both are indispensable to answering each limb of the question.

questions.q: Precision Components LLP, an Export Oriented Unit in Pune (Maharashtra) manufacturing hydraulic pump assemblies, exported goods worth ₹15 crore FOB in FY 2024-25. In April 2025, a dispute arises with the jurisdictional Development Commissioner: the LLP claims it is entitled to dispatch hydraulic seals and lubricant cartridges (spares and consumables) worth ₹28 lakh to overseas customers in the UAE and Japan for after-sale-service of pumps exported last year, under the blanket exemption available to EOUs. The Development Commissioner contends that the exemption notification's general conditions do not extend to consumables dispatched post-export, and that the 1.5% FOB threshold has already been exhausted by an earlier dispatch of ₹18 lakh in the same financial year. Analyse: (a) whether the 'notwithstanding' override in the relevant EOU notification paragraph saves the spares/consumables exemption from the general conditions, (b) how the 1.5% FOB cap is computed and whether the earlier ₹18 lakh dispatch affects the remaining entitlement, and (c) what the LLP's legal position is if the Development Commissioner refuses to certify the dispatch.
questions.why_this_chunk: Para 10A's 'notwithstanding anything contained in this notification' clause, the 1.5% FOB cap computation, and the condition linking spares/consumables to after-sale-service of the specific exported articles are all drawn directly from this chunk and are indispensable to resolving the dispute.

title: Seeks to extend the due date for furnishing of return in FORM GSTR-3B for registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir for the months of July, 2019 to September, 2019.
text:
[To be published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i)]

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue)

Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs

Notification No.60/2019 – Central Tax

New Delhi, the 26th November, 2019

G.S.R.....(E).–In exercise of the powers conferred by section 168 of the Central Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) read with sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and 
Services Tax Rules, 2017 (hereafter in this notification referred to as the said rules), the 
Commissioner, on the recommendations of the Council, hereby makes the following further 
amendment in notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of 
Revenue), No.29/2019 – Central Tax, dated the 28th June, 2019, published in the Gazette of 
India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G.S.R.455(E), dated the 
28th June, 2019, namely:–

In the said notification, in the first paragraph, for the fourth proviso, the following proviso shall 
be substituted, namely: –

“Provided also that the return in FORM GSTR-3B of the said rules for the months of July to 
September, 2019 for registered persons whose principal place of business is in the State of 
Jammu and Kashmir, shall be furnished electronically through the common portal, on or before 
the 30th November, 2019.”

2. 
This notification shall be deemed to come into force with effect from the 20th Day of

November, 2019

[F. No. 20/06/09/2019-GST]

(Ruchi Bisht)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note: The principal notification No. 29/2019 – Central Tax, dated the 28th June, 2019 was 
published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary vide number G.S.R. 455(E), dated the 28th June, 
2019 and was last amended by notification No. 54/2019 – Central Tax, dated the 14th November, 
2019 published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary vide number G.S.R. 848(E), dated the 14th 
November, 2019.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the extended GSTR-3B due date (30 Nov 2019) specifically for J&K registered persons for July–September 2019 quarters, operating via an amendment to Notification 29/2019-CT under section 168 CGST Act read with rule 61(5) CGST Rules. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving late-fee/interest liability calculations for J&K taxpayers, penalty exposure analysis, and cross-notification chain reasoning (29/2019 → 54/2019 → 60/2019).

questions.q: Himalayan Handicrafts Pvt Ltd, registered in Srinagar (J&K) with GSTIN 01AABCH1234A1ZX, filed its GSTR-3B for July 2019 on 25th November 2019 and for August 2019 on 2nd December 2019. The GST officer is now issuing a notice for late fee and interest under sections 47 and 50 of the CGST Act for both months, treating the standard due date of 20th August and 20th September 2019 as applicable. The company's CA contends that no late fee arises for either month. Is the CA correct for both months, and if not, for which month does interest under section 50 still accrue despite the extended deadline, given that the amending notification was deemed to have come into force from 20th November 2019 and not from the original due dates?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's specific proviso — extended due date of 30 Nov 2019 for J&K taxpayers for July–September 2019, deemed effective from 20 Nov 2019 — is indispensable to determine whether the July and August 2019 filings were within time and whether the retrospective deeming provision covers the interest period.

questions.q: Zaffron Exports LLP, whose principal place of business is in Jammu (J&K) with GSTIN 01AABFZ5678B1Z2, also operates a branch registered in Chandigarh (Punjab) under a separate GSTIN 03AABFZ5678B2Z1. Both GSTINs filed GSTR-3B for the quarter July–September 2019 on 28th November 2019. The Punjab GST authority has issued a demand for late fee for the Chandigarh GSTIN's September 2019 return (standard due date 20 Oct 2019), while the J&K GSTIN claims protection under an extended deadline. The LLP's counsel argues that since the beneficial notification was issued under section 168 of the CGST Act which binds the entire GSTIN ecosystem, both registrations should get the extended due date. Analyse whether this argument holds, distinguishing the jurisdictional scope of the notification's proviso which ties the extension specifically to 'principal place of business in the State of Jammu and Kashmir', and what the late-fee exposure is for each GSTIN.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's precise language — 'registered persons whose principal place of business is in the State of Jammu and Kashmir' — is the dispositive fact that determines whether the Chandigarh GSTIN qualifies, making this chunk the authoritative source for resolving the multi-jurisdiction dispute.

title: Various issues related to availment of benefit of Section 128A of the CGST Act, 2017
text:
Circular No.  248/05/2025-GST

F. No. CBIC-20001/14/2024-GST

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs

GST Policy Wing

North Block, New Delhi,

Dated: 27th March 2025

To,

All the Principal Chief Commissioners/ Chief Commissioners  
      All the Principal Directors General/ Directors General

Madam/Sir,

Subject: Various issues related to availment of benefit of Section 128A of the     
CGST Act, 2017-reg.

Based on the recommendations of the GST Council made in its 53rd and 54th

meetings, a new section 128A was inserted in the Central Goods and Services Tax 
Act,2017 (hereinafter referred to as CGST Act, 2017) and Rule 164 has been inserted in the 
Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 (hereinafter referred to as CGST Rules, 2017) 
w.e.f. 1st November 2024 to provide for waiver of interest or penalty or both relating to 
demands raised under Section 73 for the period from 1st July 2017 to 31st March 2020. In 
this regard, circular No. 238/32/2024-GST dated 15th October 2024 has also been issued 
clarifying various issues related to implementation of the said provisions. 
 
2. 
Representations have been received from trade and industry highlighting certain

issues being faced in availing the benefit provided under section 128A of the CGST Act, 
2017 such as eligibility of cases for benefit under section 128A, where payment has been 
made through GSTR-3B instead of DRC-03 and treatment of withdrawal of appeals filed 
by the taxpayer against consolidated adjudication order covering periods beyond the one 
specified under section 128A of the CGST Act, 2017 for the purpose of availing the said 
benefit. 
 
3. 
Accordingly, in view of the difficulties being faced by the trade and industry and

to ensure uniformity in the implementation of the provisions of the law across field

Page 1 of 3
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Circular 248/05/2025-GST which clarifies eligibility and procedural issues under Section 128A of CGST Act 2017 — specifically payment via GSTR-3B vs DRC-03, and treatment of appeal withdrawal against consolidated orders spanning periods beyond FY2020. These are highly specific procedural-legal intersection points that support complex practitioner queries.

questions.q: Raghunath Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCR1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) received a demand notice under Section 73 for FY 2018-19 and FY 2019-20 consolidated into a single adjudication order. The company paid the full tax amount through its GSTR-3B return for March 2020 rather than through DRC-03, and had also filed an appeal before the GST Appellate Authority challenging the consolidated order (which also covered FY 2020-21 demands). The company now wants to avail the waiver of interest and penalty under the amnesty scheme. Can Raghunath Textiles withdraw only the portion of the appeal relating to FY 2018-19 and FY 2019-20 to claim the benefit, and will the GSTR-3B payment be treated as a valid discharge of tax liability for this purpose, or must they file a fresh DRC-03?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly identifies both exact issues raised in trade representations: (1) eligibility where payment was made through GSTR-3B instead of DRC-03, and (2) treatment of withdrawal of appeals filed against consolidated adjudication orders covering periods beyond those specified under Section 128A — making this chunk the primary authoritative source for resolving both sub-questions.

questions.q: Apex Pharma Components LLP (GSTIN 29AAEFA5678K1Z3, Karnataka) has a demand raised under Section 73 of the CGST Act for the period July 2017 to March 2020 involving disputed ITC on inter-state purchases from a Gujarat supplier. The LLP paid the contested tax demand partly via GSTR-3B offset during filing of their returns and the remaining through a DRC-03 challan. They simultaneously filed an appeal before the First Appellate Authority against a single consolidated order that bundles FY 2017-18, FY 2018-19, FY 2019-20, and FY 2020-21 demands. The appeal is pending. A junior consultant advises that since the order is consolidated and cannot be split, the LLP is ineligible for Section 128A benefit entirely. Is this correct? What is the precise treatment for the partial GSTR-3B payment and for withdrawal of the appeal as it pertains only to the Section 128A-eligible period, and what procedural steps must the LLP follow as clarified by CBIC?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses both the GSTR-3B-vs-DRC-03 payment eligibility question and the consolidated adjudication order appeal-withdrawal treatment, which are the two specific industry pain-points this circular was issued to resolve — neither question can be definitively answered without this circular's clarifications.

title: Service Tax on Doordarshan and All India Radio under the category of Broadcasting Services.
text:
ST Circular No. 61/10/2003

14 th July 2003

F.NO.165/2/2003-CX-4

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise and Customs

Subject : Service Tax on Doordarshan and All India Radio under the category of Broadcasting Services.

I am directed to refer Board"s letter of even number dated 27 th March 2003, on the above cite subject. In continuation of the
referred letter, I am directed to say that the protection was available to Prasar Bharati Corporation ( Doordarshan and All Inia
Radio) from payment of the Service Tax on the basis of interpretation of secton 22 of the Prasar Bharati ( Broadcasting
Corporation of India) Act, 1990.

The section 22 of the Prasar Bharati ( Broadcasting Corporation of India) Act, 1990 hax been omitted vide secton 163 of the
Finance Act, 2002 with effect from 1 st April 2003. In light of the above, I am directed to say that with effect from 1 st April 2003
the protection under section 22 of the Prasar Bharati (Broadcasting Corporation of India) Act, 1990 is no longer available to
the Prasar Bharati Corporation ( Doordarshan and All India Radio) and they are liable to pay the Service Tax as the provider of
the Broadcasting Services with effect from 1 st April 2003.

The instructions issued vide the letter dated 27 th March is modified in above terms and the field formations should be suitably
instructed to get all units of Prasar Bharati Corporation registered immediately.

Manish Mohan

Under Secretary to the Govt. Of India

Phone: 23094558
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the removal of Prasar Bharati Corporation's (Doordarshan and All India Radio) service tax exemption effective 1 April 2003, due to omission of Section 22 of the Prasar Bharati Act via Finance Act 2002. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving transitional liability, broadcasting service tax, and cross-act statutory interpretation.

questions.q: Our client, MediaBridge Advertising LLP (Mumbai), entered into a long-term sponsorship agreement with Doordarshan's regional Kendra in Bengaluru in October 2002, under which MediaBridge was to pay a fixed annual fee for airtime slots running from November 2002 through October 2003. Doordarshan did not charge any Service Tax on invoices raised between November 2002 and March 2003, citing an internal exemption under their enabling statute. From April 2003 onwards, Doordarshan began raising invoices with Service Tax. MediaBridge is contesting the Service Tax demand on the April–October 2003 invoices, arguing that (a) the original contract was signed pre-April 2003 under the exemption regime, (b) the contract made no provision for statutory levy pass-through, and (c) Doordarshan itself was not registered as a service provider during this period. As the consultant to MediaBridge, advise whether the Service Tax liability on post-April 2003 invoices is legally sustainable, who bears the incidence, and what the registration status of Doordarshan affects in terms of reverse-charge or normal charge applicability.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the direct authority establishing that Doordarshan's exemption under Section 22 of the Prasar Bharati Act ceased on 1 April 2003 following its omission by Finance Act 2002, and mandates immediate registration — both facts are indispensable to analysing the transitional liability and registration-gap argument raised by MediaBridge.

questions.q: Prasar Bharati's All India Radio (AIR) station in Chennai entered into two separate agreements during FY 2002-03: (1) a paid political broadcast agreement with a registered political party for slots aired between January and May 2003, and (2) a commercial jingle broadcasting contract with Sundaram Auto Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN not yet applicable — pre-GST era) for slots aired between March and July 2003. The Department has issued a show-cause notice to AIR Chennai demanding Service Tax on both contracts for the full period, arguing that AIR is a Broadcasting Service provider and the exemption lapsed on 1 April 2003. AIR Chennai's defence is that (a) political broadcasts are not 'commercial' broadcasting and should not attract Service Tax, and (b) the Sundaram contract slots that aired before 1 April 2003 are covered by the statutory protection. Evaluate the merits of each limb of AIR's defence, identifying the precise statutory event that extinguished the protection and whether the apportionment of pre- and post-April 2003 liability is legally sound.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifically identifies the statutory mechanism — omission of Section 22 of the Prasar Bharati Act by Section 163 of Finance Act 2002 effective 1 April 2003 — which is the critical cut-off date for apportioning AIR's liability across the two contracts and for testing whether the 'protection' argument survives for pre-April slots.

title: Regarding Joint Ventures and levy of service tax
text:
Usually responsibility of managing the cash calls of the JV is assigned to one or some of the members of the JV, by way of a
contractual agreement, for which he/they may receive a consideration either in cash or kind (say, goods or services).

A member of JV may provide support services (for example, administrative service in the form of setting up/management of a
project office/site office) to the JV for a consideration either in cash or kind (say, goods or services).

5. JV being an unincorporated temporary association constituted for the limited purpose of carrying out a specified project
within a time frame, a comprehensive examination of the various JV agreements (at times, there could be number of inter se
agreements between members of the JV) holds the key to understanding of the taxation of transactions involving taxable
services between the JV and its members or inter-se between the members of a JV. Therefore officers in the field formations
are advised to carefully examine the leviability of service tax with reference to the specific terms/clauses of each JV
agreement.

6. All concerned are requested to acknowledge the receipt of this circular.

7. Hindi version to follow.

[Dr. Abhishek Chandra Gupta]

Technical Officer, TRU

Tel. no.: 011-23093075
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the service tax treatment of transactions within unincorporated Joint Ventures — specifically how cash call management fees and support services rendered by a JV member to the JV (or inter-se between members) are taxable, and that leviability turns on the specific contractual terms of each JV agreement.

questions.q: Our client, Shapoorji Pallonji Engineering & Construction Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, Maharashtra), entered into an unincorporated JV with Larsen & Toubro Ltd for a NHAI highway project in Rajasthan. Under the JV agreement, Shapoorji is the 'lead member' responsible for managing all cash calls — coordinating fund drawdowns from both members and making payments to sub-contractors — for which the JV agreement entitles Shapoorji to a 1.2% management fee on total project cash deployed (approx. ₹18 crore over the project life). L&T's tax team is now claiming this fee was never subject to service tax under the pre-GST regime because Shapoorji and the JV are 'the same person.' Separately, Shapoorji also seconded its project-office staff to the JV site office for which the JV reimbursed Shapoorji at cost-plus-10%. A SCN has been issued for both streams. How should Shapoorji respond, and does the taxability of each stream depend on different contractual readings?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that cash-call management responsibility assigned to a JV member for a consideration (cash or kind) is a taxable service, and that support services (e.g., administrative/project-office management) provided by a member to the JV for consideration are also taxable — directly addressing both the management-fee and the staff-secondment streams in the SCN.

questions.q: Petrotech Services LLP (registered in Gujarat) is a member of an unincorporated JV with a Korean EPC contractor for an offshore platform project under a PSC with ONGC. The JV agreement has three inter-se agreements: (a) a cash-call agreement where the Korean partner manages treasury and charges the JV a 0.8% treasury-management fee; (b) a services agreement where Petrotech provides HSE (health, safety & environment) management to the JV billed at ₹2.5 crore/quarter; and (c) a profit-sharing arrangement where each member receives a share of JV surplus in proportion to their equity stake. A service tax audit for the period 2015–2017 has raised a demand treating all three streams as taxable services rendered by members to the JV. Petrotech contests that the profit-share is not a 'consideration' for any service. Advise on the correct service tax treatment of each stream and the evidentiary approach the assessing officer should take.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the authoritative circular guidance stating that (i) treasury/cash-call management by a member for consideration is taxable, (ii) support services by a member to the JV for consideration are taxable, and (iii) field officers must examine the specific terms of each JV agreement — which is the precise legal framework needed to distinguish the taxable fee/service streams from the non-taxable profit-share.

title: Manufacture and Other Operations in Special Warehouse Regulations, 2020
text:
Manufacture and Other Operations in Special Warehouse Regulations, 2020

[ Notification No. 75 /2020-Customs (N.T.) dated the 17th August, 2020]

In exercise of the powers conferred by section 157 , read with sections 58A , 65 and 143AA of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of
1962), the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs hereby makes the following regulations, namely: -

Regulation 1. Short title and commencement. -

(1) These regulations may be called the Manufacture and Other Operations in Special Warehouse Regulations, 2020.

(2) They shall come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette.

Regulation 2. Definitions. -

(1) In these regulations, unless the context otherwise requires, -

(a) "Act" means the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962);

(b) "bond officer" means an officer of customs in charge of a warehouse;

(c) "Controller of Certifying Authorities" means the authority appointed under sub-section (1) of section 17 of the Information
Technology Act, 2000 (21 of 2000);

(d) "Form" means the form appended to these regulations;

(e) "section" means the section of the Act.

(2) The words and expressions used herein and not defined in these regulations but defined in the Act shall have the same
meanings respectively as assigned to them in the Act.

1 [Regulation 3. Application. -

These regulations shall apply to, -

(i) the units that operate under section 65 of the Act, or

(ii) the units applying for permission to operate under section 65 of the Act, in a special warehouse licensed under section 58A
of the Act. ]

1. Substituted by Notification No. 76/2020-Cus. (N.T.) dated 17-8-2020

Regulation 4. Eligibility for application for operating under these regulations. -

(1) The following persons shall be eligible to apply for operating under these regulations, -

(i) a person who applies for a license under section 58A of the Act for warehousing of goods listed at clause (1) of
notification 66/2016-Customs (N.T.) (hereinafter referred to as the specified goods), along with permission for undertaking
manufacturing or other operations in the warehouse under section 65 of the Act; or

(ii) a person who has been granted a license under section 58A of the Act for warehousing of the specified goods, in
accordance with Special Warehouse Licensing Regulations 2016.

(2) An application under these regulations shall be made to the Principal Commissioner of Customs or the Commissioner of
Customs, as the case may be, along with an undertaking to,-

(i) maintain accounts of receipt and removal of goods in digital form in such format as may be specified and furnish the same
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the eligibility criteria and application framework for units seeking to manufacture/operate inside a Special Warehouse under Customs Act sections 58A and 65, including the link to notification 66/2016-Customs (N.T.) for 'specified goods'. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving cross-act interaction (Customs + GST), multi-entity reasoning (warehouse licensor + manufacturing unit + bond officer), and legal interpretation of who qualifies and under what conditions.

questions.q: Meridian Aromatics LLP, based in Nhava Sheva (Maharashtra), imports crude essential oils (a notified 'specified good' under Customs) and wants to blend and repackage them inside a bonded facility before either clearing domestically or exporting. Their customs consultant is advising them to apply for a Special Warehouse licence and simultaneously seek permission to undertake manufacturing operations inside that warehouse. A rival consultant argues that Meridian must first obtain the warehouse licence and only then apply for the manufacturing permission in a second, separate application. The GSTIN of Meridian is 27AACFM1234A1ZK. Given that Meridian's goods are on the specified-goods list under notification 66/2016-Customs (N.T.), which of the two consultant positions is legally correct, and what is the precise statutory basis — including any undertaking Meridian must give at the time of application — that determines whether a combined or sequential application route is permissible?
questions.why_this_chunk: Regulation 4(1)(i) explicitly allows a person who 'applies for a licence under section 58A' to simultaneously apply for permission under section 65 in a single application, directly resolving the dispute; the undertaking requirement in Regulation 4(2) is indispensable to advising what Meridian must commit to at application stage.

questions.q: Primus Electronics Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-based entity (GSTIN 07AACP9876B1Z3), has already been granted a Special Warehouse licence under section 58A for warehousing notified electronic components at a facility in Kandla SEZ. It now wishes to start soldering and sub-assembly operations inside the same bonded space and seeks your advice on whether it qualifies to apply under the Manufacture and Other Operations in Special Warehouse Regulations, 2020 — and if so, to which authority the application must be submitted, since the SEZ's jurisdictional Commissioner and the parent Principal Commissioner of Customs for Kandla port are two different officers. Additionally, the company's digital accounts system currently stores data in a proprietary XML format; advise whether this will satisfy the undertaking requirements at the application stage or whether a format change is mandatory before applying.
questions.why_this_chunk: Regulation 4(1)(ii) covers the scenario of an already-licenced section 58A holder applying for manufacturing permission, making Primus eligible; Regulation 4(2) specifies application is to 'Principal Commissioner or Commissioner, as the case may be' (jurisdiction question) and mandates the digital-accounts undertaking in a format 'as may be specified', which is pivotal to advising on the XML-format issue.

title: Seeks to levy definitive anti dumping duty on nylon filament yarn imported from Vietnam and European Union.
text:
2. 
The anti-dumping duty imposed under this notification shall be effective for a period

of five years (unless revoked, superseded or amended earlier) from the 6th day of October, 
2018 and shall be paid in Indian currency. 
 
Explanation.-   For the purposes of this notification, rate of exchange applicable for the 
purpose of calculation of such anti-dumping duty shall be the rate which is specified in the 
notification of the Government of India, in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), 
issued from time to time, in exercise of the powers conferred by section 14 of the Customs 
Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), and the relevant date for the determination of the rate of exchange 
shall be the date of presentation of the bill of entry under section 46 of the said Customs 
Act.

[F. No. 354/326/2018 -TRU]

(Mohit Tewari)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the effective period of the anti-dumping duty on nylon filament yarn from Vietnam/EU (5 years from 6 Oct 2018), the currency of payment, and critically the exchange rate determination mechanism — specifically that the relevant date is the date of presentation of the bill of entry under section 46 of the Customs Act, 1962. These two rules (sunset date + exchange rate date) create complex practitioner problems involving timing, currency fluctuation, and cross-act linkage.

questions.q: Sunrise Textiles Pvt Ltd, Surat (GSTIN 24AABCS1234F1ZX), imported a consignment of nylon filament yarn (HS 5402) from a Vietnamese exporter on 15 September 2023. The bill of entry was filed on 18 September 2023, but due to a port congestion dispute the goods were not examined and the entry was re-presented (a fresh bill of entry filed) on 5 October 2023. The USD/INR exchange rate notified by the Ministry of Finance was ₹82.50 on 18 Sep and ₹83.10 on 5 Oct. Customs officers are now computing anti-dumping duty on the yarn and are using the 5 October rate, arguing the relevant date is the re-presentation date. The importer argues the original filing date governs. Additionally, the company's legal team is flagging that the anti-dumping order itself may have expired by this date. Please advise: (a) which exchange rate should govern the ADD computation, (b) whether the anti-dumping duty notification was still in force on the date of import, and (c) how both answers interact to determine the total ADD liability in INR.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states (i) the duty is effective for five years from 6 Oct 2018 (sunset analysis for Sep/Oct 2023 imports is squarely answered here) and (ii) the relevant date for exchange rate is the date of presentation of the bill of entry under section 46 — making the re-presentation vs original filing dispute directly resolvable only by reading this specific provision.

questions.q: Meridian Yarns LLP, Mumbai, is the Indian subsidiary of a European parent. It imported nylon filament yarn (HS 5402) from its EU-based sister concern in two tranches: Tranche A (bill of entry presented 4 October 2023, EUR/INR rate ₹88.00 as notified) and Tranche B (bill of entry presented 8 October 2023, EUR/INR rate ₹88.60 as notified). The customs broker is arguing that Tranche B falls outside the operative period of the anti-dumping duty notification and therefore no ADD is leviable on it, while Tranche A attracts ADD. The importer's CFO wants to know: (a) is the broker's interpretation of the sunset date correct given that the notification took effect on 6 October 2018 for a period of five years, (b) for Tranche A where ADD is clearly leviable, which date's EUR/INR exchange rate must be used — the date the goods arrived at the port, the date the bill of entry was presented, or the date of out-of-charge order — and (c) whether the related-party nature of the transaction (EU sister concern) affects the exchange rate reference point or requires a separate customs valuation adjustment before applying the ADD rate.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's two operative rules are both essential: the five-year sunset from 6 Oct 2018 resolves the Tranche A/B dispute at the boundary date; and the explicit rule that the relevant date for exchange rate is the bill-of-entry presentation date under section 46 (not arrival or out-of-charge) is the only authoritative basis for answering part (b), distinguishing it from generic customs valuation rules.

claude_reasoning: This corrigendum to Notification 26/2003-Customs (N.T.) amends the duty drawback schedule for Chapter 73 iron/steel articles by inserting the condition 'out of duty paid imported steel' against serial nos. 73.29–73.31, meaning drawback eligibility for these tariff entries is conditional on the steel input having borne customs duty — directly supporting complex questions on drawback entitlement where input-duty status is disputed.

questions.q: Arjun Alloys Pvt Ltd, a Pune-based manufacturer with IEC 0312XXXXX, exports forged steel hooks (Chapter 73) in March 2025 and files a drawback claim under the AIR schedule. The customs team at JNPT raises an objection stating that the AIR drawback rate for the relevant serial entry is not applicable because the company used a mix of domestically sourced HR coils and imported steel on which BCD was paid — but the importer's bill of entry shows the duty was provisionally assessed and the final assessment order is still pending. Can Arjun Alloys claim the AIR drawback in full, partially, or not at all, and what documentation must it furnish to establish that the imported steel component qualifies as 'duty paid' within the meaning of the amended drawback schedule entry?
questions.why_this_chunk: The corrigendum inserts 'out of duty paid imported steel' as a qualifying condition for serial nos. 73.29–73.31; the question turns entirely on whether steel under provisional assessment satisfies this condition — a fact indispensable from this specific amendment.

questions.q: Bhavani Steel Fabricators LLP, a Chennai export house, manufactures and exports articles of iron/steel covered under Chapter 73 heading, sourced from two suppliers: (a) Tata Steel (domestic), and (b) imported coils on which BCD and IGST were paid at Chennai port under a bill of entry dated 10-Jan-2025. The LLP claims AIR duty drawback at the full schedule rate for the relevant Chapter 73 serial entry. The jurisdictional drawback cell disallows the claim arguing that since the LLP is also registered under GST and has availed ITC of the IGST paid on the imported coils, the steel cannot be treated as 'duty paid imported steel' for drawback purposes because the IGST component was already neutralised. The LLP counters that BCD was not set off through ITC. How should the drawback claim be adjudicated — does availing ITC of IGST on imported inputs disqualify the goods from being 'duty paid imported steel' under the amended schedule, and does this affect the BCD component of the drawback separately?
questions.why_this_chunk: The corrigendum's insertion of 'out of duty paid imported steel' in the drawback schedule entries 73.29–73.31 is the precise trigger for the dispute; the question requires interpreting whether ITC recovery of IGST vitiates the 'duty paid' condition — an analysis rooted directly in this amendment's language.
