claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the procedural requirements for refund adjudication under CGST Rules — specifically the mandatory issuance of FORM GST RFD-06 for sanction/rejection, the electronic availability requirement, the audi alteram partem safeguard before rejection, and the bifurcated payment path (RFD-05 payment advice) when the refund is consumer-welfare-fund-exempt under section 54(8). These procedural rules interact with substantive eligibility under section 54 and create HIGH-complexity scenarios involving partial sanctions, deemed rejection timelines, and multi-jurisdictional export refund claims.

questions.q: Nexus Pharma Exports LLP, registered in Telangana (GSTIN 36AABFN7821R1ZK), filed an application for refund of accumulated ITC on account of zero-rated exports (without payment of tax) for the quarter ending March 2025, amounting to ₹48.7 lakhs. The proper officer issued a deficiency memo, the LLP re-filed, and 45 days later received a summary rejection order via the GST portal without any prior show-cause notice or personal hearing. The LLP's consultant argues the rejection is void ab initio. Separately, for ₹6.2 lakhs out of the same claim, the officer determined the amount was not borne by the exporter and should flow to the Consumer Welfare Fund. What is the correct procedural sequence the officer was required to follow — including which specific forms had to be issued at each stage, whether a hearing was mandatorily required before rejection, and whether the ₹6.2 lakh portion warranted a different order and payment instrument than the balance — and what remedies does Nexus Pharma have against the summary rejection?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly prescribes: (a) RFD-06 is the mandatory form for sanction or rejection; (b) no rejection is permissible without an opportunity of hearing — making the summary rejection void; (c) sub-rule (4) mandates a separate RFD-06 + RFD-05 payment advice specifically when the amount is payable under section 54(8) (i.e., the CWF portion), distinguishing it procedurally from the refund-to-applicant path.

questions.q: Arcturus Components Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer in Pune (GSTIN 27AARCA1234C1ZP), supplies sub-assemblies to an SEZ unit in Kandla. For FY 2024-25, it claims a refund of ₹1.14 crore in unutilised ITC under the zero-rated supply route. The refund application was processed and the proper officer passed an order sanctioning only ₹79 lakh, treating ₹35 lakh as ineligible on the ground that the inputs were used in an exempt domestic supply line. The sanctioned amount of ₹79 lakh was credited directly to the company's bank account without any prior intimation of partial rejection or hearing on the ₹35 lakh disallowance. The company's CFO is now asking: (i) Was the officer required to follow the same formal adjudication procedure for the partially rejected ₹35 lakh as he would for a full rejection, including issuance of the prescribed order form and a hearing? (ii) Does the electronic availability of the order satisfy the notice requirement, or was a separate show-cause process required for the disallowed portion? (iii) Which provisions of the refund rules explicitly require that even partial disallowances be communicated through a formal order, and how does that interact with the general time-limit for the officer to complete the refund proceeding?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk expressly states that in case of partial sanction, the officer 'mak[es] an order in FORM GST RFD-06 sanctioning the amount of refund in whole or part, or rejecting' and that 'no application for refund shall be rejected without giving the applicant an opportunity of being heard' — the proviso's language applies mutatis mutandis to the partial rejection component, making the hearing requirement indispensable to answering whether the partial disallowance was procedurally valid.

title: Seeks to amend CGST Rules notification no 3/2017-Central Tax dt 19.06.2017

Instructions:-

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the GSTR-4 return filing instructions for composition taxpayers, including auto-population rules, reverse charge supply acceptance conditions tied to time-of-supply under sections 12/13, Place of Supply reporting obligations, and amendment procedures for debit/credit notes. It supports HIGH-complexity queries around multi-party inward supply scenarios and reverse charge timing disputes.

questions.q: Navratna Traders LLP, a composition taxpayer registered in Rajasthan (GSTIN 08AABFN1234Q1Z5), received three invoices in October 2024 from a registered supplier in Maharashtra under reverse charge — dated 5-Oct, 20-Oct, and 2-Nov respectively. The LLP's accountant is filing GSTR-4 for the October quarter and wants to accept all three invoices auto-populated in Table 4B. The supplier has already reported all three in their GSTR-1. Can the LLP accept the November-dated invoice in the October GSTR-4 return, given that the time of supply for that invoice arguably falls in November? Which table captures this entry, and what is the governing condition for acceptance?
questions.why_this_chunk: Instruction 4(v) explicitly states that a tax recipient may accept invoices pertaining to reverse charge 'only when the time of supply arises in terms of section 12 or 13 of the Act' — this is the indispensable rule for determining whether the November invoice can be pulled into the October GSTR-4.

questions.q: Prism Exports Pvt Ltd, a composition dealer in Tamil Nadu (GSTIN 33AADCP5678R1ZK), received a credit note in January 2025 from an unregistered supplier in Andhra Pradesh, amending a supply originally made in November 2024. The credit note references the original invoice and reduces the taxable value by ₹1,80,000. The company's CFO is confused: (a) which table in GSTR-4 captures the original supply from the unregistered vendor, (b) which table captures the credit note amendment, and (c) whether the Place of Supply needs to be reported separately given the cross-state transaction — especially since the goods were shipped to the company's warehouse in Puducherry (a third state)?
questions.why_this_chunk: Instruction 4(iii) specifies Table 4C for unregistered supplier inward supplies; Instruction 5 governs credit note amendments including the column-filling sequence for original invoice details; and the PoS reporting condition in Instructions 4(vi) and 5 — 'only if different from the location of the recipient' — is directly at issue given the Puducherry delivery address diverging from the Tamil Nadu registration.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the General Bond (Form B-17) executed by Export Oriented Units (EOUs), specifically the bond obligations around duty payment on DTA-sourced goods, provisional assessment finalization under Rule 7 of Central Excise Rules 2017, domestic sale of EOU-manufactured articles under DGFT limitations, and restrictions on name/location changes. These bond conditions create HIGH-complexity scenarios involving EOU compliance across Customs, Central Excise, and Foreign Trade Policy.

questions.q: Precision Optics Pvt Ltd, an EOU registered in Coimbatore (Tamil Nadu) manufacturing optical lenses, procured raw materials worth ₹2.8 crore from a DTA supplier in Pune under a duty-free procurement in January 2025. The goods were provisionally assessed at the time of procurement. By March 2025, the proper officer finalized the assessment and raised a demand for Central Excise duty differential of ₹14.5 lakhs. The company's CFO is asking: (a) within what period must this duty be discharged after the written demand is received, and (b) does the obligation to pay extend to the transport leg from the Pune supplier's premises to their Coimbatore factory, or only to the manufacturing use? The company's bond is a standard Form B-17. Please advise citing the specific bond undertaking that governs this.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 9 of the bond text directly specifies the 10-day payment window after written demand for provisionally assessed goods finalized under Rule 7 of Central Excise Rules 2017, AND explicitly covers the transport leg from place of procurement to EOU premises — both facts are indispensable to answering the CFO's question.

questions.q: Sunrise Biotech LLP, an EOU in Hyderabad manufacturing pharmaceutical intermediates, received DGFT approval in February 2025 to sell 15% of its annual production into the Domestic Tariff Area. In April 2025, it sold a batch to a buyer in Mumbai. The Central Excise officer is now questioning whether Sunrise must pay: (i) Central Excise duty under section 3 of the Central Excise Act 1944 on the articles sold domestically, OR (ii) the Customs duties that were availed as exemption on the imported inputs used in manufacturing those specific goods, OR BOTH. Additionally, Sunrise recently shifted its manufacturing wing to a new building within the same SEZ without informing the Deputy Commissioner. Does this shift constitute a bond violation? Advise on both the duty liability mechanism and the location-change compliance requirement.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 10 specifies that DTA sales trigger liability under section 3 of CEA 1944 OR Customs duties availed as exemption on inputs — the 'or' versus 'and' ambiguity is legally interpretable only from this clause. Clause 13 explicitly requires written permission from the Deputy/Assistant Commissioner before any change in location of manufacturing premises, making the shift a direct bond violation — both clauses are unique to this chunk.

These specific rules and transitional relaxations anchor HIGH-complexity questions about annual return reconciliation errors and ITC classification disputes.

questions.q: Precision Pharma Equipment Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) is filing its GSTR-9 for FY 2018-19. During the year it made zero-rated supplies to an SEZ unit worth ₹1.2 crore (without payment of tax), subsequently issued credit notes of ₹18 lakh against those SEZ supplies due to quality rejections, and also made amendments to three earlier SEZ invoices in Table 9C of GSTR-1. The company's accountant wants to know: (a) whether it can report Tables 5E and 5F net of these credit notes and amendments in GSTR-9 for FY 2018-19 given that separately bifurcating them is causing a system mismatch, and (b) how the amended SEZ supply value should flow into Table 5N — specifically whether the gross pre-amendment figure or the net post-amendment figure should be used for total turnover, and whether any advance receipts against future SEZ supplies on which tax was paid but invoices not yet issued need to be added back. What is the correct treatment under the annual return instructions as amended by notifications issued in 2019 and 2020?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly grants the net-of-credit-notes and net-of-amendments option for Tables 5A–5F for FY 2017-18, 2018-19, and 2019-20 (per Notf 56/2019 and 79/2020), and Table 5N's instruction specifies inclusion of advance tax paid without invoices and exclusion of RCM inward supplies — both indispensable to answering parts (a) and (b).

questions.q: Arjun Castings LLP (GSTIN 33AAKFA5678M1ZQ, Tamil Nadu) is filing GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20. During the year it availed ITC of ₹45 lakh on capital goods imported through an SEZ co-developer, reversed ₹6 lakh of that ITC in December 2019 after a department query, and then reclaimed the same ₹6 lakh in February 2020 after producing a valid court stay order — all entries duly reflected in its GSTR-3B Table 4. It also availed ₹12 lakh ITC on services received directly from an SEZ unit. Now while preparing GSTR-9 Table 6B, the finance team is confused: should the reclaimed ₹6 lakh be included in 6B or reported separately, and does the ₹12 lakh on SEZ-received services qualify for inclusion in 6B even though SEZ supplies are otherwise zero-rated and the LLP is the recipient? Additionally, is the FY 2019-20 breakup relaxation (inputs vs capital goods vs input services) still available for this year? Advise on the correct GSTR-9 disclosure.
questions.why_this_chunk: Table 6B instructions in this chunk explicitly state that ITC which was availed, reversed, and then reclaimed must NOT be included in 6B but declared separately under 6H, and that services received from SEZs are specifically included in 6B despite the general RCM/zero-rated exclusion — both are exact rules needed to answer this query. The chunk also cross-references GSTR-3B Table 4(A)(5) and the capital goods/input services breakup relaxation.

claude_reasoning: This chunk covers import procedures for waste/scrap materials under Hazardous Waste Management Rules, specifically the documentary requirements for Part D Schedule III wastes (including rubber waste/scrap with Basel Nos. B3040/B3080) at Customs — requiring Form 6, DGFT import license, and SPCB acknowledgement. It authoritatively governs the Customs clearance documentary chain for these Basel-listed waste imports, making it indispensable for multi-agency compliance questions.

questions.q: Greenloop Recyclers Pvt Ltd, based in Chennai (Tamil Nadu), GSTIN 33AABCG1234F1Z5, has contracted to import 40 MT of waste pneumatic tyres (Basel No. B3080) from a supplier in Germany. The consignment arrives at Chennai Sea Port in March 2025. The company holds a valid DGFT import license for restricted waste categories. The Customs officer at the port is demanding (i) the MoEF&CC prior permission, (ii) Form 6 filled and submitted, and (iii) an SPCB acknowledgement letter — all three simultaneously before Out-of-Customs-Charge is granted. Greenloop's compliance manager argues that for Part D Schedule III wastes, MoEF&CC prior permission is NOT required, and only Form 6 with DGFT license suffices. Which of the three documents can Customs legally insist upon, which is legally not required, and what is the correct documentary checklist — including any Schedule VIII documents — for this specific Basel category?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that for Part D Schedule III wastes the importer does NOT require MoEF&CC permission but MUST furnish Form 6 to Customs along with documents in Schedule VIII and, where applicable, the DGFT import license — directly resolving which of the three demands is legally sustainable.

questions.q: EcoMetal Solutions LLP, registered in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AAFCE5678K1Z2), is setting up an EPR-compliant rubber recycling unit in Surat. It plans to simultaneously import two streams: (A) waste parings and scrap of rubber (Basel No. B3080) from a UAE trader, and (B) used electrical and electronic assemblies (Basel No. B1110, serial 4(f) of Schedule VIII) from a Singapore dismantler, both arriving at Mundra Port in April 2025. The company's logistics counsel is preparing a single consolidated Customs dossier. For stream B (B1110 e-waste assemblies), the Singapore exporter claims no special documentation is needed under the Hazardous Waste Rules, while the UAE trader for stream A insists an SPCB acknowledgement must accompany the Form 6. Advise EcoMetal on: (i) whether the SPCB acknowledgement is a Customs requirement for B3080 scrap at the import stage or only for the MoEF&CC application, (ii) the specific documentation gap (if any) for B1110 assemblies, and (iii) whether a single Form 6 can cover both Basel streams in one consignment or separate filings are required.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the only authoritative source distinguishing the documentary regime for Part D Schedule III wastes (Form 6 + DGFT license, SPCB acknowledgement going to MoEF&CC not Customs) from B1110 e-waste assemblies (explicitly 'no specific requirement of documentation under these rules'), directly answering all three sub-questions.

title: Intellectual Property Rights (Imported Goods) Enforcement Rules, 2007
text:
erishable goods on the basis of notice of the right holder or
his authorized representative, the right holder or his authorized representative shall join the proceedings as required under these Rules within three working
days or the extended period as provided in sub-rule (7) and in case of suspension of clearance of perishable good by the Deputy Commissioner of Customs or
Assistant Commissioner of Customs, as the case may be, on his own initiative, the right holder shall give notice, execute a bond and join the proceedings as
required under these Rules within three working days or the extended period as provided in sub-rule (7), as the case may be, failing which the goods shall be
released.

(9) If within ten working days or the extended period under sub-rule (6), as the case may be, and within three working days or the extended period as provided
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers the mandatory timelines and procedural obligations under IPR Enforcement Rules 2007 for right holders to join proceedings when clearance of perishable imported goods is suspended — specifically the 3-working-day deadline, bond execution requirement, and automatic release consequence on failure. This supports HIGH-complexity queries involving multi-party IPR disputes at customs with perishable goods under time pressure.

questions.q: Our client Perfetti Van Melle India Pvt Ltd (trademark holder for 'Mentos') has engaged us after their authorized representative in Mumbai received a notice from Customs (JNCH) on Monday 14-Apr-2025 that a consignment of imported confectionery bearing a deceptively similar mark has been detained. The detained goods are declared as perishable sugar-based confectionery. The importer, Sweetline Traders LLP (GSTIN 27AABCS1234K1Z5, Mumbai), is threatening to sue for wrongful detention if the goods are not released by week's end. Perfetti's authorized rep is currently travelling abroad and cannot execute documents until Thursday 17-Apr-2025. Given the perishable nature of the goods and the specific timelines prescribed under the IPR Enforcement Rules for right holders joining proceedings, can Perfetti's representative validly join on Thursday and prevent automatic release, or has the mandatory window already closed — and what steps (bond, undertaking, application for extension) must be completed before what exact deadline to keep the suspension alive?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly prescribes the 3-working-day deadline within which the right holder or authorized representative must join proceedings, execute a bond, and give notice when clearance of perishable goods is suspended — the precise timeline calculation and 'failing which the goods shall be released' consequence are indispensable to answering whether Thursday filing is timely.

questions.q: Himalayan Naturals LLP, an Uttarakhand-based producer of organic fruit pulps (GSTIN 05AAFCH7890M1ZQ), holds a geographical indication registration for 'Kumaoni Apricot Pulp' and has appointed Fresh Guard Services Pvt Ltd as their authorized customs representative for IPR enforcement purposes. On 09-Apr-2025 (Wednesday), Customs officers at ICD Tughlakabad on their own initiative suspended clearance of a 5-tonne refrigerated consignment of apricot pulp imported from a Turkish exporter, suspecting GI infringement. Fresh Guard was not notified until 10-Apr-2025 (Thursday). Fresh Guard now asks: (a) does the 3-working-day clock to join proceedings run from the date Customs acted suo motu (09-Apr) or from the date notice was actually received by the right holder/representative (10-Apr); (b) since the goods are perishable and Himalayan Naturals is the GI rights holder (not a trademark holder), do the same bond-execution and joinder obligations apply; and (c) if Fresh Guard misses the deadline due to a public holiday on 14-Apr-2025, does that day count as a working day for the purpose of these rules?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's sub-rule 7(8) specifically distinguishes between suspension initiated on the right holder's notice versus the Deputy/Assistant Commissioner acting on his own initiative — and prescribes that in the suo motu case the right holder must give notice, execute a bond, and join within three working days — making the trigger-date ambiguity and holiday-counting question directly governed by this provision.

title: Seeks to amend Notification No.19/2019-Customs dated 06.07.2019

Technical documentation in respect of Goods
