2. 
This notification shall come into force on the 22nd day of September, 2025.

[F. No. CBIC-190341/188/2025-TRU]

(Dheeraj Sharma)

Under Secretary to the Government of India

Note: The principal notification No. 19/2019-Customs, dated the 6th  July, 2019 was published 
in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, vide number GSR 476 (E), dated the 6th  July, 2019 and 
last amended by notification No. 02/2025-Customs dated 16th January, 2025 published in the 
Gazette of India, Extraordinary, vide number GSR 105(E), dated the 1st February, 2025.
claude_reasoning: This chunk covers Notification 37/2025-Customs amending the defence import exemption list (Notification 19/2019-Customs), specifically adding RPA for military use (Sl.No. 39), parts/sub-assemblies for defence goods with specific exclusions (Sl.No. 40), and technical documentation (Sl.No. 41), effective 22 September 2025. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because practitioners must navigate which defence imports qualify for exemption, which are explicitly carved out, and how the amendment interacts with Customs Tariff Act Section 3(3) countervailing duty provisions.

questions.q: Our client, Aero Defence Systems Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 07AABCA1234F1ZX, registered in Delhi), is importing a consignment from Israel consisting of: (a) 50 units of Remote Pilot Aircraft designated for the Indian Army's surveillance wing, (b) spare rotors and battery management sub-assemblies for those RPAs, and (c) a set of 12.7mm SRCG spare barrels and feed mechanisms. The Bill of Entry is dated 25 October 2025. The IGST officer at Delhi Air Cargo is questioning whether the entire consignment qualifies for the customs duty exemption under the defence notification, or whether the 12.7mm SRCG components must be assessed separately at standard rates with countervailing duty under Section 3(3) of the Customs Tariff Act. Can you advise on the correct classification of each line item and the applicable duty treatment, especially in light of the September 2025 amendment?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sl.No. 39 covers RPA for military use (qualifying the drones); Sl.No. 40 grants exemption for parts/sub-assemblies of defence goods but explicitly carves out '12.7mm SRCG' — making the SRCG spare barrels taxable — and the effective date of 22 September 2025 confirms applicability to the October 2025 Bill of Entry. Without this chunk's specific carve-out language, the SRCG exclusion cannot be identified.

questions.q: Bharat Tactical Solutions LLP, an authorised defence contractor in Pune (Maharashtra), has signed a government-to-government supply agreement to import a package from a French OEM comprising: (i) technical manuals and software documentation specifically for Dhanush 155mm howitzers already in service with the Indian Army, (ii) field maintenance kits (tools and testing equipment) for the same Dhanush platform, and (iii) assemblies for a newly procured 155mm/45 Cal artillery system. The import is scheduled for November 2025. A dispute has arisen at Mumbai Port between the importer and the Customs appraisal officer: the officer contends that Dhanush and 155mm/45 Cal items are explicitly excluded from the parts/accessories exemption entry and therefore technical documentation and tools for these platforms are also ineligible; the importer argues that technical documentation falls under a separate entry with no such exclusion. Which items legitimately qualify for exemption under the amended notification, and how should the importer frame its legal argument at the first appellate stage if the assessment order goes against it on the documentation and tools?
questions.why_this_chunk: Sl.No. 40 excludes 'Dhanush' and '155mm/45 Cal' from the parts/tools/testing-equipment exemption, while Sl.No. 41 separately covers 'Technical documentation in respect of Goods mentioned at Sl.Nos. 1 to 40' without repeating those exclusions — creating an interpretive question about whether Sl.No. 41's documentation exemption inherits Sl.No. 40's carve-outs. The effective date (22 Sep 2025) and the amendment lineage (19/2019 last amended by 02/2025) are also directly relevant to the appellate framing.

title: This notification exempts defective articles imported into India which are private personal property
text:
Notification No. 80-Cus., dated 29-8-1970

free warranty - articles supplied as replacement for defective ones

In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 25 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962), the Central
Government, being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby exempts articles and component parts
thereof, when imported respectively for the replacement of defective articles or of component parts thereof, from the whole of
the duty of customs leviable thereon which is specified in the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), and
from the whole of the additional duty leviable thereon under section 3 of the said Customs Tariff Act, subject to the following
conditions, namely :-

(i) the defective articles were brought into India earlier from places outside India and are private personal properties of the
importer;

(ii) the articles or component parts thereof, as the case may be, are imported within the warranty period and are supplied free
of charge by the foreign manufacturer in terms of the warranty given by the manufacturer in accordance with the established
trade practice pertaining to the articles;

(iii) the repairs including replacement of the defective parts are done free of charge by the manufacturer through his agent or
branch in India;

and

(iv) the defective articles or component parts thereof if not re-exported, are destroyed, or surrendered to the Customs.

Notification No. 80-Cus., dated 29-8-1970 as amended by Notification No. 129/86-Cus., dated 17-2-1986.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers customs duty exemption for warranty replacement imports — specifically the four cumulative conditions: private personal property origin, within-warranty-period free supply, free repair/replacement through Indian agent/branch, and re-export/destruction/surrender of defective goods. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving multi-party warranty chains, commercial vs personal property distinctions, and interplay between customs exemption conditions and GST on repair services.

questions.q: Rajesh Menon, an individual resident in Bengaluru (Karnataka), purchased a Hasselblad medium-format camera system (body + three lens modules) during a business trip to Germany in March 2024 — the camera is his private personal property. In November 2025, two lens modules developed an autofocus defect covered under the manufacturer's 2-year international warranty. Hasselblad's authorised Indian service partner, OptiPro Imaging Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCO1234F1ZK, Bengaluru), receives the replacement lens modules shipped free-of-charge from Hasselblad GmbH Hamburg. OptiPro proposes to charge Rajesh a 'handling and logistics coordination fee' of ₹8,500 plus GST for facilitating the warranty claim. At the time of customs clearance: (a) can the replacement lenses be imported free of basic customs duty and additional duty, and what documentary conditions must be strictly met at the port of entry; (b) does the proposed ₹8,500 service fee charged by OptiPro to Rajesh attract GST, and if so under which head; and (c) what must happen to the defective lens modules that Rajesh cannot re-export — does OptiPro's destruction on his behalf satisfy the customs condition or must Rajesh personally surrender them?
questions.why_this_chunk: All four enumerated conditions of the notification are directly at issue: condition (i) — private personal property origin; condition (ii) — within-warranty free supply by foreign manufacturer; condition (iii) — repairs/replacement done free of charge through the Indian agent (OptiPro); condition (iv) — re-export, destruction or surrender of defective parts. The question cannot be answered without applying each condition specifically.

questions.q: Meridian Instruments LLP (GSTIN 07AABFM5678G1ZP, New Delhi) is an MSME that imported a high-precision CNC coordinate measuring machine (CMM) from Zeiss Industrial Metrology GmbH (Germany) in January 2024 for use in its own manufacturing facility — the CMM was cleared as a capital good for the LLP's business, not as private personal property. By October 2025, a critical probe module fails within the 24-month manufacturer warranty. Zeiss's Indian subsidiary, Zeiss India Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCZ9999H1ZT, Pune), offers to ship a replacement probe module free of charge from Germany. Meridian's CFO asks: (i) can the replacement probe module qualify for the customs duty exemption on warranty replacements, given that the original CMM was imported as a business asset of the LLP and not as private personal property of any individual; (ii) if the exemption is unavailable, what is the applicable BCD and IGST on the replacement probe module's CIF value of USD 14,200; and (iii) if Meridian wishes to retain the defective probe for failure analysis rather than destroying or surrendering it, does that disqualify the entire warranty exemption claim even if all other conditions are met?
questions.why_this_chunk: Condition (i) of the notification — that the defective articles must be 'private personal properties of the importer' — is the pivotal gate that likely disqualifies a commercial LLP import. Condition (iv) — the mandatory re-export, destruction, or surrender of defective parts — is the second critical issue. The question is unanswerable without reading these specific conditions in the chunk.

title: UTGST (Andaman & Nicobar Islands )Rules, 2017
text:
deficiencies communicated under sub-rule (3).”;

(c) in rule 117, in sub-rule (1), for the second proviso, the

following proviso shall be substituted, namely:-

“Provided further that in the case of a claim under sub-section 
(1) of section 140, the application shall specify separately—

(i) the value of claims under section 3, sub-section (3) of 
section 5, sections 6 and 6A and sub-section (8) of section 
8 of the Central Sales Tax Act, 1956 made by the 
applicant;  
(ii) the serial number and value of declarations in Forms C 
or F and certificates in Forms E or H or Form I specified 
in rule 12 of the Central Sales Tax (Registration and 
Turnover) Rules, 1957 submitted by the applicant in 
support of the claims referred to in sub-clause (i); and 
(iii) the value of claims of Octroi levied by the Port Blair 
Municipal Council in exercise of powers conferred under 
Section 80 (1) (e) of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands 
(Municipal) Regulations, 1994.”;

(d) in rule 117, clauses (a) and (b) of sub-rule (4) shall be

omitted;

(e) for rule 119, the following rule shall be substituted,

namely:-

“Declaration of stock held by a principal and agent.- 
Every person to whom the provisions of sub-section (14) 
of section 142 apply shall, within ninety days of the 
appointed day, submit a declaration electronically in 
FORM GST TRAN-1, specifying therein, the stock of the 
inputs, semi-finished goods or finished goods, as 
applicable, held by him on the appointed day.”

(f) the following explanation shall be inserted at the end of

these rules, namely:- 
 
‘Explanation.- For the purposes of these rules, it is hereby 
clarified that all references to section 140 of the Central 
Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, shall be construed to 
refer to section 18 of the Union Territory Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017.’

[F.No. S-31011/25/2017-ST-I-DOR]

(S.R.MEENA)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the UTGST transitional credit rules specific to Andaman & Nicobar Islands, including the CST declaration requirements (Forms C/F/E/H/I), Octroi claims against Port Blair Municipal Council, the TRAN-1 filing obligation for principal-agent stock arrangements, and the critical Explanation mapping CGST s.140 to UTGST s.18 — all of which are indispensable to answering transition-credit disputes in this UT jurisdiction.

questions.q: Sunrise Traders, a proprietorship based in Port Blair (Andaman & Nicobar Islands) with GSTIN 35AABCS1234A1ZX, was a registered dealer under the pre-GST Central Sales Tax regime and had made inter-state sales to buyers in Tamil Nadu on Form C declarations and to an export-house in Chennai using Form H certificates, with aggregate CST credit claims of ₹8.4 lakhs pending as of 30-Jun-2017. It also had ₹1.2 lakhs of Octroi paid to the Port Blair Municipal Council on goods imported into the islands. When Sunrise's tax consultant filed TRAN-1 under the UTGST regime to carry forward these credits, the jurisdictional officer rejected the Octroi component entirely and also questioned whether the TRAN-1 filing was governed by CGST section 140 or a separate UTGST provision. How should the consultant respond to both objections, and what specific line items and declarations must appear separately in the TRAN-1 application to substantiate the CST and Octroi claims?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's substituted second proviso to rule 117(1) explicitly requires separate specification of CST claims under ss.3, 5(3), 6, 6A, 8(8) of the CST Act along with Form C/F/E/H/I serial numbers, AND separately the Octroi claims under s.80(1)(e) of the A&N Islands (Municipal) Regulations 1994 — the only provision in the entire GST framework that recognises Port Blair Municipal Octroi as a creditable transition item. The Explanation at the end resolves the officer's jurisdictional objection by mapping s.140 CGST to s.18 UTGST.

questions.q: Marigold Fisheries LLP, registered in Port Blair under UTGST (GSTIN 35AACFM5678B1ZP), operated as a consignment agent for a principal based in Kolkata, holding stock of frozen fish worth ₹22 lakhs in its cold-storage on 1-Jul-2017 (the GST appointed day). The principal had not separately registered in A&N Islands. The LLP's CA is advising on whether the LLP or the principal (or both) must file a TRAN-1 declaration for this agent-held stock, what the deadline was, and whether failure to file within the window extinguishes the transitional credit entitlement on those inputs — given that the applicable provision in A&N Islands is a Union Territory enactment and not the CGST Act. Additionally, would the answer differ if the LLP had also paid Octroi on the same frozen fish when it was brought into Port Blair?
questions.why_this_chunk: The substituted rule 119 in this chunk directly governs the principal-agent stock declaration obligation — 'every person to whom sub-section (14) of section 142 apply shall within ninety days submit FORM GST TRAN-1 specifying stock held on the appointed day' — and the Explanation clarifies that s.140 CGST references are to be read as s.18 UTGST, which is the operative provision for the UT, resolving the jurisdictional question the CA faces. The Octroi angle ties back to rule 117(1)'s requirement to separately itemise Octroi claims, creating a multi-provision dependency.

title: Certification Scheme for Unmanned Aircraft Systems
text:
110  
THE GAZETTE OF INDIA : EXTRAORDINARY 
   [PART II—SEC. 3(ii)]

1. 
सामान्य ितें

1.1 अिेदक (जनमाथता/अयातक) िर समय योजना की अिश्यकताओं का पालन करने के जलए सिमत िै जैसा दक ितथमान

में लागू िै और समय-समय पर संिोजधत िै।  अिेदक ऄपने प्रमाणन जनकाय के माध्यम से क्यूसीअइ को प्रमाणन चि 
(5 ििथ) के जलए प्रिासन िुल्क का भुगतान करने के जलए भी सिमत िोगा।

1.2 अिेदक आस संजिदा में जनजित प्रमाणन के साथ-साथ क्यूसीअइ के साथ ऄपनी संजिदा के ऄनुसार प्रमाणन की ितों का

पालन करने के जलए सिमत िोगा।

1.3 आस योजना का ईद्देश्य यूएएस जनमाथता/अयातक को, यूएएस प्रमाणन ऄपेक्षाओं के जलए लागू प्रमाणन योजना को पूरा

करने की ईनकी क्षमता के जलए प्रमाजणत करना िै।

1.4 अिेदक प्रचार सामग्री, पैम्रलेट, लेटर िेड्स, आसी प्रकार की ऄन्य स्ट्टेिनरी में  दकसी भी संचार के अदान-प्रदान के

जलए मीजडया, योजना के बारे में जागरूकता को बढािा देने अदद कायों के जलए, प्रमाणन जचह्न का ईपयोग कर 
सकता िै। िालांदक, अिेदक यि सुजनजित करने के जलए ईपरोक्त दस्ट्तािेजों का ईपयोग करते समय ध्यान रखने के 
जलए सिमत िै दक जचह्न का ईपयोग केिल यूएएस जनमाथता/अयातक के संबंध में दकया जाता िै और यि धारणा निीं 
देगा दक प्रमाजणत जिस्ट्ताररत ईत्पादों के ऄलािा गैर-प्रमाजणत,  दायरे के ऄंतगथत अने िाले कायाथलयों या संबंजधत 
कंपनी भी प्रमाजणत िैं।

1.5 अिेदक यूएएस योजना प्रमाणन जचह्न का ईपयोग केिल यूएएस जनमाथता/अयातक के संबंध में करने के जलए सिमत

िै जो आसे ददए गए प्रमाणीकरण के तित िाजमल दकया गया िै और प्रमाणन मानदंडों का पालन करना जारी रखेगा।

1.6 अिेदक सिमत िै दक िि िमेिा मौजूदा योजना के ऄनुसार और समय-समय पर संिोजधत प्रमाणीकरण ऄपेक्षाओं को

पूरा करेगा और केिल िैधता ऄिजध के दौरान प्रमाणीकरण जचह्न का ईपयोग करेगा।

1.7 अिेदक यूएएस योजना प्रमाणन जचह्न या क्यूसीअइ के नाम का ईपयोग आस प्रकार निीं करने के जलए सिमत िै जो

क्यूसीअइ के जलए भ्रामक या ऄस्ट्िीकायथ िो सकता िै।

1.8 अिेदक केिल ईस सीमा तक प्रमाणन के दािे करने के जलए सिमत िै जो जििेि रूप से प्रमाणन के ऄंतगथत अते िैं।

1.9 अिेदक आस तरि से जचह्न का ईपयोग निीं करने के जलए सिमत िै जजससे क्यूसीअइ या योजना की प्रजतष्‍ट ठा कम िो

और/या जनता का जिश्वास समाप्त िो जाए।

1.10 
अिेदक के नाम, स्ट्िाजमत्ि या स्ट्थान, जजसके जलए अिेदक ने प्रमाणन प्राप्त दकया िै, में दकसी भी मित्िपूणथ

पररितथन के बारे में जलजखत रूप में क्यूसीअइ को सूजचत करने के जलए अिेदक सिमत िै।

1.11 
अिेदक जबना दकसी देरी के क्यूसीअइ को ईन मामलों के बारे में सूजचत करेगा जो प्रमाणन ऄपेक्षाओं के ऄनुरूप

िोने की ईसकी क्षमता को प्रभाजित कर सकते िैं।

1.12 
अिेदक योजना के संचालन के संबंध में QCI द्वारा मांगी गइ कोइ भी जानकारी देने के जलए सिमत िै

1.13 
अिेदक आस बात से सिमत िै दक ईसका नाम, स्ट्थान और प्रमाणन का दायरा QCI द्वारा ऄनुरजक्षत और प्रकाजित

जनदेजिका में िाजमल िै।

1.14 
अिेदक, ईसे अिंरटत जचह्न के ईपयोग के संदभथ में, घोजित/ऄघोजित/प्रलोभन मामलों में अिेदक की ऄनुपालना

को सत्याजपत करने के जलए और अिेदक के बारे में क्यूसीअइ द्वारा प्राप्त जिकायतों के संबंध में और समय के भीतर आस 
तरि के िुल्क का भुगतान करने के जलए सिमत िै,  जैसा दक क्यूसीअइ द्वारा सूजचत दकया गया िै।

1.15 
अिेदक ईस जतजथ से प्रमाणन जचह्न के ईपयोग को बंद करने के जलए सिमत िै जब से प्रमाणपत्र जनलंजबत, रद्द,

और िापस ले जलया गया िै या बंद िो गया िै।

1.16 
आसके प्रमाणन को जनलंजबत या िापस लेने/रद्द करने पर, अिेदक तत्काल प्रभाि से प्रमाणन जचह्नों के ईपयोग के

संदभथ में सभी जिज्ञापन सामग्री का ईपयोग बंद कर देगा और क्यूसीअइ को आस अिय की एक घोिणा प्रस्ट्तुत करेगा। 
यूएएस योजना के तित प्रमाणन के संबंध में दकसी भी रूप में दािा करने से भी बचेगा।
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the contractual obligations of a UAS manufacturer/importer under the QCI certification scheme — specifically: the 5-year certification cycle admin fee, conditions for mark usage, and the immediate cessation of all advertising/claims upon suspension or cancellation (clauses 1.10–1.16). It supports HIGH-complexity questions where certification status has downstream customs clearance and GST ITC consequences.

questions.q: Skytech Robotics Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCS1234F1ZX, Bengaluru) imported 120 consumer-grade quadcopters from China in February 2025 under their QCI UAS certification. In April 2025, QCI suspends Skytech's certification after a complaint that the certification mark was being used on a product variant not covered under the certified scope. At the time of suspension, 45 units remain unsold in their Bengaluru warehouse and 20 units are already invoiced and in transit to a Delhi dealer. Skytech's counsel wants to know: (1) under the certification scheme's own terms, what immediate obligations arise regarding advertising material and claims — does this include the already-dispatched commercial invoices referencing the UAS certification? (2) given that IGST was paid at import on all 120 units and ITC has been partially passed to the Delhi dealer, does the certification suspension trigger any reversal obligation under GST, and can the suspended status affect the Delhi dealer's ability to claim ITC on the 20 in-transit units?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 1.16 of the chunk is directly indispensable: it specifies that upon suspension the applicant must 'with immediate effect' stop using all advertising material referencing the certification mark and submit a declaration to QCI — this governs whether already-dispatched invoices citing UAS certification constitute continued prohibited use, which is the hinge for the customs/GST downstream analysis.

questions.q: AeroDyne Solutions LLP (Hyderabad, GSTIN 36AAHFA5678B1ZP) paid ₹3,40,000 as administration fees to their QCI-accredited certification body for a 5-year UAS certification cycle in January 2025, covering their role as both a manufacturer of nano-category UAS and an importer of micro-category UAS from Taiwan. In March 2025, the partners resolve to restructure: the manufacturing vertical will be transferred to a newly incorporated wholly-owned subsidiary (AeroDyne Mfg Pvt Ltd, same Hyderabad address), while the LLP retains the import vertical. The LLP's GST consultant raises three issues: (a) the QCI scheme requires written intimation of any significant change in name, ownership, or location — does a partial business transfer that splits the certified scope constitute such a 'significant change' triggering fresh certification for the subsidiary, and if so, does the ₹3,40,000 already paid need to be apportioned or re-paid? (b) is the administration fee paid to the certification body for the 5-year cycle a 'supply of service' attracting 18% GST, and can AeroDyne LLP claim full ITC given the fee covers both manufacturing and import activity? (c) if the certification for the manufacturing scope is effectively cancelled upon transfer and the subsidiary must re-apply, does the LLP need to reverse any ITC attributable to the manufacturing vertical under the proportionate reversal rules?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause 1.10 is indispensable — it specifically obligates the applicant to notify QCI in writing of 'any significant change in name, ownership, or location' for which certification was obtained; this is the precise trigger for determining whether a partial business transfer splits the certification and creates a fresh fee/ITC reversal obligation, a determination no generic GST chunk can provide.

title: Valuation under the new Section 4
text:
1. Obviously, where tariff value has been fixed or any goods, as per provisions of sub-section (2) of sec. 3, value under

section 4 is not determinable and the tariff value will be the assessable value.

2. The definition of "transaction value" needs to be carefully taken not of as there is fundamental departure from the

erstwhile system of valuation that was essentially based on the concept of "Normal Wholesale Price", even though sales
were effected at varying prices to different buyers or class of buyers from factory gate or depots etc., had to be
determined.

3. The new section 4 essentially seeks to accept different transaction value which may be charged by the assessee to

different customers, for assessment purposes so along as these are based upon purely commercial practices rather than
looking for a notionally determined value.

4. "Transaction Value" includes receipts/recoveries or charges incurred or expenses provided for in connection with the

manufacturing, marketing, selling of the excisable goods to be not be part of the price payable for the goods sold. In other
words, whatever elements which enrich the value of the goods before their marketing and were held by Hon'ble Supreme
Court to be includible in "value" under the erstwhile section 4 would continue to form part of section 4 value even under
new section 4 definition. It may also be noted that where the assessee charges an amount as price for his goods, the
amount so charged and paid or payable for the goods will form the assessable value. If, however, in addition to the
amount charged a price from the buyer, the assessee also recovers any other amount by reason of sale or in connection
with sale, then such amount shall also form part of the transaction value for valuation and assessment purposes. Thus, if
assessee splits up his pricing system and charges a price for the goods and separately charges for packaging, the
packaging charges will also form part of assessable value as sit is charge in connection with production and sale of the
goods recovered from the buyer. Again, if any assessee charges warranty charges for any goods in particular
transaction, then the warranty charges shall be included in the transaction value for the goods and duty will be payable on
this part value recovered from the buyer. This will be so even if such warranty charges do not already form part of the
price charged by the assessee for such transaction. In other words, if the warranty charges are charged separately and
not considered as "price" of goods by the assessee, then also warranty charges will be includable in the transaction
value forming basis of valuation. In this context, it may be clarified that it is immaterial whether the warranty is optional or
mandatory. Since the value can be different for different transactions, wherever warranty charges are paid or payable to
the assessee, in those transactions warranty charges shall form part of the assessable value. In those transactions where
warranty charges are not recovered, the question of including warranty charges in transaction value does not arise.

5. It would be seen from the definition of "transaction value" that any amount which is paid or payable by the buyer to or on

behalf of the assessee, on account of the factum of sale of goods, then such amount cannot be claimed to be not part of
the transaction value. In other words, if, for example, an assessee recovers advertising charges or publicity from his
buyers, either at the time of sale of goods or even subsequently, the asessee cannot claim that such charges are not
includable in the transaction value. The law recognises such payment to be part of the transaction value that is
assessable value for those particular transactions.
claude_reasoning: This chunk is an authoritative CBIC circular clarifying the scope of 'transaction value' under the new Section 4 of Central Excise — specifically covering split pricing (goods + packaging/warranty separately billed), the inclusion of ancillary recoveries in assessable value, and the departure from Normal Wholesale Price. It supports HIGH-complexity questions involving multi-party transactions where pricing is structured to exclude certain charges and the legal interpretation of what constitutes assessable value.

questions.q: Precision Hydraulics Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer of industrial pumps in Pune (Maharashtra), sells 500 units to a bulk buyer — Bharat Infrastructure LLP in Chennai — at Rs. 48,000 per unit. The invoice splits the price as: Rs. 42,000 for the pump, Rs. 3,500 for mandatory 2-year warranty coverage, and Rs. 2,500 for heavy-duty export-grade packaging, each billed as separate line items under distinct SAC/HSN heads. The company's CFO argues that since warranty and packaging are invoiced as 'services' under separate heads and are not 'price of goods,' they should be excluded from the assessable value for Central Excise duty computation on the pump. The tax manager disagrees. Given that the transaction predates GST and falls under the erstwhile Central Excise regime, how should the assessable value per unit be determined, and what is the legal basis for including or excluding the warranty and packaging charges?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that packaging charges billed separately still form part of assessable value as they are 'charged in connection with production and sale,' and that warranty charges — whether optional or mandatory, whether separately billed or not — are includable in transaction value whenever recovered from the buyer. These two rulings are directly indispensable to resolving the CFO's argument.

questions.q: Electrotech Components Ltd, a registered Central Excise assessee in Bengaluru, manufactures PCB assemblies and supplies them to three different categories of buyers: (a) OEM clients at Rs. 1,20,000 per batch under long-term contracts that bundle in a 3-year warranty and on-site calibration, included in the negotiated price; (b) spot-market distributors at Rs. 95,000 per batch with no warranty, but with a separately recovered 'after-sales support levy' of Rs. 8,000 billed post-delivery; and (c) an export-linked EOU client in Noida at Rs. 1,05,000 per batch where warranty is optional and only 30% of buyers in that segment opt in, paying Rs. 6,000 additionally. The excise officer proposes to use a uniform 'normal wholesale price' across all three categories for valuation. Electrotech contests this, arguing transaction-specific valuation applies. Advise Electrotech on (i) whether a single assessable value can be applied across all three buyer categories, (ii) how the after-sales support levy in category (b) should be treated, and (iii) whether the optional warranty in category (c) should be included only in transactions where it is actually recovered or uniformly across all EOU-category transactions.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses: (1) the rejection of the Normal Wholesale Price concept in favor of transaction-specific valuation; (2) that amounts recovered 'by reason of sale or in connection with sale' (such as the after-sales support levy) are includable; and (3) the explicit clarification that optional warranty charges are included only in those specific transactions where they are actually recovered — making this chunk the primary authority for all three sub-questions.

title: Export Warehousing- specifying class of exporters under sub-rule 2 of Rule 20 of the Central Excise Rules,(No.2) 2001 read with Notification No. 46/2001-CE(NT) dated 26.06.2001
text:
Circular No. 832/09/2006-CX

F.No. 209 /11/2006-CX-6

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

***

New Delhi, dated  4 th September, 2006.

Subject:- Export Warehousing- specifying class of exporters under sub-rule 2 of Rule 20 of the Central Excise Rules,(No.2)

2001 read with Notification No. 46/2001-CE(NT) dated 26.06.2001.

***

I am directed to refer to Board's Circular No. 581/18/2001-CX dated 29 th June, 2001, which interalia specifies conditions,
procedures, class of exporters and places under sub-rule (2) of rule 20 of Central Excise Rules, 2002 for warehousing of
excisable goods for   the purpose of export.  Vide para 2(1) of the said Circular, Board has specified the class of exporters
who are eligible for availing this facility under Notification No. 46/2001-CE (NT) dated 26.06.2001.

2.         The Foreign Trade Policy, 2004-09 has renamed certain categories of exporters such as 'Super Star Trading House'
and 'Star Trading House' as 'Four Star Export House' and 'Five Star Export House' respectively.  Accordingly the said circular
is required to be suitably amended. The circular also prescribes the eligibility criteria of Export houses to set up warehouses
for export purposes. However, Para 3.5.2.1 (viii) of the Foreign Trade Policy, 2004-09 now permits all export houses
recognized as Two Star Export Houses and above to establish Export Warehouses as per the guidelines issued by the
Department of Revenue. Therefore, as a measure to facilitate trade and in order to align the provisions of the departmental
circular with that of the Foreign Trade Policy, 2004-09, para 2(1) of the aforesaid Circular is being suitably amended as
follows:

(1)        Exporters: "The exporters who have been accorded status of Two Star Trading House and above as per the provisions
of the Foreign Trade Policy, 2004-09; the foreign departmental stores of repute and the automobiles manufacturers who have
signed Memorandum of Understanding with Directorate General of Foreign Trade in the Ministry of Commerce and Industry."

3.            Trade & field formations may please be informed suitably.

4.            Receipt of the Circular may be acknowledged.

5.            Hindi version will follow.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the eligibility criteria for setting up export warehouses under Rule 20 of the Central Excise Rules — specifically, which class of exporters (Two Star Export House and above, foreign departmental stores, automobile manufacturers with DGFT MoU) qualify under Notification No. 46/2001-CE(NT) as amended by Circular 832/09/2006-CX. It supports HIGH-complexity queries involving exporter status, warehouse eligibility, and interplay between Foreign Trade Policy and Central Excise rules.

questions.q: Sunrise Auto Components Pvt Ltd, a Pune-based manufacturer of automotive parts with a DGFT-recognized 'One Star Export House' status, wants to set up a private export warehouse in JNPT SEZ to store excisable goods duty-free pending shipment to its buyer in Germany. The consignment is valued at ₹4.2 crore and the company has not yet signed any MoU with DGFT. Their CE consultant is arguing that as an automobile-sector exporter they qualify under the export warehousing scheme. Does Sunrise Auto qualify to set up the warehouse, and if not, what is the precise path to eligibility — including whether signing a DGFT MoU alone would suffice or whether the MoU route is restricted to automobile manufacturers in a specific sense?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that automobile manufacturers who have signed an MoU with DGFT qualify as a distinct category — separate from the Star Export House track — which is indispensable to evaluating whether Sunrise Auto's sector alone confers eligibility or whether the MoU is the operative trigger.

questions.q: Meridian Exports LLP, a Chennai-based textile exporter, held 'Star Trading House' status under the old Foreign Trade Policy and had been operating an export warehouse under the Central Excise export warehousing scheme since 2003. Following the Foreign Trade Policy 2004-09, its status was administratively relabelled as 'Four Star Export House' by DGFT. A Central Excise audit in 2007 is now questioning whether Meridian's warehouse authorisation remains valid post-renaming, arguing that the original approval was granted to a 'Star Trading House' and no fresh approval was obtained after the rename. Meridian's counsel contends continuity of status. Who has the stronger legal position, and what is the interaction between the FTP 2004-09 renaming, the amended circular, and the original Notification No. 46/2001-CE(NT) on the question of warehouse authorisation continuity?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly records that 'Star Trading House' was renamed 'Four Star Export House' under FTP 2004-09 and that the departmental circular was amended to align with this — the authoritative basis for arguing status continuity and whether a fresh approval is legally required.

title: Customs and Central Excise Duties Drawback Rules, 2017
text:
ned Foreign Mission of India about the fact of non-recovery of sale
proceeds from the buyer, the amount of drawback paid to the exporter or the claimant shall not be recovered.

Rule 19. Power to relax. -

If the Central Government is satisfied that in relation to the export of any goods, the exporter or his authorised agent has, for
reasons beyond his control, failed to comply with any of the provisions of these rules, and has thus been entitled to drawback, it
may, after considering the representation, if any, made by such exporter or agent, and for reasons to be recorded in writing,
exempt such exporter or agent from the provisions of such rule and allow drawback in respect of such goods.

Rule 20. Repeal and saving. -

(1) From the commencement of these rules, the Customs, Central Excise Duties and Service Tax Drawback Rules, 1995 shall
cease to operate.

(2) Notwithstanding such cesser of operation -

(a) every application made by a manufacturer or an exporter for the determination or revision of the amount or rate of
drawback in respect of goods exported before the commencement of these rules but not disposed of before such
commencement shall be disposed of in accordance with the provisions of the Customs, Central Excise Duties and Service
Tax Drawback Rules, 1995 as if these rules had not been made;

(b) any claim made by an exporter or his authorised agent for the payment of drawback in respect of goods exported before
the commencement of these rules but not disposed of before such commencement shall be disposed of in accordance with
the provisions of the Customs, Central Excise Duties and Service Tax Drawback Rules, 1995 as if these rules had not been
made;

(c) every amount or rate of drawback determined under the Customs, Central Excise Duties and Service Tax Drawback
Rules, 1995 and in force immediately before the commencement of these rules shall cease to operate in respect of goods
exported on or after commencement of these rules.
claude_reasoning: Rule 19 grants the Central Government discretionary power to waive non-compliance with Drawback Rules 2017 for reasons beyond the exporter's control, and Rule 20 governs the transitional regime between the 1995 Rules and the 2017 Rules — both anchors for high-complexity practitioner scenarios involving force majeure drawback relief and mid-shipment regulatory transition.

questions.q: Meridian Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZX, Mumbai) shipped a consignment of processed marine products worth ₹1.8 crore to a buyer in Rotterdam in January 2017 under the then-applicable 1995 Drawback Rules, but due to a port labour strike and subsequent COVID-precursor customs freeze, they could not file the drawback claim before the 2017 Rules came into force on 01-Oct-2017. The shipping bill was dated 15-Aug-2017 and the drawback claim was filed only on 20-Nov-2017. The jurisdictional Commissioner has rejected the claim saying it must now be processed under the 2017 Rules, while the company argues the export pre-dates the 2017 Rules and the 1995 Rules should govern. Additionally, they argue that the delay in filing was due to reasons entirely beyond their control (port strike + systems migration). Can Meridian Exports Pvt Ltd simultaneously invoke the transitional savings clause and seek discretionary relief from the Central Government for the procedural delay, and which set of rules — 1995 or 2017 — will actually govern the substantive determination of the drawback amount?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 20(2)(b) directly governs whether a claim for exports made before 01-Oct-2017 but filed after commencement is processed under 1995 or 2017 Rules; Rule 19 is indispensable for the force majeure / beyond-control relief argument for the procedural delay — both rules appear verbatim in this chunk.

questions.q: Pinnacle Pharma LLP (Hyderabad, GSTIN 36AAFCP5678K1ZM) exported active pharmaceutical ingredients to a distributor in Nairobi under a Letter of Credit dated 10-Sep-2017. The export took place on 25-Sep-2017 — five days before the 2017 Drawback Rules came into force. Due to an RBI-mandated banking channel change for Kenya-bound remittances, the foreign exchange realisation certificate (FIRC) could not be obtained in time, and Pinnacle was unable to comply with the export proceeds realisation condition required for drawback eligibility. The bank confirmed the funds were received but routed through an intermediary correspondent bank in UAE, causing a 14-month delay in FIRC issuance. The drawback application (filed under the 1995 Rules per their understanding of the savings clause) was rejected on two grounds: (a) the claim disposition should follow 2017 Rules since it was filed post-commencement, and (b) non-realisation of proceeds disqualifies drawback. Pinnacle now wants to know: does the savings clause protect their right to have the claim adjudicated under 1995 Rules, and separately, can they petition the Central Government to condone the FIRC-related non-compliance as a circumstance beyond their control, given that the banking restriction was a regulatory directive and not voluntary?
questions.why_this_chunk: Rule 20(2)(b) is the pivotal provision on whether post-commencement filing of a pre-commencement export claim is governed by 1995 or 2017 Rules; Rule 19's 'reasons beyond his control' standard is the only legal basis for condoning the FIRC non-compliance caused by the RBI banking directive — both are sourced exclusively from this chunk.

title: Seeks to amend Notification No 05/2017- Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28.06.2017
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i) OF THE GAZETTE OF INDIA,

EXTRAORDINARY]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 23/2023- Integrated Tax (Rate)

New Delhi, the 19th October, 2023

G.S.R.     (E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by clause (ii) of the proviso to sub-section (3) of section

54 of  the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) read with section 20 of the Integrated Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017 (13 of 2017) the Central Government, on the recommendations  of  the  Council,  hereby  
makes  the  following  further  amendments  in  the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance 
(Department of Revenue), No. 5/2017-Integrated  Tax  (Rate),  dated  the  28th  June,  2017,  published  in  the  Gazette  
of  India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 670(E), dated the 28th  June, 2017, 
namely :-

In the said notification, in the TABLE, after S. No. 6A and the entries relating thereto, following S.No. and

the entries shall be inserted, namely:-

(1) 
(2) 
(3)

“6AA. 
5605 
Imitation zari thread or yarn made out of Metallised polyester film /plastic film; 
 
Explanation: This entry shall apply for refund of input tax credit only on polyester film /plastic 
film”;

2. 
This notification shall come into force with effect from the 20th day of October, 2023.

[F. No. CBIC-190354/195/2023-TO(TRU-II)-CBEC]

Vikram Wanere

Under Secretary

Note: - The principal notification No.5/2017- Integrated  Tax  (Rate), dated the 28th day of June, 2017, was published 
in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 670(E)., dated the 28th 
day of June, 2017, and was last amended by notification No. 9/2022 – Integrated  Tax  (Rate), dated the 13th July, 
2022, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) vide number G.S.R. 560(E)., 
dated the 13th July, 2022.
claude_reasoning: This chunk inserts S.No. 6AA (HSN 5605) into the inverted-duty-structure refund notification (No. 5/2017-IT Rate) under section 54(3) of CGST Act, but restricts the ITC refund exclusively to polyester film/plastic film as input — not all inputs used in manufacture of imitation zari thread. This creates high-complexity practitioner issues around refund computation methodology, input segregation, and cross-act linkage between IGST Act s.20 and CGST Act s.54(3).

questions.q: Shree Textile Udhyog LLP (GSTIN 24AAFFS1234A1ZX), a Surat-based manufacturer of imitation zari thread (HSN 5605) using metallised polyester film, sizing chemicals, dyes, and carton packaging, filed a refund claim for accumulated ITC of ₹38 lakhs for the quarter ending December 2023. The jurisdictional GST officer is proposing to restrict the refund to only the ITC attributable to polyester film/plastic film purchases, disallowing ITC on chemicals, dyes, and packaging even though all these inputs bear GST and go into the same final product sold at a lower rate. The LLP's CA argues the formula under Rule 89(5) does not make this distinction and refund should be on net ITC. Who is correct, and how should the eligible refund be computed — specifically, does the explanation inserted by the October 2023 notification override the Rule 89(5) formula or does it only limit eligibility of inputs for the purpose of that S.No.?
questions.why_this_chunk: The Explanation under S.No. 6AA explicitly states 'This entry shall apply for refund of input tax credit only on polyester film/plastic film' — this is the operative restriction the officer is relying on and the LLP is contesting; the chunk is the sole authoritative source for whether the refund scope is input-specific or formula-driven for this HSN.

questions.q: Zari Creations Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCZ5678B1ZQ), Maharashtra, manufactures imitation zari thread (HSN 5605) and supplies it on an inter-state basis to a garment exporter in Tamil Nadu — the garment exporter uses the zari thread in apparel that is then physically exported. Zari Creations wishes to claim a refund of accumulated IGST input tax credit under the inverted duty structure on its inter-state supplies of zari thread. Two issues arise: (a) does Notification 23/2023-IT(Rate), which amends the IGST Rate notification via section 20 of the IGST Act read with section 54(3) CGST, apply to IGST ITC accumulated on inputs (polyester film imported by Zari Creations and paying IGST at customs), and (b) can the garment exporter separately claim a refund under LUT/bond on the IGST paid to Zari Creations, or does the restriction in the Explanation for S.No. 6AA affect downstream refund chains? Advise on both refund entitlements with the correct legal basis.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's legal authority derives from s.54(3) CGST read with s.20 IGST Act — this cross-act invocation is directly in the notification's preamble and determines whether IGST ITC on imported polyester film qualifies; the Explanation's scope ('refund of input tax credit only on polyester film/plastic film') must be interpreted in the context of both IGST and CGST refund regimes simultaneously.

title: Seeks to amend No. 18/2022-Central Excise, dated the 19th July, 2022 to reduce the Special Additional Excise Duty on production of Petroleum Crude and increase the Special Additional Excise Duty on export of ATF.
text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-
SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 41/2023-Central Excise

New Delhi, the 18th December, 2023

G.S.R......(E).–In exercise of the powers conferred by section 5A of the Central Excise Act, 1944  (1  of

1944) read  with  section  147  of  the Finance  Act,  2002  (20  of  2002), the  Central Government, on being 
satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby makes the following further amendments in the 
notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 18/2022-Central 
Excise, dated the 19th July, 2022, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section 
(i), vide number G.S.R. 584 (E), dated the 19th July, 2022, namely:-

In the said notification, in the Table, -

(i) 
against S. No. 1, for the entry in column (4), the entry “Rs. 1300 per tonne” shall be substituted;

(ii) 
against S. No. 2, for the entry in column (4), the entry “Rs. 1 per litre” shall be substituted

2. 
This notification shall come into force on the 19th day of December, 2023.

[F. No. 354/15/2022-TRU]

(Amreeta Titus)

Deputy Secretary to the Government of India

Note: The principal notification No. 18/2022-Central Excise, dated the 19th July, 2022 was published in the 
Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 584 (E), dated the 19th 
July, 2022, and was last amended vide notification No. 40/2023-Central Excise, dated the 30th November, 2023, 
published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 871(E), 
dated the 30th November, 2023.
claude_reasoning: This chunk is an amendment notification that sets specific SAED rates effective 19-Dec-2023: Rs.1300/tonne on Petroleum Crude (S.No.1) and Rs.1/litre on motor spirit/ATF (S.No.2), amending the windfall tax framework under Notification 18/2022-CE. It authoritatively anchors questions requiring knowledge of the exact rate, effective date, and the amendment chain (18/2022 → 40/2023 → 41/2023).

questions.q: Hindustan Petro Extractions Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCH1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) operates an upstream crude oil production block in Rajasthan and lifts 18,000 tonnes of Petroleum Crude during the billing fortnight 19-Dec-2023 to 31-Dec-2023. Their tax team is computing the SAED liability for that fortnight. The prior fortnight (1-Dec to 18-Dec-2023) was governed by rates under Notification 40/2023-CE. They want to know: (a) what SAED rate per tonne applies to crude lifted on and after 19-Dec-2023, (b) whether the rate change mid-fortnight requires them to split the fortnight into two separate self-assessment challans, and (c) how the amendment chain from the original Notification 18/2022-CE through 40/2023-CE to the present notification affects which entry in the SAED table governs their liability — particularly given that both notifications amend the same S.No.1 column (4) entry sequentially.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly substitutes the S.No.1 column (4) entry with Rs.1300 per tonne effective 19-Dec-2023, and identifies the immediately preceding amendment as 40/2023-CE dated 30-Nov-2023 — both facts are indispensable to determining the correct rate for the split fortnight and tracing the amendment chain.

questions.q: Bharat Fuels & Chemicals LLP, a refinery in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AAKFB9876Q1ZA), produces motor spirit (petrol) and also blends it with ethanol before dispatch ex-refinery. For the period 19-Dec-2023 onwards, their in-house counsel needs to determine: (i) the applicable SAED rate per litre on motor spirit under the latest amendment, (ii) whether the blended petrol (say 90% MS + 10% ethanol) attracts SAED on the full volume or only the petroleum-derived portion, given that SAED under the windfall-tax framework is levied on 'production' and ethanol is not petroleum crude-derived, and (iii) how the interplay between the Central Excise Act 1944 (section 5A exemption/rate-fixing power) and Finance Act 2002 section 147 (which extends excise provisions to the SAED levy) affects the legal validity of applying this amended rate to blended fuels not specifically mentioned in the notification's table.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk sets Rs.1/litre as the substituted S.No.2 rate effective 19-Dec-2023 and invokes both s.5A of the Central Excise Act 1944 and s.147 of the Finance Act 2002 as the dual legislative basis — the legal-interpretation question about blended fuels and the validity of the dual-statute basis cannot be answered without these specific provisions and the exact rate in this notification.

title: This notification exempts from the operation of rule 9 (Registration) of the Central Excise (No. 2) Rules, 2001.
text:
26 th June, 2001

Notification No.35/2001-Central Excise(N. T.)

In exercise of powers conferred by rule 9 of the Central Excise (No.2) Rules, 2001, (hereinafter referred to as the said rules) the
Central Board of Excise and Customs hereby specifies the conditions, safeguards and procedures for registration of a person
under the said rules and exemptions from registration in specified cases: -

(1) Application for registration: Every person specified under sub-rule (1) of rule 9, unless exempted from doing so by the
Board under sub-rule (2) of rule 9, shall get himself registered with the jurisdictional Superintendent of Central Excise by
applying in the form specified in Annexure-I;

(2) Registration of different premises of the same registered person: If the person has more than one premises requiring
registration, separate registration certificate shall be obtained for each of such premises.

(3) Registration Certificate and Number: Registration Certificate in the Form specified in Annexure-II containing registration
number shall be granted within seven days of the receipt of the duly complete application.

(4) Transfer of Business: Where a registered person transfers his business to another person, the transferee shall get
himself registered afresh.

(5) Change in the Constitution: Where a registered person is a firm or a company or association of persons, any change in
the constitution of firm, company or association, shall be intimated to the jurisdictional Central Excise Officer within thirty days
of such change.

(6) De-registration: Every registered person, who ceases to carry on the operation for which he is registered, shall de-register
himself by making a declaration in the form specified in Annexure-III and depositing his registration certificate with the
Superintendent of Central Excise.

(7) Revocation or suspension of registration: A registration certificate granted under this rule may be revoked or
suspended by the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise or the Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise, if the holder of
such certificate or any person in his employment, is found to have committed breach of any of the provisions of the Act or the
rules made thereunder or has been convicted of an offence under section 161, read with section 109 or with section 116 of the
Indian Penal Code (45 of 1860).

Annexure-I

FORM A-1

Application for Registration to cure/produce/manufacture/conduct wholesale trade/act as a broker

or commission agent/obtain excisable goods, for industrial purpose etc.

[ Rule 9 ]

(Delete the letters and words not applicable)

To

The Superintendent of Central Excise,

Sir,

I/We_____________________________Son/Daughter(s) of___________________ residing at
____________________hereby request that I/We _______________________ may be issued a registration certificate
under Rule 9 for the purposes indicated in the schedule. Other details required are also furnished in the schedule.

2. I/We___________________ agree to abide by all the terms and conditions of the registration which may be imposed from
time to time.

3. I/We_________________________ agree to abide by all the provisions of Central Excise (No.2) Rules, 2001 and any
orders issued thereunder.

4. I/We_________________ declare to the best of my/our knowledge and belief the information furnished herein is true and
complete.

Place:

Date:

Signature of the applicant
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers Central Excise registration procedures under Notification 35/2001-CE(NT) — specifically rules on transfer of business requiring fresh registration, change-in-constitution intimation within 30 days, separate registration for multiple premises, and revocation/suspension triggers. These procedural rules create HIGH-complexity scenarios when layered with corporate restructuring, multi-location manufacturing, and legacy excise compliance obligations that survive into GST transition.

questions.q: Aravind Polymers Pvt Ltd, a Central Excise registered manufacturer of plastic granules (ECC No. AAACA1234AXM001) operating from a factory in Silvassa (Dadra & Nagar Haveli), underwent a private equity-driven merger in March 2006 where it was absorbed into Reliance Plastics LLP, with the LLP subsequently opening a second manufacturing unit in Pune. The successor LLP never obtained fresh Central Excise registration and continued operating on the old Aravind Polymers certificate for nearly 18 months before a Central Excise audit in September 2007. The audit team is now proposing to treat all clearances made from both the Silvassa and Pune units during this period as clearances without registration, invoking extended period of limitation. The LLP's advocate argues that since Aravind Polymers' registration was never formally revoked, the LLP's operations were covered. Assess the validity of the department's position versus the LLP's defence, specifically with reference to: (a) the obligation arising upon transfer of business, (b) the requirement for separate premises registration, and (c) whether non-registration alone triggers extended limitation or whether suppression must be separately established.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (4) of the notification mandates fresh registration by the transferee upon business transfer — directly defeating the LLP's argument that the old certificate survived. Clause (2) requires separate registration for each premises — making the Pune unit's clearances independently unregistered. Both provisions are indispensable to the department's case and the limitation analysis.

questions.q: Bharat Forge Components Pvt Ltd, registered under Central Excise at its Aurangabad plant (CEID: AABCB5678CXM002) for manufacture of forged steel auto parts, underwent a Board resolution in January 2004 converting itself from a private limited company into a partnership firm — Bharat Forge Components & Associates — with three partners, two of whom were the original promoter-directors. The firm did not intimate the jurisdictional Superintendent within the prescribed period. Fourteen months later, one of the three partners retired from the firm, which was again not intimated. When the firm applied for CENVAT credit on capital goods procured during this period, the Range Officer denied the credit on the ground that the registration was void ab initio due to non-compliance with change-in-constitution requirements, and separately initiated proceedings for revocation of the certificate under the notification's revocation provisions. The firm's CA contends that (i) the 30-day intimation window is directory not mandatory, (ii) CENVAT credit entitlement is independent of registration technicalities, and (iii) revocation requires a finding of breach of the Act or conviction under the IPC — neither of which occurred here. Evaluate each of the firm's three contentions in light of the specific procedural obligations and revocation grounds set out in the notification.
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (5) of the notification imposes the 30-day intimation obligation for change in constitution of a firm or company — directly applicable to both the conversion to partnership and the partner's retirement. Clause (7) defines the exhaustive grounds for revocation (breach of Act/rules or IPC conviction), which is the linchpin for evaluating whether non-intimation alone can support revocation proceedings.

title: Seeks to supersede Notification No. 116/2010-Customs, dated 01-11-2010 (Special dispensation for Brunei Darussalam under ASEAN-India FTA)
text:
TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE)

Notification

No. 63 /2012-Customs

New Delhi, dated the 31 st December, 2012

G.S.R. 948  (E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 25 of the Customs Act, 1962 (52 of 1962),
and in supersession of the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue),
No.116/2010-Customs, dated the 1 st November, 2010, published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-
section (i) vide number G.S.R. 875 (E), dated the 1 st November, 2010, except as respects things done or omitted to be done
before such supersession,the Central Government, on  being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, hereby
exempts Crude Petroleum oils and oils obtained from bituminous minerals falling under the tariff item  2709 00 00 of the First
Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 (51 of 1975), when imported into India from Brunei Darussalam, from whole of the 
duty of customs leviable thereon under the First Schedule to the Customs tariff Act, 1975 ( 51 of 1975):

Provided that the importer proves to the satisfaction of the Deputy Commissioner of Customs or Assistant Commissioner of
Customs, as the case may be, that the goods in respect of which the benefit of this exemption is claimed are of the origin of
Brunei Darussalam, in accordance with provisions of the  Customs Tariff [Determination of Origin of Goods under the
Preferential Trade Agreement between the Governments of Member States of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations
(ASEAN) and the Republic of India] Rules, 2009, published in the notification of the Government of India in the Ministry of
Finance (Department of Revenue), No. 189/2009-Customs (N.T.), dated the 31st December 2009.

2.      This notification shall come into force with effect from 1st day of January, 2013.

[F. No. 354/64/2003-TRU (Pt.I)]

(Akshay Joshi)

Under Secretary to the Government of India
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the nil customs duty exemption on Crude Petroleum (CTH 2709 00 00) imported from Brunei Darussalam under the ASEAN-India FTA, conditional on origin proof per the 2009 ASEAN origin rules — a highly specific provision combining tariff classification, preferential trade agreement origin criteria, and supersession of a prior notification.

questions.q: Reliance Petro Imports Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, IEC AABCR1234A) has contracted to import 50,000 MT of crude petroleum from a Brunei-based supplier, Brunei Energy Co Ltd, at USD 72/bbl CIF Nhava Sheva, scheduled for arrival on 10-Feb-2025. The supplier proposes to route the shipment through Singapore for transshipment, and the Bill of Lading will show Singapore as the port of loading. The supplier can provide a Form AI (ASEAN-India FTA Certificate of Origin) issued by the Brunei authority certifying Brunei origin. Customs at Nhava Sheva is questioning whether the nil duty exemption on crude petroleum from Brunei under the ASEAN-India FTA is still available given (a) transshipment through Singapore, (b) the fact that Notification 116/2010-Customs was the original dispensation and it is unclear if it was validly superseded, and (c) whether tariff item 2709 00 00 covers all grades of crude or only specific grades. Advise Reliance Petro Imports on each of these three issues and the risk of duty demand if the exemption is denied on a 50,000 MT shipment.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk is the operative notification (63/2012-Customs) that supersedes 116/2010-Customs and grants nil duty specifically on CTH 2709 00 00 crude petroleum from Brunei; it also specifies that origin must be proved per the 2009 ASEAN origin rules — both the supersession question and the origin-proof mechanism are answerable only from this chunk.

questions.q: Bharat Hydrocarbon LLP (GSTIN 27AABFB5678M1ZK, Mumbai) imports crude petroleum from Brunei Energy Resources Sdn Bhd under a long-term supply agreement. For the January 2025 shipment (B/E filed 15-Jan-2025), the LLP claimed nil Basic Customs Duty under the ASEAN-India FTA Brunei dispensation. However, the Assistant Commissioner of Customs issued a show-cause notice alleging that: (i) the Certificate of Origin submitted is a retrospectively issued certificate (issued 20-Jan-2025 for goods that arrived 12-Jan-2025), and (ii) the goods were blended with 8% condensate of Malaysian origin at the Brunei terminal before loading, potentially destroying Brunei origin status under the applicable preferential origin rules. Additionally, the LLP's CFO asks whether, if BCD exemption is lost, IGST on the import (calculated on Assessable Value + BCD) would also increase and whether input tax credit of such IGST is fully available given that crude petroleum is a restricted ITC item under GST. Analyse all three issues.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's proviso requiring the importer to prove Brunei origin 'in accordance with the 2009 ASEAN origin rules' to the Deputy/Assistant Commissioner is directly at issue for both the retrospective COO question and the blending/substantial transformation question; without this chunk the applicable origin-proof standard and the competent officer cannot be identified.

title: Import OF SELENIUM BEARING WASTES (A1010) AND WASTE HAVING SELENIUM; SELENIUM COMPOUNDS OR CONSTITUENTS OR CONTAMINANTS (A1020) IS PROHIBITED.
