claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the multi-layered pre-import compliance requirements for e-waste and electronic assemblies under the Hazardous Waste import rules — specifically the DGFT import authorisation, statutory consents under Water/Air Acts, one-time trader authorisation (Form 7), Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) authorisation under E-Waste (Management & Handling) Rules 2011, customs document-verification gate, and the 60-day Ministry of Environment review with SPCB comments. These interlock multiple regulatory authorities and legal instruments, making them ideal anchors for HIGH-complexity practitioner questions.

questions.q: Electronica Refurb LLP, registered in Tamil Nadu (GSTIN 33AABFE1234Q1ZX), is a trading house that imports second-life laptop motherboards and CPU modules — listed under Schedule VIII (Basel B1110) — on behalf of three actual users: two IT companies in Pune and one in Hyderabad. The LLP's customs house agent in Chennai has received a show-cause notice alleging that separate authorisations should have been obtained for each consignment and each actual user, whereas the LLP claims a single one-time authorisation suffices. Simultaneously, the Pune actual users argue they need EPR authorisation only in their own names, not the LLP's. Please advise: (a) what is the correct authorisation architecture — one combined authorisation vs. per-user per-consignment — and which specific form and enclosure must accompany each Bill of Entry; (b) whether the EPR obligation under the E-Waste (Management & Handling) Rules 2011 attaches to the LLP as importer-trader or to the actual users as producers; and (c) what documentary gate does Customs apply before allowing clearance, and who bears the liability if documents are found deficient post-clearance?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (3) of the chunk explicitly provides the one-time authorisation mechanism via Form 7 for a trader importing on behalf of actual users, requiring a copy to be annexed with Form 6 — directly resolving (a). Clause (3) sub-part also mandates that when the imported goods are listed under Schedule I of E-Waste Rules 2011, the importer must obtain EPR authorisation as a 'producer' — directly resolving (b). Clause (4) specifies that Customs verifies documents per Schedule VIII column (3) before clearance — resolving (c).

questions.q: Greentek Circuits Pvt Ltd, a Maharashtra-based manufacturer (GSTIN 27AABCG5678R1Z1), wants to import used printed circuit board assemblies (Schedule III Part D waste) consigned from a recycler in Germany, for in-house dismantling and metal recovery at its Nagpur facility. The company already holds valid consents under the Water and Air pollution Acts from Maharashtra SPCB. Its procurement head asks: (a) is DGFT import authorisation a mandatory pre-condition for this consignment, or can customs clear on the basis of SPCB consents alone; (b) what is the specific document-verification step that the customs authority at Nhava Sheva must perform before approving the shipment, and which schedule column governs this; and (c) if the Ministry of Environment's review process is triggered, what is the maximum statutory window before a decision must be communicated, and what external body's comments must the Ministry consider — and can Greentek's shipment be held at port during this entire window without any provisional release?
questions.why_this_chunk: Clause (क) of the chunk establishes DGFT import authorisation as a distinct, independent requirement ('if applicable') separate from pollution-control consents — resolving (a). Clause (4) mandates Customs verify documents per Schedule VIII column (3) before approving Schedule III Part D waste shipments — resolving (b). Clause (5) specifies the 60-day window and the mandatory consideration of State Pollution Control Board comments before MoEFCC grants import permission — directly resolving (c).

title: Circular No. 52/26/2018-GST dated 09.08.2018 i.r.o. clarification regarding applicability of GST rates on various goods and services.
text:
8.3 
According to the General Rules for Interpretation [GRI- 3(b)] of the First Schedule to

the Customs Tariff Act (CTA), 1975, “Mixtures, composite goods consisting of different 
materials or made up of different components, and goods put up in sets for retail sale, which 
cannot be classified by reference to 3 (a), shall be classified as if they consisted of the material 
or component which gives them their essential character, insofar as this criterion is 
applicable.” Since primary function of the article should be taken into consideration while 
deciding the classification, it is clear that the essential character of the wipes in the instant case 
is imparted by the components which are to be mixed with the textile material. 
  
8.4 
As per the explanatory notes to the HSN, the HS code 5603 clearly excludes non-

woven, impregnated, coated or covered with substances or preparations such as perfumes or 
cosmetics, soaps or detergents, polishes, creams or similar preparations. The HSN is 
reproduced as follows : “The heading also excludes:

Nonwoven, impregnated, coated or covered with substances or preparations [i.e.

perfumes or cosmetics (Chapter 33), soaps or detergents (heading 3401), polishes, creams, or 
similar preparations (heading 3405), fabric, softeners (heading 3809)] where the textile 
material is present merely as a carrying medium. Further, HS code 3307 covers wadding, felt 
and non-woven, impregnated, coated or covered with perfumes or cosmetics. The HS code 
3401, would cover paper, wadding, felt and non-woven impregnated, coated or covered with 
soap or detergent whether or not perfumed”.

8.5 
Further, as per the explanatory notes to the HSN, the heading 3307 includes wadding,

felt and nonwovens impregnated, coated or covered with perfume or cosmetics. Similarly, as 
per explanatory notes to the HSN, the heading 3401 includes wipes made of paper, wadding, 
felt and nonwovens, impregnated, coated or covered with soap or detergent, whether or not 
perfumed or put up for retail sale.

8.6 
Thus, the wipes of various kinds (as stated above) are classifiable under heading 3307

or 3401 depending upon their constituents as discussed above. Therefore, if the baby wipes are 
impregnated with perfumes or cosmetics, then the same would fall under HS code 3307 and 
would attract 18% GST rate. Similarly, if they are coated with soap or detergent, then it would 
fall under HS code 3401 and would attract 18% GST.

9.1 
Classification and applicable GST rate on real zari Kasab (thread): Certain doubts

have been raised regarding the classification and applicable GST rate on Kasab thread (a 
metallised yarn) as yarn falling under heading 5605 attracts 12% GST, as per entry 137 of the 
Schedule-II-12% of the notification No.01/2017-Central Tax (rate) dated 28.06.2017, while 
specified embroidery product falling under 5809 and 5810 attracts GST @ 5%, as per entry no. 
220 of the Schedule-I-5% of the above-mentioned notification.

9.2 
The heading 5809 and 5810 cover embroidery and zari articles. These heading do not

cover yarn of any kinds. Hence, while these headings apply to embroidery articles, embroidery 
in piece, in strips, or in motifs, they do not apply to yarn, including Kasab yarn.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers two specific GST classification disputes: (1) baby wipes classified under HS 3307 vs 3401 depending on impregnating substance, both attracting 18% GST via GRI-3(b) essential-character rule; and (2) Kasab/zari thread classified under 5605 at 12% — not under 5809/5810 at 5% — because those headings cover embroidery articles not yarn. Both rulings involve cross-act interaction (Customs Tariff Act GRI + GST rate notifications) and legal interpretation of HSN exclusions/inclusions.

questions.q: Sparkle Impex Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-based importer and distributor (GSTIN 07AABCS1234F1ZX), sources two SKUs from a Bengaluru manufacturer: (a) 'BabyFresh' wet wipes impregnated with chamomile fragrance and aloe-vera cosmetic extract, and (b) 'HygieneFirst' antibacterial wet wipes coated with a sodium lauryl sulphate detergent solution. The manufacturer has been classifying both SKUs under HS 5603 (non-wovens) and charging 12% GST. Sparkle's in-house counsel suspects the classification is wrong but the manufacturer argues the textile substrate gives the essential character. A GST audit notice has now been issued for FY 2023-24 demanding differential tax. What is the correct HS classification and GST rate for each SKU, which interpretive rule determines essential character here, and what is the differential tax exposure if Sparkle's purchases were ₹4.8 crore for SKU (a) and ₹3.2 crore for SKU (b) across the audit period?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's GRI-3(b) essential-character analysis, the HSN exclusion of impregnated non-wovens from HS 5603, and the specific rule that fragrance/cosmetic wipes fall under 3307 at 18% while soap/detergent wipes fall under 3401 at 18% are indispensable to resolving the differential-tax calculation for each SKU.

questions.q: Vastra Zari Works LLP, a Surat-based manufacturer (GSTIN 24AABFV5678K1ZM), produces two products on the same production line: (i) raw Kasab metallised yarn (sold in spools to embroidery units in Rajasthan), and (ii) finished embroidered Kasab strips already woven into fabric motifs (sold to garment exporters in Tirupur). The LLP has been applying a uniform 5% GST rate on both products, relying on the embroidery/zari heading, and has accumulated ₹60 lakh of ITC claims at that rate. The Tirupur exporters are now seeking LUT-based zero-rated supply treatment and have asked for revised tax invoices. A show-cause notice from the Surat CGST Commissionerate alleges misclassification of the raw yarn and demands 12% on those supplies. Can the LLP defend the 5% rate on raw Kasab yarn, and separately, what is the correct treatment for the finished embroidered strips — and how does the answer change the ITC reversal obligation and the export-refund entitlement for the Tirupur buyers?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's ruling that headings 5809/5810 do not cover yarn of any kind — including Kasab yarn — and that 5605 at 12% is the correct heading for raw metallised yarn, while 5809/5810 apply only to finished embroidery articles, is the pivotal fact that determines the misclassification, ITC reversal quantum, and downstream export-refund implications.

title: Seeks to provide concessional rate of Central Excise duty on specified goods of Chapter 84-98
text:
ANNEXURE

Condition 
No. 
Conditions

1.  
The duty would be leviable as if the value of the spinnerettes were equal to- 
(i) 
the cost of exchange, that is to say, the aggregate of- 
(a)  labour charges; 
(b)  price charged for that quantity of gold, platinum and rhodium, if any, which is in 
excess of the respective quantity of gold, platinum or rhodium contained in the 
worn out or damaged spinnerettes; and 
(c) any other charges paid for the exchange of such spinnerettes; and 
(ii) 
the insurance and freight charges, both ways.

2.  
The exemption shall be allowed if it has been proved to the satisfaction of an officer not 
below the rank of the Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise or the Assistant 
Commissioner of Central Excise, as the case may be, having jurisdiction, that such goods 
are cleared for the intended use specified in column (3) of the Table. 
3.  
Where such use is elsewhere than in the factory of production, the exemption shall be 
allowed if the procedure laid down in the Central Excise (Removal of Goods at 
Concessional Rate of Duty for Manufacture of Excisable Goods) Rules, 2001, is followed. 
4.  
If, a certificate issued by the Collector/ District Magistrate/ Deputy Commissioner of the 
District in which the plant is located, is produced to the Deputy Commissioner of Central 
Excise or the Assistant Commissioner of Central Excise, as the case may be, having 
jurisdiction, to the effect that such goods are cleared for the intended use specified in 
column (3) of the Table. 
5.  
If no credit of duty paid on the chassis and compressor has been taken under rule 3 or rule 
11 of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2002. 
6.  
If,- 
(i) 
made from unrecorded articles falling under heading 8523; and 
(ii)   (a) not intended for sale; or 
        (b)  intended for sale or supply to All India Radio or any other Department of 
Government of India in the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting; or 
       (c) intended for sale or supply, in the form of U-matic video tapes formats of width 
not less than 19 millimetres, to Doordarshan. 
7.  
If,- 
(a) intended for use by the Indian Railways or the Konkan Railway Corporation; and 
(b) the ownership of the said goods vests in the Indian Railways or the Konkan Railway 
Corporation. 
8.  
(a) The manufacturer pays duties of excise at the rate specified under the First Schedule 
and the Second Schedule read with exemption contained in any notification of the 
Government of India in the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue), at the time 
of clearance of the vehicle; 
(b) the manufacturer takes credit of the amount equal to the amount of duty paid in excess 
of that specified under this exemption, in the Account Current, maintained in terms of 
Part V of the Excise Manual of Supplementary Instructions issued by the Central 
Board of Excise and Customs and thereafter files a claim for refund of the said 
amount of duty before the expiry of six months from the date of payment of duty on
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the specific conditions attached to concessional Central Excise duty notifications for Chapter 84-98 goods, including: (1) the precise valuation formula for spinnerette exchanges (precious-metal differential + labour + freight), (2) the procedural gateway of the CE (Removal of Goods at Concessional Rate) Rules 2001 when goods are used outside the factory of production, and (3) the refund-via-Account-Current mechanism under Condition 8 with a hard 6-month limitation. These are narrow, operationally specific rules that support practitioner-grade scenario questions.

questions.q: Fibratex Synthetics Pvt Ltd, a synthetic textile manufacturer in Surat (GSTIN 24AABCF1234A1Z5), operates spinnerette-based filament extrusion lines. Their German OEM, Enka GmbH, offers an exchange programme: Fibratex ships worn-out spinnerettes (containing 18g gold, 4g platinum, 0.5g rhodium each) and receives refurbished ones back. The refurbished units contain 20g gold, 4.5g platinum, and 0.6g rhodium each. Enka charges: (a) EUR 210 per unit as labour/reworking, (b) EUR 85 per unit for the precious-metal top-up (excess gold/platinum/rhodium), and (c) EUR 30 per unit handling. Ocean freight both ways is EUR 40/unit and insurance is EUR 6/unit. The consignment is cleared at Nhava Sheva and then dispatched to Fibratex's plant. Their Central Excise consultant in Surat is arguing that only the labour charge (EUR 210) forms the assessable value because the spinnerettes 'belong' to Fibratex throughout. The jurisdictional AC-CE disagrees. Under the conditions annexed to the concessional-rate notification for Chapter 84-98 goods, what is the correct method to arrive at the value on which Central Excise duty is leviable for the exchanged spinnerettes, and which specific cost components must be included or excluded?
questions.why_this_chunk: Condition 1 of the Annexure sets out the exhaustive valuation formula for spinnerette exchange: labour charges + price of precious-metal excess quantities (gold/platinum/rhodium above what was in the worn-out units) + any other charges + insurance and freight both ways. This formula directly resolves the dispute — the precious-metal top-up and freight/insurance both ways are expressly included, refuting Fibratex's consultant's position.

questions.q: Ashok Motors Ltd, a commercial-vehicle manufacturer in Pune, cleared 120 units of a 16-tonne chassis-mounted refrigerated vehicle in March 2006 under a concessional-rate Central Excise notification for Chapter 84-98 goods. The applicable concessional rate was 8% ad valorem. However, at the time of clearance the company's ERP system erroneously applied the standard Schedule-I rate of 16% and debited the excess duty to its Account Current as required. The additional controller of accounts now wants to file a refund claim for the excess duty paid (approximately Rs. 1.14 crore) but the claim is being filed in October 2006 — seven months after the date of duty payment on the first batch. The DC-CE (Pune Commissionerate) is refusing the refund, citing time-bar. Separately, the company's internal audit has flagged that CENVAT credit was availed on the compressor units fitted to these vehicles. Advise Ashok Motors on: (i) whether the October 2006 refund claim is maintainable given the 6-month window stipulated in the Annexure conditions, and (ii) whether the prior availment of CENVAT credit on the compressors independently disentitles the company from the concessional rate, even if the time-bar issue were resolved in its favour.
questions.why_this_chunk: Condition 8 of the Annexure prescribes the exact mechanism — pay at Schedule-I/II rates, credit the excess to Account Current, then file a refund claim 'before the expiry of six months from the date of payment of duty.' This is the specific rule that creates the time-bar question. Condition 5 independently bars the concessional rate if CENVAT credit has been taken on the chassis or compressor, creating the second prong of the analysis.

title: Application for Registration as Tax Deductor at source (u/s 51) or Tax Collector at source (u/s 52)
text:
goods received from SEZs shall be declared here. It may be noted that the total 
ITC availed is to be classified as ITC on inputs and capital goods. Table 
4(A)(1) of FORM GSTR-3B may be used for filling up these details. 
[For FY 2017-18 and 2018-19, the registered person shall have an option to 
either report the breakup of input tax credit as inputs and capital goods or 
report the entire input tax credit under the ―inputs‖ row only.]87 
[For FY 2019-20, the registered person shall report the breakup of input tax

remaining amount as inputs and input services or report the entire remaining 
amount under the ―inputs‖ row only.]88

Details of input tax credit availed on import of services (excluding inward 
supplies from SEZs) shall be declared here. Table 4(A)(2) of FORM GSTR-
3B may be used for filling up these details.

6G 
Aggregate value of input tax credit received from input service distributor 
shall be declared here. Table 4(A)(4) of FORM GSTR-3B may be used for

6H 
Aggregate value of input tax credit availed, reversed and reclaimed under the 
provisions of the Act shall be declared here.

6J 
The difference between the total amount of input tax credit availed through 
FORM GSTR-3B and input tax credit declared in row B to H shall be 
declared here. Ideally, this amount should be zero.

6K 
Details of transition credit received in the electronic credit ledger on filing of 
FORM GST TRAN-I including revision of TRAN-I (whether upwards or 
downwards), if any shall be declared here.

6L 
Details of transition credit received in the electronic credit ledger after filing 
of FORM GST TRAN-II shall be declared here.

6M 
Details of ITC availed but not covered in any of heads specified under 6B to 
6L above shall be declared here. Details of ITC availed through FORM ITC-
01 and FORM ITC-02 in the financial year shall be declared here.

and

7H

Details of input tax credit reversed due to ineligibility or reversals required 
under rule 37, 39, 42 and 43 of the CGST Rules, 2017 shall be declared here. 
This column should also contain details of any input tax credit reversed under 
section 17(5) of the CGST Act, 2017 and details of ineligible transition credit 
claimed under FORM GST TRAN-I or FORM GST TRAN-II and then 
subsequently reversed. Table 4(B) of FORM GSTR-3B may be used for 
filling up these details. Any ITC reversed through FORM ITC -03 shall be 
declared in 7H. If the amount stated in Table 4D of FORM GSTR-3B was not 
included in table 4A of FORM GSTR-3B, then no entry should be made in 
table 7E of FORM GSTR-9. However, if amount mentioned in table 4D of 
FORM GSTR-3B was included in table 4A of FORM GSTR-3B, then entry

claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers ITC classification rules in GSTR-9 annual return — specifically the breakup requirements for inputs vs capital goods across FY 2017-18, 2018-19, and 2019-20, ITC reversal heads (rules 37, 39, 42, 43, section 17(5)), transition credit (TRAN-I/TRAN-II), ISD credits, and the reconciliation row 6J. It supports HIGH-complexity queries because practitioners must navigate year-specific relaxations, multi-rule reversal triggers, and cross-form reconciliation simultaneously.

questions.q: Bharat Infra Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCB1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) is filing its GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20. During the year it availed ₹48 lakh ITC — ₹12 lakh on capital goods (two CNC machines purchased in July 2019), ₹22 lakh on input services, and ₹14 lakh on raw material inputs. Additionally, it received ₹3.2 lakh ITC from its Input Service Distributor (HO in Delhi). In table 4A of GSTR-3B, the finance team had clubbed all ₹48 lakh under 'inputs' without breaking out capital goods. Now at annual return time, the CFO wants to know: (a) Is the company legally required to report the ₹12 lakh capital goods ITC separately in GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20, or can it continue clubbing under 'inputs' as done in GSTR-3B? (b) Where exactly must the ₹3.2 lakh ISD credit be declared, and does it flow into the row 6J reconciliation? (c) If row 6J does not reconcile to zero after these adjustments, what is the exposure?
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's FY 2019-20 specific instruction mandates that capital goods ITC must be broken out separately (unlike FY 2017-18/2018-19 where full clubbing under 'inputs' was permitted), while only the remaining amount has the option to be clubbed — directly answering (a). Row 6G governs ISD credit declaration and row 6J governs reconciliation — directly answering (b) and (c).

questions.q: Sunrise Pharma LLP (GSTIN 29AACFS9876K1ZQ, Karnataka) had availed ₹9.5 lakh ITC through FORM GST TRAN-I in FY 2017-18 on closing stock of excise-paid goods. Of this, ₹2.1 lakh was subsequently found ineligible by the GST officer during scrutiny and was reversed in a later GSTR-3B period. Also, during FY 2017-18, the LLP availed ₹6 lakh ITC on imported services from its Singapore-based parent (no PE in India), and separately reversed ₹1.4 lakh ITC on exempt supplies under rule 42 of CGST Rules. While preparing GSTR-9 for FY 2017-18, the tax manager is confused about: (a) whether the ₹2.1 lakh ineligible TRAN-I credit reversal should appear in table 7E or 7H or some other head; (b) whether the ₹6 lakh import-of-services ITC goes into row 6E or 6F; and (c) given the FY 2017-18 relaxation, can the entire ITC (including the import-of-services credit) be lumped under 'inputs' in the breakup rows, or does the relaxation apply only to domestic inputs vs capital goods?
questions.why_this_chunk: Row 7H specifically covers ITC reversed through FORM ITC-03, while the chunk states that ineligible transition credit claimed under TRAN-I and subsequently reversed must be declared in rows 7A–7H — resolving (a). Row 6F governs import of services (excluding SEZ inward supplies) — resolving (b). The FY 2017-18 relaxation clause in the chunk (option to report entire ITC under 'inputs' row only) — resolving (c), including whether it extends to all ITC categories.

title: Duty Free Import Authorization (DFIA) Scheme - availment of facility under rule 18(rebate of duty paid on materials used in the manufacture of resultant product) or sub-rule (2) of rule 19 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002 or Cenvat credit
text:
(d) as regards the authorizations issued prior to 1.4.2007, the DOC in para 4.4.6 of the FTP(2008) has provided that,
exemption from payment of additional duty of customs /excise duty shall continue to be available, even after endorsement of
transferability. In view of this, no action need be taken to recover revenue in such cases.

This has the approval of the Competent Authority.

10.        In this background the Commissioners of Customs/Customs & Central Excise, through whose jurisdiction exports
under DFIA scheme have taken place, shall review all cases of such exports and take appropriate measures to recover duties
wherever required in terms of these instructions. The Commissioners will accordingly device suitable procedures in this regard.
A suggested questionnaire has been appended to this circular which may be used to get requisite  information from the
exporters. The recovery action may thereafter be initiated after following the due procedure of law.

11.        The jurisdictional Chief Commissioner of Customs/Customs & Central Excise as the case may be, may supervise the
above process and send a factual report to the Board on or before 31 st May, 2009. The report may inter-alia contain the
duties, which need to be recovered and the status of such recoveries, year wise.

12.        As regards, future cases, the Commissioners may kindly go through the provisions of the notification No.17 dated
19.2.09 and take action accordingly.

13.        These instructions may be brought to the notice of the trade / exporters by issuing suitable Trade / Public Notices. 
Suitable Standing orders/instructions may be issued for the guidance of the assessing officers.  Difficulties faced, if any in
implementation of the Circular may please be brought to the notice of the Board at an early date.

14.        These instructions are being issued in terms of section 151A of the Customs Act, 1962.

15.       Receipt of this Circular may kindly be acknowledged.

Yours faithfully,

Sd/-

(P.V.K. Rajasekhar)

OSD(DBK)

Questionnaire

1.                  Whether the inputs used in the manufacture of the goods exported under the DFIA scheme were imported or
procured indigenously?

2.                  If the goods were procured indigenously, whether the facility under rule 18  (rebate of duty paid on materials used
in the manufacture of resultant product) or sub-rule (2) of rule 19 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002 or Cenvat credit under
CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004 was availed in respect of such inputs?

3.                  The details of the shipping bills under which the above exports took place and the details of the authorizations
issued against the said exports and the port of registration.

4.                  Whether the DFIAs so obtained have been used to import / procure duty free replenishments?

5.                  If so, whether the said replenishments have been used in the manufacture of dutiable goods. If yes, a certificate
from the jurisdictional Central Excise Superintendent to this effect may be furnished. If not, then the proof of payment of all
duties of customs on replenishments used in the manufacture of non dutiable goods may be furnished.

6.                  If the replenishments have been transferred, whether applicable additional customs duty/ excise duty has been
paid in terms of Para 4.72 of the Hand Book of Procedures. The proof of payment i.e the copy of TR-6 Challan may be
furnished.

7.                  Whether the DFIA so obtained has been transferred? If yes, the details i.e. the name, address and IEC number of
the transferee may be furnished.
claude_reasoning: This chunk authoritatively covers the DFIA scheme's duty-recovery framework, specifically the carve-out for pre-1.4.2007 authorizations (exemption survives transferability endorsement), the dual-trigger rule-18/rule-19(2)/CENVAT-credit bar on DFIA eligibility, and the obligation to pay additional customs/excise duty on transferred DFIAs under Para 4.72 HBP — all of which create layered cross-act liability questions ideal for HIGH-complexity queries.

questions.q: Precision Forgings Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZK, Maharashtra) exported alloy-steel flanges in FY 2005-06 and FY 2008-09 under two separate DFIA authorisations. The 2005-06 authorisation was endorsed as transferable in March 2007 and subsequently sold to a Mumbai trader; the 2008-09 authorisation was used by Precision itself to import nickel alloy inputs duty-free. During a post-export audit in 2024, the jurisdictional Commissioner of Customs (JNCH) raises a show-cause notice alleging (a) that CENVAT credit on input chemicals was availed before the 2005-06 exports, making those exports ineligible for DFIA duty exemption, and (b) that the transferred 2005-06 authorisation's buyer never paid the additional customs duty required on transfer. Precision argues that the 2005-06 authorisation, having been issued prior to 1 April 2007, carries a blanket exemption from additional duty of customs even post-transfer under the DOC policy in the then-current FTP. Advise Precision on: (i) whether the pre-1.4.2007 carve-out insulates the transferred authorisation from duty recovery despite CENVAT availed on inputs; (ii) what evidentiary steps the Commissioner's questionnaire requires Precision to satisfy for the 2008-09 authorisation; and (iii) which authority (JNCH or the Chief Commissioner) must submit the recovery-status report and by what deadline.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's para (d) explicitly states that for authorisations issued prior to 1.4.2007, exemption from additional duty/excise duty continues even after transferability endorsement — this is the precise carve-out Precision relies on. The questionnaire items 2 and 6 in the chunk define the CENVAT/rule-18/rule-19(2) disqualification test and the TR-6 Challan proof requirement for transferred DFIAs. Para 11 specifies the Chief Commissioner reporting deadline of 31 May 2009 (and the reporting structure), which is the jurisdictional-supervision question.

questions.q: Axiom Chemicals LLP (Rajasthan, IEC 0814012345) exported speciality dyes in 2006-07 under DFIA and received an authorisation that was later endorsed as transferable. Axiom sold that authorisation to Vertex Dyes Pvt Ltd (Gujarat), which used it to import duty-free titanium dioxide. Vertex subsequently transferred the titanium dioxide to a job-worker who used it to manufacture pigments for exempt goods (non-dutiable). In a 2024 enforcement review, the Ahmedabad Customs Commissionerate issues notices to both Axiom and Vertex, contending: (1) Axiom had availed rebate under rule 18 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002 on inputs used in the exported dyes, which bars the DFIA benefit; and (2) Vertex's use of the duty-free replenishment in manufacture of non-dutiable goods triggers full customs duty recovery. Vertex counters that since Axiom's original authorisation pre-dates 1 April 2007, any additional duty question is extinguished. Separately, Axiom argues that even if rule-18 rebate was availed, the circular's own questionnaire procedure must be completed before any recovery action can be initiated, and that a certificate from the jurisdictional Central Excise Superintendent is the required evidentiary standard. Analyse: (i) whether the pre-1.4.2007 exemption under para (d) of this circular shields Vertex from additional customs duty on the transferred DFIA; (ii) whether Axiom's rule-18 availed status independently breaks the DFIA chain regardless of the pre-2007 date; and (iii) what documentary proof Vertex must produce to the Commissioner to discharge its burden regarding the non-dutiable end-use of the replenishments.
questions.why_this_chunk: The chunk's questionnaire item 5 specifically addresses duty liability when replenishments are used in manufacture of non-dutiable goods and requires a Central Excise Superintendent certificate or proof of duty payment — this is the precise evidentiary standard at issue for Vertex. Para (d) of the chunk is the only source for the pre-1.4.2007 transferability exemption that Vertex invokes. Questionnaire item 2 defines the rule-18/rule-19(2)/CENVAT bar which is the basis for Ahmedabad Customs' notice against Axiom.
